revision.comment: /* Early life and education */ organized info to flow better...he grew up in Monongah, which is just outside Fairmont, where he was born

{{Short description|American football coach (born 1951)}} {{distinguish|Nick Sagan}} {{pp-blp|small=yes}} {{Use mdy dates|date=June 2025}} {{Infobox college coach | name = Nick Saban | image = Nick Saban (cropped).jpg | alt = | caption = Saban at the [[White House]] in 2018 | birth_date = {{Birth date and age|1951|10|31}} | birth_place = [[Fairmont, West Virginia]], U.S. | death_date = | death_place = | alma_mater = | player_years1 = 1970–1972 | player_team1 = [[Kent State Golden Flashes football|Kent State]] | player_positions = [[Defensive back]] | coach_years1 = 1973–1974 | coach_team1 = [[Kent State Golden Flashes football|Kent State]] ([[Graduate assistant|GA]]) | coach_years2 = 1975–1976 | coach_team2 = Kent State (LB) | coach_years3 = 1977 | coach_team3 = [[Syracuse Orange football|Syracuse]] (OLB) | coach_years4 = 1978–1979 | coach_team4 = [[West Virginia Mountaineers football|West Virginia]] (DB) | coach_years5 = 1980–1981 | coach_team5 = [[Ohio State Buckeyes football|Ohio State]] (DB) | coach_years6 = 1982 | coach_team6 = [[Navy Midshipmen football|Navy]] (DB) | coach_years7 = 1983–1987 | coach_team7 = [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]] ([[Defensive coordinator|DC]]/DB) | coach_years8 = 1988–1989 | coach_team8 = [[Houston Oilers]] (DB) | coach_years9 = 1990 | coach_team9 = [[Toledo Rockets football|Toledo]] | coach_years10 = 1991–1994 | coach_team10 = [[Cleveland Browns]] (DC) | coach_years11 = 1995–1999 | coach_team11 = Michigan State | coach_years12 = 2000–2004 | coach_team12 = [[LSU Tigers football|LSU]] | coach_years13 = 2005–2006 | coach_team13 = [[Miami Dolphins]] | coach_years14 = 2007–2023 | coach_team14 = [[Alabama Crimson Tide football|Alabama]] | overall_record = 292–71–1 (college){{#tag:ref|Saban's on-the-field record in 2007 was 7–6 (4–4 SEC).{{Cite web |title=2007 Southeastern Conference Year Summary |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfbconferences/sec/2007.html |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} The NCAA ruled that Alabama must vacate 21{{nbsp}}victories due to sanctions stemming from textbook-related infractions. The infractions, and 16{{nbsp}}of the vacated victories, began under previous coach [[Mike Shula]], and continued until they were discovered during the 2007 season, Saban's first at Alabama, and thus the official NCAA record for that year reflects a 2–6 mark.{{Cite web |date=March 23, 2010 |title=Alabama's penalty from '09 ruling stands |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=5021464 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160118045302/http://sports.espn.go.com/ncf/news/story?id=5021464 |archive-date=January 18, 2016 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |agency=[[Associated Press]]}}|group="n"|name=2007season}}
15–17 (NFL) | bowl_record = 19–12 | tournament_record = 9–5 ([[College Football Playoff|CFP]]) | championships = {{Plainlist| * 7 [[College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS|National]] ([[2003 NCAA Division I-A football season|2003]], [[2010 BCS National Championship Game|2009]], [[2012 BCS National Championship Game|2011]], [[2013 BCS National Championship Game|2012]], [[2016 College Football Playoff National Championship|2015]], [[2018 College Football Playoff National Championship|2017]], [[2021 College Football Playoff National Championship|2020]]) * [[Mid-American Conference|MAC]] ([[1990 Toledo Rockets football team|1990]]) * 11 [[List of SEC conference champions#Football|SEC]] ([[2001 SEC Championship Game|2001]], [[2003 SEC Championship Game|2003]], [[2009 SEC Championship Game|2009]], [[2012 SEC Championship Game|2012]], [[2014 SEC Championship Game|2014]]–[[2016 SEC Championship Game|2016]], [[2018 SEC Championship Game|2018]], [[2020 SEC Championship Game|2020]], [[2021 SEC Championship Game|2021]], [[2023 SEC Championship Game|2023]]) * 16 [[List of SEC conference champions#Divisional champions|SEC West Division]] (2001–2003, 2008, 2009, 2012–2018, 2020–2023) }} | awards = {{Plainlist| * ESPY Icon Award ([[2024 ESPY Awards|2024]]) * [[George Munger Award]] (2016){{Cite web |title=The George Munger Award |url=https://maxwellfootballclub.org/the-george-munger-award/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227201615/https://maxwellfootballclub.org/the-george-munger-award/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Maxwell Football Club}} * [[Bobby Dodd Coach of the Year Award|Bobby Dodd Coach of the Year]] (2014){{Cite web |last=Enoch |first=Ed |date=December 30, 2014 |title=Alabama head coach Nick Saban named Bobby Dodd Coach of the Year |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2014/12/30/alabama-head-coach-nick-saban-named-bobby-dodd-coach-of-the-year/29945006007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230417182702/https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2014/12/30/alabama-head-coach-nick-saban-named-bobby-dodd-coach-of-the-year/29945006007/ |archive-date=April 17, 2023 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=The Tuscaloosa News}} * 3× [[Bobby Bowden National Collegiate Coach of the Year Award|Bobby Bowden Coach of the Year]] (2009, 2011, 2012){{Cite web |last=Breland |first=Tony |date=March 4, 2013 |title=Nick Saban, Bobby Bowden Share the Stage for Coach of the Year Award |url=https://bamahammer.com/2013/03/04/nick-saban-bobby-bowden-share-the-stage-for-coach-of-the-year-award/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227203552/https://bamahammer.com/2013/03/04/nick-saban-bobby-bowden-share-the-stage-for-coach-of-the-year-award/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Bama Hammer}} * 2× [[Walter Camp Coach of the Year Award|Walter Camp Coach of the Year]] (2008, 2018){{Cite web |title=Walter Camp Coach of the Year Award Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/camp-coy.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230127235505/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/camp-coy.html |archive-date=January 27, 2023 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} * [[Home Depot Coach of the Year Award|Home Depot Coach of the Year]] (2008){{Cite web |title=Home Depot Coach of the Year Award Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/home-depot-coy.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227201841/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/home-depot-coy.html |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} * [[Sporting News College Football Coach of the Year|Sporting News Coach of the Year]] (2008){{Cite web |date=December 17, 2008 |title=Saban Sporting News National Coach of the Year |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2008/12/17/Saban_Sporting_News_National_Coach_of_the_Year |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227202006/https://rolltide.com/news/2008/12/17/Saban_Sporting_News_National_Coach_of_the_Year |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Alabama Crimson Tide|University of Alabama Athletics]]}} * [[Liberty Mutual Coach of the Year]] (2008){{Cite web |last=Walsh |first=Christopher |date=December 31, 2008 |title=Saban named Liberty Mutual Coach of Year |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2008/12/31/saban-named-liberty-mutual-coach-of-year/27780475007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227202055/https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2008/12/31/saban-named-liberty-mutual-coach-of-year/27780475007/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=The Tuscaloosa News}} * 2× [[Paul "Bear" Bryant Award]] (2003, 2020){{Cite web |title=Paul "Bear" Bryant Coach of the Year Award Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/bryant.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227201257/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/bryant.html |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} * 2× [[Associated Press College Football Coach of the Year Award|AP College Football Coach of the Year]] (2003, 2008){{Cite web |title=AP Coach of the Year Award Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/ap-coy.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227201734/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/ap-coy.html |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} * 2× [[Eddie Robinson Coach of the Year Award|Eddie Robinson Coach of the Year]] (2003, 2008){{Cite web |title=Eddie Robinson Coach of the Year Award Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/robinson.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221201234738/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/robinson.html |archive-date=December 1, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} * 5× [[Southeastern Conference football individual awards#Coach of the Year|SEC Coach of the Year]] (2003, 2008, 2009, 2016, 2020){{Cite web |title=SEC Coach of the Year Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/sec-coy.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221208032629/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/sec-coy.html |archive-date=December 8, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} }} | coaching_records = | CFBHOF_year = 2025 | CFBHOF_id = 2560 }} '''Nicholas Lou Saban Jr.''' ({{IPAc-en|'|s|eɪ|b|ən}} {{respell|SAY|bən}}; born October 31, 1951){{Cite web |last=Schexnayder |first=C. J. |date=April 26, 2013 |title=Nick Saban |url=http://www.encyclopediaofalabama.org/article/h-3443 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221209034426/http://www.encyclopediaofalabama.org/article/h-3443 |archive-date=December 9, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=Encyclopedia of Alabama}} is an American [[sportscaster]] and former [[American football|football]] coach. He serves as an analyst for [[ESPN]]'s ''[[College GameDay (football TV program)|College GameDay]]'', a television program covering [[college football]]. He is widely considered to be one of the greatest football coaches of all time.{{Cite web |last=Dodd |first=Dennis |date=January 5, 2018 |title=The debate is truly over: Why Nick Saban is the greatest coach of all time |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/the-debate-is-truly-over-why-nick-saban-is-the-greatest-coach-of-all-time/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180712052131/https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/the-debate-is-truly-over-why-nick-saban-is-the-greatest-coach-of-all-time/ |archive-date=July 12, 2018 |access-date=July 11, 2018 |website=CBSSports.com}}{{Cite web |last=Culpepper |first=Chuck |date=January 9, 2018 |title=Nick Saban is the top college football coach of all time |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/sports/colleges/nick-saban-is-the-top-college-football-coach-of-all-time/2018/01/09/bcf99eac-f56a-11e7-a9e3-ab18ce41436a_story.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180712040440/https://www.washingtonpost.com/sports/colleges/nick-saban-is-the-top-college-football-coach-of-all-time/2018/01/09/bcf99eac-f56a-11e7-a9e3-ab18ce41436a_story.html |archive-date=July 12, 2018 |access-date=July 11, 2018 |newspaper=The Washington Post}} Saban served as head coach of the [[National Football League]] (NFL)'s [[Miami Dolphins]] and at four universities: the [[University of Toledo]], [[Michigan State University]], [[Louisiana State University]] (LSU), and most famously the [[University of Alabama]], where he last coached from [[2007 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|2007]] to [[2023 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|2023]] and led the team to six [[College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS|national championships]] in nine championship appearances during that period. As a college football head coach, Saban won seven national titles, the most in college football history.{{Cite web |last=Staats |first=Wayne |date=January 12, 2021 |title=College football coaches with the most national championships |url=https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2021-01-12/college-football-coaches-most-national-championships |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230312023511/https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2021-01-12/college-football-coaches-most-national-championships |archive-date=March 12, 2023 |access-date=August 25, 2021 |website=NCAA.com}} His first came when he led the [[LSU Tigers football|LSU Tigers]] to the [[BCS National Championship Game|BCS National Championship]] in 2003. He then coached the [[Alabama Crimson Tide football|Alabama Crimson Tide]] to BCS and AP national championships in 2009, 2011, 2012, and to [[College Football Playoff]] championships in 2015, 2017 and 2020. He became the first coach in college football history to win a [[College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS|national championship]] with two different [[Football Bowl Subdivision]] (FBS) schools since the inception of the [[AP Poll]] in 1936.{{Cite web |last=Maisel |first=Ivan |author-link=Ivan Maisel |date=December 6, 2009 |title=Saban, Tide good for each other |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/bowls09/columns/story?columnist=maisel_ivan&id=4796521 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100213092209/http://sports.espn.go.com/ncf/bowls09/columns/story?columnist=maisel_ivan&id=4796521 |archive-date=February 13, 2010 |access-date=April 1, 2010 |website=[[ESPN.com]]}} Saban and [[Bear Bryant]] are the only coaches to win an SEC championship at two different schools.{{Cite web |last=Kausler Jr. |first=Don |date=December 12, 2012 |title='The Process' will result in Nick Saban's 2013 induction into the Alabama Sports Hall of Fame |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2012/12/the_process_will_result_in_nic.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224220635/https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2012/12/the_process_will_result_in_nic.html |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]]}} Saban's career record as a college head coach is 292–71–1. Saban was inducted into the [[Alabama Sports Hall of Fame]] in 2013 and the [[Louisiana Sports Hall of Fame]] in 2025.{{Cite web |date=July 23, 2019 |title=Nick Saban |url=https://www.ashof.org/inductees/nick-saban/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210907144758/https://www.ashof.org/inductees/nick-saban/ |archive-date=September 7, 2021 |access-date=August 25, 2021 |website=Alabama Sports Hall of Fame}}{{cite web |last1=Vapnek |first1=Harrison |title=Nick Saban inducted into Louisiana Sports Hall of Fame |url=https://www.kalb.com/2025/06/29/nick-saban-inducted-into-louisiana-sports-hall-fame/ |publisher=KALB.com |access-date=8 September 2025 |date=28 June 2025}} He coached four [[Heisman Trophy]] winners at Alabama: [[Mark Ingram II]] (2009), [[Derrick Henry]] (2015), [[DeVonta Smith]] (2020), and [[Bryce Young]] (2021).{{Cite web |title=Heisman Memorial Trophy Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/heisman.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201005203701/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/heisman.html |archive-date=October 5, 2020 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} In 2025, Saban will be inducted into the [[College Football Hall of Fame]] as a coach.{{Cite web |date=January 10, 2025 |title=Nick Saban Elected to College Football Hall of Fame |url=https://footballfoundation.org/news/2025/1/10/nick-saban-elected-to-college-football-hall-of-fame.aspx |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250110234045/https://footballfoundation.org/news/2025/1/10/nick-saban-elected-to-college-football-hall-of-fame.aspx |archive-date=January 10, 2025 |access-date=January 10, 2025 |publisher=National Football Foundation}}{{Cite web |last=Windham |first=Katie |date=January 10, 2025 |title=Nick Saban Named to 2025 College Football Hall of Fame Class |url=https://www.si.com/college/alabama/football/nick-saban-named-to-2025-college-football-hall-of-fame-class-01jh99fx1jn3 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250110234905/https://www.si.com/college/alabama/football/nick-saban-named-to-2025-college-football-hall-of-fame-class-01jh99fx1jn3 |archive-date=January 10, 2025 |access-date=January 10, 2025 |publisher=Sports Illustrated}} {{TOC limit|3}} ==Early life and education== Saban was born in [[Fairmont, West Virginia]], to Mary and Nick Lou Saban Sr. and grew up in the nearby town of [[Monongah, West Virginia|Monongah]]. His parents owned a small service station and his father founded and coached a youth football team.{{Cite web |last=Hoge |first=Adam |date=January 6, 2013 |title=Hoge: Inside The Mind Of Nick Saban |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/chicago/news/hoge-inside-the-mind-of-nick-saban/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240218161748/https://www.cbsnews.com/chicago/news/hoge-inside-the-mind-of-nick-saban/ |archive-date=February 18, 2024 |access-date=February 18, 2024 |website=CBS News – Chicago |language=en-US}}{{Cite news |last=Bonesteel |first=Matt |date=January 11, 2024 |title=Nick Saban through the years: A timeline of his greatest hits |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/sports/2024/01/11/nick-saban-coaching-timeline-alabama-legacy/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240111175530/https://www.washingtonpost.com/sports/2024/01/11/nick-saban-coaching-timeline-alabama-legacy/ |archive-date=January 11, 2024 |access-date=February 18, 2024 |newspaper=Washington Post |language=en}} He has one sister, Dianna, and graduated from Monongah High School in 1969.{{Cite web |last=Casagrande |first=Michael |date=June 21, 2016 |title=Newspaper scrapbook of Nick Saban's high school, college glory |url=http://www.al.com/alabamafootball/index.ssf/2016/06/nick_saban_scrapbook_of_high_s.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180802162541/https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/index.ssf/2016/06/nick_saban_scrapbook_of_high_s.html |archive-date=August 2, 2018 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]]}}{{Cite web |last=Skipper |first=Clay |title=This Place Matters: The Tide Rolls Down from West Virginia |url=https://bittersoutherner.com/this-place-matters |access-date=September 26, 2024 |website=The Bitter Southerner |language=en-US}} In high school, Saban played quarterback and helped lead Monongah to the 1968 West Virginia Class A state championship his senior year.{{Cite web |last=Scarborough |first=Alex |date=November 14, 2018 |title=How Nick Saban's 1968 title explains the legendary coach |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/25267700/nick-saban-first-championship-west-virginia-high-school |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221207003105/https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/25267700/nick-saban-first-championship-west-virginia-high-school |archive-date=December 7, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]]}} Among his teammates were [[Kerry Marbury]], who went on to star for the [[West Virginia Mountaineers football|West Virginia Mountaineers]] and play in the [[Canadian Football League]].{{Cite web |last=Hrin |first=Eric |date=June 30, 2019 |title=Kerry Marbury gets final farewell |url=https://www.timeswv.com/news/kerry-marbury-gets-final-farewell/article_5aca3b68-9ade-11e9-8751-b7bd9d705834.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190709033610/https://www.timeswv.com/news/kerry-marbury-gets-final-farewell/article_5aca3b68-9ade-11e9-8751-b7bd9d705834.html |archive-date=July 9, 2019 |access-date=July 21, 2019 |website=Times West Virginian}} Saban attended [[Kent State University]] in [[Kent, Ohio]], and played defensive back for the [[Kent State Golden Flashes football|Golden Flashes]] under coach [[Don James (American football)|Don James]]. He and a roommate avoided being part of the [[Kent State shootings]], on May 4, 1970, when they decided to eat lunch before walking to the rally area.{{Cite web |date=May 5, 2008 |title=Saban remembers Kent State Shootings |url=http://www.sportsbybrooks.com/nick-saban-remembers-kent-state-shootings-17560 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090218194233/http://www.sportsbybrooks.com/nick-saban-remembers-kent-state-shootings-17560 |archive-date=February 18, 2009 |website=SPORTSbyBROOKS}} While attending KSU, he married Terry Constable, also from West Virginia, on December 18, 1971 in Fairmont.{{Cite web |last=Kelly |first=Nick |date=December 18, 2021 |title=On 50th anniversary, Nick Saban and Miss Terry share their love story |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/college/football/2021/12/18/nick-saban-wife-miss-terry-50th-wedding-anniversary-alabama-football/6468165001/ |access-date=2025-01-25 |website=Tuscaloosa News}} Saban graduated from Kent State in 1973 with a [[bachelor's degree]] in business, and in 1975, earned his [[master's degree]] in sports administration, also from Kent State. Saban's father died during his son's first year of [[graduate school]].{{Cite web |last=Grobeck |first=Joe |date=June 19, 2022 |title=Nick Saban's Dad's Early Death Inspires Him to be Great Every Single Day |url=https://fanbuzz.com/college-football/sec/alabama/nick-saban-dad/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224221619/https://fanbuzz.com/college-football/sec/alabama/nick-saban-dad/ |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=FanBuzz}} Saban is of [[Croats|Croatian]] ancestry.{{Cite web |last=Bach |first=Nenad N. |date=January 18, 2004 |title=(E) Croatian Nick Saban – Coach of the Year |url=http://www.croatia.org/crown/articles/7993/1/E-Croatian-Nick-Saban---Coach-of-the-Year.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224221241/http://www.croatia.org/crown/articles/7993/1/E-Croatian-Nick-Saban---Coach-of-the-Year.html |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=www.croatia.org}} His paternal grandfather, Stanko Saban, was born in 1895 in [[Gospić]], in the [[Lika]] region of [[Croatia]]. Stanko emigrated to [[Portland, Oregon]], in 1908, when he was 13 years old. He later married Anna Mihalic, of Croatian-American heritage. ==Coaching career== ===Early coaching career=== Saban had not intended to enter the coaching ranks until [[Don James (American football)|Don James]] hired him as a graduate assistant at Kent State, while Saban waited for his wife to graduate.{{Cite web |last=Staples |first=Andy |date=August 14, 2012 |title=Sabanization of College Football |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/2012/writers/andy_staples/08/14/sabanization-of-college-football/1.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120817015531/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/2012/writers/andy_staples/08/14/sabanization-of-college-football/1.html |archive-date=August 17, 2012 |access-date=January 11, 2012 |website=SI.com}} He later served as an assistant coach in [[NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision|NCAA Division I-A]], at several schools: [[Syracuse Orange football|Syracuse]] in 1977, [[West Virginia Mountaineers football|West Virginia]] in [[1978 West Virginia Mountaineers football team|1978]] and [[1979 West Virginia Mountaineers football team|1979]], [[Ohio State Buckeyes football|Ohio State]] in [[1980 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|1980]] and [[1981 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|1981]], [[Navy Midshipmen football|Navy]] in [[1982 Navy Midshipmen football team|1982]], and [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]] from [[1983 Michigan State Spartans football team|1983]] to [[1987 Michigan State Spartans football team|1987]].{{Cite web |last=Gutierrez |first=Matthew |date=November 2, 2017 |title=Before college football stardom, Nick Saban brought intense style to Syracuse |url=http://dailyorange.com/2017/11/college-football-stardom-nick-saban-brought-intense-style-syracuse/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240717204509/https://dailyorange.com/2017/11/college-football-stardom-nick-saban-brought-intense-style-syracuse/ |archive-date=July 17, 2024 |archive-date=July 17, 2024 |access-date=July 17, 2024 |website=The Detroit News |language=en-US}} After the 1987 season, Kent State passed over Saban for its vacant head coaching position and hired [[Dick Crum (American football)|Dick Crum]].{{Cite web |last=Scarborough |first=Alex |date=September 22, 2016 |title=Yes, Kent State once passed on hiring Nick Saban |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/17607436/the-alabama-crimson-tide-coach-nick-saban-was-passed-kent-state |url-status=live 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]]}} Saban was then hired as an assistant for the [[Houston Oilers]] in the [[National Football League]].{{Cite web |title=Nick Saban – Alabama Football Coaches Profile |url=http://alabama.rivals.com/viewcoach.asp?Year=2009&Sport=1&Coach=1924 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715190350/http://alabama.rivals.com/viewcoach.asp?Year=2009&Sport=1&Coach=1924 |archive-date=July 15, 2011 |access-date=December 15, 2008 |website=Rivals.com}} ===Toledo=== Saban began his career as a head coach when he was hired by the [[University of Toledo]] on December 22, 1989.{{Cite news |last=Bergener |first=John |date=December 22, 1989 |title=Saban named UT football coach |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=hEtQAAAAIBAJ&pg=6539%2C3088127 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230417182633/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=hEtQAAAAIBAJ&pg=6539%2C3088127 |archive-date=April 17, 2023 |access-date=February 19, 2012 |work=The Blade |location=Toledo, Ohio |page=24}} Coming off of 6–5 seasons in both [[1988 Toledo Rockets football team|1988]] and [[1989 Toledo Rockets football team|1989]], the [[Toledo Rockets football|Rockets]] found quick success under Nick Saban in [[1990 Toledo Rockets football team|1990]].{{Cite web |title=Toledo Rockets Football Record By Year |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/toledo/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224223036/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/toledo/index.html |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} With a 9–2 season, Toledo was co-champion of the [[Mid-American Conference]].{{Cite web |title=1990 Mid-American Conference Year Summary |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfbconferences/mac/1990.html |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} The two games the Rockets lost that season were by narrow margins: one point to [[1990 Central Michigan Chippewas football team|Central Michigan]] and four points to [[1990 Navy Midshipmen football team|Navy]].{{Cite web |title=1990 Toledo Rockets Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/toledo/1990-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224222944/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/toledo/1990-schedule.html |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} While coaching in Toledo, Saban turned down an application from future head coach [[Urban Meyer]], who was looking for any coaching job on Saban's staff.{{Cite web |last=Thamel |first=Pete |author-link=Pete Thamel |date=December 4, 2009 |title=Nick Saban and Urban Meyer Share a Friendship With Bill Belichick |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/12/05/sports/football/05coaches.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230202173321/https://www.nytimes.com/2009/12/05/sports/football/05coaches.html |archive-date=February 2, 2023 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[New York Times]]}} ===Cleveland Browns=== The following February, Saban resigned as Toledo's head coach after only one season in order to become [[defensive coordinator]] of the [[Cleveland Browns]] under head coach [[Bill Belichick]].{{Cite news |last=Hackenberg |first=Dave |date=February 14, 1991 |title=UT begins the search...again |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=Z1pPAAAAIBAJ&pg=6787%2C3926404 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230417182658/https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=Z1pPAAAAIBAJ&pg=6787%2C3926404 |archive-date=April 17, 2023 |access-date=February 19, 2012 |work=The Blade |location=Toledo, Ohio |page=29}} He remained in that position for four seasons.{{Cite web |title=Nick Saban Record, Statistics, and Category Ranks |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/SabaNi0.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120914104524/http://www.pro-football-reference.com/coaches/SabaNi0.htm |archive-date=September 14, 2012 |access-date=July 17, 2024 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]] |language=en}} Saban helped lead the 1994 defensive unit for the Browns that was the best in the NFL in points allowed.{{Cite web |title=1994 NFL Opposition & Defensive Statistics |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/1994/opp.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224223458/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/1994/opp.htm |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]]}} Saban later said these four years were the "worst of my life".{{Cite news |last=Schmidt |first=Patrick |date=January 13, 2014 |title=Nick Saban: '4 years with Bill Belichick were the worst of my life' |url=https://fansided.com/2014/01/13/nick-saban-4-years-bill-belichick-worst-life/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180813015900/https://fansided.com/2014/01/13/nick-saban-4-years-bill-belichick-worst-life/ |archive-date=August 13, 2018 |access-date=December 28, 2018 |work=FanSided}} ===Michigan State=== ====1995–1997 seasons==== Saban became head coach of [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]] prior to the [[1995 NCAA Division I-A football season|1995 season]].{{Cite web |date=February 3, 2018 |title=Nick Saban's timeline |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/2018/02/04/special-report-nick-sabans-timeline/15320870007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200357/https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/2018/02/04/special-report-nick-sabans-timeline/15320870007/ |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=The Tuscaloosa News}} Michigan State had not had a winning season since [[1990 Michigan State Spartans football team|1990]], and the team was sanctioned by the NCAA for recruiting violations that were committed under his predecessor and former mentor, [[George Perles]].{{Cite web |title=Michigan State Spartans Football Record By Year |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200218/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/index.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=The NCAA Register: October 7, 1996 |url=http://www.ncaa.org/databases/register/register_961007/michigan.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071024120821/http://www.ncaa.org/databases/register/register_961007/michigan.html |archive-date=October 24, 2007 |website=NCAA.org}}{{Cite web |date=May 26, 2003 |title=Infractions case: University of Michigan |url=http://fs.ncaa.org/Docs/NCAANewsArchive/2003/Division+I/infractions%2bcase_%2buniversity%2bof%2bmichigan%2b-%2b5-26-03.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304211222/http://fs.ncaa.org/Docs/NCAANewsArchive/2003/Division+I/infractions+case_+university+of+michigan+-+5-26-03.html |archive-date=March 4, 2016 |website=FS.NCAA.org}} Beginning in [[1995 Michigan State Spartans football team|1995]], Saban moderately improved Michigan State's fortunes, taking the Spartans to bowl games in each of his first three seasons.{{Cite web |title=Michigan State Spartans Bowls |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141112111949/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/ |archive-date=November 12, 2014 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} From 1995 to [[1997 Michigan State Spartans football team|1997]], Michigan State finished 6–5–1, 6–6, and 7–5.{{Cite web |title=1995 Michigan State Spartans Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1995-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200627/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1995-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=1996 Michigan State Spartans Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1996-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200616/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1996-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=1997 Michigan State Spartans Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1997-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200613/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1997-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} ====1998 season==== On November 7, [[1998 Michigan State Spartans football team|1998]], the Spartans upset the No.{{nbsp}}1 ranked [[Ohio State Buckeyes football|Ohio State Buckeyes]] 28–24 at [[Ohio Stadium]].{{Cite magazine |last=Lind |first=Andrew |date=October 6, 2022 |title=Michigan State To Wear 1998 Throwback Helmets Against Ohio State |url=https://www.si.com/college/ohiostate/football/ohio-state-football-michigan-state-to-wear-1998-throwback-helmets-against-buckeyes-spartans |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200720/https://www.si.com/college/ohiostate/football/ohio-state-football-michigan-state-to-wear-1998-throwback-helmets-against-buckeyes-spartans |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |magazine=Sports Illustrated}} However, even after the upset and an early-season rout of then-highly ranked Notre Dame the Spartans finished 6–6, including three last-minute losses featuring turnovers, defensive lapses, and special-teams misplays, and failed to earn a bowl invitation.{{Cite web |title=Notre Dame at Michigan State Box Score, September 12, 1998 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/1998-09-12-michigan-state.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200743/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/1998-09-12-michigan-state.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=1998 Michigan State Spartans Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1998-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200614/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1998-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} ====1999 season==== Saban led the [[1999 Michigan State Spartans football team|1999 Spartans]] to a 9–2 season that included wins over [[1999 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|Notre Dame]], [[1999 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]], [[1999 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State]], and [[1999 Penn State Nittany Lions football team|Penn State]]. The two losses were routs at the hands of [[1999 Purdue Boilermakers football team|Purdue]] and [[1999 Wisconsin Badgers football team|Wisconsin]].{{Cite web |title=1999 Michigan State Spartans Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1999-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200815/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/1999-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} Following the final regular-season game against Penn State, Saban abruptly resigned to accept the head coaching position with [[LSU Tigers football|LSU]].{{Cite web |date=December 1, 1999 |title=Saban Doubles Salary by Accepting LSU Job |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1999-dec-01-sp-39262-story.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226201037/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1999-dec-01-sp-39262-story.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=Los Angeles Times |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} Saban's assistant head coach and successor, [[Bobby Williams]], coached the Spartans to a [[Florida Citrus Bowl|Citrus Bowl]] victory over [[1999 Florida Gators football team|Florida]], giving the Spartans an overall record of 10–2 for the 1999 season.{{Cite web |title=Bobby Williams Coaching Record |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/bobby-williams-2.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200440/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/bobby-williams-2.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=Citrus Bowl – Michigan State vs Florida Box Score, January 1, 2000 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2000-01-01-florida.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226200949/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2000-01-01-florida.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} It was the most wins for the Spartans in a season since [[1965 Michigan State Spartans football team|1965]], and the Spartans reached their highest ranking since the [[1966 Michigan State Spartans football team|1966 team]].{{Cite web |title=Michigan State Spartans AP Poll History |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141112111949/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/michigan-state/ |archive-date=November 12, 2014 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} Future NFL head coach [[Josh McDaniels]] served as a graduate assistant on Saban's 1999 coaching staff.{{Cite web |last=Reyes |first=Lorenzo |date=February 4, 2017 |title=Patriots OC Josh McDaniels' rise began with MSU |url=https://www.lansingstatejournal.com/story/sports/college/msu/football/2017/02/04/josh-mcdaniels-patriots-super-bowl-michigan-state-msu/97429674/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230417182712/https://www.lansingstatejournal.com/story/sports/college/msu/football/2017/02/04/josh-mcdaniels-patriots-super-bowl-michigan-state-msu/97429674/ |archive-date=April 17, 2023 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=Lansing State Journal}} ===LSU=== ====2000 season==== In November 1999, LSU named Nick Saban as their 31st head football coach.{{Cite web |date=October 31, 1999 |title=A New Leader for a New Era |url=http://www.lsusports.net/ViewArticle.dbml?SPSID=27815&SPID=2164&DB_OEM_ID=5200&ATCLID=158627 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181215122058/http://www.lsusports.net/ViewArticle.dbml?SPSID=27815&SPID=2164&DB_OEM_ID=5200&ATCLID=158627 |archive-date=December 15, 2018 |website=LSUSports.net}}{{Cite web |date=November 30, 1999 |title=Saban Names Four Assistants |url=https://lsusports.net/news/1999/11/30/158629/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226203104/https://lsusports.net/news/1999/11/30/158629/ |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=LSUSports.net}} In [[2000 LSU Tigers football team|2000]], the Tigers went 8–4 and won the [[Peach Bowl]] over [[2000 Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football team|Georgia Tech]].{{Cite web |date=December 29, 2000 |title=Peach Bowl – LSU 28, Georgia Tech 14 |url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/2000/12/29/Peach-Bowl-LSU-28-Georgia-Tech-14/4331978066000/ |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=UPI}} The season was somewhat marred by several lopsided losses, including a 34–17 loss to the [[2000 Auburn Tigers football team|Auburn Tigers]], and a 41–9 loss to the [[2000 Florida Gators football team|Florida Gators]].{{Cite web |title=2000 LSU Fighting Tigers Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2000-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226203533/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2000-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} ====2001 season==== Saban led LSU to a 10–3 record in [[2001 LSU Tigers football team|2001]], including an SEC Championship and a Sugar Bowl victory.{{Cite web |title=2001 LSU Fighting Tigers Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2001-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226203535/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2001-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} After a 35–24 loss to the [[2001 Ole Miss Rebels football team|Ole Miss]] Rebels, the Tigers finished the year with six straight wins, including a 31–20 win over #2 [[2001 Tennessee Volunteers football team|Tennessee]] in the [[2001 SEC Championship Game]], and a 47–34 win over [[2001 Illinois Fighting Illini football team|Illinois]] in the [[2002 Sugar Bowl]].{{Cite web |title=Ole Miss at LSU Box Score, October 27, 2001 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2001-10-27-louisiana-state.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190218082212/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2001-10-27-louisiana-state.html |archive-date=February 18, 2019 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |date=December 7, 2001 |title=SEC CHAMPS: LSU Right on the 'Mauck' in Title Game, 31–20 |url=https://lsusports.net/news/2001/12/07/161598/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226204200/https://lsusports.net/news/2001/12/07/161598/ |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=LSUSports.net}}{{Cite web |date=December 31, 2001 |title=Even Sweeter: LSU KO's Illinois, 47–34, in 2002 Sugar Bowl |url=https://lsusports.net/news/2001/12/31/161645/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226204243/https://lsusports.net/news/2001/12/31/161645/ |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=LSUSports.net}} It was the first outright [[Southeastern Conference|SEC]] championship for LSU since [[1986 LSU Tigers football team|1986]], and the first time the Tigers had won the Sugar Bowl since [[1968 LSU Tigers football team|1968]].{{Cite web |title=LSU Fighting Tigers Football Record By Year |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221114045610/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/index.html |archive-date=November 14, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=LSU Fighting Tigers Bowls |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190718022542/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/ |archive-date=July 18, 2019 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} ====2002 season==== The [[2002 LSU Tigers football team|2002 season]] opened with high expectations, but a 26–8 loss at the hands of [[2002 Virginia Tech Hokies football team|Virginia Tech]] in the Tigers' season opener raised serious questions about their outlook.{{Cite web |title=LSU at Virginia Tech Box Score, September 1, 2002 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2002-09-01-virginia-tech.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226204530/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2002-09-01-virginia-tech.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} However, the Tigers would rebound to win their next six straight, but after a mid-season injury to quarterback [[Matt Mauck]], LSU lost four of its last six games to close the season, including a 21–20 loss at [[2002 Arkansas Razorbacks football team|Arkansas]], which knocked the Tigers out of the SEC Championship Game.{{Cite web |date=October 17, 2002 |title=Louisiana State's Mauck Is Sidelined Indefinitely |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2002-oct-17-sp-colfbnotes17-story.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226204836/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2002-oct-17-sp-colfbnotes17-story.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=Los Angeles Times}}{{Cite web |title=2002 Southeastern Conference Year Summary |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfbconferences/sec/2002.html |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=2002 LSU Fighting Tigers Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2002-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226203942/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2002-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} LSU also suffered a 35–20 loss to [[2002 Texas Longhorns football team|Texas]] in the [[2003 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl Classic]], and finished 8–5.{{Cite web |title=Cotton Bowl – Texas vs LSU Box Score, January 1, 2003 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2003-01-01-louisiana-state.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226204618/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2003-01-01-louisiana-state.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} ====2003 season==== The [[2003 LSU Tigers football team|2003]] Tigers started the season with five wins, including a 17–10 victory in [[Tiger Stadium (LSU)|Tiger Stadium]] over the defending SEC champion, and then-undefeated, [[2003 Georgia Bulldogs football team|Georgia Bulldogs]]. LSU lost the following week to [[2003 Florida Gators football team|Florida]], 19–7. After the loss to Florida, LSU did not lose again in the regular season and ended its regular season with a win over the [[2003 Arkansas Razorbacks football team|Arkansas Razorbacks]] to win the [[Southeastern Conference#Current members|SEC West]].{{Cite web |title=2003 LSU Fighting Tigers Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2003-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226204658/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2003-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}}{{Cite web |title=2003 Southeastern Conference Year Summary |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfbconferences/sec/2003.html |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} After winning the SEC West, the Tigers defeated the Georgia Bulldogs 34–13 in the [[2003 SEC Championship Game|SEC Championship Game]] in Atlanta.{{Cite web |last=Gomila |first=Billy |date=August 14, 2013 |title=Wednesday WayBack: The 2003 SEC Championship Game |url=https://www.andthevalleyshook.com/2013/8/14/4619512/wednesday-wayback-2003-no-3-lsu-34-no-5-georgia-13 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226205335/https://www.andthevalleyshook.com/2013/8/14/4619512/wednesday-wayback-2003-no-3-lsu-34-no-5-georgia-13 |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=And The Valley Shook}} They were ranked No. 2 in the BCS standings and advanced to play the BCS No.{{nbsp}}1 [[Oklahoma Sooners football|Oklahoma Sooners]] in the [[Sugar Bowl]], which was the host of the [[BCS Championship Game]] in 2003.{{Cite web |title=2003 Polls |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/years/2003-polls.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180318121103/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/years/2003-polls.html |archive-date=March 18, 2018 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} The Tigers won the game 21–14. The win gave LSU the BCS national championship.{{Cite web |date=January 5, 2015 |title=Remembering The Day LSU And USC Split The College Football National Title |url=http://www.thepostgame.com/blog/throwback/201501/lsu-usc-split-national-championship-0 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211019225238/http://www.thepostgame.com/blog/throwback/201501/lsu-usc-split-national-championship-0 |archive-date=October 19, 2021 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=ThePostGame.com}} and a 13–1 finish for the season. The 13 wins for LSU set a new single-season record, breaking the mark of 11 wins set by the 1958 National Championship team. ====2004 season==== LSU finished the [[2004 LSU Tigers football team|2004 season]] 9–3, after losing to the [[2004 Iowa Hawkeyes football team|Iowa Hawkeyes]] in the [[2005 Capital One Bowl|Capital One Bowl]] 30–25 on a final play touchdown pass.{{Cite web |last=Gomila |first=Billy |date=July 23, 2014 |title=Wednesday WayBack: the 2005 Capital One Bowl |url=https://www.andthevalleyshook.com/2014/7/23/5928397/wednesday-wayback-iowa-30-lsu-25-the-saban-era-ends |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226205847/https://www.andthevalleyshook.com/2014/7/23/5928397/wednesday-wayback-iowa-30-lsu-25-the-saban-era-ends |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=And The Valley Shook}} Other losses that season were on the road at [[2004 Auburn Tigers football team|Auburn]] 10–9, and a loss on the road to [[2004 Georgia Bulldogs football team|Georgia]] 45–16.{{Cite web |title=2004 LSU Fighting Tigers Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2004-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226205652/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/louisiana-state/2004-schedule.html |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]]}} At the end of the [[2004 NCAA Division I-A football season|2004 season]], Saban left LSU to coach the [[Miami Dolphins]].{{Cite web |date=December 24, 2004 |title=Saban Leaving LSU to Coach the Miami Dolphins |url=https://lsusports.net/news/2004/12/24/166309/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221226205807/https://lsusports.net/news/2004/12/24/166309/ |archive-date=December 26, 2022 |access-date=December 26, 2022 |website=LSUSports.net}} ===Miami Dolphins=== ====2005 season==== Saban accepted the head coaching position for the [[Miami Dolphins]] on December 25, 2004.{{Cite news |last=Maske |first=Mark |date=December 26, 2004 |title=LSU's Saban Accepts Offer to Coach Dolphins |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/sports/2004/12/26/lsus-saban-accepts-offer-to-coach-dolphins/43675f56-4b14-4a49-ad6c-6af3272765d5/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220926032638/https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/sports/2004/12/26/lsus-saban-accepts-offer-to-coach-dolphins/43675f56-4b14-4a49-ad6c-6af3272765d5/ |archive-date=September 26, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |newspaper=Washington Post}} He was the sixth coach in the franchise's history.{{Cite web |title=Miami Dolphins Coaches |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/coaches.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227205130/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/coaches.htm |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]]}} The Nick Saban era officially kicked off with a 34–10 win over the [[2005 Denver Broncos season|Denver Broncos]] in [[2005 Miami Dolphins season|2005]].{{Cite web |date=September 12, 2005 |title=Fins' Saban wins debut vs. Broncos |url=https://www.gainesville.com/story/news/2005/09/12/fins-saban-wins-debut-vs-broncos/31703656007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227205400/https://www.gainesville.com/story/news/2005/09/12/fins-saban-wins-debut-vs-broncos/31703656007/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Gainesville Sun |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} From there, however, the Dolphins struggled, losing seven of their next nine games to fall to 3–7. The two wins came over the [[2005 Carolina Panthers season|Carolina Panthers]] and the [[2005 New Orleans Saints season|New Orleans Saints]], a game that took place in [[Tiger Stadium (LSU)|Tiger Stadium]] due to [[Hurricane Katrina]].{{Cite web |date=October 31, 2005 |title=Saban happy in La., Saints not |url=https://www.tampabay.com/archive/2005/10/31/saban-happy-in-la-saints-not/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227211316/https://www.tampabay.com/archive/2005/10/31/saban-happy-in-la-saints-not/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Tampa Bay Times}} After a frustrating two months, however, the Dolphins would rally late in the season, as they won their final six games, including a 28–26 win to end the season in [[Foxboro, Massachusetts]], over the [[2005 New England Patriots season|New England Patriots]]. The team finished the year with a 9–7 record, and narrowly missed the playoffs in Saban's first season.{{Cite web |title=Miami Dolphins 2005 Games and Schedule |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/2005/gamelog/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227205439/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/2005/gamelog/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]]}} ====2006 season==== Going into the [[2006 Miami Dolphins season|2006 season]], the Dolphins were expected to contend for a playoff spot. The season, however, turned out to be unsuccessful. The Dolphins were considering quarterback [[Drew Brees]], who had just been released from the [[San Diego Chargers]] due to a career-threatening shoulder injury and subsequent contract dispute, but instead traded for [[Daunte Culpepper]], who was still recovering from a knee injury from the previous season.{{Cite web |date=March 12, 2006 |title=Brees meets with Saints, Dolphins |url=https://www.patriots.com/news/brees-meets-with-saints-dolphins-95061 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227211542/https://www.patriots.com/news/brees-meets-with-saints-dolphins-95061 |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Patriots.com}}{{Cite web |last1=Marvez |first1=Alex |last2=Skolnick |first2=Ethan J. |last3=Hyde |first3=Dave |date=January 30, 2007 |title=Saints' Brees proves medical experts wrong |url=https://www.sun-sentinel.com/news/fl-xpm-2007-01-30-0701290275-story.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227212755/https://www.sun-sentinel.com/news/fl-xpm-2007-01-30-0701290275-story.html |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Sun Sentinel}}{{Cite web |last1=Pasquarelli |first1=Len |author-link1=Len Pasquarelli |last2=Clayton |first2=John |author-link2=John Clayton (sportswriter) |date=March 14, 2006 |title=Vikings deal Culpepper to Miami for second-round pick |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2368259 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190504094551/http://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2368259 |archive-date=May 4, 2019 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]]}}{{Cite web |date=March 14, 2006 |title=Culpepper bound for Miami |url=https://www.gainesville.com/story/news/2006/03/15/culpepper-bound-for-miami/31478029007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227212917/https://www.gainesville.com/story/news/2006/03/15/culpepper-bound-for-miami/31478029007/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Gainesville Sun |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} Saban later said that the team's decision to pass on Brees was the moment he knew that he would leave the team.{{Cite web |last=Sigler |first=Josh |date=July 23, 2021 |title=Nick Saban says Drew Brees fiasco led to Dolphins departure |url=https://saintswire.usatoday.com/2021/07/23/nick-saban-drew-brees-injury-dolphins-news |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230101063531/https://saintswire.usatoday.com/2021/07/23/nick-saban-drew-brees-injury-dolphins-news/ |archive-date=January 1, 2023 |access-date=January 1, 2023 |website=usatoday.com}} Culpepper never fully recovered and was ultimately benched after the fourth game of the season, when the Dolphins lost to the [[2006 Houston Texans season|Houston Texans]].{{Cite web |title=Miami Dolphins at Houston Texans – October 1st, 2006 |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/200610010htx.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190301074616/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/200610010htx.htm |archive-date=March 1, 2019 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]]}}{{Cite web |last=Yotter |first=Tim |date=October 12, 2006 |title=Culpepper Quit Rehab Drills |url=https://247sports.com/nfl/minnesota-vikings/Article/Culpepper-Quit-Rehab-Drills-104363229/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230417182706/https://247sports.com/nfl/minnesota-vikings/Article/Culpepper-Quit-Rehab-Drills-104363229/ |archive-date=April 17, 2023 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=247Sports}} He was eventually put on [[injured reserve]].{{Cite web |date=December 12, 2006 |title=Dolphins place Culpepper on IR |url=https://www.patriots.com/news/dolphins-place-culpepper-on-ir-98046 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227213036/https://www.patriots.com/news/dolphins-place-culpepper-on-ir-98046 |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Patriots.com}} After starting the season 1–6, however, the Dolphins got hot. They won four straight games, including wins over the [[2006 Chicago Bears season|Chicago Bears]], who were previously unbeaten and made it to the [[Super Bowl XLI|Super Bowl]] that year, and the [[2006 Kansas City Chiefs season|Kansas City Chiefs]].{{Cite web |title=Miami Dolphins 2006 Games and Schedule |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/2006/gamelog/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227214813/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/2006/gamelog/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]]}} Suddenly, the Dolphins were back in the playoff hunt at 5–6, but a 24–10 loss the following week to the [[2006 Jacksonville Jaguars season|Jacksonville Jaguars]] all but ended their playoff hopes.{{Cite web |last=Wine |first=Steven |date=December 3, 2006 |title=Garrard shines in Jaguars' 24–10 win over Dolphins |url=https://www.ocala.com/story/news/2006/12/04/garrard-shines-in-jaguars-24-10-win-over-dolphins/31177050007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227214817/https://www.ocala.com/story/news/2006/12/04/garrard-shines-in-jaguars-24-10-win-over-dolphins/31177050007/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=The Star Banner |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The Dolphins rebounded the following week with a 21–0 win over the [[2006 New England Patriots season|New England Patriots]], the Dolphins' last victory of the 2006 season.{{Cite web |date=December 10, 2006 |title=Patriots drown in Dolphins drink, 21–0 |url=https://www.patriots.com/news/patriots-drown-in-dolphins-drink-21-0-136476 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227214813/https://www.patriots.com/news/patriots-drown-in-dolphins-drink-21-0-136476 |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Patriots.com}} Quarterback [[Joey Harrington]] was eventually benched in favor of third-string quarterback [[Cleo Lemon]].{{Cite web |last=Wine |first=Steven |date=December 28, 2006 |title=Lemon to make first NFL start |url=https://www.gainesville.com/story/news/2006/12/28/lemon-to-make-first-nfl-start/31506927007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227213835/https://www.gainesville.com/story/news/2006/12/28/lemon-to-make-first-nfl-start/31506927007/ |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=Gainesville Sun |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The defense ranked as a top-five unit in points and yards allowed.{{Cite web |title=2006 NFL Opposition & Defensive Statistics |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/2006/opp.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221012060127/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/years/2006/opp.htm |archive-date=October 12, 2022 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]]}} However, the offense was anemic, with the only bright spot being running back [[Ronnie Brown]], who gained over 1,000 rushing yards on the season.{{Cite web |title=2006 Miami Dolphins Rosters, Stats, Schedule, Team Draftees |url=https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/2006.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190103013348/https://www.pro-football-reference.com/teams/mia/2006.htm |archive-date=January 3, 2019 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=[[Pro Football Reference]] |language=en}} The Dolphins lost their next two games to the [[2006 Buffalo Bills season|Buffalo Bills]] and the [[2006 New York Jets season|New York Jets]] to finish 6–10, Saban's first losing season as a head coach. On November 27, 2006, the [[University of Alabama]] announced the firing of head coach [[Mike Shula]]. Saban was rumored to be at the top of Alabama's wish list, but Saban refused to discuss the job while his NFL season was still underway.{{Cite web |date=December 21, 2006 |title=Saban Denies Interest in the Alabama Coaching Job |url=http://www.floridafb.com/ViewArticle.dbml?DB_OEM_ID=11200&ATCLID=729980 |url-status=usurped |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090108210032/http://www.floridafb.com/ViewArticle.dbml?DB_OEM_ID=11200&ATCLID=729980 |archive-date=January 8, 2009 |access-date=December 16, 2008 |website=FloridaFB.com |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} During the month of December 2006, Saban was repeatedly questioned by the media about the Alabama job, and he repeatedly denied the rumors in his weekly press conferences, stating on December 21 "I guess I have to say it. I'm not going to be the Alabama coach." But his wife Terry talked Nick into having a meeting with [[Mal Moore]].{{Cite web |last=Pasquarelli |first=Len |date=January 3, 2007 |title=Saban rolls with the Tide, takes Alabama job |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=2718488 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220114140205/https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=2718488 |archive-date=January 14, 2022 |access-date=October 16, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |agency=[[Associated Press]]}}{{Cite web |date=December 21, 2006 |title=Saban: 'I'm not going to be the Alabama coach' |url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2705288 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221016224758/https://www.espn.com/nfl/news/story?id=2705288 |archive-date=October 16, 2022 |access-date=October 16, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} Saban met with Alabama officials on January 1, 2007,{{Cite web |date=January 4, 2007 |title=Press Conference Transcript: Nick Saban Introduction |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2007/1/4/Press_Conference_Transcript_Nick_Saban_Introduction.aspx |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200126040355/https://rolltide.com/news/2007/1/4/Press_Conference_Transcript_Nick_Saban_Introduction.aspx |archive-date=January 26, 2020 |access-date=December 27, 2022 |website=University of Alabama Athletics}} which was shortly after the Dolphins' season-ending loss to the [[2006 Indianapolis Colts season|Indianapolis Colts]]. ===Alabama=== {{Main|Alabama Crimson Tide football under Nick Saban}} ====2007 season==== On January 3, 2007, following a meeting with Dolphins owner [[Wayne Huizenga]], Saban announced that he had accepted an offer to become [[Alabama Crimson Tide Football|Alabama]]'s 27th{{nbsp}}head coach.{{Cite web |date=January 4, 2007 |title=Saban accepts Alabama coaching job |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2007-jan-04-sp-saban4-story.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221222200620/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2007-jan-04-sp-saban4-story.html |archive-date=December 22, 2022 |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} His initial eight-year contract totaling {{US$|32{{nbsp}}million|link=yes}} made him one of the highest-paid football coaches,{{Cite web |last=Casagrande |first=Michael |date=May 3, 2017 |title=History of Saban's rising pay, how it's been viewed |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2017/05/the_10-year_history_of_nick_sa.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221222200659/https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2017/05/the_10-year_history_of_nick_sa.html |archive-date=December 22, 2022 |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} professional or [[college football|collegiate]], in the United States at the time.{{Cite web |last=Pasquarelli |first=Len |date=January 1, 2007 |title=After repeated denials, Saban takes Bama job |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=2718488 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070104195108/http://sports.espn.go.com/ncf/news/story?id=2718488 |archive-date=January 4, 2007 |access-date=December 15, 2008 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} On January 4, 2007, at a press conference on the Alabama campus, Saban was officially introduced as the head football coach of The University of Alabama.{{Cite web |last=Stephens |first=Hannah |date=January 4, 2020 |title=13 years ago today, Nick Saban became Alabama's head coach |url=https://rolltidewire.usatoday.com/2020/01/03/13-years-ago-today-nick-saban-became-alabamas-head-coach/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221222200835/https://rolltidewire.usatoday.com/2020/01/03/13-years-ago-today-nick-saban-became-alabamas-head-coach/ |archive-date=December 22, 2022 |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=Roll Tide Wire |publisher=USA Today |language=en-US}} On September 1, [[2007 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|2007]], his Crimson Tide opened the season with a 52–6 win over the [[2007 Western Carolina Catamounts football team|Western Carolina Catamounts]], scoring more points than during any game in the [[2006 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|2006 season]].{{Cite web |title=Western Carolina at Alabama Box Score, September 1, 2007 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2007-09-01-alabama.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221222200916/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2007-09-01-alabama.html |archive-date=December 22, 2022 |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} Saban became the fifth Alabama coach since 1900 to start his first season 3–0, earning a win over then-ranked No.{{nbsp}}16 [[2007 Arkansas Razorbacks football team|Arkansas Razorbacks]].{{Cite web |date=September 15, 2007 |title=Caddell TD caps wild finish as Bama upsets Arkansas |url=http://sports.espn.go.com/ncf/recap?gameId=272580333 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121025195424/http://sports.espn.go.com/ncf/recap?gameId=272580333 |archive-date=October 25, 2012 |access-date=August 16, 2008 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} Alabama ended the regular season with a 6–6 record, including a four-game losing streak, a particularly humiliating loss at home to [[2007 Louisiana–Monroe Warhawks football team|Louisiana-Monroe]], and a sixth straight loss to [[2007 Auburn Tigers football team|Auburn]] in the [[Iron Bowl]]. All six of Alabama's losses were by seven points or fewer.{{Cite web |last=Solomon |first=Jon |date=September 24, 2015 |title=The day Louisiana-Monroe beat Alabama and Nick Saban |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/the-day-louisiana-monroe-beat-alabama-and-nick-saban/ |url-status=live |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=CBSSports.com |language=en}}{{Cite web |last=Harney |first=Erin |date=November 24, 2019 |title=On this day in Alabama history: Auburn beat Alabama for sixth |last=Elkins |first=Ashley |date=December 30, 2007 |title=Speedy Alabama rolls past Colorado in Independence Bowl, 30–24 |url=https://www.djournal.com/sports/speedy-alabama-rolls-past-colorado-in-independence-bowl-30-24/article_cf6db04b-b30a-5912-9a96-f73554145066.html |url-status=live |archive-date=December 22, 2022 |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=Northeast Mississippi Daily Journal |language=en}} ====2008 season==== During his [[2008 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|second year]] as head coach of the Tide, Saban led his team from a sub-par season in 2007 to a perfect 12–0 regular season record. Saban finished the regular season undefeated for the first time in his career as a head coach as he led the Crimson Tide to its first undefeated regular season since [[1994 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|1994]].{{Cite web |title=Alabama Crimson Tide Football Record By Year |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/alabama/index.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221224223852/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/alabama/index.html |archive-date=December 24, 2022 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} He appeared on the September 1, 2008, cover of ''[[Forbes]]'' magazine as "The Most Powerful Coach in Sports."{{Cite web |last=Burke |first=Monte |date=August 7, 2008 |title=The Most Powerful Coach in Sports |url=https://www.forbes.com/forbes/2008/0901/092.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230119115546/https://www.forbes.com/forbes/2008/0901/092.html |archive-date=January 19, 2023 |access-date=December 24, 2022 |website=Forbes.com}} His second season as Alabama head coach began with a 34–10 victory over the No.{{nbsp}}9 ranked [[2008 Clemson Tigers football team|Clemson Tigers]] in the [[Chick-fil-A College Kickoff]] in the [[Georgia Dome]].{{Cite web |title=Clemson vs Alabama Box Score, August 30, 2008 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2008-08-30-alabama.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180120124750/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2008-08-30-alabama.html |archive-date=January 20, 2018 |access-date=December 24, 2022 Kentucky]] before entering SEC play.{{Cite web |title=Tulane at Alabama Box Score, September 6, 2008 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2008-09-06-alabama.html |url-status=live 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} In the following game, Alabama had a convincing 49–14 road-win over [[2008 Arkansas Razorbacks football team|Arkansas]].{{Cite web |title=Alabama at |last=Forde |first=Pat |date=September 27, 2008 |title=Forde: Rest in pieces, Bulldogs |url=https://www.espn.com/espn/columns/story?columnist=forde_pat&sportCat=ncf&id=3613594 |url-status=live Rebels football team|Ole Miss Rebels]] and finished the month of October with a 29–9 victory over the [[2008 Tennessee Volunteers football team|Tennessee Volunteers]].{{Cite web |title=Kentucky at Alabama Box Score, October 4, 2008 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2008-10-04-alabama.html |url-status=live |website=Rocky Top Talk |language=en}} Following a 35–0 homecoming victory over [[2008 Arkansas State Red Wolves football team|Arkansas State]], the Crimson Tide rose to No.{{nbsp}}1 in [[2008 NCAA Division I FBS football rankings|all major polls]] in Week 10{{nsmdns}}following a loss by No.{{nbsp}}1 [[2008 Texas Longhorns football team|Texas]] to the [[2008 Texas Tech Red Raiders football team|Texas Tech Red Raiders]].{{Cite web |title=Arkansas State at Alabama Box Score, November 1, 2008 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2008-11-01-alabama.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180205130311/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2008-11-01-alabama.html |archive-date=February 5, 2018 |access-date=December 24, 2022 1980 AP Football Rankings |url=http://appollarchive.com/football/ap/teams/by_season.cfm?teamid=25&seasonid=1980 |url-status=dead It was Alabama's first victory over Auburn since the [[2001 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|2001 season]].{{Cite web |last=Hurt |first=Cecil |date=November 29, 2008 |title=No. 1 Alabama ends Championship]], 32–13, in a rematch of the previous year's championship game.{{Cite web |last=Pow |first=Chris |date=December 7, 2009 |title=2009 SEC Championship full recap |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The championship represented Alabama's 22nd SEC title and its first since 1999.{{Cite web |date=December 5, 2009 |title=SEC CHAMPIONS: No. 2 Alabama Wins Its 22nd SEC Championship, Beating No. 1 Florida 32–13 |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2009/12/5/sec_champions_no_2_alabama_wins_its_22nd_sec_championship_beating_no_1_florida_32_13.aspx |archive-date=December 28, 2022 |access-date=December 28, 2022 |website=University of Alabama Athletics |language=en}} Saban's Crimson Tide ended the season with a 37–21 victory over the [[2009 Texas Longhorns football team|Texas Longhorns]] in the [[2010 BCS National Championship Game]] to finish a perfect 14–0 to give Alabama their first National Championship since 1992.{{Cite web |last=Flanagan |first=Ben |date=November 30, 2023 |title=Nick Saban's first Alabama SEC Championship team: Where are they now? Following the victory over the Longhorns, the University of Alabama announced that it would unveil a statue of Saban in the week prior to the kickoff of the 2010 season.{{Cite web |last=Kausler Jr. |first=Don |date=August 4, 2010 |title=Saban statue to be dedicated during Tide's 2010 season at a date to be determined |url=https://www.al.com/sports/2010/08/saban_statue_to_be_dedicated_d.html 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} On April 16, 2011, a life-sized bronzed statue of Saban was unveiled at the 2011 A-Day spring game, making him Alabama's fifth coach to be immortalized outside the north end zone of [[Bryant–Denny Stadium]].{{Cite web |date=April 16, 2011 |title=Alabama displays 9-foot statue of coach Saban Alabama was overwhelmingly chosen as the preseason No.{{nbsp}}1 team in both the AP and Coaches Poll.{{Cite web |date=August 6, 2010 |title=Alabama No. 1 in USA Today preseason poll; Tide got 55 of 59 |first=Jim |date=November 26, 2010 |title=Auburn Stays Perfect With 28–27 Victory Over Alabama |url=https://www.sfgate.com/sports/article/Auburn-Stays-Perfect-With-28-27-Victory-Over-2455618.php |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The following week at homecoming, Alabama shut out [[2011 Vanderbilt Commodores football team|Vanderbilt]], defeating them 34–0.{{Cite web |last=Kausler Jr. |first=Don |date=October 9, 2011 |title=Alabama 34, Vanderbilt 0: Grading the Tide |url=https://www.al.com/sports/2011/10/alabama_34_vanderbilt_0_gradin.html |url-status=live |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} Alabama traveled to Oxford and destroyed [[2011 Ole Miss Rebels football team|Ole Miss]] 52–7.{{Cite web |date=October 15, 2011 |title=Alabama crushes Ole Miss 52–7 |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2011/10/16/alabama-crushes-ole-miss-52-7/28391826007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221229154729/https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2011/10/16/alabama-crushes-ole-miss-52-7/28391826007/ |archive-date=December 29, 2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=The Tuscaloosa News |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} In week{{nbsp}}8, Alabama defeated their rival [[2011 Tennessee Volunteers football team|Tennessee]] 37–6 by scoring 31 unanswered points in the second half.{{Cite web |title=Tennessee at Alabama Box Score, October 22, 2011 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2011-10-22-alabama.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170722092853/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2011-10-22-alabama.html |archive-date=July 22, 2017 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} After a bye week, Alabama played host to [[2011 LSU Tigers football team|#1 LSU]], losing in overtime [[2011 LSU vs. Alabama football game|9–6]], a [[Game of the Century (college football)#2011 LSU vs. Alabama|Game of the Century]] matchup between conference teams ranked No.{{nbsp}}1 and No.{{nbsp}}2.{{Cite web |last=Tsoukalas |first=Tony |date=November 5, 2021 |title=Reliving the 'Game of the Century' on its 10th anniversary |url=https://alabama.rivals.com/news/reliving-the-game-of-the-century-on-its-10th-anniversary |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221229155531/https://alabama.rivals.com/news/reliving-the-game-of-the-century-on-its-10th-anniversary |archive-date=December 29, 2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=TideIllustrated}} Alabama rebounded the next week after struggling in the first half, with a win over [[2011 Mississippi State Bulldogs football team|Mississippi State]] 24–7.{{Cite web |date=November 13, 2011 |title=Trent Richardson rushes for 127 yards as Alabama rebounds from loss |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/313160344 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221229155738/https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/313160344 |archive-date=December 29, 2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The next week, Alabama defeated [[Football Championship Subdivision|FCS]] 3rd ranked [[2011 Georgia Southern Eagles football team|Georgia Southern]] 45–21 on Senior Day.{{Cite web |last=Zenor |first=John |date=November 19, 2011 |title=No. 3 Alabama powers past Georgia Southern, 45–21 |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/news/2011/11/19/no-3-alabama-powers-past-georgia-southern-45-21/28398055007/ |url-status=live
