2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=The Tuscaloosa News |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} This win gave Saban his fourth consecutive 10-win season, tying [[Paul Bear Bryant|Bear Bryant]] from 1977 to 1980. In the [[Iron Bowl]], Alabama defeated [[2011 Auburn Tigers football team|Auburn]] 42–14.{{Cite web |last=Roberts |first=Lenn |date=November 27, 2011 |title=Alabama defeats Auburn, on target for BCS berth |url=https://nypost.com/2011/11/27/alabama-defeats-auburn-on-target-for-bcs-berth/ |url-status=live |website=New York Post |language=en-US}} On December 4, Alabama was selected to face LSU in the [[2012 BCS National Championship Game|BCS National Championship Game]] by finishing No.{{nbsp}}2 in the final BCS rankings, the first time in college football history that two teams from the same conference (much less the same division of the same conference) played each other for the BCS Championship.{{Cite web |last=Tsoukalas |first=Tony |date=October 30, 2018 |title=RIVALRY REWIND: Alabama gets revenge over LSU in national championship game |url=https://alabama.rivals.com/news/rivalry-rewind-alabama-gets-revenge-over-lsu-in-national-championship-game |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221229161749/https://alabama.rivals.com/news/rivalry-rewind-alabama-gets-revenge-over-lsu-in-national-championship-game |archive-date=December 29, 2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=TideIllustrated}} In the rematch, Alabama defeated the Tigers 21–0 with a dominating defensive performance, improving Saban's record to 3–3 against [[Les Miles]] and his former employer, LSU.{{Cite web |date=January 9, 2012 |title=Alabama blanks LSU 21–0 for BCS crown |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/alabama-blanks-lsu-21-0-for-bcs-crown/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120111144636/http://www.cbsnews.com/8301-400_162-57355707/alabama-blanks-lsu-21-0-for-bcs-crown/ |archive-date=January 11, 2012 |access-date=January 12, 2012 |website=CBS News}} The win secured Saban his third BCS Championship, his second with Alabama, and the 14th{{nbsp}}National Championship for the Alabama football team.{{Cite web |date=January 9, 2012 |title=Crimson Tide Wins 14th National Championship, 21–0, Over LSU |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2012/1/9/Crimson_Tide_Wins_14th_National_Championship_21_0_Over_LSU.aspx |url-status=live |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=University of Alabama Athletics |language=en}}{{Cite web |last=Parks |first=James |date=July 13, 2022 |title=College football coaches with most national titles |url=https://www.si.com/fannation/college/cfb-hq/ncaa-football-rankings/college-football-coaches-most-national-championships-ranked |url-status=live |archive-date=December 29, 2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=College Football HQ |language=en}} He is the only coach in college football to win three BCS Championships and the first coach since Nebraska's [[Tom Osborne]] to win three National Championships.{{Cite web |last=Low |first=Chris |date=July 13, 2012 |title=Nick Saban joins elite company |archive-date=December 22, 2022 |access-date=December 22, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en}} ====2012 season==== At the start of his [[2012 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|sixth season]], schools since the [[2000 Orange Bowl]] with Alabama winning 41–14.{{Cite web |date=September 2, 2012 |title=Alabama, defending national champions, destroys Michigan 41–14 in season opener |first=Scott |date=December 3, 2022 |title=SEC Championship Game history: year-by-year game results, MVPs, all-time standings |archive-date=December 29, 2022 |access-date=December 29, 2022 |website=NOLA.com |language=en}} The win also clinched a spot in a BCS bowl game for the fourth time in five years.{{Cite web [[2013 BCS National Championship Game]].{{Cite web |last=Russo |first=Ralph D. |date=January 7, 2013 |title=Alabama bashes Notre Dame 42–14 in BCS title game On Senior Day, the Tide knocked off FCS opponent [[2013 Chattanooga Mocs football team|Chattanooga]] 49–0.{{Cite web |date=November 23, 2013 |title=No. 1 Alabama Posts Methodical 49–0 Win Over Chattanooga on Senior Day |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2013/11/23/No_1_Alabama_Posts_Methodical_49_0_Win_Over_Chattanooga_on_Senior_Day.aspx |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230101173709/https://rolltide.com/news/2013/11/23/No_1_Alabama_Posts_Methodical_49_0_Win_Over_Chattanooga_on_Senior_Day.aspx |archive-date=January 1, 2023 |access-date=January 1, 2023 |website=University of Alabama Athletics |language=en}} The next game was the [[Iron Bowl]], which was hosted by [[2013 Auburn Tigers football team|#4 Auburn]] in a matchup between top five teams. The game was tied 28–28 with only a single second remaining in regulation, as Alabama and Auburn appeared headed for overtime. Extra time wasn't necessary as Saban decided to attempt a 56-yard field goal instead of either running out the clock or attempting a Hail Mary from Auburn's 39-yard line. The decision proved costly, as the field goal was short but caught nine yards deep in the Auburn end zone by the Tigers' [[Chris Davis (cornerback)|Chris Davis]], who returned the failed attempt [[Kick Bama Kick|109 yards for a touchdown]] as time expired.{{Cite magazine |last=Rickman |first=Martin |date=November 30, 2013 |title=Video: Auburn beats Alabama 34–28 with missed field goal return for a touchdown |url=https://www.si.com/campus-union/2013/12/01/auburn-tigers-alabama-crimson-tide-chris-davis |url-status=live 2023 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us}}{{Cite web |last=Erickson |first=Joel A. |date=December 1, 2013 |title=Davis dash lifts resilient No. 4 Auburn to Iron Bowl win over top-ranked |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The 28–34 loss knocked Alabama out of contention for the SEC Championship and dashed The Tide's hope for a national championship.{{Cite web |date=December 3, 2017 |title=Alabama sneaks into College Football Playoff despite not making conference championship game |archive-date=January 25, 2022 |access-date=December 19, 2022 |newspaper=Washington Post |language=en-US}} In the rematch of the previous two National Championship games, Alabama avenged the previous |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/bowls/sugar-bowl.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221219014637/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/bowls/sugar-bowl.html |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} In the [[Iron Bowl]], Alabama avenged the previous season's only loss, to rival [[2018 Auburn Tigers football team|Auburn]], defeating the Tigers 52–21.{{Cite web |last=Blum |first=Sam |date=November 24, 2018 |title=Auburn instant analysis: Tigers' upset bid fails |url=https://www.al.com/auburnfootball/2018/11/instant-analysis-auburns-upset-bid-fails-in-52-21-iron-bowl-loss.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218003322/https://www.al.com/auburnfootball/2018/11/instant-analysis-auburns-upset-bid-fails-in-52-21-iron-bowl-loss.html |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The win secured Saban's fourth undefeated regular season.{{Cite web |last=Waack |first=Terrin |date=November 26, 2018 |title=Alabama avenges Auburn |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/2018/11/26/processing-tigers-alabama-avenges-auburn/8287476007/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218003443/https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/2018/11/26/processing-tigers-alabama-avenges-auburn/8287476007/ |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=The Tuscaloosa News |language=en-US}} In the [[2018 SEC Championship Game|SEC Championship]], Alabama, behind backup quarterback [[Jalen Hurts]], overcame a 14-point deficit to defeat [[2018 Georgia Bulldogs football team|#4 Georgia]] 35–28.{{Cite web |date=December 2, 2018 |title=Hurts rallies No. 1 Alabama to 35–28 win over No. 4 Georgia |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/401056705 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218003856/https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/401056705 |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The win gave Alabama its 27th SEC Championship.{{Cite web |last=Rogers |first=Ken |date=December 1, 2018 |title=Hurts rallies Alabama past Georgia in SEC Championship, 35–28 |url=https://dothaneagle.com/sports/alabama/hurts-rallies-alabama-past-georgia-in-sec-championship-35-28/article_c21a3668-f5ea-11e8-b291-27123db03131.html |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=Dothan Eagle |language=en}} It was Saban's eighth conference title, sixth with Alabama. The win also gave Saban's senior class its 54th win, breaking the previous year's NCAA record for most wins.{{Cite web |date=December 1, 2018 |title=Alabama vs. Georgia Postgame Notes |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2018/12/1/football-vs-georgia-postgame-notes |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218004124/https://rolltide.com/news/2018/12/1/football-vs-georgia-postgame-notes |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=University of Alabama Athletics |language=en}} The following day, Alabama was selected for the [[College Football Playoff]] for the fifth year in a row. They were selected as the #1 seed for the third time and played the #4 seed [[2018 Oklahoma Sooners football team|Oklahoma]] at the [[2018 Orange Bowl|Orange Bowl]].{{Cite magazine |last=Shapiro |first=Michael |date=December 2, 2018 |title=Alabama, Clemson, ND, OU reach College Football Playoff |url=https://www.si.com/college/2018/12/02/college-football-playoff-alabama-clemson-notre-dame-oklahoma-selection-committee |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218004211/https://www.si.com/college/2018/12/02/college-football-playoff-alabama-clemson-notre-dame-oklahoma-selection-committee |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us}} In the first meeting since the [[2014 Sugar Bowl]], the Tide ended a three-game losing streak to Oklahoma, winning 45–34.{{Cite web |last=Long |first=A. Stacy |date=December 29, 2018 |title=Orange Bowl expanded scoring summary: Alabama Crimson Tide 45, Oklahoma Sooners 34 |url=https://www.montgomeryadvertiser.com/story/sports/college/alabama/2018/12/29/expanded-scoring-summary-key-big-plays-alabama-crimson-tide-45-oklahoma-sooners-34-tackles-defense/2443256002/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218004424/https://www.montgomeryadvertiser.com/story/sports/college/alabama/2018/12/29/expanded-scoring-summary-key-big-plays-alabama-crimson-tide-45-oklahoma-sooners-34-tackles-defense/2443256002/ |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=Montgomery Advertiser |language=en-US}} It was Alabama's first Orange Bowl win since [[1966 Orange Bowl|1966]].{{Cite web |last=Evans |first=Erik |date=December 30, 2018 |title=HIGHLIGHTS: Tide wins first Orange Bowl in 52 years |url=https://www.rollbamaroll.com/2018/12/30/18161210/highlights-tide-wins-first-orange-bowl-in-52-years |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218004507/https://www.rollbamaroll.com/2018/12/30/18161210/highlights-tide-wins-first-orange-bowl-in-52-years |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=Roll 'Bama Roll |language=en}} The Tide advanced to the [[2019 College Football Playoff National Championship|National Championship]] game for the fourth consecutive year and played the #2 seed [[2018 Clemson Tigers football team|Clemson Tigers]]. |first=Tyler |date=October 3, 2020 |title=Alabama Football Races Past Texas A&M, 52–24 |url=https://www.si.com/college/alabama/bamacentral/alabama-football-races-past-texas-a-m-52-24 |url-status=live 18, 2022 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en}} The following week Saban took on former assistant [[Lane Kiffin]]. In a shootout, Alabama defeated [[2020 Ole Miss Rebels football team|Ole Miss]] 63–48.{{Cite web |last=Inabinett |first=Mark |date=November 12, 2022 |title=Alabama vs. Ole Miss by the numbers |url=https://www.al.com/sports/2022/11/alabama-vs-ole-miss-by-the-numbers-tide-good-for-40-against-rebels.html |url-status=live |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The 111 points scored was the most in a non-overtime conference game in SEC history.{{Cite web |date=October 10, 2020 |title=No. 2 Alabama beats Ole Miss 63–48 in record SEC outburst |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/ncaaf/2020/10/10/no-2-alabama-beats-ole-miss-63-48-in-record-sec-outburst/42743797/ |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=USA TODAY |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} On October 14, 2020, Saban tested positive for COVID-19, along with the university athletic director, Greg Byrne.{{Cite web |last=Blinder |first=Alan |date=October 14, 2020 |title=Nick Saban, Alabama's Football Coach, Tests Positive for Coronavirus |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/10/14/sports/ncaafootball/coronavirus-nick-saban-alabama.html |url-status=live 15, 2020 |website=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}} That Saturday, Saban tested negative and was able to coach Alabama in a top 3 matchup against [[2020 Georgia Bulldogs football team|#3 Georgia]]. The Tide prevailed 41–24 to improve to 22–0 versus former assistants.{{Cite web |last=Hale |first=David M. |date=October 17, 2020 |title=Alabama remains the SEC king, Trevor Lawrence remains unstoppable and more from Week 7 |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/page/gamedayfinal201017/alabama-remains-sec-king-trevor-lawrence-remains-unstoppable-more-week-7 |url-status=live |archive-date=October 18, 2020 |access-date=October 18, 2020 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en-US}} In the [[Third Saturday in October]], Alabama won its 14th straight over rival [[2020 Tennessee Volunteers football team|Tennessee]] 48–17.{{Cite web |title=Alabama at Tennessee Box Score, October 24, 2020 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2020-10-24-tennessee.html Alabama Box Score, October 31, 2020 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2020-10-31-alabama.html |url-status=live new No. 1, Notre Dame climbs and Clemson falls |url=https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2020-11-08/college-football-rankings-new-no-1-notre-dame-climbs-and-clemson-falls |url-status=live |last=Manning |first=Bryan |date=November 22, 2020 |title=Alabama destroys Kentucky 63–3 to remain undefeated |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=Roll Tide Wire |language=en-US}} On November 25, Saban returned a positive COVID-19 test. He missed the following game against [[2020 Auburn Tigers football team|#22 Auburn]] in the [[Iron Bowl]], which Alabama won 42–13.{{Cite web |last=Scarborough |first=Alex |date=November 25, 2020 |title=Alabama's Saban positive, out for Auburn game |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/30386828/alabama-coach-nick-saban-tests-positive-showing-covid-19-symptoms |url-status=live |archive-date=November 26, 2020 |access-date=November 26, 2020 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en}} The next week, Saban was back on the sideline, as Alabama routed [[2020 LSU Tigers football team|LSU]] 55–17.{{Cite magazine |last=Martin |first=Tyler |date=December 5, 2020 |title=Alabama Football Thrashes LSU, 55–17 |url=https://www.si.com/college/alabama/bamacentral/alabama-football-thrashes-lsu-55-17 |url-status=live 18, 2022 |website=River Region Sports |language=en-US}} In the [[2020 SEC Championship Game|SEC Championship]], Alabama hung on in a high scoring game against [[2020 Florida Gators football team|#7 Florida]] 52–46.{{Cite magazine |last=Blackwell |first=Joey |date=December 20, 2020 |title=Instant Analysis: No. 1 Alabama 52, No. 7 Florida 46 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=Touchdown Alabama |language=en-US}} The following day, Alabama was selected for the [[College Football Playoff]] for the sixth time in seven years. Alabama was selected as the #1 seed and played the #4 seed [[2020 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|Notre Dame]] in the [[2021 Rose Bowl|Rose Bowl]].{{Cite web |last=Rodak |first=Mike |date=December 20, 2020 |title=Tide to face Notre Dame in Rose Bowl playoff semifinal |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2020/12/alabama-to-face-notre-dame-in-playoff-semifinal.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218014302/https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2020/12/alabama-to-face-notre-dame-in-playoff-semifinal.html |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} In the first meeting since the [[2013 BCS National Championship Game]], the Tide defeated the Irish 31–14. It was Alabama's first appearance and win in the Rose Bowl since [[1946 Rose Bowl|1946]].{{Cite web |last=Casagrande |first=Michael |date=January 2, 2021 |title=The day the Rose Bowl came to cold, windy Dallas suburbs |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2021/01/the-day-the-rose-bowl-came-to-cold-windy-dallas-suburbs.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218014358/https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2021/01/the-day-the-rose-bowl-came-to-cold-windy-dallas-suburbs.html |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} On January 5, 2021, [[DeVonta Smith]] became Saban's third [[Heisman Trophy]] winner. [[Mac Jones]] and [[Najee Harris]] finished third and fifth respectively in voting.{{Cite web |title=2020 Heisman Trophy Voting |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/heisman-2020.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211217111535/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/heisman-2020.html |archive-date=December 17, 2021 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} Alabama became the second program to have three players finish in the top five in Heisman voting, joining Army's 1946 team.{{Cite web |last=Russo |first=Ralph D. |date=December 25, 2020 |title=Heisman Finalists: Tide Teammates Plus Lawrence and Trask |url=https://www.nbcphiladelphia.com/news/sports/heisman-finalists-tide-teammates-plus-lawrence-and-trask/2646491/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218014748/https://www.nbcphiladelphia.com/news/sports/heisman-finalists-tide-teammates-plus-lawrence-and-trask/2646491/ |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=NBC10 Philadelphia |language=en-US}} In the [[2021 College Football Playoff National Championship]], Alabama defeated [[2020 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|#3 Ohio State]] 52–24 to win its 18th National Championship.{{Cite web |last=Benedetto |first=James |date=January 11, 2021 |title=Alabama Defeats Ohio State 52–24, Secures 18th National Title |url=https://tuscaloosathread.com/alabama-defeats-ohio-state-52-24-secures-18th-national-title/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221218014959/https://tuscaloosathread.com/alabama-defeats-ohio-state-52-24-secures-18th-national-title/ |archive-date=December 18, 2022 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=Tuscaloosa Thread |language=en}} The win gave Saban his seventh overall National Title, and sixth with the Crimson Tide. Saban passed [[Bear Bryant|Paul W. Bryant]] for most titles all time.{{Cite web |last=Hummer |first=Chris |date=January 11, 2021 |title=Nick Saban built Alabama's 2020 champion (and dynasty) on a foundation of change |url=https://247sports.com/Article/Nick-Saban-national-championships-Alabama-dynasty-willingness-to-adapt-change-Bear-Bryant-159103815/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210112054043/https://247sports.com/Article/Nick-Saban-national-championships-Alabama-dynasty-willingness-to-adapt-change-Bear-Bryant-159103815/ |archive-date=January 12, 2021 |access-date=December 18, 2022 |website=247Sports |language=en-US}} It was Saban's second undefeated season and first since 2009. The Tide finished the season 13–0.{{Cite web |title=2020 Alabama Crimson Tide Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/alabama/2020-schedule.html |url-status=live |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} ====2023 season==== In Saban's 17th season, Alabama started the year ranked #4 in the preseason [[AP poll]] and #3 in the coaches poll.{{Cite web |last=Alvarez |first=Nick |date=August 14, 2023 |title=Alabama debuts at No. 4 in 2023 Associated Press Top 25 poll |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/08/alabama-football-debuts-at-no-4-in-2023-associated-press-top-25-poll.html |access-date=September 8, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}}{{Cite web |last=Stahl |first=Matt |date=August 7, 2023 |title=Alabama football ranked No. 3 nationally in first coaches poll of 2023 |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/08/alabama-football-ranked-no-3-nationally-in-first-coaches-poll-of-2023.html |access-date=September 8, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} In the season opener, Alabama defeated [[2023 Middle Tennessee Blue Raiders football team|Middle Tennessee]] 56–7.{{Cite web |last=Alvarez |first=Nick |date=September 2, 2023 |title=Rewinding as Alabama football stomps Middle Tennessee for Week 1 win |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/09/live-updates-alabama-football-opens-2023-season-against-middle-tennessee-state.html |access-date=September 8, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The following week, the Tide hosted [[2023 Texas Longhorns football team|#11 Texas]] in the Allstate Crossbar Classic losing 34–24.{{Cite web |date=September 10, 2023 |title=No. 11 Texas, Ewers pull off 34–24 victory at No. 3 Alabama |url=https://www.nbcsports.com/college-football/news/no-11-texas-ewers-pull-off-34-24-victory-at-no-3-alabama |access-date=October 1, 2023 |website=NBC Sports |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The loss snapped a 57-game winning streak against non conference opponents in the regular season.{{Cite web |date=September 9, 2023 |title=No. 3/3 Alabama Falls at Home to No. 11/10 Texas, 34–24 |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2023/9/9/football-no-3-3-alabama-falls-at-home-to-no-11-10-texas-34-24.aspx |access-date=October 1, 2023 |website=University of Alabama Athletics |language=en}} The next week Alabama travelled to [[Tampa, Florida|Tampa, FL]] to play [[2023 South Florida Bulls football team|South Florida]]. The Tide struggled but won 17–3.{{Cite web |last=Backus |first=Will |date=September 17, 2023 |title=Alabama vs. South Florida score, takeaways: Revolving QB door keeps turning as No. 10 Tide sluggish in win |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/alabama-vs-south-florida-score-takeaways-revolving-qb-door-keeps-turning-as-no-10-tide-sluggish-in-win/live/ |access-date=October 1, 2023 |website=CBSSports.com |language=en}} Alabama opened up conference play with a 24–10 win over [[2023 Ole Miss Rebels football team|#15 Ole Miss]].{{Cite web |last=Conlon |first=Riley |date=September 24, 2023 |title=Milroe, No. 13 Alabama's defense prevail 24–10 over No. 15 Ole Miss |url=https://www.wvtm13.com/article/alabama-ole-miss-football-jalen-milroe/45285296 |access-date=October 1, 2023 |website=WVTM |language=en}} The next week they defeated [[2023 Mississippi State Bulldogs football team|Mississippi State]] 40–17 for its sixteenth consecutive win in the series.{{Cite web |last=Faulk |first=Robbie |date=October 1, 2023 |title=No. 12 Alabama beats Mississippi State for 16th straight time, 40–17 in Starkville |url=https://apnews.com/article/alabama-crimson-tide-mississippi-state-bulldogs-sec-ec83f74a7ab8621a23a016b4f73753cf |access-date=October 1, 2023 |website=AP News |language=en}} Alabama traveled to [[College Station, Texas|College Station, TX]] winning a close game over [[2023 Texas A&M Aggies football team|Texas A&M]] 26–20.{{Cite web |last=Alvarez |first=Nick |date=October 7, 2023 |title=Saban shares good and bad news from Alabama's 26–20 win over Texas A&M |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/10/live-updates-nick-saban-talks-alabamas-26-20-win-over-texas-am.html |access-date=November 1, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} On homecoming, Alabama won a close game over [[2023 Arkansas Razorbacks football team|Arkansas]] 24–21.{{Cite web |last=Alvarez |first=Nick |date=October 14, 2023 |title=Recapping as No. 11 Alabama outlasts Arkansas, 24–21, for homecoming win |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/10/live-updates-scores-injuries-no-11-alabama-hosts-arkansas-for-homecoming.html |access-date=November 1, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} The win gave Saban his 200th on-field win at Alabama.{{Cite magazine |last=Byler |first=Blake |date=October 14, 2023 |title=Notebook: Nick Saban Picks Up 200th Win at Alabama |url=https://www.si.com/college/alabama/football/notebook-nick-saban-picks-up-200th-win-at-alabama |access-date=November 1, 2023 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en}} In the [[Third Saturday in October]], Alabama avenged last season's loss against [[2023 Tennessee Volunteers football team|#17 Tennessee]] winning 34–20.{{Cite web |last=Stephenson |first=Creg |date=October 21, 2023 |title=Alabama's comeback win one of its largest under Saban |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/10/alabamas-comeback-win-over-tennessee-one-of-its-largest-under-nick-saban.html |access-date=November 1, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}} Following a bye week, Alabama avenged their other regular season loss from last season defeating [[2023 LSU Tigers football team|#14 LSU]] 42–28.{{Cite web |last=Blackwood |first=Stacey |date=November 5, 2023 |title=Everything Nick Saban said following Alabama's 42–28 win over LSU |url=https://rolltidewire.usatoday.com/lists/alabama-football-everything-nick-saban-said-following-alabamas-42-28-win-over-lsu/ |access-date=November 6, 2023 |website=Roll Tide Wire |publisher=USA Today |language=en-US}} The next week Alabama defeated [[2023 Kentucky Wildcats football team|Kentucky]] 49–21 to win the SEC Western Division title.{{Cite magazine |last=Gibson |first=Mathey |date=November 11, 2023 |title=No. 8 Alabama Secures SEC West Title, defeats Kentucky 49–21 |url=https://www.si.com/college/alabama/football/no-8-alabama-secures-sec-west-title-defeats-kentucky-49-21 |access-date=December 2, 2023 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en}} It is Saban's 15th overall division title and 13th with Alabama. On Senior Day, Alabama routed FCS opponent [[2023 Chattanooga Mocs football team|Chattanooga]] 66–10.{{Cite web |date=November 18, 2023 |title=Milroe, No. 8 Alabama roll past FCS team Chattanooga, 66–10 for 9th straight win |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/gametracker/recap/NCAAF_20231118_TNCHAT@BAMA/ |access-date=December 2, 2023 |website=CBSSports.com |language=en |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The win gave Saban his sixteenth straight 10-win season.{{Cite web |last=McNair |first=Kirk |date=November 18, 2023 |title=Alabama Extends NCAA Record With 16th Straight 10-Win Season |url=https://247sports.com/college/alabama/article/notes-from-alabamas-66-10-win-over-tennessee-chattanooga-on-senior-day-as-tide-completes-home-schedule-220722229/ |access-date=December 2, 2023 |website=247Sports |language=en}} In the [[Iron Bowl]], quarterback [[Jalen Milroe]] on 4th and goal threw a 31-yard touchdown pass with 32 seconds left to defeat [[2023 Auburn Tigers football team|Auburn]] 27–24.{{Cite web |last=Stahl |first=Matt |date=December 1, 2023 |title=Jalen Milroe found his confidence. It saved Alabama's season |url=https://www.al.com/alabamafootball/2023/12/jalen-milroe-found-his-confidence-it-saved-alabamas-season.html |access-date=December 2, 2023 |website=[[The Birmingham News|AL.com]] |language=en}}{{Cite web |date=November 26, 2023 |title=Milroe's TD pass to Bond on fourth-and-31 rescues No. 8 Alabama in 27–24 win over Auburn |url=https://www.theoaklandpress.com/2023/11/25/milroes-td-pass-to-bond-on-fourth-and-31-rescues-no-8-alabama-in-27-24-win-over-auburn/ |access-date=December 2, 2023 |website=The Oakland Press |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The win made Saban the first Alabama coach since [[Bear Bryant]] in 1981 to defeat Auburn four years in a row.{{Cite web |last=Derr |first=Cam |date=November 26, 2023 |title=No. 8 Alabama pulls out thrilling fourth quarter victory over Auburn in Iron Bowl |url=https://www.waff.com/2023/11/26/no-8-alabama-pulls-out-thrilling-fourth-quarter-victory-over-auburn-iron-bowl/ |access-date=December 2, 2023 |website=WAFF |language=en}} The Tide finished the regular season 11–1.{{Cite web |title=2023 Alabama Crimson Tide Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/alabama/2023-schedule.html |access-date=December 2, 2023 |website=[[Sports Reference]] |language=en}} In the [[2023 SEC Championship Game|SEC Championship]], Alabama defeated [[2023 Georgia Bulldogs football team|#1 Georgia]] 27–24, snapping Georgia's 29 game win streak.{{Cite web |last=Newberry |first=Paul |date=December 2, 2023 |title=No. 8 Alabama knocks off No. 1 Georgia 27–24 for SEC title. Both teams await postseason fate |url=https://www.seattletimes.com/sports/no-8-alabama-knocks-off-no-1-georgia-27-24-for-sec-title-both-teams-await-postseason-fate/ |access-date=December 29, 2023 |website=The Seattle Times |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} The win secured Alabama its 30th SEC Championship.{{Cite web |date=December 2, 2023 |title=No. 8/8/8 Alabama Upsets No. 1/1/1 Georgia, 27–24, to Capture 30th SEC Championship |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2023/12/2/football-no-8-8-8-alabama-upsets-no-1-1-1-georgia-27-24-to-capture-30th-sec-championship.aspx |access-date=December 29, 2023 |website=University of Alabama Athletics |language=en}} The win gave Saban his eleventh SEC title and ninth with Alabama.{{Cite web |last=Low |first=Chris |date=December 3, 2023 |title=Tide beat UGA then state CFP case: We belong |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/39029380/alabama-ends-georgia-29-game-win-streak-takes-sec-title |access-date=December 29, 2023 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en}} The following day, Alabama was selected for the [[College Football Playoff]] for the eighth time.{{Cite web |last=Paschall |first=David |date=December 3, 2023 |title=Alabama in, Florida State and Georgia out of CFP four-team field |url=https://www.timesfreepress.com/news/2023/dec/03/alabama-in-florida-state-and-georgia-out-of-cfp-four-team-field/ |access-date=December 29, 2023 |website=Chattanooga Times Free Press |language=en}} They were selected as the #4 seed and would play the #1 seed [[2023 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] in the [[2024 Rose Bowl|Rose Bowl]].{{Cite web |last=McCollough |first=J. Brady |date=December 29, 2023 |title=Rose Bowl Game: Five things to know about Michigan vs. Alabama |url=https://www.latimes.com/sports/story/2023-12-29/michigan-alabama-rose-bowl-cfp-semifinal-jalen-milroe-j-j-mccarthy |access-date=December 29, 2023 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US}} In the first meeting since the [[2020 Citrus Bowl]], Michigan knocked off Alabama 27–20 in overtime.{{Cite web |date=January 2, 2024 |title=Michigan beats Alabama 27–20 in overtime on Blake Corum's TD run to reach national title game |url=https://www.nbcsports.com/college-football/news/michigan-beats-alabama-27-20-in-overtime-on-blake-corums-td-run-to-reach-national-title-game |access-date=January 3, 2024 |website=NBC Sports |language=en-US |agency=[[Associated Press]]}} It was Saban's first playoff semifinal loss since 2014.{{Cite web |last=Gharib |first=Anthony |date=January 2, 2024 |title=Jim Harbaugh becomes Michigan man and more trends since Alabama's last CFP semifinals lost |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/39225211/alabama-crimson-tide-last-cfp-semifinals-loss-rose-bowl |access-date=January 3, 2024 |website=[[ESPN.com]] |language=en}} The loss snapped a fifteen-year streak of being ranked #1 in the polls at any time during the season and marked the first time at Alabama that Saban went three consecutive seasons without winning a national championship.{{Cite web |last=Smith III |first=Kennington |date=January 2, 2024 |title=Was Alabama's season a success? Crimson Tide reflect on painful ending to 'amazing' year |url=https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/5174180/2024/01/02/alabama-football-rose-bowl-nick-saban/ |access-date=January 3, 2024 |website=The Athletic |language=en}} The Tide finished the season 12–2. Saban announced his retirement from coaching on January 10, 2024.{{Cite web |date=January 10, 2024 |title=Nick Saban Announces Retirement after 17 Seasons at Alabama |url=https://rolltide.com/news/2024/1/10/football-nick-saban-announces-retirement-after-17-seasons-at-alabama.aspx |access-date=January 10, 2024 |website=Rolltide.com ([[Alabama Crimson Tide]])}} Saban finished his coaching career at Alabama with a record of 201–29.{{Cite web |last=Goodbread |first=Chase |date=January 10, 2024 |title=Nick Saban career, by the numbers: Alabama football record, championships and more |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/college/football/2024/01/10/nick-saban-career-alabama-football-coach-retires/71802166007/ |access-date=January 11, 2024 |website=The Tuscaloosa News |language=en-US}} In 2024, the University of Alabama named the field at Bryant-Denny Stadium after Saban. On September 7, the field officially became known as Saban Field at Bryant-Denny Stadium at a dedication.{{Cite web |last=Zenor |first=John |date=September 7, 2024 |title=Alabama officially adds Saban Field to stadium name in pre-game ceremony |url=https://apnews.com/article/alabama-football-nick-saban-field-4317aa95248d85c539099552179bf999 |access-date=September 8, 2024 |website=AP News}} In June 2024, ''[[The Tuscaloosa News]]'' reported that Saban currently serves in an advisory role for Alabama's athletics program with an annual salary of $500,000 and also has an office at Bryant–Denny Stadium.{{Cite web |last=Kelly |first=Nick |date=June 27, 2024 |title=How much Nick Saban makes in new role at UA after retiring from Alabama football |url=https://www.tuscaloosanews.com/story/sports/college/football/2024/06/27/nick-saban-salary-alabama-football-retirement-espn-college-gameday-athletics/74237650007/ |access-date=December 15, 2024 |website=The Tuscaloosa News |language=en-US}} ===Record against conference opponents=== In his time as a head coach, Saban recorded a 161–33 (.829) record in SEC Conference play.{{Cite web |last=Nagel |first=Cody |date=January 11, 2024 |title=Nick Saban retires: Career record against every SEC team for Alabama coaching legend |url=https://247sports.com/longformarticle/nick-saban-retires-career-record-against-every-sec-team-for-alabama-coaching-legend-225117696/ |access-date=May 31, 2024 |website=247Sports |language=en}} The following are his records against each team: *Alabama: 4–1 *Arkansas: 20–2 *Auburn: 14–8 *Florida:10–4 *Georgia: 10–3 *Kentucky: 9–0 *LSU: 13–6 *Mississippi State: 21–1 *Missouri: 4–0 *Ole Miss: 19–3 *South Carolina: 4–1 *Tennessee: 18–2 *Texas A&M: 10–2 *Vanderbilt: 5–0 == Broadcasting career == [[File:MBN S24G02 TEXAS-14.jpg|thumb|Saban (left) alongside [[Lee Corso]] (center) and [[Kirk Herbstreit]] (right) during a [[College GameDay (football TV program)|College GameDay]] broadcast in 2024. ]]Since 2023, Saban has appeared as a weekly guest on ''[[The Pat McAfee Show]]'' during the college football season.{{Cite web |last=Kasabian |first=Paul |date=September 8, 2023 |title=Alabama's Nick Saban to Make Weekly 'Pat McAfee Show' Appearances During 2023 Season |url=https://bleacherreport.com/articles/10088754-alabamas-nick-saban-to-make-weekly-pat-mcafee-show-appearances-during-2023-season |access-date=February 8, 2024 |website=Bleacher Report |language=en}} On February 7, 2024, Saban was hired by ESPN as an analyst for ''[[College GameDay (football TV program)|College GameDay]]'', beginning with the 2024 college football season. The network announced that Saban would contribute to its NFL Draft and SEC Media Days coverage as well.{{Cite web |last=McKay |first=Julie |date=February 7, 2024 |title=Nick Saban Joins ESPN |url=https://espnpressroom.com/us/press-releases/2024/02/nick-saban-joins-espn/ |access-date=February 7, 2024 |website=ESPN Press Row}} Saban had appeared on the network previously as a guest analyst during [[College Football Playoff]] coverage.{{Cite magazine |last=Scott |first=Jelani |date=January 9, 2023 |title=Nick Saban Gives Priceless Reaction After David Pollack Says Georgia Has Taken Over College Football |url=https://www.si.com/extra-mustard/2023/01/10/nick-saban-reaction-david-pollack-georgia-taken-over-college-football |access-date=February 8, 2024 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us}} ==Personal life== Saban is a devout [[Catholic]]; he arranged for himself and his players who were Catholic to receive [[Mass in the Catholic Church|Mass]] before each game, usually local clergy presided over the brief services.{{Cite web |last=Medina |first=Carlos E. |date=September 30, 2011 |title=Alabama Crimson Tide football team rolls into Ocala Friday afternoon |url=http://www.ocala.com/article/20110930/ARTICLES/110939983?p=2&tc=pg |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140102200130/http://www.ocala.com/article/20110930/ARTICLES/110939983?p=2&tc=pg |archive-date=January 2, 2014 |access-date=March 25, 2014 [[Bill Belichick]] and Saban are friends, having known each other since 1982.{{Cite magazine |last=Vrentas |first=Jenny |date=January 18, 2018 |title=Belichick & Saban: The stories behind a friendship |url=https://www.si.com/nfl/2018/01/18/bill-belichick-nick-saban-friendship-relationship-patriots-alabama-browns |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230119045823/https://www.si.com/nfl/2018/01/18/bill-belichick-nick-saban-friendship-relationship-patriots-alabama-browns |archive-date=January 19, 2023 |access-date=January 19, 2023 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us}} In 2019, when Saban spoke about their relationship, he said: "Sometimes people have a real true love and respect for someone. You really love to see them have this success and continue to have this success. I don't know how many people can really understand that kind of relationship. That's kind of how I feel about Bill."{{Cite web |last=Eric Levenson |date=January 12, 2024 |title=The many connections of Bill Belichick and Nick Saban, departing titans of football |url=https://www.cnn.com/2024/01/12/sport/bill-belichick-nick-saban-connections/index.html |access-date=May 6, 2024 |publisher=CNN}} [[File:Nick Saban, who is the Alabama team coach, gives interviews and watches all the plays during this important spring scrimmage at University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa, Alabama LCCN2010638313 (cropped).jpg|thumb|Saban in 2010]] Saban shares his last name with another famous football coach, [[Lou Saban]]. They were called "distant cousins" in a 2005 article where Lou comments on the younger Saban's success.{{Cite web |last1=Fialkov |first1=Harvey |last2=Hijek |first2=Barbara |date=December 4, 2005 |title=Sabans Know Coaching |url=http://articles.sun-sentinel.com/2005-12-04/sports/0512040104_1_nick-lou-saban-browns-buffalo-bills-coach |url-status=dead |access-date=November 27, 2022 |website=Sun-Sentinel}} Upon the death of Lou Saban, his widow Joyce Saban said the two men might have been second cousins,{{Cite web |date=March 30, 2009 |title=Lou Saban dies at 87; NFL coach and Yankees executive |url=http://www.latimes.com/local/obituaries/la-me-lou-saban30-2009mar30-story.html |url-status=live requested to simply repeat a conversation he had with [[Michael Oher]] rather than follow the script, which director [[John Lee Hancock]] allowed him to do.{{Cite web |last=Spencer |first=Adam This foundation has been used by the Sabans to help support children's needs ever since Saban started head coaching. revision.text:
{{Short description|New Zealand local shipping and transport company}} {{Use dmy dates|date=September 2017}} {{Use New Zealand English|date=September 2017}} [[File:1922 Northern Steamship, Auckland.jpg|thumb|The east coast Northern Steamship fleet at Auckland in 1922, during a strike.]] [[File:Northern Steam Ship Company -The Northern Steam Ship Company's annual summer cruise. 1.- SS Ngapuhi. Photo by Jones and Coleman (Auckland, N.Z. (Postcard). (21065156802).jpg|thumb|''Ngapuhi'' on The Northern Steam Ship Company's annual summer cruise at [[Kawau Island|Kawau]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ngapuhi, Kawau |url=https://hocken.recollect.co.nz/nodes/view/54054 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=Hocken Digital Collections}}</ref> about 1907]] The '''Northern Steam Ship Company Ltd''' (NSS) served the northern half of the [[North Island]] of [[New Zealand]] from 1881 to 1974. Its headquarters, the Northern Steam Ship Company Building, remains in use on [[Quay Street, Auckland|Quay Street]], [[Auckland]] as a bar and is listed by [[Heritage New Zealand]] as a [[List of category 1 historic places in Auckland|Category I Historic Place]].
== Origins == Initially there were very few roads and they were muddy and narrow, so a constant theme in early papers was a demand from small coastal settlements for a regular shipping service to link them with the major ports.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=DSC18650220.2.12&srpos=74&zto=1|title=Steam Subsidy To The North.|date=1865-02-20|work=[[Daily Southern Cross]]|pages=4|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> For example, in 1874 a steamer service from [[Onehunga]] to [[Raglan, New Zealand|Raglan]] and [[Port Waikato]] was given a subsidy by [[Auckland Province]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&d=WT18740528.2.7&e&zto=1|title=Raglan.|date=1874-05-28|work=Waikato Times|pages=2|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref>
Capt. Alexander McGregor had the steam ship ''Rowena'' built in Auckland in 1872. He joined with a syndicate of owners to run the ''Argyle'', ''Iona'', ''Glenelg'', ''Staffa'', ''Rowena'', ''Fingal'' and ''Katikati'', as Auckland Steam Packet Co.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/birth.htm|title=The birth of the Northern Steam Ship Company|website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=2016-05-27|archive-date=3 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160403171432/http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/birth.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref> ASP went into liquidation in 1878<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&d=NZH18780624.2.49&zto=1|title=The Auckland Steam Packet|date=1878-06-24|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=6|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> due to losses on a ship for the [[Fiji]] trade, the ''SS Llewellyn''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1301&dat=18780130&id=Xt1jAAAAIBAJ&sjid=IJEDAAAAIBAJ&pg=6887,3207384&hl=en|title=The Sydney Morning Herald - Google News Archive Search|website=news.google.com|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> On 10 January 1878 ASP had sold ''Southern Cross'' for £7000, ''[[SS Go Ahead|Go-Ahead]]'' for £2500, ''Pretty Jane'' for £2350 and the ''Cantera'' hulk and her coal for £384, to what was described as a newly formed Auckland company.<ref>{{Cite web|date=12 Jan 1878|title=TELEGRAPHIC. COLONIST|url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TC18780112.2.5|access-date=2020-08-28|website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
On 11 May 1881 nineteen businessmen, including some of the syndicate, formed NSS, buying the ASP's ships. Thomas Morrin, David Cruickshank, Alexander McGregor, [[Thomas Ball (New Zealand politician)|Thomas Ball]], James Macfarlane and [[James Clark (businessman)|James McCosh Clark]] were the first directors.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |date=12 May 1881 |title=Northern Steamship Company. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18810512.2.3.7 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
NSS paid a 10% dividend in its first year<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH18820522.2.3&srpos=56&e&zto=1|title=Our Home Letter.|date=1882-05-22|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=2 3|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> and started acquiring extra ships. The 256 ton ''Macgregor'' entered service in September 1881.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |url= http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/biographies/2m6/mcgregor-alexander |title=McGregor, Alexander |last=Taonga |first=New Zealand Ministry for Culture and Heritage Te Manatu |website=www.teara.govt.nz |language=en |access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> In 1883 Capt. McGregor went to Britain to buy new steamers for the [[Tauranga]] and [[Russell, New Zealand|Russell]] routes. In 1884 ''Clansman'' and ''Gairloch'' arrived.<ref name=":0" /> In 1886 the ''Waimarie'' was one of the first to have electric lighting.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 August 1929 |title=LATE SHIPPING. Star (Christchurch) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TS19290805.2.111 |access-date=2023-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
NSS was also active in opening up new ports, such as at [[Te Puke]] in 1881<ref>{{cite news |url= https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=BOPT18810521.2.5 |title=(editorial) |newspaper=The Bay of Plenty Times |via=Papers Past |date=21 May 1881 |access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> and ports north of [[Whangārei]] in 1893.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH18930211.2.39&srpos=65&zto=1|title=A New Northern Trade.|date=1893-02-11|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=5|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref>
== 1887–1921 == [[File:Rotomahana_(centre_right)_at_Auckland_in_1903.jpg|thumb|Auckland in 1903 ''Wakatere'' (left), ''Rotomahana'' (centre), ''Terranora'' (right)]] On 6 June 1888 Capt. McGregor was replaced as manager by Charles Ranson. He retired in 1921, after greatly expanding the company.<ref name=":7">{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/ranson.htm|title=Northern Steam Ship Company- The Ranson Period 1887|website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=2016-05-27|archive-date=3 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160403183540/http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/ranson.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== 1887 industrial dispute === By 1887 the depression<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/economic-history/page-5|title=5. – Economic history – Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand|last=Taonga|first=New Zealand Ministry for Culture and Heritage Te Manatu|website=www.teara.govt.nz|language=en|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> was affecting the company; wages and overtime pay were cut,<ref name=":2" /> and seamen who objected were replaced with non-unionists.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/biographies/2m47/millar-john-andrew|title=Millar, John Andrew|last=Taonga|first=New Zealand Ministry for Culture and Heritage Te Manatu|website=www.teara.govt.nz|language=en|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> In response the [[Maritime Union of New Zealand|Federated Seamen's Union]] formed the co-operative Jubilee Steam Ship Co, competing on the northern and [[Waitara, New Zealand|Waitara]] routes. It wasn't expected to succeed,<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&d=NZH18871005.2.11&zto=1|title=The Jubilee Steamship Company.|date=1887-10-05|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=3|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> but, between September 1887 and October 1888, the FSU claimed that Jubilee lost £14,000 and NSS £22,000.<ref>{{Cite thesis|url=http://researcharchive.vuw.ac.nz/xmlui/bitstream/handle/10063/1636/thesis.pdf?sequence=2|title=Manufacturing Consensus? New Zealand - Press Attitudes Toward the Labour Movement in 1890|last=Keating|first=James|date=2011|publisher=Open Access Te Herenga Waka-Victoria University of Wellington |doi=10.26686/wgtn.16985248.v1 |type=thesis }}</ref> It also caused another company to withdraw from the west coast,<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS18880113.2.58&srpos=32&zto=1|title=Sale Of The Oreti.|date=1888-01-13|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=8|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> which in the end was helpful to NSS. When Ranson took over, one of his first acts was to re-engage all the FSU members, though he also cut staff. He remained a lifelong friend of the union boss, [[John A. Millar]],<ref name=":3">{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS19210801.2.72&srpos=624&zto=1|title=Mr. Chas. Ranson Retires.|date=1921-08-01|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=7|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> and received an award from staff in 1897 for his considerate attitude to them.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH18971225.2.30&srpos=137&zto=1|title=Presentation To Mr. Chas. Ranson.|date=1897-12-25|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=5|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> However, in the [[1913 Great Strike]], NSS backed a ‘[[Strikebreaker|scab’ union]] (the Auckland Seamen and Firemen's Union).<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://nzsf.org.nz/system/comfy/cms/files/files/000/000/071/original/2009_A_Voice_for_Shipping_full_book.pdf|title=A voice for shipping : the New Zealand Shipping Federation story|last=McLean|first=Gavin|date=2009|access-date=29 May 2016|archive-date=22 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160122063325/http://nzsf.org.nz/system/comfy/cms/files/files/000/000/071/original/2009_A_Voice_for_Shipping_full_book.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Charles Ranson, manager === [[File:Charles Ranson.jpg|thumb|Auckland Weekly News [https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/244989 obituary]]] Charles Ranson was born, in [[Ipswich]]<ref name=":4">{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19000926.2.72.22&srpos=190&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Co., Ltd.|date=1900-09-26|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=4|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> about 1850. He emigrated in 1876, managed an auctioneering and shipping business in [[Hāwera]], then went to Auckland as an accountant in 1881. In 1887, he was about to return to England, when NSS director, James McCosh Clark, mentioned that the board had decided to [[Liquidation|wind up]] the company. When Charles took over, NSS had 8<ref name=":5">{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NA19250114.2.19&srpos=667&zto=1|title=Obituary.|date=1925-01-14|work=Northern Advocate|pages=4|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> (or 9)<ref name=":4" /> vessels, and 100 employees. When he retired, in July 1921,<ref>{{cite news |url= https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19210713.2.43 |title=Northern Company |newspaper=The Bay of Plenty Times |date=13 July 1921 |via=Papers Past |language=en |access-date=2017-03-03}}</ref> NSS had 40 vessels<ref name=":5" /> and 850 employees. Cargo had increased from 51,000 tons a year to 220,000 and passengers from 39,000 to 190,000.<ref name=":3" />
After his appointment, NSS bought some second-hand screw-steamers, the ''[[SS Rotomahana (1876)|Rotomahana]]'', ''Waiotahi'' and ''Ohinemuri'' and small [[paddle steamer]]s, ''Te Aroha'' and ''Enterprise'', to work on the [[Waihou River]].<ref name=":4" /> This got rid of another competitor, Hauraki Steamship Co,<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.ohinemuri.org.nz/historic-texts/karangahake-school-70th-jubilee/302-early-water-transport?highlight=WyJub3J0aGVybiIsInN0ZWFtc2hpcCIsIm5vcnRoZXJuIHN0ZWFtc2hpcCJd|title=Ohinemuri - Early Water Transport|website=www.ohinemuri.org.nz|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref> who sold out to NSS.<ref name=":7"/> Much of the expansion of the company was by takeovers.<ref name=":3" /> Thames River Steam Navigation Co Ltd was bought on 1 August 1890.<ref name=":822">{{Cite web |date=7 August 1890 |title=Te Aroha. Waikato Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WT18900807.2.37 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In July 1906 NSS bought Manukau Steam Shipping Company.<ref name=":24">{{Cite web |date=28 July 1906 |title=Manukau Steam Shipping Company. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19060728.2.68 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 1 October 1908 Settlers' Steamship Company (SSC) merged with NSS,<ref name=":310">{{Cite web |date=2 October 1908 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19090526.2.18 |access-date=2024-03-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> adding six steamers to the fleet.<ref name=":10">{{Cite web |date=3 September 1908 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19080903.2.3.3 |access-date=2024-03-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> SSC had been formed in 1905 by the merger of Coastal Steamship and McGregor Steamship Companies.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 October 1905 |title=Settlers' Steamship Co. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19051028.2.36 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Coastal Steamship was formed in 1898.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 July 1898 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18980706.2.31.7 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
The company slowly recovered from its near liquidation. Until 2{{frac|1|2}}% was paid in 1890, no dividends had been paid since 1881, but then 5% was paid in 1891, 6% 1892-6,<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS18960515.2.54&srpos=115&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Company.|date=1896-05-15|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=4|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref> 7% in 1897<ref name=":4" /> and for many years,<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19000926.2.72.22&srpos=190&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Co., Ltd.|date=1900-09-26|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=4|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19020510.2.70&srpos=233&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Company, Limited.|date=1902-05-10|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=6|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19030516.2.70&srpos=274&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Company.|date=1903-05-16|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=6|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19070524.2.10&srpos=372&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Company.|date=1907-05-24|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=3|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS19110526.2.15&srpos=460&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Company.|date=1911-05-26|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=2|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://nzetc.victoria.ac.nz/tm/scholarly/tei-Cyc02Cycl-t1-body1-d1-d62-d4.html|title=Northern Steamship Company, Limited {{!}} NZETC|website=nzetc.victoria.ac.nz|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> and 8% in 1925.<ref name=":6">{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS19250514.2.10.1&srpos=674&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Co.|date=1925-05-14|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=4|access-date=2016-05-27}}</ref> 5% was paid in 1944.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=EP19440626.2.38&srpos=34&zto=1|title=Commercial|date=1944-06-26|work=[[The Evening Post (New Zealand)|The Evening Post]]|pages=3 4|access-date=2016-06-04}}</ref>
By 1895 NSS was asking for urgent extension of its wharf space at Auckland to serve its growth.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH18951127.2.42&srpos=100&zto=1|title=Berthage Accommodation|date=1895-11-27|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=5|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref> In the 1890s the fleet grew rapidly, with new iron ships built in Britain and timber ships in Auckland.<ref name="paperspast.natlib.govt.nz">{{Cite web |date=5 February 1896 |title=The Northern Steamship Company. Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS18960205.2.24 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
In 1909 NSS and [[Union Company|Union Steam Ship]] (Union) aimed to save money on their repairs, when they set up a jointly owned repair company, United Repairing Co Ltd,<ref name=":18">{{Cite web |date=26 May 1909 |title=Company meetings. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19090526.2.18 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at Quay Street, Auckland,<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 February 1909 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19090225.2.3.6 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> which was next door to the NSS offices until 1962.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Union Fish Company Building |url=https://www.heritage.org.nz/list-details/666/Listing#details |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=Welcome to Heritage New Zealand |language=en-NZ}}</ref> [[Viaduct Harbour#Shipyards|Senior Foundry]] was bought by NSS in 1927<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 November 1927 |title=Foundry shares sale. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19271102.2.135 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to repair plates and boilers.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 May 1928 |title=The Northern Company. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19280518.2.24 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
Charles was also a steward of the [[Ellerslie Racecourse|Auckland Racing Club]], an enthusiastic bowler, keenly interested in regattas and a church warden. He lived in Bassett Rd, Remuera, where he died on 13 January 1925.<ref name=":5" />
Capt. Hammond took over as manager in 1921,<ref name=":7"/> having formerly been manager of [[Kaipara Harbour|Kaipara]] Steamship Company.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 September 1945 |title=Mariner's death. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19450912.2.94 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
=== The Northern Steam Ship Company Building, Quay St === [[File:1898 Northern Steam Ship Company Ltd building.jpg|thumb|[http://www.aucklandmuseum.com/collection/object/am_library-photography-69275 NSS building] - date not stated - compare with a [http://www.aucklandmuseum.com/collection/object/am_library-photography-2201 post 1921 photo] and with [https://www.google.co.nz/maps/@-36.8439019,174.7697275,3a,75y,193.28h,91.01t/data=!3m6!1e1!3m4!1sKpIM6h9mbQXR5fxVcAjzPg!2e0!7i13312!8i6656?hl=en Google street scene]]] [[File:Northern_Steamship_bar.jpg|thumb|Northern Steamship bar]] Charles Ranson commissioned Arthur Pollard Wilson (who also designed [[Queen Street, Auckland#Buildings and attractions|Strand Arcade]], [[Karangahape Road#Pitt Street intersection|Naval and Family Hotel]], [[Customs Street#Gallery|A H Nathan Warehouse]] and [[List of category 1 historic places in Auckland|Isaacs’ Bonded Stores]])<ref>{{Cite web |date=May 2017 |title=Heritage Evaluation - Darby Building, 8-18 Darby Street, Auckland Central |url=https://www.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/UnitaryPlanDocuments/pc7-02792-darby-building.pdf |website=Auckland Council}}</ref> to design the £6000 (or £5000)<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19000926.2.72.22&srpos=1200&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Co., Ltd.|date=1900-09-26|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=4|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref> building, with a frontage of {{Convert|66|ft|abbr=on}} to the street, and a depth of {{Convert|85|ft|abbr=on}},<ref name=":422">{{Cite web |date=26 September 1900 |title=Northern Steamship Co., Ltd. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19000926.2.72.22 |access-date=2024-01-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> opposite the wharf used by their steamers, on newly reclaimed land, leased from [[Ports of Auckland|Auckland Harbour Board]] (AHB). In 1899 NSS moved from Palmerston Buildings on [[Queen Street, Auckland|Queen St]] to their new 2-storey brick building with dark green joinery, a public office, manager's office, space for other staff, telephone room and a boardroom. It had high ceilings and on each level the floor above was supported by cast iron columns. On Quay St a central door, gave access to the main office and floor above, and an eastern door accessed the wharves (now Marsden Wharf)<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://nzhistory.govt.nz/media/photo/northern-steamship-company-building|title=Northern Steamship Company Building {{!}} NZHistory, New Zealand history online|website=www.nzhistory.net.nz|access-date=27 May 2016}}</ref> and warehouse to the rear of the building. A boardwalk allowed passengers to cross to the steamers without walking through mud. A third floor was added in 1921 to provide accommodation and a laundry. In the 1940s many of the original features were lost. Originally the building stood alone. The land on the other side the building was their yard until the 1950s when AHB roofed it over as a garage in an attempt to compete with trucking firms. When NSS ceased trading, the building reverted to AHB.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/buildingl.htm|title=The Northern Steam Ship Company building |website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=27 May 2016}}</ref> In July 1988 the [[Heritage New Zealand|Historic Places Trust]] listed it as "Category 1".<ref>{{NZHPT|622|Northern Steamship Company Building|27 May 2016}}</ref> It is now a bar.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.northernsteamship.co.nz/|title=Northern Steamship Mac's Brewbar|website=www.northernsteamship.co.nz|access-date=27 May 2016}}</ref>
== Decline == [[File:Apanui on the Awanui River.jpg|thumb|''Apanui'' on the [[Awanui]] River, Northland - passenger service closed May 1931<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.donhammondimage.co.nz/AWANUIpages/awanui5.htm|title=Awanui and the Northern Steamship Company.|website=www.donhammondimage.co.nz|access-date=2016-05-30}}</ref> <sub>[http://natlib.govt.nz/records/23144462 Alexander Turnbull Library]</sub>]] NSS gradually succumbed to rail and road competition and the [[Recession of 1920–1921|recession]] from 1921.<ref name=":59">{{Cite web |date=15 June 1921 |title=Ships laid up. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19210615.2.83 |access-date=2025-07-25 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
When the [[North Island Main Trunk]] (NIMT) opened in 1908 between Auckland and [[Wellington]], [[New Plymouth]] passenger traffic dropped. NSS and Union agreed there wasn't enough traffic for two steamers and formed a joint service.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&d=NZH19100526.2.92&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Co.|date=1910-05-26|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=7|access-date=2016-05-28}}</ref> The office at Canaan Landing, served from May 1881<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 May 1881 |title=Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18810528.2.7 |access-date=2024-01-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and providing for freight between [[Te Puke]] and [[Maketu]], closed on 29 September 1917.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 September 1917 |title=Te Puke Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TPT19170914.2.4 |access-date=2024-01-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> By 1922, reductions in traffic resulted in 9 vessels laid up.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 June 1922 |title=Northern SS company. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19220601.2.22 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Pay cuts later in the year resulted in a 12 week strike, with union members replaced by 'free labour'.<ref name=":26">{{Cite web |date=4 May 1923 |title=Northern Steamship Co Ltd. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19230504.2.107 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> A pay cut in 1933 again provoked a strike.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 June 1933 |title=Northern Steam Co. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19330601.2.22 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
In 1925 opening of the [[North Auckland Line|railway]] to Whangārei cut passenger traffic and required reductions of fares and freight rates;<ref name=":6" /> the [[Onerahi]] passenger service from Whangārei ended on 24 September 1926,<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 Sep 1926 |title=ONERAHI TO AUCKLAND. AUCKLAND STAR |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19260904.2.108 |access-date=2022-12-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> due to fewer passengers and charges by the railway for cargo and coal over their wharf.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 May 1927 |title=Northern SS Co. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19270517.2.98 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Bus competition<ref name="PATUMAHOE. FRANKLIN TIMES">{{Cite web |date=14 Jun 1926 |title=PATUMAHOE. FRANKLIN TIMES |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/FRTIM19260614.2.14 |access-date=2022-12-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> saw the [[Waiuku]] ferry end on 11 April 1928,<ref name="The Log Book SUN AUCKLAND">{{Cite web |date=12 Apr 1928 |title=The Log Book SUN (AUCKLAND) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19280412.2.29.3 |access-date=2022-12-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref name="ReferenceA">{{Cite web |date=15 Dec 1928 |title=The Log Book SUN (AUCKLAND) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19281215.2.27 |access-date=2022-12-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with a cargo replacement for a short time.<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 Nov 1928 |title=NEW ZEALAND HERALD |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19281122.2.5.8 |access-date=2022-12-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Competition from cars,<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 March 1929 |title=Steamer service. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19290327.2.27 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> completion of the [[East Coast Main Trunk|railway to Tauranga]] in 1927, trains not connecting with the steamer<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=BOPT19290327.2.27&srpos=290&zto=1|title=Steamer Service|date=1929-03-27|work=Bay of Plenty Times|pages=3|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> and complaints of undercutting,<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19280526.2.124&srpos=217&zto=1|title=Railway Competition.|date=1928-05-26|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=13|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> brought an end to the Tauranga passenger service on 2 April 1929.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19290328.2.45&srpos=292&zto=1|title=Steamer Run To Cease.|date=1929-03-28|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=12|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> From the 1890s NSS had competition from oil-engined [[Glossary of nautical terms (A–L)|auxiliary]] [[schooner]]s, which were much cheaper to run.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 July 1897 |title=Touching Those Auxiliary Oil Englnes. Observer |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TO18970724.2.3.2 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> By 1930 NSS had 9 of its own cargo-only auxiliary ships; ''Hauturu'', ''Motu'', ''Paroto'', ''Otimai'', ''Toa''. ''Tuhoe''. ''Pono'', ''Torea'' and ''Waka''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 February 1930 |title=Waka takes the water. Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19300226.2.153 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Many ships were laid up in [[Little Shoal Bay]] in the late 1920s.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Northern Steamship Company vessels laid up in the upper Waitematā Harbour, 1920s |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/29737/rec/31 |access-date=2024-02-24 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> The Onehunga-New Plymouth passenger service closed on 3 May 1930,<ref>{{cite news|url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19300502.2.21|title=DECLINE IN SEA TRAVEL. (New Zealand Herald, 1930-05-02)|website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz National Library of New Zealand|language=en|access-date=2017-03-11}}</ref> a few years after a bus service had started<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&d=NZH19261116.2.5.8&zto=1&zto=1|title=Page 3 Advertisements Column 8|date=1926-11-16|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=3|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> on the upgraded [[State Highway 3 (New Zealand)|State Highway 3]] (SH 3). Rail competition was also cited as the reason to end the passenger service to [[Russell, New Zealand|Russell]] in 1931.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS19340809.2.57&srpos=28&zto=1|title=Ships For Sale.|date=1934-08-09|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=7|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> In 1932 dairy company shareholders at [[Whakatāne]] responded to a threat to close the railway by instructing the company to transfer its freight from NSS to the railway.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19320328.2.87&srpos=399&zto=1|title=Goods On The Railway.|date=1932-03-28|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=9|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> In 1933 'truck rates' were introduced on the [[North Auckland Line|North Auckland railway]], which undercut NSS's charges.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 May 1934 |title=Northern Co. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19340531.2.84 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The {{Convert|70|mi|km|abbr=on}}<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.ohinemuri.org.nz/journals/40-journal-14-october-1970/693-first-voyage-fiasco?highlight=WyJub3J0aGVybiIsInN0ZWFtIiwic2hpcCIsInNoaXAncyIsInNoaXAnLiIsIm5vcnRoZXJuIHN0ZWFtIiwibm9ydGhlcm4gc3RlYW0gc2hpcCIsInN0ZWFtIHNoaXAiXQ==|title=Ohinemuri - "First Voyage Fiasco"|last=orhj|website=www.ohinemuri.org.nz|access-date=2016-06-03}}</ref> Auckland-[[Paeroa]] passenger service lasted until 1937,<ref>{{Cite journal|date=19 February 1937|title=Paeroa Boat Service|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS19370219.2.59&srpos=33557|journal=Auckland Star |page=6 }}</ref> despite the railway opening to Te Aroha in 1885,<ref name=":12">{{Cite web |last=Scoble |first=Juliet |title=Names & Opening & Closing Dates of Railway Stations in New Zealand 1863 to 2010 |url=http://www.railheritage.org.nz/assets/dates_and_names.pdf |website=Rail Heritage Trust of New Zealand |access-date=4 February 2024 |archive-date=24 July 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200724032358/http://www.railheritage.org.nz/assets/dates_and_names.pdf |url-status=dead }}</ref> though, after 1896, the river became too silted<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 June 1910 |title=Conclusion of Paeroa sitting. Ohinemuri Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/OG19100606.2.11.1 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and the charges at [[Paeroa railway station#Railway wharf|Paeroa Railway Wharf]] were at a level to encourage use of the railway, so that Junction Wharf, further downstream, was used.<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |date=September 1997 |title=Ohinemuri – Railway Wharf at Paeroa |url=https://www.ohinemuri.org.nz/journals/69-journal-41-september-1997/1522-railway-wharf-at-paeroa |access-date=2023-03-12 |website=www.ohinemuri.org.nz}}</ref> In 1937 passenger services still ran to Algies, [[Coromandel, New Zealand|Coromandel]], [[Great Barrier Island|Great Barrier]], [[Matakana]], Mahurangi, [[Marsden Bay, New Zealand|One Tree Point]], [[Waiheke Island|Waiheke]], [[Warkworth, New Zealand|Warkworth]] and [[Whangārei]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=AS19371116.2.17.2&srpos=901&zto=1|title=Page 3 Advertisements Column 2|date=1937-11-16|work= [[Auckland Star]]|pages=3|access-date=2016-06-29}}</ref> In 1938 ''Kawau'' was advertised as running summer weekend excursions to Waiheke.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19381210.2.31.4|title=Page 10 Advertisements Column 4 (New Zealand Herald, 1938-12-10)|website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz|publisher=National Library of New Zealand|access-date=2016-10-17}}</ref> By 1939 the only remaining passenger steamer was [[Port vessels of the Royal New Zealand Navy|''Hauiti'']].<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19390320.2.114&srpos=5433&zto=1|title=Service Ended|date=1939-03-20|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=12|access-date=2016-06-04}}</ref>
Auckland registered ships dropped from 226 in 1904 to 55 in 1944 and their tonnage from 20,546 to 5,627.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19450222.2.52&srpos=4561&zto=1|title=Rival Transport|date=1945-02-22|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=8|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref> In 1902 NSS had 28 ships totalling 6000 tons and costing an average of £34 per ton.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/cgi-bin/paperspast?a=d&cl=search&d=NZH19020813.2.64&srpos=240&zto=1|title=Northern Steamship Company Of Auckland.|date=1902-08-13|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]] |pages=6|access-date=2016-06-06}}</ref>
== 1945–1974 == [[File:StateLibQld 1 140351 Poranui (ship).jpg|thumb|862 GRT coaster ''Poranui'', built in 1956 by [[List of shipbuilders and shipyards|J Bodewes]]]] By 1945 NSS was down to 10 ships, ranging from 56 to 351 tons.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nssfleet.php#1305226|title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Fleet list|website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=2016-05-28}}</ref> NSS had lost most of its trade to road and rail, so switched to inter-island trade, which it gradually lost to [[Interislander|Cook Strait ferries]] from the 1960s, when it increasingly turned to coal and grain, including some trade with Australia, before losing out to coal fuelled gasworks being replaced by [[Kapuni|natural gas]], a shipwreck (''Maranui''),<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/shipwrecks/page-5|title=5. – Shipwrecks – Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand|last=Taonga|first=New Zealand Ministry for Culture and Heritage Te Manatu|website=www.teara.govt.nz|language=en|access-date=2016-05-29}}</ref> [[Economy of New Zealand|trade fluctuations]] and reliance on the [[grain trade]], which faltered due to a poor harvest in 1974.<ref name=":8">{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzcoastalshipping.com/Northern%20S.S.Co.html|title=NORTHERN STEAM SHIP COMPANY LTD.|website=New Zealand Coastal Shipping}}</ref>
NSS briefly tried replacing a ship with a partly owned trucking company, when it closed its Paeroa service in 1947.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.ohinemuri.org.nz/journals/38-journal-12-october-1969/633-brenan-and-company-continued?highlight=WyJub3J0aGVybiIsInN0ZWFtIiwic2hpcCIsInNoaXAncyIsInNoaXAnLiIsIm5vcnRoZXJuIHN0ZWFtIiwibm9ydGhlcm4gc3RlYW0gc2hpcCIsInN0ZWFtIHNoaXAiXQ==|title=Ohinemuri - Brenan and Company continued|last=orhj|website=www.ohinemuri.org.nz|access-date=2016-06-03}}</ref>
Captain A. G. Hardy, formerly New Plymouth manager for the Waterfront Industry Commission, became NSS manager from 5 January 1947.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 November 1947 |title=Personal. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19471125.2.9 |access-date=2024-03-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
After the [[World War II|war]] NSS switched from serving the northern ports to linking some of them to [[South Island]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/depression_WW2.htm|title=Northern Steam Ship Company - Depression and World War 2|website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=2016-05-28|archive-date=23 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160123234328/http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/depression_WW2.htm|url-status=dead}}</ref> This required increasingly larger ships, so NSS bought ''Apanui'' (1948–61), ''Tainui'' (1949–63), ''Hotunui'' (1950–67), ''Ratanui'' (1952-56), and then started building ''Maranui'' (1953–68), ''Maunganui'' (1955–71), ''Poranui'' (1956–69) and ''Tawanui'' (1959–73), ''Moanui'' (1961–66), ''Awanui'' (1962-73).<ref name=":8" /> A major contract in this period was shipping materials for the [[Manapouri Power Station|Manapouri Tunnel]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/post_war.htm|title=The Northern Steam Ship Company - Post-War and Containerisation 1950 – 1968|website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=2016-05-28}}</ref> A second ''Moanui'' (1967–75)<ref name=":8" /> and ''Seaway Princess'' (1967–69) were added to the fleet, the latter for a weekly Onehunga-[[Lyttelton, New Zealand|Lyttelton]] [[Roll-on/roll-off|ro-ro]] service. However, as AHB didn't build a ramp at Onehunga, she had to use the only one at Auckland, sharing it with Union, which had priority, hence her short service with NSS.<ref name=":8" /> In the 1960s NSS had offices at Onehunga, Tauranga and Whangārei, as well as its head office.<ref name=":110">{{Cite web |date=3 August 1963 |title=Modern coastal fleet. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19630803.2.34 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> It closed Tauranga-Timaru in July 1966<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 July 1966 |title=N Steam Co. ships. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19660720.2.236 |access-date=2024-03-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and Whangārei-Lyttelton from 30 January 1967.<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 December 1966 |title=Line stops service. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19661222.2.28 |access-date=2024-03-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>
