By 1970 three of the remaining five ships were carrying bulk grain from South Island to Auckland, Tauranga, and, less often, Wellington, New Plymouth and [[Whanganui]].<ref name=":8" /> ''Dido'' (1970–75) was added to the fleet and, being largest,<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.ships-photos.net/picture/show/26392/New-Zealand-coaster-Dido|title=New Zealand coaster|website=www.ships-photos.net|access-date=2016-05-29|archive-date=24 June 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160624202744/http://www.ships-photos.net/picture/show/26392/New-Zealand-coaster-Dido|url-status=dead}}</ref> was used for 10 grain trips to Australia. At that time [[Brierley Investments Ltd|Brierley Investments]] made an unsuccessful bid for the company.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 February 1970 |title=Brierley May Be Too Weak. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19700221.2.161 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> NSS made a record profit in 1970.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 June 1970 |title=Nth Steam Now In Strong Position. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19700613.2.139.1 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> By 1971 ''Awanui'' was the only general cargo vessel, running an Onehunga-[[Bluff, New Zealand|Bluff]]-[[Jackson Bay]] round trip, but she was also adapted to carry grain in 1972. NSS added the 1562 ton ''Tainui II'' (1973–76), but there was a poor grain harvest and, after they were used to import grain from Australia, all three ships were soon idle. NSS blamed the problem on the [[Second National Government of New Zealand|government's]] [[State trading enterprises|grain price]] policy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 December 1974 |title=Grain price hits ships. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19741209.2.84 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=8 November 1974 |title=Govt blamed for shortage. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19741108.2.138 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Tainui II''<nowiki/>'s last voyage brought coal to Auckland from Westport on 6 November 1974, ''Moanui'' 12 days later<ref name=":8" /> (or another account says her last cargo was barley from the South Island to Auckland in September)<ref name=":9">{{Cite web|url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/twilight.htm|title=Northern Steam Ship Company - The Twilight Years|website=www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz|access-date=2016-05-28}}</ref> from Dunedin and ''Dido'', the last NSS ship ever to berth, arrived on Sunday 8 December 1974.<ref name=":8" /> When NSS failed to change government policy, it decided in April 1975 to sell the ships<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 April 1975 |title=Nthn Steam selling ships. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19750417.2.137 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and the company was taken over by Brierley.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 April 1976 |title=New Tasman fleet? Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19760426.2.160 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> For a few years NSS continued operating [[forklift]]s and being an agent for the [[Nippon Yusen|Japan Line]].<ref name=":9" />
== Fleet == {| class="wikitable sortable mw-collapsible" !'''Ship''' !Built !'''In fleet''' !'''Builder''' !'''[[Gross register tonnage|grt]]''' !'''Type''' |- |Albatross |1874 |1909–1910 |James Barbour, [[Whangapoua Harbour|Whangapoua]] |61 |Auxiliary schooner launched on 22 April 1874<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 April 1874 |title=Launch of the schooner Albatross. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18740422.2.14.4 |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for Christopher Atwell Harris (1847-1903)<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 September 1903 |title=Death of Mr C A Harris. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19030905.2.18 |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at his timber mill.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 April 1874 |title=The Albatross. Daily Southern Cross |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/DSC18740424.2.5 |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|79.3|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|20.4|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|7.4|ft|abbr=on}} deep, her registered weight dropped from 77<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 May 1874 |title=Port of Auckland. Daily Southern Cross |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/DSC18740521.2.4.1 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to 61 tons between 1902 and 1906.<ref name=":14" /><ref name=":282" /> She was sold at [[Levuka]] in 1882,<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 July 1882 |title=Departures today. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18820720.2.3 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in Fiji in 1886,<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 October 1886 |title=Imports. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18861006.2.7.5 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at [[Sydney]] in 1892<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 May 1892 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18920526.2.13 |access-date=2024-04-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was at [[Botany, New South Wales|Botany]] when damaged by a gale in 1898.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1898-05-07 |title=SCHOONERS ASHORE AT BOTANY. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article239549758 |access-date=2024-04-03 |work=Daily Telegraph}}</ref> In March 1909 she replaced ''Waitangi'' ,<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 March 1909 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19090308.2.20 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> when NSS chartered her to run a cargo only service to Waitara, until the lengthening of ''Claymore'' reduced her draught enough to replace her<ref name="ReferenceB">{{Cite web |date=26 May 1910 |title=Northern Steamship Company. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19100526.2.78 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in August 1910.<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 August 1910 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19100822.2.34 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> It seems she then traded cement between Whangārei and Auckland until October 1914,<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 October 1914 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19141010.2.15 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> when she was at the centre of a drowning mystery.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 October 1914 |title=A sordid story. NZ Truth |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZTR19141024.2.52 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She wasn't registered in 1915.<ref name=":25">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping Corrected 1915 by Australian National Maritime Museum - Issuu |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__b3148c9798d263 |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> |- |Alexander Craig |1891 |1908–1926 |William Phillip Cameron, [[South Maitland, Nova Scotia|South Maitland]], Canada |520 |[[File:Alexander_Craig.jpg|thumb|''Alexander Craig'']][[Barque]] used as a coal hulk.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Alexander Craig |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1100215 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083909/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1100215 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-22}}</ref> She was built of wood for Auckland owners, including Donald Ross and Captain Davies, originally intended to carry timber, but mostly used as a collier,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 June 1892 |title=The Kathleen Hilda. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18920615.2.28 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|163.1|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|34.7|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|13.5|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name=":033">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1924-1925 by Australian National Maritime Museum - Issuu |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__7f72af695ff3d6 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> Her original name was ''Kathleen Hilda,''<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 September 1921 |title=The coal hulks.New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19210910.2.129.5 |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> until [[Mount Eden#Notable buildings|Joseph James Craig]] (who also owned [[James Craig (barque)|''James Craig'']] and ''[[c:Category:Selwyn Craig (ship, 1868)|Selwyn Craig]]'') renamed her in 1905,<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 July 1905 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19050727.2.102.5 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In March 1908 she was badly damaged in a storm on her way from Melbourne.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 March 1908 |title=Storm tossed. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19080314.2.31 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Next month she was stripped to a hulk<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 April 1908 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19080403.2.42 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and sold to NSS in October.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 October 1908 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19081016.2.22 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1926 she was beached on [[Motukorea]] and burnt,<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 September 1926 |title=Another hulk gone. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19260925.2.81 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though her bottom timbers drifted to [[Saint Heliers]], where they remained in 1930.<ref name=":15">{{Cite web |date=10 July 1930 |title=Ship burials. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19300710.2.229 |access-date=2024-03-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Apanui |1905 |1905–1937 |[[Dunlop Bremner & Company|D.J.Dunlop & Co]], [[Port Glasgow#Shipbuilding|Port Glasgow]] |243 |[[File:Apanui_in_1905.jpg|thumb|''Apanui'' at Auckland in 1905]]Steamer launched on 16 June 1905 for NSS,<ref name=":042">{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer APANUI built by David J Dunlop & Co in 1905 for Northern Steamship Company of New Zealand. Auckland., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ship_listPage=2&a1Order=Sorter_name&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=4068&ref=6000&vessel=APANUI |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> arrived in Auckland via [[Port Said]] and [[Fremantle]] on 5 October,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 October 1905 |title=Northern company's new steamers. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19051005.2.14 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> was planned to carry 148 tons of cargo,<ref name=":1822">{{Cite web |date=10 December 1904 |title=The Northern Company's new steamers. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19041210.2.34 |access-date=2024-02-06 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with a 3-cylinder (8.5, 14.25, 28.5 - 15)[[Prime (symbol)#Designation of units|″]] triple expansion engine,<ref name=":042" /> of {{Convert|27|hp|abbr=on}}, steel ship, {{Convert|110|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|22.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|9.2|ft|abbr=on}} deep,<ref name=":0322">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1924-1925 by Australian National Maritime Museum - Issuu |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__7f72af695ff3d6 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> carrying about 50 passengers.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 July 1905 |title=Launch of the Apanui. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19050719.2.76 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 28 October 1909 she collided with schooner ''Greyhound'' at the mouth of the Awanui River, was blown ashore at [[Waiharara]] Heads, but floated off undamaged on the next high tide on 3 September 1913. On 25 October 1929 she lost her propeller and was towed to Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 October 1929 |title=Lost her propeller. Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19291025.2.123 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> From 28 May 1931,<ref name=":1922">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Apanui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1122906 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083837/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1122906 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-06}}</ref> she was replaced on the Awanui route by ''Motu'',<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 May 1931 |title=Northland Age |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NORAG19310527.2.30.4 |access-date=2024-02-06 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> probably because of problems with silting of the river,<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 January 1932 |title=Silting of Awanui River. Northland Age |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NORAG19320129.2.2 |access-date=2024-02-06 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> but continued to serve [[Tutukaka]], [[Matapouri]], [[Whananaki]], Sandy Bay, Helena Bay, Mimiwhangata, [[Whangaruru]], [[Kerikeri]], [[Houhora]] and [[Parengarenga Harbour|Parenga]] until 23 August 1931.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 August 1931 |title=Northland Age |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NORAG19310819.2.33.2 |access-date=2024-02-06 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was then laid up at Auckland and, in 1937, sold to McKendrick Bros for breaking up at Auckland.<ref name=":1922" /> |- |Apanui |1938 |1948–1961 |G. Renck, Jnr., [[Hamburg]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Apanui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1166615 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084120/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1166615 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-22}}</ref> |296 |[[File:StateLibQld_1_133305_Apanui_(ship).jpg|right|frameless]]Motor ship built for H.J. Wilson, London as ''Goldfinder'',<ref name=":0223">{{Cite web |last=Foundation |first=Lloyd's Register |title=Letter from William Alxeander, General Manager, Ringsend Dockyard (Dublin) Ltd, to R B Grier Esq, Lloyd\'s Register Surveyor, Dublin, regarding Goldfinder, 25th June 1942 |url=https://hec.lrfoundation.org.uk/archive-library/documents/lrf-pun-003838-003845-0140-l |access-date=2024-01-22 |website=hec.lrfoundation.org.uk |language=en}}</ref> {{Convert|130|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|27.5|ft|abbr=on}}, draught {{Convert|9|ft|abbr=on}}, able to carry about 480 tons, with a crew of 10.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 December 1948 |title=New coastal vessel. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19481204.2.25 |access-date=2025-07-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1942 her 8-cylinder engine was replaced by a {{Convert|250|hp|abbr=on}} 6-cylinder engine.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Foundation |first=Lloyd's Register |title=Copy of a Letter from W E Alexander, General Manager, Ringsend Dockyard (Dublin) Ltd, to R B Grier Esq, Surveyor of Lloyd\'s Register, Dublin, regarding Goldfiner, 25th June 1942 |url=https://hec.lrfoundation.org.uk/archive-library/documents/lrf-pun-003838-003845-0135-l |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=hec.lrfoundation.org.uk |language=en}}</ref> In 1948 her steering gear broke in the [[Red Sea]], when she was on her way to join NSS,<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 November 1948 |title=Goldfinder Due With Phosphate Tomorrow. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19481129.2.64 |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> who renamed her ''Apanui'' in 1949, when she began a Raglan-South Island route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 January 1949 |title=Goldfinder Now Apanui. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19490104.2.68.2 |access-date=2024-10-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1961 she was sold to H. Williams, [[Cook Islands]] and belonged to D.C. Brown, before being declared unseaworthy and scuttled off [[Avatiu]] on 24 December 1965.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Apanui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1166615 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084120/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1166615 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-06}}</ref> |- |Aranui |1946 |1946–1947 |United Ship and Boatbuilders Ltd, Auckland<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Aranui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171447 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084111/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171447 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-22}}</ref> |243 |Motor ship,<ref name=":02222">{{Cite web |last=Foundation |first=Lloyd's Register |title=Letter from William Alxeander, General Manager, Ringsend Dockyard (Dublin) Ltd, to R B Grier Esq, Lloyd\'s Register Surveyor, Dublin, regarding Goldfinder, 25th June 1942 |url=https://hec.lrfoundation.org.uk/archive-library/documents/lrf-pun-003838-003845-0140-l |access-date=2024-01-22 |website=hec.lrfoundation.org.uk |language=en}}</ref> one of four sisters lighters, ''[[List of shipwrecks in 1953#August|Avon]]'', ''Melva'' and ''Rosalie'',<ref name=":47">{{Cite web |date=16 August 1955 |title=Coastal Vessel Melva Has Had Varied Career. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19550816.2.176 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|31.49|m|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|7.19|m|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Aranui |url=https://www.ultimatenzsoccer.com/Onehunga/aranui.htm |access-date=2025-07-27 |website=www.ultimatenzsoccer.com}}</ref> She was launched on 25 September 1945 as [[Type B ship#Freight (Lighter) Barges (YF – YFN)|US Navy YF]]-1046, but was amongst orders cancelled, she was completed in May 1946 and chartered by the government to NSS. September 1947 sold to Winstone Ltd. and employed mainly carrying cement from Portland to Auckland. 1960 sold to Wong Wing of Papeete and subsequently traded in the Tahiti area.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Aranui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171447 |access-date=2024-03-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084111/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171447 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> On 15 September 1977 she ran aground in a storm on [[Marutea Sud|South Marutea Atoll]] and was abandoned.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 August 2015 |title=MV Aranui (+1977) |url=https://wrecksite.eu/wreck.aspx?240145 |website=wrecksite}}</ref> |- |Arapawa |1908 |1914–1938 |[[Paisley, Renfrewshire#Industrial decline|J. Fullerton]], Merksworth Works, [[Paisley, Renfrewshire|Paisley]] |291 |[[File:Arapawa_aground_at_Waitara_in_1919.jpg|thumb|''Arapawa'' aground - Waitara in 1919]]Steamer ''Arapawa'' was a {{Convert|120.5|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|22.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|9.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|47|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":033" /> single-screw, schooner-rigged, steel ship, launched on 31 July 1908, with a 3-cylinder (11,18 & 31-22in) engine by Renfrew Brothers, [[Irvine, North Ayrshire#Irvine Harbour|Irvine]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer ARAPAWA built by J. Fullerton & Co in 1908 for J. H. Williams, Wellington. New Zealand, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=25&ship_listOrder=Sorter_types&ship_listDir=DESC&a1Order=Sorter_name&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=1622&ref=8083&vessel=ARAPAWA |access-date=2024-01-30 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> built for [[Days Bay#James Herbert Williams|James Herbert Williams]]' Coastal Steamship Company, Wellington (CSC formed in 1898).<ref name=":132">{{Cite web |date=21 November 1898 |title=The Coastal Steamship Company, Limited. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18981121.2.69 |access-date=2024-01-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In August 1905 CSC and McGregor Steamship Company merged,<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 October 1905 |title=Death of Mr W A McGregor. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19051031.2.23 |access-date=2024-03-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to form the SSC.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 October 1905 |title=Settlers' Steamship Co. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19051028.2.36 |access-date=2024-03-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1912 Patea Shipping Company re-organised. Williams took ''Kapiti'' and ''Arapawa'', for the Wellington-Whanganui trade. ''Kiripaka'', ''Mana'', ''Kapuni'' and ''Hawera'' remained with Patea for its Wellington-Pātea route.<ref name=":232">{{Cite web |date=11 March 1912 |title=Patea Shipping Company. Patea Mail |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/PATM19120311.2.13 |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In July 1914 ''Arapawa'' was sold to NSS<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 July 1914 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19140722.2.19 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for the Whanganui-[[Ōpunake]]-Waitara route,<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 July 1914 |title=Northern Steamship Company. Hawera & Normanby Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HNS19140707.2.14.4 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though initially she ran between Onehunga, Raglan, [[Kawhia Harbour|Kawhia]] and Waitara,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 September 1914 |title=Stratford Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/STEP19140922.2.2.4 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> from 6 August 1914.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 August 1914 |title=Stratford Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/STEP19140801.2.2.4 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was mainly a cargo ship, with space for 6 passengers.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 July 1914 |title=The coastal trade. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19140704.2.6 |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Due to silting caused by a strong wind,<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 October 1919 |title=Nautical court's finding. Hawera & Normanby Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HNS19191009.2.52.4 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> she was stranded at the entrance to the [[Waitara River]] for 3 weeks in August 1919, but little damaged.<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 August 1919 |title=The Arapawa refloated. Taranaki Daily News |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TDN19190827.2.46 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1931 she was dismantled at Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Arapawa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1108090 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084009/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1108090 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-30}}</ref> In March 1941 she was stripped and partly broken up, before being beached in Boulder Bay, [[Rangitoto Island#Since European colonisation|Rangitoto]] on 8 April 1941.<ref name=":332">{{Cite web |last=Bennett |first=Kurt |date=2014 |title=Rich Pickings |url=https://www.academia.edu/11749065 |website=Academia}}</ref> |- |Argyle |1876 |1881–1897 |[[Henry Niccol#Career|McQuarrie & McCallum]], Auckland |159 |[[File:SS_Argyle.jpg|thumb|''SS Argyle'']]Steamer with 22 berths, launched on 1 January 1876,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 February 1876 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18760222.2.3 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|114.3|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|20|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|7.3|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|45|hp|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":22">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1893-1894 by Australian National Maritime Museum |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__de6311005192af |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> Built for A. McGregor, for the Auckland-Whangārei route, as a 3-masted schooner, with a [[pōhutukawa]] frame, [[Agathis|kauri]] planks, a [[Phoenix Foundry, Auckland#Ships|Fraser & Tinne]] compound engine, fitted aft, with 16- and 30-inch cylinders, 15-inch stroke, 4-blade screw, 5 feet 7 inches in diameter, 9 ft × 10 ft boiler of [[Low Moor Ironworks|Low Moor]] plate, producing an average {{Convert|10|knot|abbr=on}}. Her maiden voyage was on 28 February 1876.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 February 1876 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18760229.2.3 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1881 she was taken over when NSS was formed. In October 1894 she helped rescue passengers from the ''[[SS Wairarapa]]'' wreck.<ref name=":193">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Argyle |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1070362 |access-date=2024-03-16 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083627/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1070362 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> Her last NSS voyage was from Waiheke on 23 July 1897.<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 July 1897 |title=Northern company's movements. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18970722.2.19.5 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was then sold to J.A. Elleker, [[Melbourne]] to trade to [[Gippsland]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 August 1897 |title=Telegraphic shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18970805.2.39.3 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in 1898 she moved to [[Adelaide]], with [[Alexander Wallace Sandford|A.W. Sandford]], in May 1903 P. Weir,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1903-05-16 |title=CONCERNING PEOPLE. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article162447402 |access-date=2024-03-16 |work=Adelaide Observer}}</ref> in 1906 H. Weir and was broken up in 1915.<ref name=":193" /> |- |Aupouri |1905 |1905–1932 |D.J. Dunlop & Co., Port Glasgow |463 |[[File:SS_Aupouri.jpg|thumb|''SS Aupouri'']]Steamer ''Aupouri'' launched on 21 August 1905 for NSS, the older sister of ''Ngatiawa,''<ref name=":202">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Ngatiawa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1122916 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083854/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1122916 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-03}}</ref> both able to carry 500 tons of cargo, but only draw {{Convert|5.75|ft|abbr=on}},<ref name=":1822"/> {{Convert|152|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|27.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|10|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name=":033" /> She was a steel, twin screw steamer, with two triple compound (8.5, 14.25 & 28.5 - 15)in. {{Convert|55|hp|abbr=on}} engines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer AUPOURI built by David J Dunlop & Co in 1905 for Northern Steamship Company Ltd., Auckland., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=50&ship_listPage=10&a1Order=Sorter_year_built&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=394&ref=6001&vessel=AUPOURI |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> In August 1912 ''Ngapuhi'' replaced her on the Tauranga route and she was kept to replace other ships during overhauls.<ref name=":20">{{Cite web |date=23 May 1913 |title=Northern S.S. Company. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19130523.2.13 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her last overhaul was in September 1926.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 September 1926 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19260906.2.17 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In February 1927 she collected wool from the beach at [[Lake Harihari|Harihari]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 February 1927 |title=Kawhia Settler and Raglan Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/KSRA19270204.2.6 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> but was laid up by 1928<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 December 1928 |title=Queer nesting place. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19281214.2.142 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in Shoal Bay due to motor competition. She was mainly used on east coast routes. She was sold for dismantling in August 1932.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 August 1932 |title=Steamer Aupouri sold. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19320805.2.55 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1933,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 March 1933 |title=Reduced to scrap. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19330315.2.33.3 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> she was cut up in [[Saint Marys Bay, New Zealand|Saint Marys Bay]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 December 1932 |title=Old ships go. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19321230.2.34 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> scrap being stored on the [[Western Reclamation]]. Scrap from ''Aupouri'' and ''Wakatere'' was sent to Japan in 1934, when it was commented, "It will come back to us as bullets".<ref name=":362">{{Cite web |date=9 June 1934 |title=Acre of metal. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19340609.2.26 |access-date=2024-03-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Awanui |1962 |1962–1973 |Pacific Island Shipbuilding Company, [[Hong Kong]] |1185 |Motor ship built for NSS for trading on the east coast.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 December 1962 |title=Awanui Ends Trials. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19621229.2.169 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> An investigation into bulges in her bow plates was begun in February 1963 and she was repaired.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 February 1963 |title=New Ship To Be Repaired. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19630225.2.176 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In June 1966 she was the last NSS ship when the [[Mount Maunganui]]-Lyttelton route was closed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 June 1966 |title=Line To Stop Service. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19660623.2.190 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> After her steel trade switched to rail, ''Awanui'' was laid up in September 1969.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 September 1969 |title=Northern S.S. Co. Lays Up Coaster. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19690919.2.17 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was in use again by May 1970<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 May 1970 |title=Lyttelton shipping. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19700501.2.152 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and shipping [[Prumnopitys taxifolia|matai]] timber from [[Okuru]] by October 1971.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 October 1971 |title=Thriving timber community deep in South Westland. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19711009.2.68 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1973 she was sold to Unique Shipping and Trading (Private) Company, [[Port of Singapore|Singapore]], renamed ''Bonawind'', to Singapore Straits Pilotage Co. Pte. Ltd in 1978, renamed ''Wild Rover''. 1982 renamed ''Concord Angel'' and owned by Concord Marine Ltd. 1986 sold to Mekong Trading S.A., [[Panama Canal|Panama]] and renamed ''Mekong Express''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Awanui |url=http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315720 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084151/http://www.nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315720 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-28}}</ref> She was {{Convert|72.04|m|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|9.9|m|abbr=on}} wide and reported as still in the Panama area in 2023.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 October 2023 |title=MEKONG EXPRESS, IMO 5401314, Flag Panama |url=https://directory.shipfix.com/ship/mekong-express_IMO-5401314 |access-date=2024-02-04 |website=directory.shipfix.com |language=en}}</ref> |- |Bell Bird |1906 | -1945 |George Fraser, [[Johnsonville, Victoria#Caravan park|Johnsonville]]<ref name=":33"/> |88 |[[File:The_Bell_Bird_under_construction_at_Johnsonville.jpg|right|thumb|''Bell Bird'' being built at Johnsonville]]''Bell Bird'', sometimes referred to as ''Bellbird'', was launched on 9 May 1906, built of [[Red gum (Australia)|red gum]], for her builder to emigrate<ref>{{Cite news |date=1906-05-15 |title=JOHNSONVILLE. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article85128136 |access-date=2024-04-09 |work=Bairnsdale Advertiser and Tambo and Omeo Chronicle}}</ref> to [[Helensville]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 May 1907 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19070525.2.3.3 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> via Melbourne, [[Hobart]], Bluff, Dunedin, Lyttelton, Wellington, [[Nelson, New Zealand|Nelson]] and New Plymouth.<ref name=":33">{{Cite web |date=2 May 1907 |title=The SS Bellbird. Colonist |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TC19070502.2.3 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Kaipara Steamship Company bought her in 1907<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 October 1907 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19071023.2.29 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and George Turnbull Niccol bought that company from its receivers in December 1912.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 December 1912 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19121206.2.15 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> He was also a NSS director from 1918 to 1925<ref>{{Cite web |date=1996 |title=Niccol, George Turnbull |url=https://teara.govt.nz/en/biographies/3n8/niccol-george-turnbull/print |website=Dictionary of New Zealand Biography}}</ref> and was registered as owner of ''Bell Bird'', which was {{Convert|85|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|18.7|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|7.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep and had a {{Convert|15|hp|abbr=on}}<ref name=":033" /> triple expansion engine able to drive her at up to {{Convert|9|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":33" /> In 1917 she was damaged by fire at Helensville.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 March 1917 |title=Outbreak of fire on SS Bellbird. Kaipara and Waitemata Echo |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/KWE19170315.2.15 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1929 she sprang a leak and had to be towed by ''Ruawai''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 May 1929 |title=Sprang a leak. Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19290511.2.5 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The Helensville-[[Dargaville]] service ended when the [[Dargaville Branch|railway]] opened in 1942.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 August 1942 |title=Steamer service. Nelson Evening Mail |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NEM19420829.2.67 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Bellbird'' then became a Class S-120<ref>{{Cite web |title=The motor vessel repair ship Bellbird (S-120) of the Small Ships Section, United States Army Services |url=https://trove.nla.gov.au/work/229149406 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=trove.nla.gov.au}}</ref> [[Rescue and salvage ship|salvage and repair ship]] with a diesel engine<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 June 1945 |title=Aucklander saved. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19450614.2.48 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> the alterations costing £33,000. After the war she was put up for sale in Sydney as a NSS ship.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 June 1945 |title=War tasks over. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19450621.2.20 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Chelmsford |1886 |1889–1928 |James Halstead, [[Lavender Bay, New South Wales|Lavender Bay]], Sydney |122 |[[File:Chelmsford_sank_in_1928.jpg|thumb|''Chelmsford'' sank in 1928]]Steamer {{Convert|89.8|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|18.5|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, built for NSS<ref name=":033" /> was launched on 23 July 1886. Her topsides were of Oregon, the deck of kauri and she had space for about 25 passengers.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1886-02-24 |title=IMPORTS.—FEBRUARY 22. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article13612332 |access-date=2024-03-18 |work=Sydney Morning Herald}}</ref> The [[Plenty and Son]]s, {{Convert|40|hp|abbr=on}} compound surface engines, fitted by Perdrian and West, [[Balmain, New South Wales|Balmain]],<ref>{{Cite news |date=1886-02-24 |title=IMPORTS.—FEBRUARY 22. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article13612332 |access-date=2024-03-17 |work=Sydney Morning Herald}}</ref> drove a single screw<ref name=":312">{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900. |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1900-I.2.3.2.33 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at up to {{Convert|9.75|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1886-03-19 |title=EXPORTS.—MARCH 18. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article13614607 |access-date=2024-03-17 |work=Sydney Morning Herald}}</ref> She was chartered by Hawkesbury River Steam Co. for Sydney-[[Hawkesbury River|Hawkesbury]] trade,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1886-03-09 |title=Along the Wharves. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article111183256 |access-date=2024-03-17 |work=Evening News}}</ref> until sale to Edward C. Windsor, Auckland, for £3,200 in June 1886.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1886-06-04 |title=IMPORTS.—JUNE 3. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article13644515 |access-date=2024-03-17 |work=Sydney Morning Herald}}</ref> She replaced ''Hannah Mokau'', serving ports along the coast from Manukau,<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 May 1886 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18860511.2.8 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> being first modified to carry cattle.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 June 1886 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18860615.2.14 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1886<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 June 1886 |title=Shipping casualties. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18860629.2.37 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>-88 she ran aground thrice at Ōpōtiki.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Chelmsford |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1089398 |access-date=2024-03-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083709/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1089398 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> In 1889 bought by NSS for Whangārei to East Coast services for £2,500.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 June 1889 |title=Northern Steamship Company. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18890601.2.4 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was laid up from her Whangārei run during the 1921 recession,<ref name=":59" /> though her last trip was probably in December 1921.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 December 1921 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19211201.2.76.1 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> However, when she sank in a storm in 1928,<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 May 1928 |title=Damaged by storm. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19280529.2.108 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> it was reported she had only been laid up at Shoal Bay for 3 years. On 22 June she was sunk<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 June 1928 |title=Old steamer's end. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19280623.2.92 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in the Western Reclamation,<ref name=":402">{{Cite web |date=October 2010 |title=Historic Heritage Assessment Additional Waitemata Harbour Crossing |url=https://www.nzta.govt.nz/assets/projects/awhc/docs/form-assessment-study-report/awhc-fasr-appendix-l-historic-heritage-assessment.pdf |website=NZTA}}</ref> though her bow rose in 1930.<ref name=":15" /> |- |Clansman |1884 |1884–1934 |[[James Lamont & Co|Blackwood & Gordon]], [[Port Glasgow#Shipbuilding|Port Glasgow]] |591. 635 from 1904 |[[File:Clansman_at_Kawau_about_1910.jpg|thumb|''Clansman'' at [[Mansion House, Kawau Island|Kawau]] about 1910]][[File:Clansman_was_rebuilt_in_1904.jpg|thumb|''Clansman'' was rebuilt in 1904]][[File:Skylight_from_Clansman_1884-1934.jpg|thumb|Skylight on display at the [[New Zealand Maritime Museum|Auckland Maritime Museum]]]]Steamer launched on 14 April 1884, with 3 masts and a screw<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer CLANSMAN built by Blackwood & Gordon in 1884 for The Northern Steam Ship Co Ltd, Auckland., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=2435 |access-date=2024-02-25 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> for NSS to serve Russell, [[Whangaroa]], and [[Mangōnui]].<ref name=":422"/> {{Convert|191.7|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|26.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|10.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|99|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":033" /> with a compound engine (26, 48 - 36)″, driving her at {{Convert|12.5|knot|abbr=on}}. The saloon had revolving chairs, 61 berths, white and gold decoration and skylights with Scottish scenes etched on the glass. ''Clansman'' left Port Glasgow on 15 July 1884 and partly steamed and sailed via [[Waterford]], [[São Vicente, Cape Verde|St. Vincent]] for coal, [[Saint Helena|St. Helena]] for water, [[Sandy Bay, Cape Town|Sandy Bay]] and Hobart for coal,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1884-10-06 |title=ARRIVAL OF THE S.S. CLANSMAN. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article9094141 |access-date=2024-02-02 |work=Mercury}}</ref> arriving at Auckland, rigged as a [[Barquentine|barque]], on 16 October 1884. On 10 August 1888 she collided with ''Mana'' off [[Mercury Islands|Mercury Island]].<ref name=":142">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Clansman |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1087520 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083659/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1087520 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-02}}</ref> In 1904 a motor boat was added on her deck, which was relaid, and smaller masts, electric lights and a new boiler were fitted.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 October 1904 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19041006.2.35 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 2 September 1907 she collided with the [[scow]], ''Herald'', on 24 June 1914 sank the scow ''Pahiki'' and on 26 August 1926 collided with ''Elsie Mary'', all in Auckland Harbour. On 16 March 1931 she broke her [[Tail shaft|tail-shaft]] off Bird Rock, [[Motukokako Island (Piercy Island)|Motukōkako Island]], was towed to Auckland by paddle tug ''Lyttelton'', stripped of all her fittings within a week,<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 March 1931 |title=Stripped bare. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19310321.2.145 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> probably due to railway competition on the Bay of Islands and Whangārei routes, and sold for scrap in August 1934.<ref name=":152">{{Cite web |date=9 August 1934 |title=Ships for sale. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19340809.2.57 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref name=":142" /> |- |Clansman |1930 |1930–1957 |[[Henry Niccol#Career|George Turnbull Niccol]], Freemans Bay |235 |Motor ship launched as ''Waka'' on 25 February 1930 for NSS,<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 February 1930 |title=Waka takes the water. Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19300226.2.153 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> of steel, with 2 steel masts, served Tauranga from 7 April 1930 and renamed ''Clansman'' from 1 October 1934, {{Convert|116|ft|abbr=on}}) long, {{Convert|28|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|6.6|ft|abbr=on}} draught, with twin {{Convert|152|hp|abbr=on}} [[L. Gardner and Sons|Gardner]] diesels, burning about {{Convert|13|impgal|l|abbr=on}} an hour, from {{Convert|3600|impgal|l|abbr=on}} tanks and driving her at up to {{Convert|10|knot|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 April 1930 |title=New motor vessel. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19300408.2.25 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with a carrying capacity of about 300 tons,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 April 1930 |title=New motor vessel. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19300405.2.83 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In August 1939 she was damaged on a reef at [[Cape Colville]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 August 1939 |title=Clansman refloated. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19390822.2.114 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and grounded at Whakatāne in 1940.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 November 1940 |title=Clansman goes aground, but Is refloated. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19401116.2.118 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In May 1956 she was stranded as a constructive total loss<ref>{{Cite web |date=May 1956 |title=Photo of Clansman on rocks at Whakatane, 1956 |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/312988 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> on rocks at Whakatāne Bar.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Clansman on rocks at Whakatane, 1956 |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/312988/ |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> 1957 sold to [[Viaduct Harbour#Shipyards|Charles Bailey & Son Limited]]. 1963 broken up at Auckland after lying derelict in St Mary's Bay for many years.<ref name=":162">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1153988 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084056/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1153988 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-03}}</ref> |- |[[Port vessels of the Royal New Zealand Navy#Boom defence vessels|Claymore]] |1902 |1908–1940 |[[Lithgows#Founding|Murdoch & Murray]], Port Glasgow |210. 258 from 1910 |[[File:Claymore_1902.jpg|thumb|''Claymore'' 1902]]Steamer built of steel, {{Convert|113|ft|abbr=on}} long (from 1910 {{Convert|133.2|ft|abbr=on}}), {{Convert|21.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|9.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|54|hp|abbr=on}}, 3-cylinder engine (12, 19, 31 - 22)″<ref name=":033" /> by Muir & Houston Ltd., [[Kinning Park#Economic development|Kinning Park]] for W.A. McGregor, Auckland. She was launched on 3 June 1902, had a single screw<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer CLAYMORE built by Murdoch & Murray in 1902 for McGregor SS Co - W. A. MacGregor, Auckland. - Northern Steamship Co., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=15980 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> and oak and walnut panelling. Her delivery voyage from 24 July to 26 October, via St. Vincent, [[Cape Town#Sea|Cape Town]] and Hobart<ref name=":05">{{Cite web |date=27 October 1902 |title=New steamer for Auckland. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19021027.2.37 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> was mainly under sail as a [[brigantine]].<ref name=":210">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Claymore |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115188 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083912/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115188 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-27}}</ref> She was used between Auckland, [[Waiwera]] and Warkworth.<ref name=":05" /> 1905 sold to SSC. 23 December 1905 sank the ''Kapanui'', with the loss of 5 lives. 1907 sold to William J. Parker and James Smith, Auckland. On 1 October 1908 SSC merged with NSS,<ref name=":310" /> adding ''Claymore'', ''Gael'', ''Kapanui'', ''Kawau'', ''Kotiti'' and ''Orewa'' to the fleet.<ref name=":10" /> 1910 cut in half and lengthened.<ref name=":210" /> In 1930 her saloon was enclosed to add 8 berths.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 August 1930 |title=Shipping news. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19300813.2.18 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1938 laid up. 1940 used as a salvage vessel working on the recovery of bullion from the ''[[RMS Niagara#1941|Niagara]]''. 1940 used by the Navy and as a [[Boom defence vessel|Boom Defence Vessel]]. She was then laid up at [[Devonport Naval Base]].<ref name=":210" /> In February 1953 she was to be filled with old explosives and used by ''[[HMS Black Prince (81)#Royal New Zealand Navy|Black Prince]]'' for target practice off the Hauraki Gulf.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 February 1953 |title=General News. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19530205.2.40 |access-date=2024-03-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> However, instead, on 20 April 1953, she was sold for £125 and broken up at Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Have you heard of the ship HMNZS Claymore? The Claymore Spring 2017 |url=https://deewhimp.files.wordpress.com/2017/05/the-claymore94.pdf |website=Wordpress.com}}</ref> |- |Coromandel |1879 |1895–1912 |James Holmes, North Shore |68 |[[File:SS_Coromandel_in_1905.jpg|thumb|''Coromandel'' in 1905 in Whangārei harbour]]Steamer of {{Convert|25|hp|abbr=on}}, with a compound engine driving a single screw.<ref name=":312" /> She was launched on 26 April 1879<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 April 1879 |title=Launch of a steamer. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18790428.2.26 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and her first trial trip from Thames was on 24 June 1879, for Coromandel Steam Boat Co. Ltd (CSB), with a {{Convert|25|hp|abbr=on}} 2-cylinder (13{{frac|1|2}}, 23 -15)″ compound, surface-condensing engine, by [[A&G Price]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 June 1879 |title=The s.s. Coromandel. Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS18790624.2.10 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> she was {{Convert|99.8|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|16.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|6.6|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name=":282">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1906-1907 by Australian National Maritime Museum - Issuu |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__bde33d6a64ef70 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> In August 1895 NSS took over CSB,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 August 1895 |title=Coromandel news. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18950805.2.43.6 |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> around 1898<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 May 1898 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18980521.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to 1900<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 April 1900 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19000402.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Rotomahana'' took over the Coromandel route and ''Coromandel'' ran Waiheke and [[Rotoroa Island|Ruthe's Island]] trips.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 November 1899 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18991120.2.2.2 |access-date=2024-03-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1912 dismantled at Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Coromandel |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1078374 |access-date=2024-03-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083746/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1078374 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Daphne |1907 |1908–1931 |[[William Hoile Brown]], Auckland |192 |[[File:Daphne_launch_in_1907.jpg|thumb|''Daphne'' launch in 1907]]Steamer {{Convert|111.6|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|20.5|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|8.1|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|55|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":033" /> {{Convert|11|knot|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 June 1907 |title=A new steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070606.2.20 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> built of kauri and [[Podocarpus totara|totara]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 March 1907 |title=A new steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070315.2.71 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> launched on 16 March 1907<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 March 1907 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070316.2.13 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for A.J. Farmer, Kawau & W.H. Brown, Auckland.<ref name=":43">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Daphne |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1122922 |access-date=2024-03-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083915/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1122922 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> Her maiden voyage to Waiwera was on Saturday 13 July 1907, taking 2hrs 15mins;<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 July 1907 |title=A trial of speed. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070715.2.87 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Kawau took about 3hrs.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 December 1907 |title=Kawau. Observer |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TO19071228.2.34 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The next month she was stranded for a fortnight on a shoal in the [[Mahurangi River]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 August 1907 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070814.2.25 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1908 she was sold to NSS for the Kawau-[[Mangawhai|Mangawai]] route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 September 1908 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19080915.2.3.4 |access-date=2024-03-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her last scheduled run was to Coromandel and [[Colville, New Zealand|Colville]] on 27 August 1928.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 August 1928 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19280821.2.5.8 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was then laid up at Paeroa, brought to Auckland and then stripped in 1931.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 May 1931 |title=Sold to shipbreakers. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19310521.2.16 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her hull was sold to A.G. Frankham Ltd, who fitted her with a {{Convert|248|hp|abbr=on}} [[Fairbanks-Morse#Marine diesel engines|Fairbanks Morse]] diesel engine, her first trip to Awanui being on 24 November 1938, renamed as ''Awanui''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 September 1938 |title=Powerful engines. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380912.2.87 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 5 September 1939 the Navy chartered her as an [[Examination vessel#Vessels which served as examination vessels|examination vessel]]. 18 November 1941 released for coastal trade. In August 1942 ''Awanui'' was sold to South Taranaki Shipping Company, but the hull was condemned, beached in St Mary's Bay and broken up in 1956.<ref name=":43" /> In 1943 the engines were put into ''Tiroa''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 October 1943 |title=Tiroa rebuilt. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19431023.2.13 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Dido |1963 |1970–1975 |[[Charles Hill & Sons]], Bristol |1589 |Motor ship launched on 10 June 1963 and completed in October 1963 for Bristol Steam Navigation Co {{Convert|261|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|39.9|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|16.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with a {{Convert|2175|hp|abbr=on}} 9-cyl. 2 S.C.S.A. (340 mm × 570 mm) Polar MN19S engine by [[British Polar Engines|British Polar Engines Ltd]]., Glasgow,<ref name=":16" /> driving her at up to {{Convert|13.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 December 1969 |title=Dido bound for New Zealand. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19691210.2.215 |access-date=2024-04-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1970 NSS bought her to carry wheat from South Island to Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 March 1970 |title=The 1589 ton Dido. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19700310.2.194.10 |access-date=2024-04-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 9 December 1974 she was the last NSS ship to be laid up<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 December 1974 |title=Dido crew paid off. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19741210.2.185.18 |access-date=2024-04-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was sold to Metcalfe Motor Coasters, London in 1975 and renamed ''Gorsethorn'' in 1977.<ref name=":16">{{Cite web |date=2017-02-19 |title=DIDO |url=https://www.shipsnostalgia.com/media/dido.418097/ |access-date=2024-04-01 |website=Ships Nostalgia |language=en-US}}</ref> She ended in controversy, after being overhauled by Marine Services Convoyages, [[La Rochelle]] in 1990. She was renamed ''Déesse de la Démocratie'' and was to be an [[Offshore radio|offshore radio station]] for [[Protest and dissent in China|Chinese dissidents]]. On 13 May 1990 she arrived at [[Keelung]] to have a transmitter fitted, but 2 days later was placed under armed guard and the transmitter was seized by Customs. In April 1991 a businessman, Wu Meng-wu, bought the ship from the China Shipping Association for $550,000, for a [[1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre|Tianaman Square]] museum and moved her to [[Anping District|Anping]]. In 2003 [[Port of Kaohsiung|Kaohsiung port authority]] said the ship had paid no fees, was listing, rusty and a safety hazard,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 March 2024 |title=The Sad Goddess of Democracy – Offshore Radio Nostalgia |url=https://offshoreradio.info/the-sad-goddess-of-democracy/ |access-date=2024-04-01 |website=offshoreradio.info}}</ref> so that in September 2003 she went for scrapping in [[Taipei]].<ref name=":16" /> |- |Douglas |1882 |1882–1898 |[[Henry Niccol#Career|H. Stubbs, Waitara.
