McGregor, replacing Kawau on the Mangawai run.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 May 1904 |title=Launch of a steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19040507.2.31 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was built of kauri and [[ironbark]], with an engine from ''[[Union Company#Fleet|Southern Cross]]'' and had cabins for about 22 passengers.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 April 1904 |title=McGregor company's new steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19040429.2.37 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|84.8|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|17.5|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|6.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with a {{Convert|20|hp|abbr=on}} engine<ref name=":282" /> driving her at up to {{Convert|10.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 June 1904 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19040607.2.31 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1905 she was sold to SSC<ref name=":38">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Gael |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1118965 |access-date=2024-04-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083919/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1118965 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> and joined NSS on its merger with SSC on 1 October 1908.<ref name=":310" /> Her regular route from 1910<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 October 1910 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19101017.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to 1926 was Auckland to [[Pipiroa]], Hopai, [[Ngatea]] (Orchard), [[Kerepehi]], Waikaka and [[Tahuna, Waikato|Tahuna]], along the [[Piako River]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 October 1926 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19261009.2.11.4 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> From 1927 she was laid up, until sold to South Westland Shipping Company (SWSC) and converted to a motor ship, with a Gardner engine in May 1931.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 June 1931 |title=Ships, cargoes and mails. Dominion |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/DOM19310609.2.158 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 28 January 1941 she was chartered as HMS ''Gael'', returning to SWSC on 27 April 1941. In 1948 she was sold to Southern Cross Shipping Company (managed by John [[Holm & Co|Holm]] Ltd) and, on 18 April 1952, wrecked<ref name=":38" /> on The Beef Barrels (Kahikatoa), on the approach to [[French Pass Lighthouse#Navigation|French Pass]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Beef Barrels (Kahikatoa), Marlborough |url=https://www.topomap.co.nz/NZTopoMap/nz41682/The-Beef-Barrels-(Kahikatoa)/ |access-date=2024-01-25 |website=NZ Topo Map |language=en}}</ref> whilst carrying apples from Motueka to Nelson. Her crew were rescued.<ref name=":38" /> The engine, propeller and other items were salvaged in 1955.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 February 1955 |title=Wreck of the Gael. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19550218.2.67 |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Gairloch |1884 |1884–1903 |Blackwood & Gordon, Port Glasgow |373 |[[File:Northern SS Co Gairloch, 1033149 (cropped).jpg|thumb|SS ''Gairloch'' at [[Onehunga]] in June 1899]]Steamer ''Gairloch'' was a {{Convert|164.9|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|23|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, twin screw, passenger/cargo, steel ship, with a 2-cylinder (16, 31 × 21in), {{Convert|85|hp|abbr=on}} engine, launched on 22 August 1884 for NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer GAIRLOCH built by Blackwood & Gordon in 1884 for Northern Steamship Co. Ltd., Auckland, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=100&a1Order=Sorter_owner_1&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=307&ref=2439&vessel=GAIRLOCH |access-date=2024-02-25 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> In 1886 she was lengthened by {{Convert|17.5|ft|abbr=on}} by Cable & Co, Wellington. In 1887 she broke her port propeller shaft between New Plymouth and Onehunga.<ref name=":40" /> She had a major refit in 1901.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 August 1901 |title=The Northern Company's steamer Gairloch. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19010829.2.26 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 5 January 1903 she was wrecked, without loss of life, on rocks off [[Ōakura]], on passage from Kawhia to [[Ōpunake]].<ref name=":40">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Gairloch |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1087524 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083703/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1087524 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-25}}</ref> An inquiry found that the captain kept too close to the shore on a dark night and suspended his certificate for 3 months.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 February 1903 |title=The Gairloch Wreck Inquiry. Opunake Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/OPUNT19030206.2.10 |access-date=2024-02-25 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Parts of the wreck remained on the beach in 2020.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-04-15 |title=The Wreck of the Gairloch – Kiwi Adventures |url=https://adventure.nunn.nz/2020/04/15/wreck-of-the-gairloch/ |access-date=2024-02-25 |language=en-NZ}}</ref> |- |Glenelg |1878 |1881–1904 |Blackwood & Gordon, Port Glasgow |288 |[[File:Glenelg_at_Railway_Wharf.jpg|thumb|''Glenelg'' at Railway Wharf in 1905]]Steamer was a {{Convert|134.8|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|22.2|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|9.1|ft|abbr=on}} deep, single-screw, passenger/cargo, iron ship, with a 2-cylinder (22, 40 x 27in), {{Convert|75|hp|abbr=on}} engine, launched on launched on 11 October 1878 from the Castle Yard for Alexander McGregor,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900. |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1900-I.2.3.2.33 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with berths for 48 passengers. She left Greenock on 21 November 1878 and came via [[Cape Verde]] and Hobart,<ref>{{Cite news |date=1879-03-03 |title=ARRIVAL OF THE S.S. GLENELG. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article8974349 |access-date=2024-06-23 |work=Mercury}}</ref> where her captain loaded 10 tons of coal, but declined another 10. She ran short of coal near [[Manawatāwhi / Three Kings Islands|Manawatāwhi]] and sent a boat ashore near [[Cape Maria van Diemen|Cape Maria]] to send a message. The boat overturned and a crewman drowned.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 March 1879 |title=The steamer Glenelg. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18790331.2.41 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Glenelg'' arrived at [[Mangōnui]] on 23 March, towed by ''Argyle'', and Auckland next day.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 March 1879 |title=Arrival of the Glenelg. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18790324.2.38 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> During trials she reached just over {{Convert|9|knot|abbr=}}, then narrowly avoided ''[[SMS Albatross (1871)|Albatross]]'', but sank her captain's [[Gig (boat)|gig]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 April 1879 |title=The SS Glenelg. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18790403.2.8 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her maiden voyage was to [[Whitianga]] and Tauranga on 4 April 1879.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 April 1879 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18790405.2.7 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1881 ''Glenelg'' was taken over when NSS was formed.<ref name=":41">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Glenelg |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1076187 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083634/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1076187 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-05}}</ref> She was one of the ships which failed to tow ''[[SS Hilonian (1880)#1884-1887|Triumph]]'' off the rocks at [[Tiritiri Matangi Island|Tiritiri Matangi]] in 1883.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 December 1883 |title=The Triumph catastrophe. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18831203.2.28 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1903 she ran aground at Whanganui when avoiding another ship.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 May 1903 |title=The magisterial inquiry. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19030502.2.5.1 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In late 1904 she was sold to James Smith to carry coal from Whangārei,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 November 1904 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19041115.2.40 |access-date=2024-06-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1933 to W.R.McKenzie, Auckland for a hulk, 1934 to G.H. George & Company, Auckland, 4 December 1944 sunk beside ''Wainui'' as a [[Whangaparāoa Peninsula|Whangaparāoa]] breakwater.<ref name=":41" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Glenelg |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=75&ship_listPage=7&a1Order=Sorter_ship_list_bld_ref&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=39&ref=2396&vessel=GLENELG |website=clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> |- |Greyhound |1899 |1913–1919 |Lane & Brown, [[Whangaroa#History|Totara North]] |103 |[[File:Greyhound_in_1902.jpg|thumb|''Greyhound'' in 1902]]Auxiliary schooner built for J.A. Subritsky, Awanui, with a kauri hull, {{Convert|60|hp|abbr=on}} Union Oil engine,<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 June 1899 |title=New schooner Greyhound. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990613.2.37.2 |access-date=2024-03-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|95.2|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|21.2|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|6.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name=":14" /> In 1902 she searched for survivors from ''[[Awanui#Elingamite tragedy|SS Elingamite]]''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 November 1902 |title=An unsuccessful quest. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19021113.2.51.3 |access-date=2024-03-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1913 NSS bought her to serve Hokianga.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 August 1927 |title=Schooner Greyhound stranded on reef. Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19270809.2.18 |access-date=2024-03-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 5 October 1918 she was damaged by fire and laid up, until sold to Morris Hedstrom Ltd, [[Suva]] in June 1919.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 June 1919 |title=Shipping. Sun (Christchurch) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNCH19190626.2.15 |access-date=2024-03-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1923 to Fiji Shipping Company, Suva. On 27 July 1927 she was wrecked on Nasilai Reef,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Greyhound |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102315 |access-date=2024-03-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084000/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102315 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> near Suva, where ''[[Syria (ship)|Syria]]'' was wrecked in 1863. |- |Hauiti |1911 |1911–1941 |[[Viaduct Harbour#Shipyards|George Niccol]], Freemans Bay |148. 156 from 1947. |[[File:Hauiti.jpg|thumb|''Hauiti'' on her maiden voyage to Warkworth on 10 January 1912]]Steamer launched on 23 November 1911 for NSS,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Buchanan |first=Arthur Victor |date=1941 |title=Hauiti |url=https://digitalnz.org/records/36449173 |access-date=2024-04-17 |website=DigitalNZ |language=en}}</ref> with engines from ''Kapanui'',<ref name=":17" /> initially for the Warkworth route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 May 1912 |title=Northern steam company. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19120528.2.9 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had 30 berths,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 January 1912 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19120105.2.21 |access-date=2024-12-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> a speed of {{Convert|10.5|knot|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 January 1912 |title=The S.S. Hauiti. Rodney and Otamatea Times, Waitemata and Kaipara Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ROTWKG19120110.2.30 |access-date=2024-04-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> was {{Convert|100.2|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|20|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|9.4|ft|abbr=on}} deep and later used for Waiheke services.<ref name=":19" /> In 1917 she worked to Tahuna.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 April 2016 |title=SS Hauiti on the Piako River |url=https://teara.govt.nz/en/photograph/30490/ss-hauiti-on-the-piako-river |website=Te Ara - the Encyclopedia of New Zealand}}</ref> On 15 November 1939 the Navy took her as an examination vessel at Auckland.<ref name=":19" /> By 1946 she was used as [[Tāmaki]] Base tender.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Buchanan |first=Arthur Victor |date=1941 |title=Hauiti |url=https://digitalnz.org/records/36449173 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=DigitalNZ |language=en}}</ref> 1947 sold to South Westland Shipping Company. Converted to a motor ship and passenger areas removed. 24 April 1954 wrecked on [[The Brothers (New Zealand)#Islands|Awash Rocks]] in Cook Strait,<ref name=":19">{{Cite web |date=26 April 1954 |title=Coaster Hauiti wrecked. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19540426.2.51 |access-date=2024-04-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> on passage Motueka-Wellington.<ref name=":17">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Hauiti |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1130093 |access-date=2024-03-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083944/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1130093 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> A court decided there was an error of judgement, but not neglect of duty.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 December 1954 |title=Loss of Hauiti. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19541203.2.19 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Hauturu |1927 |1927–1952 |[[George Brown & Co]] [[Greenock]] |284 |[[File:AMV Hauturu at New Plymouth 1033139, 01 (cropped).jpg|thumb|''Hauturu'' at New Plymouth]][[File:StateLibQld_1_107228_Estrella_del_Mar_(ship).jpg|thumb|''Estrella del Mar'' about 1954]]Motor ship launched on 7 April 1927 for NSS. {{Convert|133.1|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|27.5|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with 2 × {{Convert|150|hp|abbr=on}} 2SA oil engines by [[Bolinder-Munktell#Marine engines|J & G C Bolinder]], driving a twin screw.<ref name=":372">{{Cite web |title=Motor Vessel HAUTURU built by George Brown & Co in 1927 for Northern Steamship Co. Ltd., Auckland, Coaster |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=3075 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> She was similar to ''Otimai''. On her delivery voyage via Panama she averaged {{Convert|8.1|knot|abbr=on}}, using sails in favourable winds.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 July 1927 |title=Greenock to Auckland. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19270726.2.91 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She replaced ''Ngapuhi'' when the New Plymouth service became cargo only and where, in 1931, she broke a propeller shaft.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 September 1931 |title=Propeller shaft broken. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19310914.2.6 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1952 she was too small for the Lyttelton route, so she was replaced by ''Ratanui'',<ref name=":382">{{Cite web |date=2 July 1952 |title=Shipping. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19520702.2.112 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> sold to E. Savoie, [[Nouméa]] and renamed ''Estrella Del Mar''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Hauturu |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1153970 |access-date=2024-03-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084047/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1153970 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> From 1953 William Collins & Sons Pty. Ltd, [[Brisbane]] used her as a gravel [[Lighter (barge)|lighter]], selling to Kleinschmitt Brothers in 1960. She was scuttled in [[Moreton Bay]] on 17 December 1983, as part of Curtin Artificial Reef.<ref name=":372" /> |- |Hotunui |1949 |1950–1967 |[[:sv:Kalmar varv|Kalmar Varv]], [[Kalmar]] |594 |Motor ship {{Convert|52.4|m|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|8.7|m|abbr=on}} wide,<ref>{{Cite web |title=HOTUNUI (IMO 5155575, General Cargo) |url=https://www.vesseltracking.net/ship/hotunui-5155575 |access-date=2024-03-02 |website=www.vesseltracking.net}}</ref> built as ''Fenja'' for Rederi A/B Eystresalt, [[Västervik]]. 1950 sold to NSS, renamed ''Hotunui'' and used between Whangārei, Tauranga, Lyttelton and Dunedin.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 August 1950 |title=Delivery voyage. Otago Daily Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19500830.2.47 |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1967 sold to Luggate Packers and used as helicopter base-ship for South Island deerstalkers. 1969 chartered by Capricorn Fisheries, Wellington for cray fishing. Named ''CHM 190''. 1972 sold to Alcon Company, New Hebrides and took tuna from fishing boats to Singapore.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Hotunui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1191793 |access-date=2024-03-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084127/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1191793 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> Inn 1977 she was renamed ''Alco Trento'' and then ''Jado Trader''.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hotunui - IMO 5155575 |url=https://www.shipspotting.com/photos/2283782 |access-date=3 March 2024}}</ref> She was in [[Guanaja]] harbour for about 5 years, until 1987, when she was sunk as a [[Wreck diving|dive wreck]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Diving / Snorkeling |url=https://www.flyfishguanaja.com/diving--snorkeling.html |access-date=2024-03-02 |website=FLY FISH GUANAJA |language=en}}</ref> to the south of the island, east of South West Cay.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Guanaja Fish Card (No.32) |url=https://frankosmaps.com/products/guanaja-fish-card |access-date=2024-03-02 |website=Franko Maps |language=en}}</ref> |- |Huia |1878 |1924–1927 |Richard Henry Yeoman, Auckland |129 |[[File:SS_Huia.jpg|thumb|''Huia'', probably at Wellington in 1902]]Steamer launched on 14 November 1878 for a cargo and passenger service between Wellington and Whanganui<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 November 1878 |title=Launch of an iron steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18781115.2.3.4 |access-date=2024-04-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for Charles Seager, who from March 1879 was also in partnership with W. Bishop and P. Moeller, then S.J. Johnston.<ref name=":39" /> She was {{Convert|120|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|16|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with a 2-cylinder (13, 22 - 16)″ compound engine, by Thomas Taylor Masefield & Co, Auckland<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 April 1878 |title=New steamer for Wellington. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18780406.2.3.4 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> (previously Vickery & Masefield),<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 May 1908 |title=Death of Mr Masefield. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19080501.2.65 |access-date=2025-07-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> of {{Convert|25|hp|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 November 1878 |title=Launch of an iron steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18781115.2.3.4 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In March 1881 she ran aground at Whanganui,<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 March 1881 |title=Port of Wanganui. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC18810310.2.3.1 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in September 1889<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 September 1889 |title=Interprovincial news. Feilding Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/FS18890926.2.13 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and July 1898 at Foxton,<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 July 1898 |title=A steamer stranded. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18980704.2.31 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Patea in June 1900<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 June 1900 |title=The stranded Huia. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19000620.2.25 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and Motueka in February 1903.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 February 1903 |title=SS Huia floated. Colonist |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TC19030224.2.11 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1884 she was bought by J. Joseph<ref name=":39" /> and her engine was moved forward about 1900.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 June 1900 |title=Shipping news. New Zealand Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZTIM19000613.2.10 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Wellington & [[Whanganui|Wanganui]] Steam Packet Company Limited bought her in 1901. After WWSP was wound up in 1917<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 March 1917 |title=No sale for steamers. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19170330.2.37 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> she was sold to W.H. Brewer, 1924 to C.V. Clare & H.M. Warner.<ref name=":39">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Huia |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1078367 |access-date=2024-04-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084040/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1078367 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> In 1921 she ran for Waikato Shipping Co, between Onehunga and Port Waikato until the company failed.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 October 1921 |title=Waikato coastal service - Onehunga to Hamilton under one flag. Waikato Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WT19211001.2.57 |access-date=2024-04-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> A 1924 letter from NSS says they were 'friends' and owners of the ship in trust for the Company. She was bought as she had been 'interfering with our [[Whangārei]] trade'.<ref name=":39" /> In 1927 she and ''Kanieri'' were dismantled at Auckland and their hulls sunk in deep water on the west side of the Western Reclamation.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 March 1927 |title=Old coasters' end. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19270318.2.32 |access-date=2024-04-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Iona |1875 |1881–1899 |H. Niccol, North Shore |220 |Steamer of {{Convert|65|hp|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|140|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|19.2|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.6|ft|abbr=on}},<ref name=":14" /> launched on 16 January 1875,<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 January 1875 |title=Launch of the Iona. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18750116.2.19 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with a hull of kauri and pōhutukawa, 2-cylinder (20", 38"), compound engine driving a single screw, by Fraser & Tinne, for Captain McGregor and his syndicate. She was always based in Auckland and replaced ''Rowena''<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 January 1875 |title=The new steamer Iona. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18750115.2.3.4 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> on the east coast route to [[Mangōnui#History and culture|Mangōnui]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 April 1875 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18750406.2.2.1 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 22 March 1879 she collided very slowly with the brigantine, ''Linda Weber'', when approaching the Railway Wharf at night, damaging equipment on both ships.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 March 1879 |title=Shipping summary. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18790331.2.58 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1881, she was among the first in the NSS fleet and was mentioned as being laid up in 1900.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 September 1900 |title=Northern Steamship Co Ltd. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19000926.2.72.22 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> However, her last sailings seem to have been to [[Kūaotunu]], Mercury Bay and [[Kennedy Bay]] in 1898,<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 August 1898 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18980819.2.3.3 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in 1899 she dragged her mooring, whilst laid up,<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 August 1899 |title=Vessels adrift. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990814.2.34.1 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and may have been transferred to G. Fraser in 1900. 1903 dismantled. 1909 broken up.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1070210 |access-date=2024-06-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083637/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1070210 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Kaituna |1906 |1906–1927 |Bailey & Lowe |8 |[[File:Kaituna_at_Katikati.jpg|thumb|Kaituna at Katikati]]Motor launch on [[Kaituna River]] serving [[Maketu]] and Canaan's Landing, near [[Te Puke]]. An oil engine, built by Union Gas Engine Co, drove her at {{Convert|7.5|knot|abbr=on}},<ref name=":52" /> {{Convert|35|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|10|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|3.9|ft|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 April 1906 |title=Vessel tor the Kaituna River. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19060430.2.9 |access-date=2025-07-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with space for 10 tons of cargo. She was towed from Auckland by ''Aupouri'' and arrived on 25 June 1906.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 June 1906 |title=Local and general. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19060625.2.5.1 |access-date=2025-07-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1907 she replaced ''Katikati''<ref name=":54">{{Cite web |date=14 October 1907 |title=Local and general. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19071014.2.5 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> on a twice weekly Tauranga-[[Katikati#European settlement|Katikati]] service. From 1917 she was a tender in Auckland, had a new engine in 1922 by J. Adams, sold to Compton & Morrison and renamed ''Lone Star'' in 1927 and, on 19 December 1928, caught fire off [[Narrow Neck, New Zealand|Narrow Neck]] and her remains drifted onto the rocks at [[North Head (New Zealand)|Maungauika]].<ref name=":52">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Kaituna |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000065 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083849/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000065 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-27}}</ref> |- |Kanieri |1886 |1893–1927 |[[William Denny and Brothers|William Denny & Bros.]] |203 |[[File:StateLibQld_1_148699_Kanieri_(ship).jpg|thumb|''Kanieri'' at Blenheim, maybe in 1887]]Steamer of {{Convert|20|hp|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900. |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1900-I.2.3.2.33 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with a 2-cylinder (15, 26 x 15"), compound engine driving a single-screw, {{Convert|105|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|21.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8|ft|abbr=on}}, launched on 29 October 1886 for Union.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer KANIERI built by William Denny & Bros in 1886 for Union Steamship Co of New Zealand, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=75&a1Page=195&ref=14910&vessel=KANIERI |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> Her delivery voyage from Port Glasgow to [[Port Chalmers]] took 118½ days, rigged as a brigantine, passing by [[Inaccessible Island]] and the [[Crozet Islands]]. She was designed for the Wellington-[[Blenheim, New Zealand|Blenheim]] service, with 24 berths,<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 April 1887 |title=Shipping. Evening Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ESD18870425.2.30 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> working with ''Waihi'', her predecessor on the route,<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 May 1887 |title=Local and general news. Marlborough Express |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/MEX18870506.2.5 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and first arrived in Blenheim on 10 May 1887.<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 May 1887 |title=The SS Kanieri. Marlborough Daily Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/MDTIM18870511.2.10 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was laid up in 1891, due to silting of the [[Ōpaoa River]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 April 1891 |title=The freight question. Marlborough Express |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/MEX18910428.2.11 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and sold to NSS in July 1893.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 June 1893 |title=Telegrams. Otago Daily Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT18930612.2.20 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She served Raglan, Kawhia and Waitara.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 August 1893 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18930809.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1896 she caught fire at Onehunga,<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 January 1896 |title=The SS Kanieri on fire. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18960104.2.34 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> causing £300 worth of damage.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Kanieri |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1084490 |access-date=2024-06-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083739/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1084490 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> She ran aground at Waitara in June 1902, due to steering problems.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1903 |title=Return of Wrecks and Casualties to Shipping reported to the Marine Department |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/appendix-to-the-journals-of-the-house-of-representatives/1903/I/3648 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1903 she was replaced on the west coast route by ''Kia ora,''<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 December 1903 |title=Raglan. Waikato Argus |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WAIGUS19031214.2.13 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ran on the east coast to Whangārei, until September 1922,<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 September 1922 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19220907.2.31 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was then dismantled.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 October 1922 |title=The last voyage. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19221016.2.102 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1911 she grounded at Whangārei.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 May 1911 |title=Left by the tide in the Whangarei River. New Zealand Graphic |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/148953 |access-date=2024-06-10 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> She was buried on the west side of the Western Reclamation,<ref name=":402" /> with ''Huia'', about March 1927, so that their masts were below low tide level,<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 March 1927 |title=Old coasters end. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19270318.2.32 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though possibly because they were flattened.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 January 1930 |title=Where old ships lie. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19300116.2.132 |access-date=2024-06-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Kapanui |1898 |1908–1909 |[[Robert Logan Sr.]], Auckland |129 |[[File:Kapanui_on_the_Devonport_Steam_Ferry_slip_after_the_collision_in_1905.jpg|thumb|''Kapanui'' on the Devonport Steam Ferry slip after the collision in 1905]]Steamer was {{Convert|102|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|18.6|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|7|ft|abbr=on}} deep,<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 October 1898 |title=A new steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18981003.2.57 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> of {{Convert|32|hp|abbr=on}}, with a compound engine driving a single screw<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900. |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1900-I.2.3.2.33 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at up to {{Convert|11.5|knot|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 October 1898 |title=The Kapanui. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18981025.2.24.9 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> launched on 3 October 1898 for the CSC, which became SSC,<ref name=":262">{{Cite web |title=SS "Kapanui" |url=https://ehive.com/collections/3023/objects/842446/ss-kapanui |access-date=2024-01-30 |website=eHive |language=en}}</ref> and joined NSS on its merger with SSC on 1 October 1908.<ref name=":310" /> On 23 March 1899 ''Kapanui'' was hit by McGregor Steamship's ''Rose Casey'' at Warkworth, though without major damage.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 March 1899 |title=Collision at Warkworth. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990323.2.37 |access-date=2024-03-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=3 July 1899 |title=Nautical enquiry. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990703.2.48 |access-date=2024-03-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 23 December 1905 she was sunk in collision with ''Claymore'' as the ships rounded [[North Head (New Zealand)|North Head]], five lives being lost. A hearing decided that the ''Kapanui's'' captain changed course without warning when he noticed a navigation light had gone out. His certificate was suspended for a year,<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 January 1906 |title=The Kapanui disaster. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19060117.2.77 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> but a jury found him not guilty of manslaughter.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 February 1906 |title=The Kapanui disaster. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19060212.2.84 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Kapanui'' was later salvaged and the hole repaired between 15 January<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 January 1906 |title=Local & general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19060115.2.23 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and 7 February 1906.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 February 1906 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19060208.2.68.4 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Photographs before<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 December 1904 |title=S.S. 'KAPANUI.' Auckland Weekly News |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/182636 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> and after the collision show some alterations were made during repair.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 February 1908 |title=S.S. Kapanui off for St. Heliers. New Zealand Graphic |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/139606 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> At Warkworth,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kapanui on fire at Warkworth (photo) |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/153723 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> on 12 August 1909, she was burnt out, the fire probably starting in the engine room. She was towed back to Auckland, where the engines were salvaged<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 August 1909 |title=The Kapanui. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19090813.2.66 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and used in ''Hauiti''.<ref name=":262" /> |- |Kapiti |1902 |1938–1950 |J. Fullerton, Merksworth Works, Paisley |249 |[[File:Kapiti_at_Whanganui_in_1925.jpg|thumb|''Kapiti'' at Whanganui in 1925]]Motor ship ''Kapiti'' was a {{Convert|119.4|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|21.2|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|35|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":033" /> single-screw, passenger/cargo, steel ship, launched in October 1902, with a 2-cylinder (14 & 29 - 20in) {{Convert|125|psi|abbr=on}} engine by Ross & Duncan, Glasgow,<ref name=":522">{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer KAPITI built by J. Fullerton & Co in 1902 for J. H. Williams, Wellington., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?year_built=1902&builder=&a1Order=Sorter_ship_list_bld_ref&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=16&ref=8298&vessel=KAPITI |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> for CSC and registered in Wellington on 10 March 1903.<ref name=":132" /> As above, in 1912 Patea Shipping Company re-organised. Williams took ''Kapiti'' and ''Arapawa'', for Wellington-Whanganui.<ref name=":232" /> In 1930 she was converted to a motor ship, with a 6-cylinder {{Convert|137|hp|abbr=on}} engine by [[L. Gardner and Sons|Norris, Henty & Gardner & Co]].<ref name=":522" /> In October 1938 she was sold to NSS,<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 October 1938 |title=Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/evening-post/1938/10/06/17 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to work to Whangārei,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 July 1939 |title=Coastal shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390715.2.22 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> in 1950 to E. Savoie, [[Nouméa]] and renamed ''El Retiro'', in 1952 to D. Gubbay and on 6,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Kapiti |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1108078 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084105/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1108078 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-01}}</ref> or, more probably, 21 May 1953, was wrecked in [[Segond Channel]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Lloyd's Register merchant ships totally lost, broken up, etc. during the quarter ended 31st March, 1953 |url=https://lloyds-production.s3.amazonaws.com/_file/general/1953-casualty-returns.pdf}}</ref> whilst salvaging war material dumped off [[Naval Advance Base Espiritu Santo#Million dollar point|Million Dollar Point]], after touching a reef and being holed.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1953-05-27 |title=TRADER AGROUND |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article194244232 |access-date=2024-02-01 |work=Daily Examiner}}</ref> |- |Kapui |1902 |1908–1915 |Lane & Sons, Totara North |59 |[[File:Kapui_at_Silverdale.jpg|thumb|''Kapui'' at Silverdale]]Steamer began running in December 1902 for CSC,<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 December 1902 |title=The steamer Kapui. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19021218.2.13 |access-date=2024-03-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> which became SSC in 1905<ref name=":53">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Kapui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115191 |access-date=2024-03-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083925/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115191 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> and merged with NSS on 1 October 1908.<ref name=":310" /> {{Convert|60.1|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|14.6|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|5.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|30|hp|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":282" /> In 1915 she was sold to E.J. Jones and in 1919 to Northern Fishing Company, both of Whangārei. 1920 to Frank B. Lett, Waitara. 1926 to Arthur B. Ogle, Waitara. 1928 to Taranaki Shipping Company Limited. 1931 steam replaced by oil engine.<ref name=":53" /> 21 August 1934, wrecked [[Mōkau River]] bar after her engine was swamped by a wave and soon covered with sand.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 August 1934 |title=Salvage work abandoned. Taranaki Daily News |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TDN19340825.2.39 |access-date=2024-03-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Katikati |1878 |1881–1911 |T. Niccol, North Shore |36 |[[File:Katikati_at_Victoria_Wharf,_Tauranga_in_1883.jpg|thumb|''Katikati'' at Victoria Wharf, Tauranga 1883]]A small, shallow draught,<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 November 1878 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18781118.2.4.3 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> composite, steamer, with fore-and-aft schooner rig, launched by Niccol on 22 October 1878, to link the new settlement of Katikati with ''Rowena'' at Tauranga, {{Convert|55|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|13.5|ft|abbr=on}}, draught {{Convert|3.5|ft|abbr=on}} with an 8 passenger cabin and about 25 tons of cargo, with a Fraser & Tinne<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 October 1878 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18781024.2.3 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|8|hp|abbr=on}}, compound engine and a single screw<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900. |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1900-I.2.3.2.33 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at about {{Convert|8|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 November 1878 |title=Arrival of the Katikati launch. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18781107.2.4 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Built for the ''Rowena's'' owners, James Macfarlane & Partners, Auckland, she passed to NSS on its formation in 1881,<ref name=":46">{{Cite web |title=Katikati |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1078363 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083641/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1078363 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> by which time she had been outgrown by the trade<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 November 1881 |title=New Steamer for the Tauranga and Katikati Service. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18811124.2.9.1 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was also serving Maketu,<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 April 1881 |title=Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18810412.2.8 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Canaan and Te Puke.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 July 1881 |title=Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18810723.2.6 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In April 1885 she was transferred to Whangārei,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 April 1885 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18850422.2.3.5 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> where she was scuttled at the wharf to extinguish a fire in August 1885.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 August 1885 |title=The ss Katikati scuttled. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18850814.2.13 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> By 1900 she was trading between Tauranga and Maketu.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 January 1900 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19000125.2.43 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1911 her engine was removed and she became a lighter<ref name=":46" /> at Ōpōtiki,<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 September 1911 |title=A punt to be provided. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19110901.2.18 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though she was also reported as being towed there in 1914.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 January 1914 |title=Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19140105.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She remained registered as a NSS hulk until after 1925.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1925-1926 |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__4405258486f263 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=Issuu |language=en}}</ref> |- |Kawau |1899 |1908–1939 |Robert Logan Sr., Auckland |99 |[[File:SS_Kawau_at_Auckland.jpg|thumb|Kawau at Auckland]]Kauri steamer for 40 passengers and 60 tons of cargo, {{Convert|85|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|17.5|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|6.5|ft|abbr=on}}, A&G Price compound engine (10, 22)″ working at up to {{Convert|130|psi|abbr=on}}, launched on 25 July 1899 by Logan Bros<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 July 1899 |title=New steamer Kawau. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990725.2.30 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for Mr Hick-Ross, manager of [[Kawau Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 November 1898 |title=A new steamer. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18981125.2.100 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She reached {{Convert|10.5|knot|abbr=on}} on trial.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 August 1899 |title=The steamer Kawau. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990829.2.19.9 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her maiden voyage to [[Mangawhai#History|Mangawhai]] was on 30 August 1899.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 September 1899 |title=Mangawai notes. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18990901.2.83 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1901 she was sold to SSC<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 June 1901 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19010606.2.33 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and joined NSS on the merger with SSC on 1 October 1908.<ref name=":310" /> Her service ended in March 1939, when ''Hauiti'' took over some of her routes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 March 1939 |title=Service ended. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390320.2.114 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> At the start of the war she became an [[Examination vessel#Vessels which served as examination vessels|examination ship]], until replaced by ''Ikatere'' in 1940,<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 May 1940 |title=Examination ship. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19400501.2.129 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> after which she was dismantled and used as a yacht club-house in the [[Tāmaki River]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=S.S.Kawau, Mangawhai Museum |url=https://ehive.com/collections/3023/objects/579631/sskawau |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=eHive |language=en}}</ref> |- |Kia Ora |1896 |1903–1907 |R. Duncan |307 |[[File:1907_Kia_Ora_at_Onehunga.jpg|thumb|Kia Ora at Onehunga in 1907]]Steel, 2 screw steamer with 2 masts launched on 11 May 1896 by [[Robert Duncan and Company|Robert Duncan & Co]], Port Glasgow for McGregor Steam Ship. {{Convert|120.2|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|23.1|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|12.3|ft|abbr=on}}, 2 x triple expansion (9.5, 15, 25 x 18in), {{Convert|65|hp|abbr=on}} engines by Muir & Houston Ltd. In 1898 she was sold to Union, in 1903 to NSS and, on 6 June 1907, was wrecked on Piritoki Reef, off [[Herangi Range#Tirua Point|Tirua Point]], en route from Waitara to Kawhia<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kia Ora |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=5791 |access-date=2025-07-18 |website=Clydeships}}</ref> in fog. The captain and two passengers drowned. An inquiry found that the ship was 7 miles off course, that it was obvious perjury had been committed, recommended a law change that no liquor could be supplied to crew without the order of the captain and forwarded the chief officer's certificate to the Minister for Marine,<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 July 1907 |title=The report. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070723.2.77 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> who found no cause for further action and returned it.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 August 1907 |title=The Kia Ora disaster. Waihi Daily Telegraph |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WHDT19070823.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-19 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Kopu |1897 |1897 - c1935/1936 |John Young, Thames. |18 net |[[File:PS_Kopu.jpg|thumb|''Kopu'', preserved at Paeroa Maritime Park]][[File:Kopu_and_Manaia_about_1912.jpg|thumb|''Kopu'' and ''Manaia'' about 1912]]Paddle steamer, probably built by Robert P. Gibbons, who ran a sawmill at Kopu, with John Young fitting the engines.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 February 1897 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18970226.2.3.6 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was bought by NSS and used to tow up to two barges on the Piako and Waihou rivers.<ref name=":622">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Kopu |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=500500271 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083804/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=500500271 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-02}}</ref> She had a {{Convert|13|hp|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900 |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/appendix-to-the-journals-of-the-house-of-representatives/1900/I/3014 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 8" cylinder engine, working up to {{Convert|120|psi|abbr=on}}, an average speed around {{Convert|8|knot|abbr=on}} and was {{Convert|60|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|13|ft|abbr=on}} wide, or {{Convert|21|ft|abbr=on}} over her paddle boxes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 November 1897 |title=Thames Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THA18971116.2.33 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1935 she sank at her Waihou River mooring, near Ngahina Wharf, north west of Paeroa, probably due to vandals turning a valve and filling her with water.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine News 1979 Vol 30 Issue 01 |url=https://nzshipmarine.com/nodes/view/1794 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=New Zealand Ship and Marine Society |language=en}}</ref> In 1980 she was raised and moved to a mud berth and, in 1999, her remnants preserved at Paeroa Historical Maritime Park.<ref name=":622" /> |- |Kotiti |1898 |1908–1926 |Robert Logan Sr., Auckland |58, 62 from 1926. |[[File:Kotiti (S.S.), 10168.jpg|thumb|''Kotiti'' in 1901]] Steamer<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 June 1898 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Mail |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZMAIL18980616.2.129 |access-date=2024-01-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> launched on 12 April 1898, {{Convert|75|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|17|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|6|ft|abbr=on}}, machinery by S. McCoskrie & Son, Chapel Street,<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 April 1898 |title=Launch of the Kotiti. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18980412.2.34.3 |access-date=2025-07-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|14|hp|abbr=on}}, with a compound engine, driving a single screw,<ref name=":323">{{Cite web |title=Marine Department. Annual Report for 1899-1900. |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1900-I.2.3.2.33 |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> built for R. Logan Jnr. Designed for the trade to Warkworth up the Mahurangi River. 1898 sold to CSC, which became SSC in 1905<ref name=":63">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Kotiti |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102306 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083931/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102306 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-31}}</ref> and merged with NSS on 1 October 1908.<ref name=":310" /> She was laid up from her Matakana run during the 1921 recession<ref name=":59" /> and sold to C.T.J. Anderson, Lyttelton in 1926, to Kotiti Shipping Company, Lyttelton in 1927 and an oil engine installed, 1929 to H. Soares, [[Karamea]], 1929 to Oparara Shipping Company, Westport. 10 October 1931 missing with all hands between Westport and Foxton.<ref name=":63" /> |- |Lion | |1910c<ref>{{Cite web |date=1910 |title=Puke Wharf, Paeroa |url=https://natlib.govt.nz/records/22612100 |access-date=2024-01-23 |website=natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> - 1919 |Not known |15<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 January 1920 |title=Small steamer burned. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19200101.2.30 |access-date=2024-01-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |Steam tug<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Lion |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000068 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084212/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000068 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-23}}</ref> burnt to the waterline at Paeroa wharf on 30 December 1919. The engine and boiler were salvaged.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 January 1920 |title=Small steamer burned. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19200101.2.30 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Little George | |1883–1912 or later |Not known |4<ref name=":323" /> |Steam launch with a {{Convert|4|hp|abbr=on}}, non condensing engine, driving a single screw,<ref name=":323" /> in use from at least 1883.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 December 1883 |title=Mercury Bay. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18831224.2.46 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was valued at £275 in 1885.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 May 1885 |title=Northern Steamship Co. annual meeting. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18850520.2.32 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Use as a feeder vessel at Whitianga and reportedly taken in 1912.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Little George |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000054 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083706/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000054 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> She linked to [[Coroglen#History|Gumtown]] and Mercury Bay,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 July 1907 |title=The Kia Ora inquiry. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19070705.2.4 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> carrying cargo such as [[kauri gum]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 August 1900 |title=A chapter of accidents. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19000808.2.37 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The design of ''Ariana'', at Historical Maritime Park, Paeroa, is based on that of ''Little George''.<ref>{{Cite web |title=River cruises |url=https://historicalmaritimepark.co.nz/park-services/river-cruises-5/ |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=historicalmaritimepark.co.nz}}</ref> |- |Macgregor |1881 |1881–1888 |McQuarrie & McCallum, Auckland |255 |[[File:Macgregor.jpg|thumb|''Macgregor'']]Steamer built for A. McGregor, Auckland, to serve Waitara, {{Convert|133|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|21|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|8.9|ft|abbr=on}}, with berths for 24 passengers, built of kauri, on iron frames. On her trial trip on 29 September 1881 her Fraser & Tinne {{Convert|70|psi|abbr=on}} engines drove her at {{Convert|9.25|knot|abbr=}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 October 1881 |title=Official trial trip of the SS Macgregor. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18811010.2.28 |access-date=2025-07-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Named to distinguish her from the 1871 Frisco Mail Steamer ''McGregor''. In 1881, she was among the first in the NSS fleet. 4 May 1885 collided with ''Wellington'' in the Hauraki Gulf. On 11 August 1887 she hit the Whanganui bar, breaking her rudder and propeller. She used her sails and was towed to Onehunga by ''Staffa''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 August 1887 |title=The Macgregor safe. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18870817.2.44 |access-date=2025-07-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In January 1888 she was sold to the French government for use as a gunboat in New Caledonia.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 January 1888 |title=Telegrams. Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/EP18880113.2.20 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Glenelg replaced her on services to Whanganui<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 January 1888 |title=the ss Glenelg. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC18880121.2.3.3 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and she left for [[Nouméa]] about 21 January.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 January 1888 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18880117.2.5.3 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her registration transferred to S.A. Joseph, Sydney and then J. Montefiore of Noumea, both in 1888, to Burns Philp & Co in 1895, 1898 to G.H. Gallop & Laurie, Perth, 1900 to Bernard Byrnes, Sydney, 1907 converted to hulk.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - MacGregor |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1082695 |access-date=2024-07-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083644/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1082695 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Manaia |1898 |1912–1926 |William Denny & Bros. |1159 |[[File:SS_Manaia_aground.jpg|thumb|''Manaia'' aground]][[File:A_48th_scale_model_of_SS_Manaia_1898-1926.jpg|thumb|A 48th scale model of ''Manaia'']]Steamer built as ''Rotoiti'' for Union<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Manaia |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1101481 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083947/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1101481 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-22}}</ref> was a {{Convert|220|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|33.1|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|13.2|ft|abbr=on}} deep, steel, with 2 x 3-cylinder (13{{frac|1|2}}, 20{{frac|1|2}}, 31{{frac|1|2}} × 27)″ engines of {{Convert|108|hp|abbr=on}}, driving twin screws.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer ROTOITI built by William Denny & Bros in 1898 for Union Steamship Co. of New Zealand Ltd., Dunedin, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=50&ship_listPage=1&ship_listPageSize=75&a1Order=yard_number&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=736&ref=15151&vessel=ROTOITI |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> In August 1912 she was sold to NSS for the Whangārei route, renamed ''Manaia'' and her accommodation increased for 350 passengers,<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 August 1912 |title=The Whangarei service. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19120823.2.111 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 209 saloon and 73 [[steerage]]. She replaced ''Ngapuhi'' on the route,<ref name=":20" /> providing 3 overnight trips a week in each direction.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 December 1924 |title=Passengers penalised. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19241215.2.85 |access-date=2024-07-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 10 June 1926, en route from Tauranga to Auckland, she was caught by a [[Wind setup|strong set]] and grounded on [[Slipper Island|Slipper Island (Whakahau)]],<ref name=":212">{{Cite web |date=29 June 1926 |title=Inquiry into loss of Manaia. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19260629.2.13 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> without loss of life, close to where ''Te Teko'' was wrecked in 1920.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 June 1926 |title=Little hope for steamer Manaia. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19260612.2.10 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her captain and helmsman were held not to blame.<ref name=":212" />[[File:StateLibQld_1_173631_Rotoiti_(ship).jpg|thumb|''Rotoiti'' when a Union ship]] |- |Mangapapa |1902 |1903–1908 |Wm. Ford Jnr. |164 |[[File:Mangapapa.jpg|thumb|Mangapapa after 1903 additions]]Collier launched by W. M. Ford at [[Berrys Bay#Shipbuilding|Berry's Bay]] on 24 September 1902 for Taranaki Collieries Ltd, {{Convert|105|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|25|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|6.5|ft|abbr=on}}, 2-cylinder (13, 26, - 18)″. {{Convert|120|psi|abbr=on}} compound surface condensing engines by [[Vauxhall Motors#Independent (1857–1925)|Vauxhall Iron Works]], average speed {{Convert|10|knot}}, schooner-rigged, of Australian hardwood and kauri, able to carry 160 tons of coal from the shallow [[Mōkau River]], accommodation for 16 crew.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1902-09-25 |title=LAUNCH OF A STEAMER. |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article14492216 |access-date=2025-07-22 |work=Sydney Morning Herald}}</ref> Despite her design she had problems navigating the river, so ''Ngunguru'' was sold to Taranaki and ''Mangapapa'' bought from them by NSS in 1903.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 July 1903 |title=Sale of the Ngunguru. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19030729.2.25 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1903 her appearance was greatly changed,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Showing a view of the S.S. Mangapapa |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/200735/rec/8 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> when NSS had passenger accommodation for 26 men and 10 women and electric lighting, added by [[Robert Logan Sr.|Robert Logan]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 September 1903 |title=The Mangapapa. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19030921.2.38.13 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1908 NSS replaced her on the Whakatāne route with ''Tasman''<ref name=":18" /> and sold her to Karamea Steamship Company in 1908 to replace the wrecked ''Ngunguru''.<ref name=":272">{{Cite web |date=27 November 1908 |title=Shipping news. New Zealand Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZTIM19081127.2.88 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 20 July 1909 ''Mangapapa'' ran aground on the North Spit of the [[Karamea River]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 July 1909 |title=The Mangapapa refloated. West Coast Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WCT19090723.2.8.2 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and on 10 October 1914 she too was wrecked there,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mangapapa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1112600 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083828/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1112600 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> due to recent silting.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 October 1914 |title=Grey River Argus |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GRA19141031.2.20 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Karamea Steamship Company closed after the wreck.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 November 1914 |title=Karamea Co's sale. New Zealand Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZTIM19141107.2.3 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Maranui |1953 |1953–1968 |[[:nl:Royal Bodewes|J. An inquiry blamed shifting of her wheat cargo, lack of attention to weather forecasts and failure to close pipes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 January 1969 |title=Marunui loss. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19690121.2.9 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|Matangi]] |1908 |1923–1929 |A. January 2004 scrapped at Kadim Rasul, [[Kumira Union|Kumira]].<ref name=":50" /> |- |Melva |1946 |1946 |United Ship and Boatbuilders, Auckland and Steel Ships Limited. |243 |Like ''Aranui'' and ''Rosalie'', built of kauri, {{Convert|500|hp|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|8|knot|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|103|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|24|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|9.5|ft|abbr=on}}<ref name=":47" /> built as powered lighter YF-1047, chartered by NSS for a few weeks in 1946, from 26 October to 12 November, to carry cargo to Great Barrier, Tauranga, Whangaroa and Napier,<ref name=":48" /> to cover during Kapiti's annual overhaul.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 October 1946 |title=Shipping from Auckland. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19461030.2.6 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Holm & Co took her for 2 years to carry dolomite from [[Onekaka]] to Wellington,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 November 1946 |title=Power Lighters For Coastal Trade Service. Hawke's Bay Herald-Tribune |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HBHETR19461122.2.32 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> until September 1948, when Union Manufacturing & Export Co, [[Petone]], first chartered and then, in 1949, bought her to salvage US war material. 1949 sold to same company. Damaged off New Guinea and holed on a reef near [[Mackay, Queensland|Mackay]]. 1950 laid up at Wellington. 1951 Sold to [[Greymouth]] Shipping Company to run Greymouth-Wellington-Wanganui.<ref name=":48" /> August 1952 new twin {{Convert|320|hp|abbr=on}} diesels installed.<ref name=":47" /> 1954 laid up Wellington. 1956 Sold to Captain H C Williams, Rarotonga, for local trading. 1957 Sold to Dartim Shipping Co. Hong Kong, to run between [[Timor]] and Australia. 1961 Arrived [[Townsville]] for repairs. Arrested for debt on 2 September 1966 Scuttled {{Convert|50|mi|abbr=on}} off Townsville.<ref name=":48">{{Cite web |title=Melva |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171449 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084114/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171449 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> |- |Moanui |1961 |1961–1966 |Pacific Island Shipbuilding Company |975 |Motor ship built for NSS, {{Convert|211|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|36|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|12|ft|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|12|knot|abbr=}}. She was the first ship in the fleet to have [[bipod mast]]s.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 December 1961 |title=New coastal ship. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19611212.2.159 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1966 sold to John Burke, Brisbane and renamed ''Alagna''. 1973 sold to Cairns owners and renamed ''Eurika''. 1975 sold to Poly Shipping Co. Ltd. Soc. Anon, Singapore and renamed ''Aik Lai''. 1977 sold to Gill Shipping Co. (Pte) Ltd. (Heng Leong Shipping Co).1985 reported still sailing.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Moanui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315709 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084148/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315709 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-22}}</ref> |- |Moanui |1957 |1967–1975 |[[Ardrossan#History|Ardrossan]] Dockyard Limited |1289 |Motor ship launched on 10 December 1957 and delivered in June 1958, as ''Bay Fisher'', to James Fisher & Sons Ltd, Barrow-in-Furness, who chartered her to [[Atlantic Steam Navigation Company|Atlantic Steam Navigation Co.]] Ltd, until 1967, when NSS first chartered, then, in March 1970, bought her, renaming her ''Moanui''. She was {{Convert|220.9|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|38.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|16.4|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with 6-cylinder (340x570mm) [[British Polar Engines]] and a single screw. In 1975 she was sold to Crossworld Navigation Services, Singapore and renamed ''Chantala Fortune'', in 1977 to Pacific International Lines, Singapore as ''Kota Pahlawan'' and in 1984 to Maritime Express Inc., Panama as ''Pahlawan''. On 27 October 1984 her classification was suspended for lack of a survey and by 1986 was under arrest near [[Chennai Port]]. ''Feng Nan'' took her in tow for Singapore, but, on 11 May 1986, the tow rope snapped and she ran aground near Gravet Point, Sri Lanka, becoming a total loss.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Motor Vessel BAY FISHER built by Ardrossan Dockyard Ltd in 1958 for James Fisher & Sons.Ltd., Barrow-in-Furness, Coaster |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=872 |access-date=2024-02-04 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Bay Fisher |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1187979 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084159/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1187979 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-04}}</ref> In Feb 1989 she was broken up.<ref name=":83">{{Cite web |title=NORTHERN STEAM SHIP COMPANY LTD. |url=http://www.nzcoastalshipping.com/Northern%20S.S.Co.html |website=New Zealand Coastal Shipping}}</ref> |- |Motu |1920 |1920–1949 |G.T. Niccol |197 |Schooner built for NSS. Later became an auxiliary ketch.<ref name=":30" /> Whilst waiting for her engines to arrive, she was towed from Whangārei to Auckland, carrying cement, before starting on Bay of Plenty routes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 August 1920 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19200826.2.5 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The {{Convert|160|hp|abbr=on}} twin [[Semi-diesel engine|semi diesel]] engines were replaced in 1938, with twin {{Convert|240|hp|abbr=on}} Fairbanks Morse diesels.<ref name=":31">{{Cite web |date=6 August 1938 |title=New engines. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380806.2.162 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1949 sold to Karamea Shipping Company Limited. In February 1962, while berthed at Lyttelton, she was crushed by Union's ''[[Union Company#Fleet|Maori]]'' and damaged beyond repair. Towed to Dunedin and dismantled there.<ref name=":30">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Motu |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136881 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084021/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136881 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-06}}</ref> |- |Muritai |1884 |1897–1908 |[[Bow, McLachlan and Company#1872–1914|J. McArthur & Co]], Paisley |225 |[[File:Muritai_on_Muritai_Point.jpg|thumb|''Muritai'' on Muritai Point]]1884 launched on 12 August 1884 as ''Bellinger'' for G.W. Nicoll, Sydney. She was {{Convert|125|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|22.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|8.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, 2-masted, iron ship, with a {{Convert|45|hp|abbr=on}} 2-cylinder (17, 32 - 21)″ engine by William Kemp, [[Govan#Ships built at Govan|Govan]], driving a single screw at up to {{Convert|9.5|knot|abbr=on}}. 1884 sold to [[Howard Smith Limited#Investments|Belfast & Koroit S.N. Co]]. 1887 sold to Bruce B. Nicholl of Sydney, N.S.W.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer BELLINGER built by J McArthur & Co in 1884 for G W Nicoll, Sydney NSW, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=75&ship_listPage=2532&a1Order=yard_number&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=202&ref=25243&vessel=BELLINGER |access-date=2024-05-13 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> 1890 sold to E.T. Miles, Hobart. 1894 sold to T.A. Reynolds & Partners, Hobart. 1896 sold to Union. 1897 sold to NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Muritai |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1089319 |access-date=2024-05-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083807/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1089319 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> 27 May 1908 wrecked with no casualties on [[Hen and Chicken Islands#Lady Alice Island|Lady Alice Island]] in a fog.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 July 1908 |title=The last of the SS Muritai. Auckland Weekly News |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/185002 |access-date=2024-05-13 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> The second mate had his certificate suspended for 13 months, and he and the captain had to pay the costs of the enquiry.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 June 1908 |title=The Muritai inquiry. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19080624.2.17 |access-date=2024-05-13 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1972 the wreck site was named Muritai Point.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Muritai Point |url=https://gazetteer.linz.govt.nz/place/3884 |access-date=2024-05-13 |website=gazetteer.linz.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Nellie | |1906 - c1921 |Not known | |Motor launch used for towing.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Nellie |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000064 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083853/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000064 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-23}}</ref> |- |Ngapuhi |1900 |1900–1941 |[[Gourlay Brothers]], Dundee |691. 703 from 1922 |[[File:Ngapuhi_dining_saloon.jpg|thumb|''Ngapuhi'' dining saloon]]Steamer launched on 18 April 1900 and completed in June 1900, {{Convert|190|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|30|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|12.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with two sets of 3-cylinder (13, 21, 34 - 24)″ {{Convert|160|hp|abbr=on}} engines, driving twin screws,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer NGAPUHI built by Gourlay Brothers & Co. in 1900 for Northern Steam Ship Co. Ltd., Auckland, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?official_number=&imo=&builder=&builder_eng=&year_built=&launch_after=&launch_before=&role=&type_ref1=&propulsion=&owner=&port=&flag=&disposal=&lost=&ref=56174&vessel=NGAPUHI |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> built for NSS to replace ''Gairloch'' on the Onehunga-New Plymouth route,<ref name=":292">{{Cite web |date=12 October 1900 |title=Arrival of the Ngapuhi. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19001012.2.7 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> which she could cover in as little as 10 hours 35 minutes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 December 1900 |title=Taranaki Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TH19001201.2.8 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had 5 watertight bulkheads, polished ash, oak, mahogany and walnut panels, with New Zealand scenes etched on the glass windows.<ref name=":292" /> On 9 November 1904 she ran down the schooner ''Rimu'' on a hazy night. An inquiry found that the second officer should have kept a better lookout, but returned his certificate.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 November 1904 |title=Ngapuhi-Rimu collision. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19041119.2.20 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> From 1904<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 January 1904 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19040106.2.71.6 |access-date=2024-02-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to 1908 ''Ngapuhi'' was used each February for 8-day pleasure cruises from Auckland to [[Mangōnui]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 January 1908 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19080104.2.77.1 |access-date=2024-02-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1905 so many bookings were received that a second cruise was considered.<ref name=":172">{{Cite web |date=20 May 1905 |title=The Northern Steamship Company, Limited.
