New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19050520.2.8 |access-date=2024-02-05 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1909 her boiler was replaced.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 September 1909 |title=A big lift: unshipping a 50-ton boiler |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/153555/rec/91 |access-date=2024-05-09 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> In August 1912 ''Manaia'' replaced ''Ngapuhi'' on the Whangārei route and she served Tauranga, replacing ''Aupouri''.<ref name=":20" /> She took over the New Plymouth run from ''Rarawa'', until she was replaced by ''Hauturu'' from 3 May 1930.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 April 1930 |title=End of ferry service. Taranaki Daily News |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TDN19300423.2.65 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> After reduction of the New Plymouth service, she regularly ran in the [[Hauraki Gulf]], until converted to a hulk in 1930. In 1941 the Navy planned to use her as a mine-sweeper, but on 3 November 1944, the iron hull was beached in Boulder Bay, where her stern remained in 2014.<ref name=":332" /> |- |Ngatiawa |1906 |1906–1934 |D.J. Dunlop & Co., Port Glasgow |451 |[[File:Ngatiawa_aground_in_1920.jpg|thumb|''Ngatiawa'' aground in 1920]]Steamer built for NSS as a sister ship of ''Aupouri,''<ref name=":202" /> both able to carry 500 tons of cargo capacity, but only draw {{Convert|5.75|ft|abbr=on}} and planned to be {{Convert|152|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|27|ft|abbr=on}} wide and {{Convert|10.25|ft|abbr=on}} deep,<ref name=":1822"/> though actually {{Convert|152.5|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|27.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|10|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|55|hp|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":033" /> Launched on 23 May 1906, she was a passenger/cargo, steel ship, with twin-screws, driven by two 3-cylinder (8.5, 14 & 23.5 - 15)″ engines.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer NGATIAWA built by David J Dunlop & Co in 1906 for Northern Steamship Company Ltd., Auckland., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?year_built=&builder=&a1Order=Sorter_types&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=742&ref=6002&vessel=NGATIAWA |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> From 18 February to 7 March 1920 she was stuck<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 March 1920 |title=Ngatiawa refloated. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19200308.2.12 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> on the west side of [[Ōpōtiki]] bar.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1920 |title=Return of Wrecks and Casualties to Shipping reported to the Marine Department |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/appendix-to-the-journals-of-the-house-of-representatives/1920/I/2442 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She started a new twice weekly service to Port Waikato in March 1926,<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 March 1926 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19260331.2.60 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> then ran on several other routes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 September 1926 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19260920.2.13.1 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=30 December 1927 |title=Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19271230.2.24.5 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She hit a pier at Whitianga on 18 January 1928<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 February 1928 |title=Coromandel notes. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19280225.2.150 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and her last trip was probably from Great Barrier a week later.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 January 1928 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19280120.2.4.8?end_date=31-12-1928 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was laid up in Shoal Bay,<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 December 1931 |title=Idle shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19311230.2.110 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> then sold for scrap in August 1934.<ref name=":152" /> A pump has been preserved at the [[New Zealand Maritime Museum|Maritime Museum]] in Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Steam engine, SS NGATIAWA |url=https://collection.maritimemuseum.co.nz/objects/10476/steam-engine-ss-ngatiawa |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=New Zealand Maritime Museum |language=en}}</ref> |- |Ngunguru |1893 |1896–1903 |Wm. Holmes, Devonport |105 |[[File:Ngunguru_at_Whakatāne.jpg|thumb|''Ngunguru'' at [[Whakatāne]] in 1899]]Steamer of wood, {{Convert|91.2|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|17|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|6.7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|24|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":282" /> built to serve [[Ngunguru River|Ngunguru]] Colliery<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 January 1894 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18940127.2.16 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for T.H. Barwell & Partners, Auckland, 1895 sold to William Mitchell, Auckland and in 1896 to NSS. In July 1903 NSS sold ''Ngunguru'' to Taranaki Collieries Limited, Sydney for their [[Mōkau]] coal trade and bought ''Mangapapa'' from them.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 July 1903 |title=Town & country. Lyttelton Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/LT19030730.2.24 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1904 ''Ngunguru'' was sold to S.G. Hayward & H.M. Field, Nelson, in 1905 to H.L. Nathan, Wellington, in 1906 to Blenheim Steam Ship Company Ltd and in 1907 to Karamea Steam Ship Company Ltd, Wellington.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Ngunguru |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102264 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083749/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102264 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-26}}</ref> In September 1908 she was wrecked on Karamea River bar.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 September 1908 |title=Our shipping telegrams. Greymouth Evening Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GEST19080925.2.10 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her engines and her cargo of timber were salvaged.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 October 1908 |title=Shipping news. Greymouth Evening Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GEST19081005.2.6 |access-date=2024-02-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was replaced by the NSS ship for which she'd been exchanged in 1903, ''Mangapapa''.<ref name=":272" /> |- |[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|Nikau]] |1909 |1955–1956 |[[London and Glasgow Shipbuilding Company|Mackie & Thomson]], Govan |248 |Motor ship, built as a steamship for Anchor and converted to a motor ship in 1935. In 1954 she was sold to Kiwi Shipping Co., Auckland and in 1955 to NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Nikau |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1093994 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084139/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1093994 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-23}}</ref> She was launched on 6 April 1909, had twin-screws and was {{Convert|120.2|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|22.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide and {{Convert|7.3|ft|abbr=on}} deep. She had several accidents. On 26 November 1917 her propeller was damaged in Blind Bay, as it had been in French Pass on 25 June 1917, where she ran aground on 5 February 1931. On 18 March 1921 she was damaged in Cook Strait, on 16 November 1937 stranded in Queen Charlotte Sound and again at Motueka on 4 May 1938, shortly after her 9 April 1938 collision with ''Rangitoa'' at Nelson. She had a broken shaft off Westport on 6 February 1943. On 21 November 1944 she was aground off Cape Campbell.<ref>{{Cite web |date=January 2009 |title=P&O Ship Fact Sheet - Nikau |url=https://www.poheritage.com/Upload/Mimsy/Media/factsheet/93963NIKAU-1909pdf.pdf}}</ref> She was laid up in May 1931,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 June 1935 |title=Now a motor ship. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19350622.2.170 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> until, in 1935, her passenger accommodation was removed and her steam engine was replaced by two {{Convert|150|hp|abbr=on}} [[British Polar Engines|British Polar]] diesels,<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 October 1936 |title=Cook Strait "Ghost". Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19361028.2.48 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though an official test report refers to British Auxiliaries Ltd type M35E.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 February 1935 |title=Engine no E.190 (type M35E) : official test results |url=https://collection.nelsonmuseum.co.nz/objects/569/engine-no-e190-type-m35e-official-test-results |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=Nelson Provincial Museum |language=en}}</ref> Her diesel funnel was much shorter<ref>{{Cite web |date=1940s |title=Ship Nikau, Wellington Harbour |url=https://natlib.govt.nz/records/23198374 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> than her original one.<ref>{{Cite web |date=September 2011 |title=The white ghost of Cook Strait. Issue-3 On-Deck |url=http://www.mastermariners.org.au/images/stories/pdfs/NZCMM_3_On_Deck_Sept._2011._FO.pdf |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=Trove |language=en |archive-date=7 November 2011 |archive-url=https://webarchive.nla.gov.au/awa/20111107033630/http://pandora.nla.gov.au/pan/169994/20200724-0308/www.mastermariners.org.au/images/stories/pdfs/NZCMM_3_On_Deck_Sept._2011._FO.pdf |url-status=bot: unknown }}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> In 1954 she was chartered to NSS<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 February 1965 |title=WRECKED NIKAU Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19650227.2.231 |access-date=4 July 2023 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and bought in July 1955 to work Tauranga and Whakatāne routes,<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 July 1955 |title=Coastal vessel bought. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19550725.2.17 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> before being sold to [[Burns Philp]] (South Sea) Co. Ltd in July 1956.<ref name=":392">{{Cite web |date=22 November 1956 |title=Shipping. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19561122.2.200 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 5 December 1964 she was wrecked at Loasia Point, on [[Tanna (island)|Tanna Island]]'s<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer NIKAU built by Mackie & Thomson in 1909 for Anchor Shipping & Foundry Co, Nelson, New Zealand., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=100&ship_listPage=4&a1Order=yard_number&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=107&ref=12476&vessel=NIKAU |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> east coast,<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 February 1980 |title=Ship's propeller found at French Pass. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19800212.2.37 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> when being chartered by Athol Rusden.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 January 1965 |title=Trader wrecked at Tanna. Pacific islands monthly: Vol. 36, No. 1 |url=https://nla.gov.au/nla.obj-325373625 |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=Trove |language=en}}</ref> |- |Ohinemuri |1891 |1891–1904 |Wm. Holmes, North Shore |114 |[[File:SS_Ohinemuri.jpg|thumb|SS Ohinemuri working as a tug]]Steamer ''Ohinemuri'' was built of wood, {{Convert|89.1|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|18.4|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|6.9|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|30|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":033" /> built for NSS's Paeroa service to replace ''Te Aroha''. She was launched on 8 September 1891, rigged as a ketch, with 30 berths and a ladies' cabin. G. Fraser and Sons built the compound, surface-condensing engines, with 12″ and 24″ cylinders of 15″ stroke, fed by a tubular boiler at {{Convert|100|psi|abbr=on}} working pressure.<ref name=":722">{{Cite web |date=9 September 1891 |title=The Northern S.S. Co.'s fleet. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18910909.2.14 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was used as a tug between Kaipara and Onehunga,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 June 1914 |title=Captain James Grubb. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19140622.2.127 |access-date=2025-08-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and [[Whangapē Harbour|Whangapē]], often with the former 1865 [[barquentine]] ''Sarah and Mary'',<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 September 1906 |title=Raglan. Waikato Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WT19060910.2.20 |access-date=2025-08-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=23 January 1908 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19080123.2.24 |access-date=2025-08-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> after she was damaged in 1904.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 November 1904 |title=Ship in distress. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19041115.2.48 |access-date=2025-08-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1904 ''Ohinemuri'' was sold to Mitchelson Timber Company, then to John Harrison, [[Te Kōpuru|Aratapu]], in 1908 back to Mitchelson, in 1912 to Whangape Timber Company and in 1928 to W.G. Lovell, [[Napier, New Zealand|Napier]], where she was submerged and derelict after 1945.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Ohinemuri |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1094252 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083728/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1094252 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-02}}</ref> |- |Omana |1913 |1922–1939 |G.T. Niccol |83 |[[File:Omana_at_Auckland.jpg|thumb|''Omana'' at Auckland]]Steamer launched on 31 December 1913 by G.T. Niccol, for his Dargaville to [[Tangiteroria#History and culture|Tangiteroria]] service, with space for 200 passengers, wooden hull, Ross & Duncan triple expansion engine,<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 January 1914 |title=New steamer launched. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19140101.2.24 |access-date=2024-04-06 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> of {{Convert|14|hp|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|77.4|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|16.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide {{Convert|7.4|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name=":033" /> In 1922 she was sold to NSS to replace ''Waiuku'' to Waiheke, [[Browns Bay, New Zealand|Browns Bay]], [[Silverdale, Auckland|Silverdale]] (until April 1930, when the service ended due to road improvements),<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 November 2017 |title=Silverdale heritage design guide |url=https://www.aucklandcouncil.govt.nz/plans-projects-policies-reports-bylaws/our-plans-strategies/place-based-plans/Documents/silverdale-heritage-design-guide.pdf |website=Auckland Council |page=11}}</ref> Mahurangi and Matakana. On 15 October 1926 she got stuck on a reef at [[Tiritiri Matangi Island|Tiritiri Matangi]] in fog. ''Clansman'' towed her off.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 October 1926 |title=Steamer's mishap. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19261015.2.35 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1930 In 1937 ''Omana'' was withdrawn, her engine put in a new Bluff trawler, ''Torea'', in 1940, and the hull beached at [[Duders Point|Ngataringa Bay]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=The SS Omana at Browns Bay wharf |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/33163/ |access-date=2024-04-06 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> |- |Onehunga |1948 |1962–1968 |Uddevallavarvet Aktiebolaget, [[Uddevalla]] |914 |Motor ship built as ''Dragon'' for Rederi A/B Drabant (L. Ringborg Mgr) (Swedish). 1960 renamed ''Tullan''. Owned by Partredereit for M.S. Tullan (Lars Johansson Mgrs). 1962 sold to Kaipara S.S. Co. Ltd. (she was their last vessel). Managed by NSS,<ref name=":32">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Onehunga |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315713 |access-date=2024-04-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084156/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315713 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> who crewed her for the delivery voyage from [[Fowey]], via Tasmania.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 September 1962 |title=Onehunga arrives. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19620901.2.185 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1965 sold to Fletcher Industries Limited. Managed by NSS. 1968 June sold to Reef Shipping Company Ltd, Fiji. 1968 October renamed ''Jean Philippe''. 1973 sold to Keenan Shipping (Pte) Ltd, Singapore and renamed ''Tropic Winds''. 1974 sold to Southwind Shipping Company (Pte). 1976 sold to Majulah Nav. Snd. Bhd. (Malaysian). Renamed ''Tat Lee''. 16 November 1979 Lloyd's Class withdrawn at owners request.<ref name=":32" /> |- |Orewa |1898 |1908–1925 |[[Viaduct Harbour#Shipyards|Charles & W. Bailey]], Auckland |55 |[[File:Orewa.jpg|thumb|Orewa]]Steamer launched on 20 August 1898 for A. McGregor, Auckland, though the ship fell through the slipway and had to wait for high tide to complete the launch. She was built of kauri, {{Convert|70|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|16.5|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|7|ft|abbr=on}}, replaced ''[[SS Maori (1868)#Other ships named Maori|Maori]]'' on the Auckland-[[Silverdale, Auckland#History|Wade]]-Matakana route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 August 1898 |title=A new steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18980820.2.23.9 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1900 sold to W.A. McGregor,<ref name=":73">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Orewa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102310 |access-date=2024-03-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083934/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102310 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> 1905 sold to SSC. Joined NSS on the merger with SSC on 1 October 1908.<ref name=":310" /> 1925 sold to G.A. Wood, Hokitika. 1939 sold to H.A. Wood, Hokitika. 1941 sold to H.J. and N.H. Roderique, Bluff, mainly for an oyster trawler. 1947 H.J. became sole owner. May have become a storage hulk at Bluff.<ref name=":73" /> |- |Otimai |1921 |1921–1952 |G.T. Niccol, Freeman's Bay |207 |[[File:Otimai.jpg|thumb|''Otimai'']]Auxiliary schooner, with kauri hull,<ref>{{Cite web |date=1949 |title=Northern Steamship Co. |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/indexcards/id/162291/rec/30 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> twin {{Convert|80|hp|abbr=on}} [[William Beardmore and Company|Beardmore]] engines and a shallow draught for the east coast rivers, {{Convert|105|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|25|ft|abbr=on}} wide {{Convert|7.5|ft|abbr=on}} deep, built for NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 December 1921 |title=Late shipping. Evening Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ESD19211205.2.77 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The engines were replaced in 1938 with twin {{Convert|240|hp|abbr=on}} Fairbanks Morse diesels.<ref name=":31" /> 1948 laid up. 1952 used as a barge to unload ''Moldova'' at Auckland. 1958 hull sold to McKendrick Industries Limited, Auckland and broken up. Hull beached on [[Moturekareka Island]], inside ''Rewa''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Otimai |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1151348 |access-date=2024-04-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084024/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1151348 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Paeroa |1891 |1892–1917 |Charles Bailey, Auckland |91 |[[File:Paeroa_at_Whangamumu_in_1901.png|thumb|''Paeroa'' at Whangamumu in 1901]]From about 1890 H. Adams, of Thames ran a barge and launch between Paeroa and Te Aroha (previously abandoned when the [[Thames Branch|railway]] opened to Te Aroha), moving cargo brought from Auckland by ''ss Ruby'' and NSS's Paeroa-Thames boats. Gold, timber, flour and condensed milk traffics were all increasing. Hauraki Steamship Co., built ''Paeroa'' and in opposition to her NSS, which was trading between Auckland and Thames with ''Rotomahana'' and ''Enterprise'' and between Paeroa and Thames with the ''Patiki'', built ''Ohinemuri'' to run direct between Wharf Street, Paeroa and Auckland. The fight continued for about 8 months.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Te Aroha District Jubilee Committee |title=Te Aroha and the fortunate valley 1867-1930 |date=1930 |publisher=Te Aroha News}}</ref> Steamer built for Hauraki Steam Ship Company (HSSC) to run between Paeroa and Auckland, from 19 October 1891. On 19 November NSS put the ''Ohinemuri'' on,<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 November 1891 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18911120.2.11 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though a report said ''Ohinemuri'' was 15 minutes slower to [[Turua]] than the ''Paeroa''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 November 1891 |title=Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS18911121.2.6 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Competition reached the stage of an already reduced return fare of 6s.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 December 1891 |title=Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS18911203.2.4 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> being reduced, firstly to 2s.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 December 1891 |title=Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS18911231.2.13.3 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and later to a shilling,<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 February 1892 |title=Thames Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THA18920201.2.22.2 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=14 June 1892 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18920614.2.3.1 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for the trip of almost {{Convert|70|mi|abbr=on}} each way from [[Kopu, New Zealand|Kopu]] to Auckland. Competition ended in June 1892, when HSSC sold ''Paeroa'' to NSS and agreed not to compete on the Waihou River.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 June 1892 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18920614.2.36 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Paeroa'' was repainted in NSS colours on 16 June 1892<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 June 1892 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18920616.2.33 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and began serving Paeroa twice a week for NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 August 1892 |title=New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18920825.2.14 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> From April 1896 she, ''Waimarie'' and ''Ohinemuri'' provided a service most days. By then the return fare had risen to 15s.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 April 1896 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18960416.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was replaced by ''Taniwha'' in 1898.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 April 1898 |title=Ohinemuri Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/OG18980420.2.12.1 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>[[File:Paeroa_on_Hokitika_beach_in_1920.png|thumb|''Paeroa'' on Hokitika beach in 1920]]''Paeroa'' was then used on several other routes, including [[Marsden Point]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 June 1915 |title=Port of Auckland. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19150602.2.23 |access-date=2024-01-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> [[Ōhiwa]], [[Whakaari / White Island]] and Tauranga<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 October 1914 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19141014.2.15 |access-date=2024-01-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> [[Cape Brett Peninsula#Whangamumu|Whangamumu]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 July 1901 |title=Whaling in the north. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19010719.2.22 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Cabbage Bay (Colville),<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 March 1915 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19150301.2.30 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> [[Mangōnui]], Awanui and [[Waiharara]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 June 1901 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19010613.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> [[Whangaruru]], Helena Bay, [[Tutukaka]] and [[Whananaki]] and [[Whangapoua Harbour|Whangapoua]] and [[Moehau Range#Port Charles|Port Charles]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 January 1904 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19040109.2.3.2 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In October 1917 she was sold to Hokitika Shipping Company Limited for £1,000.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 July 1920 |title=A total wreck. Grey River Argus |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GRA19200726.2.14 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had 15 berths in her main saloon and 6 in a ladies cabin,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 October 1917 |title=Purchase of the S.S. Paeroa. Hokitika Guardian |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HOG19171015.2.3 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> was built of kauri<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 September 1891 |title=Our shipbuilding. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18910905.2.31 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and {{Convert|87.1|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|16.2|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|6.1|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with a {{Convert|25|hp|abbr=on}}<ref name=":282" /> A & G Price compound engine.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 June 1891 |title=Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS18910629.2.8 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> From 1917 she ran to west coast ports and Wellington<ref>{{Cite web |title=S.S. Paeroa. Hokitika Guardian |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HOG19200127.2.3 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and also ran multi-day excursions with 20 passengers<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 December 1917 |title=Hokitika Guardian |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HOG19171220.2.30.2 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to [[Milford Sound]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 August 1920 |title=Milford Sound, with Mitre Peak on the left. In the right foreground is the ss. Paeroa, since stranded at Hokitika. Otago Witness |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/OW19200824.2.112.3 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and other fiords.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 January 1920 |title=Sounds excursion. Hokitika Guardian |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HOG19200108.2.3 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 23 July 1920 she lost her [[sternpost]] and rudder on the [[Hokitika#Shipping|Hokitika Bar]] and was beached just to the north.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 July 1920 |title=Steamer Paeroa beached. Nelson Evening Mail |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NEM19200724.2.19 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> At high tide she was buffeted by the waves.<ref>{{Cite web |title=PAEROA |url=https://hocken.recollect.co.nz/nodes/view/30244 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=Hocken Digital Collections}}</ref> The shipping company bought the wreck from the underwriters for £105, hoping to recover the engines.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 July 1920 |title=The Paeroa. Greymouth Evening Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GEST19200728.2.25 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Paroto |1914 |1914–1948 |G.T. Niccol |109 |[[File:Paroto.jpg|thumb|''Paroto'' washed ashore in a storm]]Auxiliary scow,<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 October 1914 |title=Port of Auckland. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19141030.2.30 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ketch, or schooner, launched for NSS, with twin {{Convert|60|hp|abbr=on}} Beardmore engines and a shallow draught for the [[Ōhiwa]], Whakatāne and Ōpōtiki bars,<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 June 1914 |title=Shipping. Otago Daily Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19140604.2.25 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|87.3|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|23.3|ft|abbr=on}} wide {{Convert|6.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep.<ref name=":033" /> Due to wartime delays, she wasn't completed until 1915.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 May 1915 |title=Northern Steamship Co. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19150526.2.26 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 7 August 1929 her anchor cable broke in a storm and she was blown onto the beach at [[Whakaari / White Island|Whakaari-White Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 August 1929 |title=Paroto ashore at White Island. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19290808.2.13 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was towed to Auckland for repairs.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 May 1930 |title=Northern SS Co. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19300523.2.36 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> July 1948 sold to Richardson & Company, Napier, insulated and re-engined.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 July 1948 |title=Ship bought for Tokomaru Bay meat transport. Gisborne Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GISH19480721.2.69 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1949 sold to [[Collingwood, New Zealand|Collingwood]] Shipping Company, Nelson, which was bought by Inter-Island Shipping Company in 1950 and converted to a pure motorship,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Paroto |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136852 |access-date=2024-04-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084012/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136852 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> with a short funnel and only one mast.<ref>{{Cite web |date= |title=Paroto, Wellington, about 1951 |url=https://natlib.govt.nz/records/23248381 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1956 she carried scrapped rails from [[Wellington tramway system|Wellington tramways]] to load aboard ''Matangi'' and ''[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|Totara]]''<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 November 1956 |title=Coasters sold for scrap. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19561120.2.57 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at Whakatahuri<ref>{{Cite web |date=November 1956 |title=Totara and Paroto at a ship-breakers yard in Whakatahuri |url=https://natlib.govt.nz/records/22661754 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> (in Forsyth Bay, south of [[Bird Island (Marlborough)|Bird Island]]).<ref>{{Cite web |title=Whakatahuri, Marlborough |url=https://www.topomap.co.nz/NZTopoMap/nz42491/Whakatahuri/ |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=NZ Topo Map |language=en}}</ref> In 1958 she was first to call at the reopened port of [[Kaiapoi]]. In 1960 ownership transferred to Inter Island Shipping Company.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kaiapoi Ships {{!}} Paroto |url=https://www.kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz/Kaiapoi-Ships/lc/4317/Paroto |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=www.kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz}}</ref> In 1961 she was again rebuilt and re-engined. On 3 August 1966 she ran aground in fog at Point Gibson, south of [[Gore Bay, New Zealand|Gore Bay]], and was sold for scrap,<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 August 1966 |title=Paroto tender. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19660812.2.187 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though the breakers had problems.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 August 1966 |title=Salvaging Paroto. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19660817.2.68 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> A storm in 1968 broke the remains in two<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 August 1969 |title=The remains of the Paroto. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19690808.2.90 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and, in 1978, they wrecked slipways at [[Cheviot, New Zealand#Port Robinson|Port Robinson]] during a storm.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 June 1978 |title=Volunteers battle heavy seas. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19780626.2.12 |access-date=2024-04-10 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Patiki |1881 |1893–1901 |[[A & G Price]] |59 |[[File:P.S._Patiki_at_Paeroa.jpg|thumb|''P.S. Patiki'' at Paeroa]]Paddle steamer with a {{Convert|15|hp|abbr=on}}, non condensing engine,<ref name=":323" /> built for Thames River Steam Navigation Co. Ltd. (formed in 1882, when ''Te Aroha'' was also bought), costing £2,220.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 January 1882 |title=The Thames River Steam Navigation Company. Thames Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THA18820119.2.12 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The company sold to NSS on 1 August 1893,<ref name=":822"/> when she had a comprehensive overhaul by A & G Price.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 October 1893 |title=Thames Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THA18931018.2.4 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In October 1901 ''Taniwha'' sank ''Patiki''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Patiki |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1082693 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083743/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1082693 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-01}}</ref> at night on a bend of the river; the company's give way rules were found to be confusing.<ref name=":922">{{Cite web |date=26 November 1901 |title=The Taniwha-Patiki collision case. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19011126.2.43 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Patiki'' was then converted to an unpowered barge.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 October 1901 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19011025.2.38 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Pioneer |1896 |1896 - post 1940 |[[Logan Brothers]] | |Tender, built for NSS to service hulks on Auckland Harbour. 1940 last reported date.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Pioneer |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000058 |access-date=2024-03-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083752/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000058 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Pono |1913 |1913–1947 |G.T. Niccol |56 |[[File:Pono_at_Auckland.jpg|thumb|Pono at Auckland in 1946]]Auxiliary schooner launched in December 1913 for NSS, {{Convert|60|hp|abbr=on}}, 1 screw, {{Convert|7|knot|abbr=}}<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 December 1913 |title=Shipping. Gisborne Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GIST19131218.2.4 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|67.4|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|19.7|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|5.9|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":033" /> On 13 November 1926 she ran aground at Kirita Bay, near [[Manaia, Waikato|Manaia]].<ref name=":36" /> On 30 March 1947 she was the last ship to serve Thames and Ngatea. She was then laid up for sale.<ref name=":37">{{Cite web |date=29 March 1947 |title=Sea links to be severed. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19470329.2.23 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1948 sold to H.K.H. Anderson, Auckland and dismantled.<ref name=":36">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Pono |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133286 |access-date=2024-04-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084003/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133286 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Poranui |1956 |1956–1969 |J. Bodewes, Hoogezand |892 |[[File:StateLibQld_1_140351_Poranui_(ship).jpg|right|frameless]]Motor ship launched on 14 July 1956 for NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 July 1956 |title=Dutch built ship. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19560731.2.125 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was delivered via [[Panama Canal|Panama]], rather than [[Suez Crisis|Suez]], because of the conflict there.<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 January 1957 |title=Shipping. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19570119.2.28 |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She also visited England, Genoa and Sfax, before averaging {{Convert|10.7|knot|abbr=}} across the Pacific, to arrive in Whangarei on 4 January 1957. She was {{Convert|209|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|32|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 January 1957 |title=New ship for NZ trade. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19570105.2.38 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In October 1969 she was sold to A.R. Rusden for almost $100,000,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pacific islands monthly Vol. 41, No. 3 (Mar. 1, 1970) |url=https://nla.gov.au/nla.obj-331795624 |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=Trove |language=en}}</ref> but a month later, on 17 November,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pacific islands monthly Vol. 41, No. 1 (Jan. 1, 1970) |url=https://nla.gov.au/nla.obj-331795077 |access-date=2024-03-03 |website=Trove |language=en}}</ref> she was wrecked without loss of life on [[:fr:Récif Jouan|Jouan Reef]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Poranui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1199084 |access-date=2024-03-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084142/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1199084 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> though attempts to save her continued until February.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 February 1970 |title=Poranui Now Abandoned. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19700216.2.179 |access-date=2025-07-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Pukeko | |1890?–1910<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pukeko |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000055 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083715/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000055 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> |Not known | |''Pukeko'' may have been an unofficial name for a new launch at Whakatāne in 1907.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 January 1907 |title=Gisborne Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GIST19070108.2.12 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Ranginui |1936 |1937–1957 |[[Bowling, West Dunbartonshire#Shipbuilding in Bowling|Scott & Sons, Bowling]] |158 |Steel motor coaster, launched on 9 April 1936 for G.T. Niccol, {{Convert|106.7|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|22.1|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|6|ft|abbr=on}}, 2 x {{Convert|133|hp|abbr=on}} 2S.C.SA, 5 cylinder engines by Fairbanks Morse, 2 screws,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ranginui |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=50&year_built=&builder=&a1Order=Sorter_name&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=613&ref=17208&vessel=RANGINUI |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=Clyde Ships}}</ref> driving her at up to {{Convert|9|knot|abbr=}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 October 1936 |title=Long voyage. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19361003.2.70 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> on 22 February 1937 she was sold to NSS to serve Whangārei-Auckland-Tauranga,<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 February 1937 |title=New vessel. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19370223.2.13 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> November 1957 to Collingwood Shipping Co<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 November 1957 |title=Sale of coaster. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19571129.2.204 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for Wellington–Lyttelton trips and, in November 1958, with ''Paroto'', Kaiapoi-Wellington.<ref name=":58">{{Cite web |title=Kaiapoi Ships {{!}} Ranginui |url=https://kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz/Kaiapoi-Ships/lc/4319/lcv/s/Ranginui |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz}}</ref> In May<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 May 1960 |title=Ship aground at Kaiapoi. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19600514.2.94 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and September 1960 she stuck on Kaiapoi bar.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 September 1960 |title=Ranginui on sandspit. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19600901.2.163 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In November 1967 she began with her final owner, [[Tim Wallis|Tim Wallace]], of Luggate Game Packers, [[Wānaka]], with a helipad added for venison export from [[Fiordland]]. She was laid up at Port Chalmers in 1968 and returned to Kaiapoi. In August 1972 Carribbean Pacific Enterprise planned to convert her to a frigate, but didn't leave Kaiapoi untilold to Alpine Helicopters in 1976, when she returned to Fiordland, had a refit at Bluff in 1986 and later provided recreational accommodation for the Wallace family.<ref name=":58" /> On 8 May 1995 she sank in {{Convert|50|m|abbr=on}} at an unattended [[Breaksea Sound|Te Puaitaha / Breaksea Sound]] mooring,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ranginui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=5290155 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084102/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=5290155 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-22 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> where she was photographed in 2008.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2008 |title=Wreck of the Ranginui |url=https://ianskipworth.com/photo/d70uw/d701187/DSC_0017.jpg}}</ref> |- |Rangitoto |1913 |1926–1940 |Murdoch & Murray, Port Glasgow |585 |[[File:Rangitoto_steamer.jpg|thumb|''Rangitoto'']]Steamer launched on 23 July 1913 for the [[Amazon River]], but became HMS ''Ant'', a [[Auxiliary ship#Replenishment|Naval Auxiliary Tender]] at [[Southampton]], {{Convert|164.5|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|33.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.3|ft|abbr=on}} deep, single screw, {{Convert|100|hp|abbr=on}}, 3-cylinder (15, 25 & 40 - 24)″ engine by Muir & Houston Ltd, Glasgow,<ref name=":11">{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer ANT built by Murdoch & Murray in 1913 for The Admiralty, Naval Auxiliary |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?official_numb=&a1Order=Sorter_name&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=4075&ref=16649&vessel=ANT |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> driving her at {{Convert|12|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 May 1926 |title=Coastal shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19260508.2.105 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1924 brought to Auckland via Suez for excursion work by George Turnbull Niccol and renamed ''Rangitoto''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 October 1924 |title=Rangitoto for Auckland. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19241028.2.97 |access-date=2024-03-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1925 sold to NSS. 1937 laid up as the bottom plates had worn thin. 1940 taken over by N.Z.Navy as a [[Battery ship|Battery Ship]].<ref name=":11" /> In 1941,<ref name=":13">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rangitoto |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1146997 |access-date=2024-03-24 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084044/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1146997 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> or 1942,<ref name=":11" /> she was beached at Ngataringa Bay. Engine and boiler removed for use in [[Minesweepers of the Royal New Zealand Navy#Castle class|minesweeper]] ''Manuka''.<ref name=":13" /> In 1965 ''Rangitoto'' was used in demolition exercises and in 1968 broken up.<ref name=":11" /> |- |Rarawa |1903 |1903–1940 |Gourlay Brothers, Dundee |1072 |[[File:Rarawa.jpg|thumb|''Rarawa'']]Steamer built of [[Open hearth furnace#History|Siemen's Martin steel]], launched on 30 April 1903<ref name=":28">{{Cite web |date=30 September 1903 |title=The Northern Company's new steamer. Taranaki Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TH19030930.2.8 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and delivered via Port Said, [[Colombo]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 August 1903 |title=The Northern Company's new steamer. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19030810.2.27 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and [[Fremantle]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 September 1903 |title=The Rarawa. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19030911.2.36.9 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to Auckland, where she was cleaned and repainted, before going on to Onehunga, via Whangārei, Russell and Hokianga,<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 October 1903 |title=The SS Rarawa. NZ Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19031016.2.24 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> first visiting New Plymouth on 19 October 1903.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 October 1903 |title=The Northern SS Co's new steamer. Taranaki Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TH19031020.2.43 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was rigged as a schooner for NSS's Onehunga-New Plymouth route, {{Convert|210|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|31|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|14|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with 5 watertight bulkheads, water ballast tanks fore and aft for [[Draft (hull)#Of a ship|trimming]], [[bilge keel]]s to minimise rolling, berths for 142 first and 48 second-class passengers, polished oak, mahogany, [[sycamore]], [[teak]] and walnut panels, stained glass windows and steel masts for lifting heavy loads. She had two sets of [[Triple-expansion engine|triple expansion]], 3-cylinder (14, 23, 37 - 24)″, [[Surface condenser|surface condensing]] engines, fed by two [[Scotch marine boiler|boilers]], fired by six corrugated furnaces to drive her at {{Convert|13.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":28" /> She was an enlarged and improved version of ''Ngapuhi''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 September 1903 |title=Northern Steamship Company. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19030925.2.43 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1929 she was laid up in Shoal Bay, until it was decided to scrap her in 1937.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 June 1937 |title=Steamers' end. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370601.2.42 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1941 engines and internal fittings were removed and installed in the mine-sweepers ''[[HMNZS Rimu|Rimu]]'' and ''[[HMNZS Hinau (T17)|Hinau]]'' by [[Viaduct Harbour#Shipyards|Senior Foundry and Shipbuilders Ltd]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 September 1941 |title=Naval trawler. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410910.2.85 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Rarawa'' was stripped, filled with concrete and beached west of Boulder Bay in March 1941,<ref name=":332" /> or on 24 December 1940.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rarawa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115207 |access-date=2024-04-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083831/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115207 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer RARAWA built by Gourlay Brothers & Co. in 1903 for Northern Steamship Co. Ltd., Auckland., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=100&ship_listOrder=Sorter_types&ship_listDir=ASC&a1Order=Sorter_types&a1Dir=ASC&a1Page=355&ref=56188&vessel=RARAWA |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> |- |Ratanui |1946 |1952–1956 |[[Davie Shipbuilding|George T. Davie & Sons]], [[Ottawa]] |515 |Motor ship built as ''Ottawa Mayspring'' for Canadian Government, under control of General Timber Products of Quebec. Completed as ''Mayspring''. 1948 sold to Danish owners Arrenak I/S (S.A. Thomasen managers) and named ''Arrenak''. 1952 sold to NSS and renamed ''Ratanui''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Ratanui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1191805 |access-date=2024-03-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084130/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1191805 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> She had 2 masts and her engines were aft.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ratanui |url=https://www.ultimatenzsoccer.com/Onehunga/ratanui.htm |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=www.ultimatenzsoccer.com}}</ref> In 1952 ''Hauturu'' was too small for the Lyttelton route, so ''Ratanui'' replaced her,<ref name=":382" /> On 30 November 1956 ''Ratanui'' left for Suva after sale to Burns Philp (South Sea) Co. Ltd, who had bought ''Nikau'' in July 1956.<ref name=":392" /> In she was 1970 sold to Rusden & Sofrana (Fiji) Ltd, Suva.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Burns Philp & Co. |url=https://www.theshipslist.com/ships/lines/burnsphilp.shtml |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=www.theshipslist.com}}</ref> She may have been broken up at [[Madang]] in 1971,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2009-03-01 |title=Ratanui |url=https://www.shipsnostalgia.com/media/ratanui.37286/ |access-date=2024-03-17 |website=Ships Nostalgia |language=en-US}}</ref> though in January that year she was advertised for sale at Madang, "as is, where is"<ref>{{Cite news |date=1971-01-21 |title=Advertising |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article250346539 |access-date=2024-03-17 |work=Papua New Guinea Post-Courier}}</ref> and in 1977 an advert invited tenders for removal of the wreck of ''Ratanui'', lying in about {{Convert|30|ft|abbr=on}} of water on the eastern shore of Binnen Harbor at Madang.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1977-04-25 |title=Advertising |url=http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article251051809 |access-date=2024-03-17 |work=Papua New Guinea Post-Courier}}</ref> |- |Rimu |1898 |1908–1932 |[[Preston Dock#Ship building|Allsup & Co]]., [[Preston, Lancashire|Preston]] |412 |[[File:SS_Rimu.jpg|thumb|''Rimu'']]Steamer launched on 14 October 1898 for [[Keith Ramsay|Keith Ramsey]], A. D. Campbell, J. Mill and Captain Sundstrum, Dunedin, to carry up to 45 passengers along the south coast.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 November 1898 |title=NEW STEAMER FOR THE INVERCARGILL TRADE. EVENING STAR |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ESD18981126.2.22 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|142.6|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|24|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|9.6|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with {{Convert|95|hp|abbr=on}} twin triple expansion (10.25, 16, 28 x 22)″, driving twin screws<ref name=":29">{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer RIMU built by Allsup & Co. Ltd. in 1898 for Keith Ramsay, Dunedin, Coaster |url=https://shippingandshipbuilding.uk/view.php?builder=10560&a1Page=2&ref=202081&vessel=RIMU |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=shippingandshipbuilding.uk}}</ref> at up to {{Convert|11|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 March 1899 |title=THE RIMU'S EXCURSION. OTAGO DAILY TIMES |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT18990311.2.4 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She left Preston on 14 December and arrived at Port Chalmers on 25 February 1899 via [[Holyhead]], [[Malta (island)|Malta]], Port Said, [[Aden]], Colombo and [[Albany, Western Australia|Albany]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 February 1899 |title=ARRIVAL OF THE S.S. RIMU. OTAGO DAILY TIMES |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT18990227.2.8 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1907 she was chartered by Union to run the Hobart-Strahan-Melbourne route after an accident.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 November 1907 |title=Shipping. OTAGO DAILY TIMES |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19071108.2.17 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In April 1908 Invercargill Shipping Co. Ltd, Dunedin sold ''Rimu'' to NSS.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 April 1908 |title=SALE OF THE RIMU. EVENING POST |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/EP19080425.2.18 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1914, to meet demand for improved passenger accommodation between Onehunga, Raglan, Kawhia and Hokianga, electric light and 26 two-berth cabins were added,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 July 1914 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19140715.2.19 |access-date=2024-12-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> providing for 54 saloon passengers ''Rimu'' took over the route from ''Claymore''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 November 1914 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19141107.2.10 |access-date=2024-12-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1923 she grounded on a mudbank in Manukau Harbour, on her way from Raglan and Kawhia.<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 January 1923 |title=The Rimu refloated. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19230127.2.74 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She also served Hokianga, but could be delayed over a week by storms.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 July 1924 |title=Rimu's prolonged trip. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19240702.2.34 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On her last overhaul, there was concern about her hull and her engine<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 June 1926 |title=Local and general. Kawhia Settler and Raglan Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/KSRA19260604.2.8 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and her final trip was on 22 December 1926, when she left Kawhia and Raglan for Onehunga, Hokianga<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 December 1926 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19261224.2.19 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and Auckland, arriving there on 26 December,<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 December 1926 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19261227.2.24 |access-date=2024-04-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to be laid up. 1932 dismantled at Auckland and, on 10 November 1932, her hull was beached on [[Rotoroa Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rimu |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1105235 |access-date=2024-04-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083937/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1105235 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref><ref name=":29" /> |- |Ronaki |1922 |1929–1943 |G.T.Niccol |255 |[[File:Ronaki.jpg|thumb|''Ronaki'']]Auxiliary schooner, {{Convert|112|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|27|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|8.5|ft|abbr=on}}, 2 x {{Convert|135|hp|abbr=on}} Bolinder engines, using crude oil, {{Convert|9|knot|abbr=}}, 350 tons cargo, 10 crew, built for [[Portland, New Zealand|Wilson Portland Cement Company]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 June 1922 |title=A new schooner. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19220630.2.48 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1929 sold to NSS to end passenger services to Tauranga and replace ''Ngapuhi''. In 1930 she was replaced by ''Waka''<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 March 1930 |title=Will replace Ronaki. Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19300329.2.138 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and took over services from Onehunga to Hokianga (until 1941),<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 March 1941 |title=Ronaki Withdrawn From Hokianga Trade. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19410305.2.42 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Port Waikato, Raglan and Kawhia.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 December 1933 |title=Port of Onehunga. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19331218.2.26.14 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1941, en route from Kawhia to South Island, an engine failed and she used a sail to reach New Plymouth, but was described as "not built for sailing".<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 April 1941 |title=Struggle to port. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19410423.2.45 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1943 sold to government and used as a US Navy storeship. 18 June 1943 wrecked [[Norfolk Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Ronaki |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1151360 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084053/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1151360 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> |- |Rosalie |1945<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 November 1945 |title=Unwanted craft. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19451122.2.36 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |1946 |Shipbuilders Limited and Seagar Brothers Limited. |239 |Motor ship built in Auckland for New Zealand Marine Department named YF-1048, renamed ''Rosalie''.15 May 1946 to 15 Nov 1946 chartered by NSS<ref name=":49" /> to replace Clansman on the Onehunga-Picton route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 May 1946 |title=New cargo lighter to replace Clansman. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19460520.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 15 May 1946 cargo fertilizer to Whangārei and cement on return. 17 May 1946 towed barge to Tauranga. 22 May 1946 relieved ''Hauturu'' on Onehunga-Raglan-Kawhia run. 31 May 1946 Auckland to Picton. 14 June 1946 Onehunga from Picton (salt and general) via New Plymouth (wool). 2 July 1946 to Kawhia, New Plymouth and Whanganui.<ref name=":49" /> August 1947 sold to Union Manufacturing & Export to run to Norfolk Island and [[New Caledonia]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 August 1947 |title=Sale of surplus vessels. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19470812.2.21 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> April 1958 sold to A. Mouledous and Societe Liemann Mouledous, March 1961 to Capt. E Savoie and renamed ''Sorana del Mar''. May 1961 to Captain A R Rusden, [[Port Vila]]. July 1962 stranded on mudbank in [[Vanuatu]]. Refloated by ''Nikau''. 14 July 1964, off [[Espiritu Santo]], burnt to the waterline due to petroleum drums.<ref name=":49">{{Cite web |title=Rosalie |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171446 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084117/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1171446 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-20 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> |- |Rose Ann |1875 |1892-1896 |Matthew Sims, Onehunga | |A [[Cutter (boat)|cutter]] named ''Rose Ann'' was at Onehunga in 1875<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 October 1875 |title=Additional particulars. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18751027.2.16.2 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was said to have been built by Sims a few months earlier.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 November 1875 |title=Exciting cutter race. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18751102.2.13 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1885 ''Rose Ann'' was sold<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 February 1885 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18850228.2.77.3 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to A D Bennett<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 July 1885 |title=Violent gale in Auckland. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18850707.2.16 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and to NSS in July 1892<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 July 1892 |title=Port of Onehunga. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18920707.2.13.10 |access-date=2025-07-25 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> as a tender to ''Glenelg'' at Raglan, to collect cargo from harbour landings.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 May 1893 |title=The Northern Steamship Company Limited. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18930506.2.67 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She made several trips to [[Waingaro#Waingaro Landing|Waingaro]] landing for wool.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 December 1893 |title=Country news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18931218.2.62 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1893 she struck a reef at Waingaro<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 August 1893 |title=Raglan. Waikato Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WT18930812.2.23 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was repaired at Raglan and Onehunga.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rose Ann |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000056 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083734/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000056 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> In August 1896 she was returned to Onehunga,<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 August 1896 |title=Departures. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18960812.2.15.16 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> advertised for sale in November by Sims, Onehunga<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 November 1896 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18961116.2.50.2 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and available for hire there in December.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 December 1896 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18961228.2.3.4 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1898 she moved to Waitara<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 November 1898 |title=Port of Waitara. Taranaki Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TH18981119.2.3.2 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and in June 1899 was wrecked at [[Awakino]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 June 1899 |title=Wreck of a cutter. Waikato Argus |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WAIGUS18990617.2.26 |access-date=2025-07-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Rothesay |1904 |1907 - c1925 |[[Viaduct Harbour#Shipyards|Seagar Bros]], Auckland |17 |[[File:SS_Rothesay_1904.png|right|frameless]]Steamer {{Convert|52|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|11|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|4|ft|abbr=on}} deep, built for Stewart & Williams and used on Tauranga-[[Katikati]] service. Bought by NSS in 1907<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rothesay |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000066 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083857/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000066 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> and used until at least 1925.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1925-1926 by Australian National Maritime Museum |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__4405258486f263 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> |- |Rotokohu |1901 |1903–1939 |Savage, [[Ōpōtiki]] |15 |[[File:Rotokohu.jpg|thumb|''Rotokohu'']]Waihou River steam launch ''Rotokohu'' was built as ''Clematis'', with a hull of [[Agathis|kauri]] and engines by [[A & G Price]].<ref name=":1222">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rotokohu |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000062 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083834/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000062 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> In July 1907, due to increased custom, NSS put her on the Paeroa-[[Te Aroha]] route as ''Rotokohu'', a larger, 30 passenger, steamer. The trip up the [[Waihou River]] from Paeroa took 6{{frac|1|2}} hours, rather than 10 hours by ''Matuku''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 July 1907 |title=Te Aroha News |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TAN19070706.2.7 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was able to tow 80 tons of cargo in a barge.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 April 1924 |title=River traffic. Hauraki Plains Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HPGAZ19240430.2.8 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1938 it was decided she was a tug, rather than a launch, entitling her crew to an extra 10s a week.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 November 1938 |title=Tug or launch? Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS19381101.2.31 |access-date=2024-01-26 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In January 1939 she was offered for sale<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 January 1939 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390118.2.9.4 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and sold for £80 to Harold Fogden of Auckland.<ref name=":1222" /> |- |[[SS Rotomahana (1876)|Rotomahana]] |1876 |1890–1917 |Fraser & Tinne |183 |Steamer sold to NSS in 1890 for the Thames run. 1896 Transferred to the Coromandel run. 1917 broken up at Auckland and sold to McCallum Bros, who sank her to form a wharf at [[Pakihi Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rotomahana |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1075119 |access-date=2024-03-18 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083718/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1075119 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Rowena |1872 |1881–1890 |H. Niccol, Devonport |108 |[[File:Rowena.jpg|thumb|''Rowena'']]Steamer ''Rowena'' was launched on 24 August 1872, though she came to a halt about half way down the slip and had to wait for the next tide.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 August 1872 |title=Launch. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18720824.2.3.4 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had a {{Convert|30|hp|abbr=on}} compound engine,<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 August 1881 |title=Marine Department, Annual Report |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/parliamentary/AJHR1881-I.2.2.4.37 |access-date=2024-06-30 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> was {{Convert|101.6|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|17.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide x {{Convert|6.6|ft|abbr=on}} deep and, in 1872, registered in Auckland by D B Cruikshank.<ref name=":62">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1876-1877 by Australian National Maritime Museum - Issuu |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__23923b355fb6b5 |access-date=2023-07-15 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> She was built for D.A. Cruikshank, James S. Macfarlane, W.A. McGregor and partner of Auckland and, in 1881, she was among the first in the NSS fleet. 1890 sold to J.H. Ross, 1891 to J. McClelland, 1894 to H. Hayes, 1895 to C.T. Roberts, 1895 to J.H. Williams, 1900 to Wellington Steam Ferry Co. Ltd, 1902 to Westport Coal Co. Ltd. 1906 broken up at Timaru.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Rowena |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1066535 |access-date=1 July 2024}}</ref> |- |Seaway Princess |1967 |1967–1969 |[[Hong Kong and Whampoa Dock|Hong Kong and Whampoa Dock Company]] |1106 |Motor ship for the new NSS ro-ro service (mentioned above), launched on 19 September 1967. She was {{Convert|81.22|m|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|13.1|m|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.38|m|abbr=on}} deep, with {{Convert|1680|hp|abbr=on}} British Polar engines, driving a single screw<ref name=":302">{{Cite web |date=December 2009 |title=P&O Ship Fact Sheet Seaway Princess |url=https://www.poheritage.com/Upload/Mimsy/Media/factsheet/94448SEAWAY-PRINCESS-1967pdf.pdf}}</ref> at up to {{Convert|13.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 December 1967 |title=New cargo freighter. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19671202.2.225 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She arrived at Auckland in January 1968<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 January 1968 |title=The Seaway Princess, the Northern Steamship Company's latest vessel, which is due at Auckland from Hong Kong (where she was built) on January 25. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19680117.2.144 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and could carry 68 [[Wiktionary:cargon|cargon]] pallets of 16 to 28 tons. Her first voyage was delayed about a week by a dispute over staffing of the [[Ports of Auckland#Port of Auckland|Fergusson Wharf]] [[linkspan]] at Auckland<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 March 1968 |title=Dispute Settled. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19680321.2.206 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and it wasn't until February 1968 that she had a suitable terminal at Lyttelton.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 April 1968 |title=South Island terminal for Seaway Princess. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19680423.2.169 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In March she was laid up for 6 weeks for engine repairs.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 May 1968 |title=Ship given 'a good test'. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19680501.2.214 |access-date=2024-03-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 22 December 1969 she was sold to Holm & Co and renamed ''Holmlea'' on 13 April 1970, but negotiations delayed a Lyttelton-Wellington service on charter to Union<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 May 1970 |title=Holmlea Waiting To Start New Service. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19700521.2.7 |access-date=2024-03-04 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> until 5 June 1970.<ref name=":302" /> In February 1975 she was sold to a shipping company at [[Messina]], renamed ''Annarita Seconda'' and left Wellington on 8 March. In 1978 she was renamed ''Gabbiano''<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Seaway Princess |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1332169 |access-date=2024-03-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084202/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1332169 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> and in 1983 sold to a [[Genoa]]n company. On 2 November 1989 shipbreakers Eurofer SpA started work on her at [[San Giorgio di Nogaro]].<ref name=":302" /> |- |[[Senator (1848 ship)|Senator]] |1848 |Not known - 1906<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 May 1906 |title=Northern Steamship Co. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19060519.2.59 |access-date=2024-01-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |William Hoile Brown, Auckland |1012 |Coal hulk |- |Staffa |1876 |1881–1897 |Thomas Niccol, Devonport |80 |[[File:SS_Staffa.jpg|thumb|Staffa and Captain Baker - drowned 1883]]Steamer {{Convert|75|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|16|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|8|ft|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 April 1876 |title=The arrival of the SS Staffa. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18760408.2.4 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> built for A. McGregor and passed to NSS on its formation in 1881.<ref name=":57">{{Cite web |title=Staffa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1070365 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083650/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1070365 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> A Fraser & Tinne, {{Convert|70|psi|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|20|in|abbr=on}} single cylinder engine drove her at up to {{Convert|9|knot|abbr=on}} on her trial trip on 30 March .<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 April 1876 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18760401.2.3 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> From April 1876<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 March 1876 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18760329.2.2.1 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> she served the Bay of Plenty,<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 May 1876 |title=Tauranga. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18760518.2.19.2 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> as a feeder from Ōpōtiki to ''Rowena's'' Tauranga-Auckland route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 May 1876 |title=Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18760520.2.5 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had trouble with Ōpōtiki bar, being stuck on it in January 1883.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 January 1883 |title=The Staffa on the Bar. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18830124.2.17.8 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 6 February 1883, her captain was drowned whilst checking the depth of the bar in a small boat.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 March 1883 |title=Inquest at Opotiki. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18830309.2.46 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The delays resulted in complaints.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 March 1883 |title=Opotiki. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18830313.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1897 ''Staffa'' was working to [[Mangōnui|Mongōnui]] and ports to the north,<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 June 1897 |title=Northern company's movements. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18970604.2.7.5 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> as a feeder from Russell. NSS was seeking a cheaper replacement,<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 May 1897 |title=The Hercules. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18970526.2.19.7 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Staffa'' was broken up at Auckland in 1897.<ref name=":57" /> |- |Tahawai |1905 |1905 - c1924 |Hare Bros, [[Kaeo]] |12 |Motor launch built for NSS, as a feeder to collect [[kauri gum]]<ref name=":2222">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Tahawai |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000063 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083843/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000063 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> and take supplies to the [[Karikari Peninsula]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 April 1923 |title=Puheke settlement. Northland Age |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NORAG19230430.2.34.2 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> [[Kaimaumau]], [[Waipapakauri]], [[Waiharara]] and [[Awanui]].<ref name=":2222" /> In August 1921 lightning made holes in her and she was beached at the fish factory on the [[Awanui River]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 August 1921 |title=Struck by lightning. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19210803.2.39 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was still running in 1923.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 December 1923 |title=Correspondence. Northland Age |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NORAG19231217.2.9 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Tainui |1945 |1949–1963 |Kalmar Varv, Kalmar |539 |Motor ship built as ''Vestria'' for O.M. Thore, [[Jonstorp]] and sold to NSS in 1949 to service Lyttelton, Picton, New Plymouth and Raglan, she was {{Convert|175|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|23.5|ft|abbr=on}} wide, with a speed of {{Convert|10.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 December 1949 |title=Arrival of another Tainui.
