Gisborne Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GISH19491227.2.81 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She took 4 months to travel from Europe to Whangārei.<ref>{{Cite web |date=23 August 1949 |title=Local news. Grey River Argus |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GRA19490823.2.20 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1963 she was sold to Mr Suh Lee Kom of the Mayfair Rubber Co, Singapore, in December 1962 to Moh Yin Quek (Panama), July 1963 to Chagres Co. Panama Soc. Anon, 1965 renamed ''Dragon Dorado'', 1970 to Eastern Shg. and Trading Co. (Pte.) Ltd. and renamed ''Ares'' (Singapore) and 1978 to Yodpatankarn Co. Ltd. (Thai).<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Tainui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1178437 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084124/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1178437 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-28}}</ref> [[IMO number|IMO]]: 5349346 may still be in use.<ref>{{Cite web |title=ARES (IMO 5349346, General Cargo) - Ship Details and Current Position {{!}} Vessel Tracking |url=https://www.vesseltracking.net/ship/ares-5349346 |access-date=2024-02-28 |website=www.vesseltracking.net}}</ref> |- |Tainui II |1963 |1973–1976 |Astano SA, [[El Ferrol shipyards|El Ferrol]] |1.562 |[[File:Ferdinand_Retzlaff.jpg|thumb|''Tainui II'' as ''Ferdinand Retzlaff'']]Motor ship built for Sameiet Seagull A/S, Oslo as ''Seagull'', {{Convert|259.7|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|39.6|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|16.8|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with {{Convert|1699|hp|abbr=on}} [[MAN SE|M.A.N.]] engines driving her at up to {{Convert|11|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=M/S Seagull - Sjøhistorie |url=https://www.sjohistorie.no/en/skip/25423/https://www.sjohistorie.no/en/skip/25423/ |access-date=2024-04-13 |website=www.sjohistorie.no}}</ref> 1964 sold to Dr Erich Retzlaff, Bremen, renamed ''Ferdinand Retzlaff''. 1970 sold to Angbats A/B [[Kalmarsund]] and renamed ''Kalmarland''. 1973 sold to NSS. Renamed ''Tainui II''. In November 1974 she was laid up Auckland. She was the last NSS ship when she left Auckland on 24 May 1976, after sale to Ayo Shipping Company, Florida, who renamed her ''Koral''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Tainui II |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1349080 |access-date=2024-04-13 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084209/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1349080 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> On 5 November 1979 she sank after hitting mooring dolphins, about 200 miles west of [[Dry Tortugas National Park|Dry Tortugas]], between [[Dominican Republic]] and New Orleans with a cargo of sugar.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1979 Lloyds casualty returns |url=https://lloyds-production.s3.amazonaws.com/_file/general/1979-casualty-returns.pdf |page=62}}</ref> |- |Taniwha |1898 |1898–1939 |Robert Logan Sr., Auckland |263 |[[File:Taniwha_at_Paeroa_in_1912.jpg|thumb|''Taniwha'' at Paeroa in 1912]]Steamer ''Taniwha'' was a slightly larger sister of ''Waimarie,''<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 December 1897 |title=The Northern Steamship Company. Ohinemuri Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/OG18971222.2.14 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> both built for NSS, mainly for the Paeroa service, which she joined from 4 April 1898,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 April 1898 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18980405.2.20 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> serving [[Karangahake Gorge|Karangahake]], [[Waitekauri River|Waitekauri]], and [[Waihi]], with ''Waimarie'' and other ships. She was wooden, with twin-screws, lit by electricity,<ref name=":422" /> {{Convert|108.9|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|23.8|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|6.3|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with {{Convert|40|hp|abbr=on}} engines.<ref name=":033" /> Her engines and boiler, built by A&G Price, were insulated from the rest of the ship by patent [[blue asbestos]] blankets. She could reach {{Convert|11|knot|abbr=on}}, driven by two sets of direct-acting compound vertical surface-condensing engines (11.5, 23, 16)″, fed by a cylindrical multi-tubular marine boiler, {{Convert|10.5|ft|abbr=on}} in diameter and {{Convert|9.5|ft|abbr=on}} long, with a working pressure of {{Convert|110|psi|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 December 1897 |title=The Northern Steamship Company. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18971216.2.7 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In October 1901 she sank ''Patiki'' at night on a bend of the Waihou River; the company's give way rules were found to be confusing.<ref name=":922" /> On 11 August 1912<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 August 1912 |title=The Taniwha sunk. Te Aroha News |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TAN19120813.2.9 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and 30 September 1930 she struck snags and sank in the Waihou River. However, she continued running to Te Aroha until 1937,<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 June 1937 |title=Shipping.New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19370611.2.14.5 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> when she was replaced by a scow.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 February 1937 |title=Exit Taniwha. Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS19370224.2.7 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was then used on trips to Hauraki Gulf islands, until April 1938, when her last trip was to Waiheke.<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 April 1938 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19380414.2.14.3 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Whilst laid up in [[Shoal Bay, New Zealand|Shoal Bay]], on 30 October 1938, she was found to be leaking and was beached<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 October 1938 |title=Local & General. Hauraki Plains Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HPGAZ19381031.2.19 |access-date=2024-02-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> at [[Freemans Bay]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 February 1939 |title=Last haven. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390211.2.89 |access-date=2024-02-06 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> where iron was dumped in the harbour and more valuable metals sold.<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 May 1939 |title=End of old ship. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19390525.2.143 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In November 1939 she was broken up at [[Shoal Bay, New Zealand#History|Sulphur Beach]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Taniwha |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102302 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083813/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102302 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-29}}</ref> |- |[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|Tasman]] |1903 |1908–1921 |Robert Logan Sr., Auckland |179 |[[File:Tasman_at_Auckland.jpg|thumb|''Tasman'' at Auckland]]Steamer built as the ''Whangarona'' for James Cross Burford, to run between Motueka, Nelson and Wellington, but that name was "found unsuitable" and she was renamed ''Tasman'' whilst being fitted out.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 March 1903 |title=New steamer for Nelson. Colonist |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TC19030305.2.13 |access-date=2024-02-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was {{Convert|102|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|21|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|7|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with electric lights and a hull of 3 [[Cross bracing|diagonal]] layers of kauri and iron. [[Phoenix Foundry, Auckland#George Fraser & Sons Ltd 1900-1952|George Fraser & Sons']] {{Convert|280|hp|abbr=on}} compound (8{{frac|1|2}}, 22{{frac|1|2}} - 12)″ engines were {{Convert|160|psi|abbr=on}}, at 260 rpm, driving twin screws. The boiler was 9.6 ft diameter and length, with 2 [[Samson Fox#Life and career|Fox corrugated furnaces]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 April 1903 |title=Trial trip of the Tasman. Nelson Evening Mail |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NEM19030401.2.12 |access-date=2024-02-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On her delivery voyage from Auckland she steamed at {{Convert|8.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 April 1903 |title=The SS Tasman. Motueka Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/MOST19030407.2.18 |access-date=2024-02-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Due to ill health she was sold to Anchor on 28 January 1907.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 January 1907 |title=Colonist |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TC19070129.2.6 |access-date=2 July 2023 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=29 July 1908 |title=Shipping news. New Zealand Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZTIM19080729.2.70 |access-date=2024-02-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> NSS needed a larger ship to replace ''Mangapapa'' on the Whakatāne route,<ref name=":18" /> so bought ''Tasman'' on 15 August 1908. On 12 May 1921 she was wrecked on [[Rurima Island|Rurima]] Reef, without loss of life.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Tasman |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115196 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083941/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115196 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-27}}</ref> The captain and chief officer were found negligent in checking the ship's position, but they did not lose their certificates.<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 June 1921 |title=Off the proper course. New Zealand Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZTIM19210610.2.46 |access-date=2024-02-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Tawanui |1959 |1959–1973 |J. Bodewes, Hoogezand |891 |[[File:001 Littleton Harbour 1967 (51207655015).jpg|thumb|''Tawanui'' in 1967]]Motor ship built for NSS as a sister of ''Poranui'', with a crew of 11 and speed of {{Convert|11|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=31 July 1959 |title=Shipping. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19590731.2.193 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was {{Convert|209|ft|abbr=on}} long.<ref name=":110"/> On her delivery voyage she collided with a Suez Canal tug.<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 July 1959 |title=General News. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19590720.2.81 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1973 replaced by the larger ''Tainui II''<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 July 1973 |title=Nth. Steamship sees bright prospects. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19730705.2.81.1 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and sold to Tung Ho Co (Pte) Ltd, Singapore and renamed ''Tung Ho I'' and was still working in 1985.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Tawanui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084145/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1315008 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-22}}</ref> |- |Te Aroha |1876 |1890–1891 |Wm. Holmes, North Shore |51 |[[File:PS_Te_Aroha_in_1907.jpg|thumb|''Te Aroha'' in 1907]]Paddle steamer, {{Convert|89.2|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|15|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|4.9|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|14|hp|abbr=on}}, built of wood,<ref name=":14">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping 1902-1903 by Australian National Maritime Museum |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__ab6e2a205e2216 |access-date=2024-02-01 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> for J. Holmes, Auckland, who sold a part share to W. Souter in 1877, to Auckland & North Shore Ferry Co. Ltd. in 1877, to A. Hume, Thames in 1881 and to Thames River Steam Navigation Co. Ltd., Thames in 1884. NSS bought the company on 1 August 1890.<ref name=":822" /> In 1891''Te Aroha'' was found to be too small and was replaced by ''Ohinemuri''.<ref name=":722" /> In 1891 and 1892 she was run to [[Waiheke Island|Waiheke]], firstly by E.R. Thomson, Auckland,<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 December 1891 |title=Holiday amusements. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18911228.2.11 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref name=":102">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Te Aroha |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1075116 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083722/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1075116 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-01}}</ref> and then by [[James Bradney#Maritime activities|Bradney & Binns]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 July 1892 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18920716.2.17 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> "for some months". In January 1893 J.C. Bindon, of Te [[Horeke]], won a £300 per annum Government mail-carrying contract for a steam launch (''Te Aroha'') on Hokianga harbour.<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 January 1893 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18930127.2.26 |access-date=2024-02-02 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was sold to Devonport Steam Ferry Company Limited in 1896 and to T.E. Hallett, Auckland in 1907.<ref name=":102" /> On 1 April 1907 she leaked and sank off Peach Cove, [[Bream Head]] on her way to Waikato. The crew took to the lifeboat. She had no certificate for the open sea.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Small steamer wrecked. Able to carry 150 tons, to trade to Whakatāne and Ōpōtiki.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 December 1914 |title=New auxiliary scow. Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/EP19141221.2.54 |access-date=2025-07-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 6 August 1920 wrecked with loss of one crew member on Slipper Island (Whakahau),<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Te Teko |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133298 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084015/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133298 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-22}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=9 August 1920 |title=Ketch wrecked. Henderson]], [[Meadowside]] |350 |[[File:Terranora_leaving_Auckland.jpg|thumb|''Terranora'' leaving Auckland]]Paddle steamer launched on 20 August 1878, completed in September 1878, delivered via [[Suez Canal|Suez]] and registered for [[CSR Limited|Colonial Sugar Refining]] Co, Sydney on 4 February 1879.<ref name=":223">{{Cite web |title=Paddle Steamer TERRANORA built by D & W Henderson & Co. in 1878 for John Grafton Ross (Colonial Sugar Refining Co), Sydney NSW, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=9552 |access-date=2024-02-23 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> She was built of [[mild steel]],<ref name=":242">{{Cite web |date=26 May 1914 |title=From steamer to hulk. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19140526.2.83 |access-date=2024-02-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|141.5|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|27.1|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|10.4|ft|abbr=on}}, with a 2-cylinder (30, 54)″, {{Convert|94|hp|abbr=on}} engine,<ref name=":223" /> driving her at {{Convert|9|knot|abbr=on}}. In 1890 shares in ''Terranora'' were sold to Margaret Gallagher and Rev. Andrew Cassidy, Auckland, and, after Mrs Gallagher was murdered by her husband, in 1891 to T. Bayly, Waitara.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 June 1891 |title=Sale of P.S. Terranora. Patea Mail |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/PATM18910601.2.8 |access-date=2024-02-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1892 the Government bought her for [[Cable layer|cable laying]], but she was replaced by ''Tutanekai'' in 1896, when ''Terranora'' was sold to Captain F. Black, Wellington<ref name=":252">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Terranora |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1075005 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083816/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1075005 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-22}}</ref> and she was fitted with electric lights to work the Auckland-Thames route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 December 1896 |title=Thames Advertiser |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THA18961207.2.7.5 |access-date=2024-02-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1898 she was sold to E.G.R. Ford, Auckland. She competed briefly on NSS routes,<ref name=":252" /> before being bought by NSS in August 1898.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 August 1898 |title=The Terranora sold. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18980809.2.29.8 |access-date=2024-02-23 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1906 she was converted to a coal hulk at Auckland and on 23 May 1914 scuttled in Hauraki Gulf.<ref name=":242" /> |- |Toa |1928 |1928–1961 |G.T. Niccol, Freemans Bay |208 |Motor ship launched January 1928 for NSS. She was {{Convert|106|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|26|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|8.5|ft|abbr=on}}, with a twin screw, twin Bolinder engines and built of wood,<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 March 1932 |title=Vessel on rocks. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19320319.2.80 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> which was damaged when she ran aground on Whakatāne bar in 1932,<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 March 1932 |title=Toa refloated. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19320322.2.14 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1939<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 July 1939 |title=Bilges scarred. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19390707.2.80 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and 1950;<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 May 1950 |title=Coaster Toa goes aground on bar off Whakatane. Gisborne Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/GISH19500526.2.77 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> she also had to wait 4 days off the bar in 1946.<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 May 1946 |title=Toa delayed. Bay of Plenty Beacon |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BPB19460501.2.27 |access-date=2024-04-09 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1961 sold to [[Kaiapoi]] Shipping Company Limited<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Toa |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1153973 |access-date=2024-04-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084049/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1153973 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> to serve Kaiapoi and Wellington until laid up in 1963. She was converted for line fishing off Chatham Islands in 1964. In 1967 she worked with ''Ranginui'' in the venison recovery operations in Fiordland. She went fishing at Tonga in the early 1970s, and was laid up at Auckland in 1973. In 1974 she was auctioned by Auckland Harbour Board to recover unpaid charges, sold to Mahmoud Raza, Suva for $6,040 and renamed ''Paerimu''. She sank at her [[Vanua Balavu#Geography and infrastructure|Bay of Islands]] moorings in 1981.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kaiapoi Ships {{!}} Toa |url=https://www.kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz/Kaiapoi-Ships/lc/43111/Toa |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=www.kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz}}</ref> |- |Torea |1912 |1912 - c1935 |G.T. Niccol |50 |Wooden, auxiliary scow, {{Convert|69|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|19.7|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|5.4|ft|abbr=on}}, {{Convert|30|hp|abbr=on}},<ref name=":033" /> 2 screw, launched on 19 June 1912 for NSS<ref>{{Cite web |date=20 June 1912 |title=A new scow. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19120620.2.38.1 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> to link Te Puke with Tauranga, in place of ''Victory'',<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 December 1912 |title=Local and general. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19121209.2.8 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and in 1913, to Paeroa and Tauranga.<ref name=":20" /> In 1927 she was running alternate weeks from Tauranga to the [[Rangitaiki]] and Kaituna rivers.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 March 1927 |title=Te Puke Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/TPT19270329.2.2.6 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In the 1930s she was laid up in Little Shoal Bay, where she broke her mooring in 1935 and drifted undamaged onto [[Stanley Point]]. She was towed back to her mooring by a small Auckland Launch & Towboat Co. launch.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 June 1935 |title=Torea being towed to safety. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19350626.2.28.3 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=25 June 1935 |title=Scow ashore. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19350625.2.76 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1936 she was towed to Paeroa.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 February 1936 |title=Shipping news. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19360217.2.27 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Later she was sold to Island buyers.<ref name=":112">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Torea |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133267 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083950/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133267 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-28}}</ref> |- |Tuhoe |1919 |1919–1942 1945 - 1961 |G.T. Niccol |186 |[[File:Tuhoe_auxiliary_schooner.jpg|thumb|''Tuhoe'' about 1943]]Auxiliary schooner with twin screws, launched on 7 April 1919<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 April 1919 |title=Departures. Poverty Bay Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/PBH19190412.2.4.1 |access-date=2024-04-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> for NSS's Northland and Coromandel routes. Her twin {{Convert|60|hp|abbr=on}} Beardmore engines, salvaged from ''Eunice''<ref name=":34">{{Cite web |date=1 May 1919 |title=Shipping. Evening Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ESD19190501.2.34 |access-date=2024-04-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> (wrecked at Whanganui on 26 September 1917),<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 June 1918 |title=Wreck of the Eunice. Wanganui Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WH19180605.2.35 |access-date=2024-04-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> drove her at up to {{Convert|8.5|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":34" /> She was wooden, {{Convert|97.8|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|24.7|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|6.5|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":033" /> Her maiden voyage was on 1 May 1919 from Auckland to Whakatāne and Tauranga.<ref name=":35">{{Cite web |title=Kaiapoi Ships {{!}} Tuhoe |url=https://www.kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz/Kaiapoi-Ships/lc/43112/Tuhoe |access-date=2024-04-11 |website=www.kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz}}</ref> In 1934 she ran aground at [[Ōhiwa]]<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 February 1934 |title=Tuhoe ashore. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19340216.2.28 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and in 1937 she lost a propeller when she hit a snag in the Waihou River.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 October 1937 |title=Scow Loses Propeller. Hauraki Plains Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HPGAZ19371018.2.33 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had new engines in 1938.<ref>{{Cite web |date=22 September 1938 |title=Vessels in port. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19380922.2.20 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 25 October 1942 [[Lend-Lease|Lend Lease]] transferred ''Tuhoe'' (S-132) to the [[US Army Small Ships Section]] to operate around [[New Guinea]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=The auxiliary schooner Tuhoe (S-132) of the Small Ships Section, United States Army Services 1942-1945 |url=https://www.awm.gov.au/collection/C1118112 |access-date=2024-04-11 |website=www.awm.gov.au |language=en}}</ref> until sold back to NSS in 1945. Chrysler truck motors replaced her original engines, before near-new diesels were found in 1944.<ref name=":35" /> An engine failed in December 1945,<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 December 1945 |title=Northern Coaster Back After War Service. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19451218.2.33 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> as she was returning to east coast trading.<ref name=":35" /> On 31 March 1947 she was the last ship to serve Paeroa.<ref name=":37" /> In 1947 it was reported that her 2 × {{Convert|200|hp|abbr=on}} engines had been put in a [[Fairmile B motor launch|Fairmile]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 July 1947 |title=Purchase of Fairmile. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19470709.2.8 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Between 1956 and 1960 she was laid up three times. In August 1961 she was sold to T Eckford and Co, Blenheim, but laid up in Ōpaoa River until sale to Kaiapoi Shipping Company,<ref name=":35" /> in February 1962.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 February 1962 |title=Tuhoe Sold. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19620228.2.187 |access-date=2024-04-12 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She started trading between Kaiapoi, Wellington and Napier<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Tuhoe |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136875 |access-date=2024-04-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084108/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136875 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> in April 1962. In June 1963 she stranded on the [[Waimakariri River]] bar but was refloated on 1 July. The new railway ferries ended the trade in November 1963. She was converted to a line fishing vessel but declared unseaworthy and laid up at Kaiapoi. In October 1980 Cure Boating Club Inc. bought her as a clubroom. In 1982, the MV Tuhoe Preservation Society leased her from the club and began restoration for weekend river cruises. In 2000, the hull was replaced in Lyttelton. In late 2003, she was bought by [[MainPower]] and Kaiapoi Electricity, who set up MV Tuhoe Kaiapoi Rivertown Trust. Regular sailings resumed in June 2005. Over $200,000 was spent on repairs at Lyttelton, but when she returned on Sunday 27 September 2015 she grounded again on Waimakariri bar and leaked too much to be refloated.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2015-09-29 |title=Sea you later: Ship is unsalvagable |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/regional/285576/sea-you-later-ship-is-unsalvagable |access-date=2024-04-11 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}}</ref> She was taken apart and the remains burnt on 12 October 2015.<ref name=":35" /> |- |Victory |1903 |1907–1932 |Bailey & Lowe, Auckland |22. 32 from 1909 |Ketch built for Thomas & Drusella Wills, Kennedy Bay. 1905 two oil engines fitted. Tonnage changed to 26 gross.<ref name=":55">{{Cite web |title=Victory |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115198 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083859/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1115198 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> In October 1907 she was sold to NSS to serve Tauranga, Matatā and Te Puke,<ref name=":54" /> replaced in 1912 by ''Torea'', initially whilst ''Victory'' was having new engines fitted.<ref>{{Cite web |date=12 August 1912 |title=Local and general. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19120812.2.11 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In August 1928 she was sold to her master, Captain Scopes, to continue trading to Waiuku.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 August 1928 |title=Sun (Auckland) |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/SUNAK19280830.2.28 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1932 she was sold to Avoca Coal Company of Dargaville, to A. Curel of Helensville, converted to a cruising vessel and re-engined with twin [[Kelvin Diesels|Kelvin diesels]] in 1937. In 1946 she was given to Kaipara Cruising Club, Helensville as a clubhouse. In October 1962 she was condemned and burnt.<ref name=":55" /> |- |Waihou | |1897–1935 |Not known | |[[Waihou River]] barge<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details Waihou |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000061 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083810/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=580000061 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-22}}</ref> |- |Waimana |1899 |1899–1904 |Lane & Brown, Totara North |152 |[[File:Waimana.jpg|thumb|''Waimana'' in 1899]]Auxiliary ketch built for NSS, but sold in 1904 as unsuited to the work.<ref name=":172" /> She used {{Convert|1|Lcwt|kg|abbr=on}} of coal an hour in 2 × Thornycroft boilers, with 2 × 4-cylinder (5, 7, 9, 12)″ Kingdon engines, of {{Convert|50|hp|abbr=on}} each,<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 November 1899 |title=New Zealand Graphic |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/periodicals/NZGRAP18991104.2.46.1 |access-date=2024-03-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> or {{Convert|18|hp|abbr=on}}, driving a single screw.<ref name=":323" /> She then went to a succession of Melbourne owners; M. Walsh in 1904, McIlwraith, McEacharn Ltd 1914, E. Taylor 1917 (when her steam engines were removed, diesel later being fitted), G. Dunkley 1920, Yarram Shipping Co 1923, E.J. Wright 1927, I.S. Angelinavich 1930, A.J. Coch 1930, W.G. Sims 1932, J.M. Angelin 1941 and, in 1947 to Roman Catholic Mission of New Guinea.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Waimana |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102317 |access-date=2024-03-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083819/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102317 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> |- |Waimarie |1896 |1896–1931 |Robert Logan Sr., Auckland |245 |[[File:Waimarie_on_Waihou_River.jpg|thumb|''Waimarie'' on Waihou River]]Steamer built for NSS's Paeroa run (3 years before ''[[PS Waimarie]]''), costing about £7,000 and launched on 4 February 1896. She had a hull of 3 layers of kauri planks,<ref name="paperspast.natlib.govt.nz"/> two A & G Price, compound, surface condensing engines drove twin screws. The main saloon had 70 berths and the ladies' saloon 20. ''Waimarie'' was the first of the fleet to have electric lighting,<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 April 1896 |title=The trial trip of the Waimarie. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18960421.2.33 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> including a light of over 3,000 [[candlepower]] on her foremast to assist river navigation in poor light.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 April 1896 |title=Telegraphic shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18960416.2.57.3 |access-date=2024-01-29 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In the 1900s ''Waimarie'' also ran short excursions from Auckland in mid summer.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 December 1903 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19031230.2.66.6 |access-date=2024-02-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=13 January 1906 |title=The Waimarie bound for Cowes' Bay |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/143650/rec/21 |access-date=2024-02-24 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> In 1915 she was slightly damaged in a collision with ''Taniwha'' off [[Ponui Island]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 March 1915 |title=The Taniwha Waimarie collision. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19150330.2.49 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In June 1921 the Paeroa service was reduced and ''Waimarie'' was laid up in the Waihou River, in July 1929 she was sold for scrap and towed to Auckland,<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 July 1929 |title=Ships that pass. Thames Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/THS19290725.2.9 |access-date=2024-01-28 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> where she was broken up in 1931.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Waimarie |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102282 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083753/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102282 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-28}}</ref> She was {{Convert|102.2|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|22|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|7.9|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with {{Convert|48|hp|abbr=on}} engines.<ref name=":033" /> |- |Waiotahi |1891 |1891–1934 |[[Hanna, Donald & Wilson#Hanna, Donald & Wilson|Abercorn Shipbuilding]] Co., Paisley |278 |[[File:Waiotahi_around_1910.jpg|thumb|''Waiotahi'' around 1910]]Steamer launched on 9 June 1891 for NSS as a steel, passenger cargo ship, {{Convert|125.5|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|25.1|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|8.6|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with a [[Hanna, Donald & Wilson]] twin compound 2 cylinder, {{Convert|56|hp|abbr=on}} engine, driving 2 screws<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer WAIOTAHI built by Abercorn Shipbuilding Co in 1891 for Northern Steamship Co Ltd, Auckland NZ., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=75&ship_listPage=17&a1Page=219&ref=65&vessel=WAIOTAHI |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> at about {{Convert|9.5|knot|abbr=on}}. Her delivery voyage took 103 days, calling for coal at Suez and [[Thursday Island]]. She was built to carry maize from Ōpōtiki,<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 October 1891 |title=The S.S. Waiotahi. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18911030.2.35 |access-date=2024-03-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> but later was on the Parenga and Great Barrier route<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 June 1934 |title=Steamers for scrap. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19340601.2.102 |access-date=2024-03-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and ran other trips, including a Hauraki Gulf fishing excursion in 1897.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 January 1897 |title=Fishing excursion in S.S. Waiotahi. New Zealand Graphic and Ladies Journal |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/116978 |access-date=2024-03-08 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref> She had electric lights fitted in 1908.<ref name=":18" /> She was replaced by motor ships in 1929 and laid up.<ref name=":342">{{Cite web |date=31 August 1934 |title=New Waiotahi. Niccol for his own use and the last ship built at his yard, but only used for a trip to Norfolk Island.<ref name=":342" /> In 1934 she was sold to NSS, renamed ''Waiotahi'',<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Waiotahi |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1157768 |access-date=2024-03-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084059/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1157768 |archive-date=19 January 2022 }}</ref> and worked to the Bay of Plenty until 1960, and then between Picton and Onehunga, until sold to A G Frankham Ltd in 1961 for an Auckland–[[Gisborne, New Zealand|Gisborne]] route, then to Inter Island Shipping Company to link [[Kaiapoi]] and Wellington, with occasional trips to [[Golden Bay / Mohua]], until 6 November 1967.<ref name=":352">{{Cite web |title=Kaiapoi Ships {{!}} Waiotahi |url=https://kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz/Kaiapoi-Ships/lc/43113/Waiotahi |access-date=2024-03-08 |website=kaiapoimaritimeheritage.co.nz}}</ref> In 1966 she grounded near the Waimakariri River entrance, but was refloated.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Waiotahi on the beach by the entrance to the Waimakariri River... |url=https://discoverywall.nz/media/135461 |access-date=2024-03-08 |website=discoverywall.nz}}</ref> From 1967 F Fawcett-Kay used her around the [[Solomon Islands]], until she was scuttled in [[Rabaul Harbour]] in October 1973, due to the hull being eaten away by [[Teredo navalis|teredo]] worms.<ref name=":352" /> |- |[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|Waipu]] |1915 |1915–1935 |[[McGregor and Company|John McGregor]], Dunedin |205 |[[File:Waipu_in_1915.jpg|thumb|Waipu being built in 1915 near [[Pelichet Bay]]]]A steel, shallow draught steamer, designed to carry more to and from [[Waipu, New Zealand|Waipu]] than ''Victory''<ref name=":44">{{Cite web |date=7 April 1916 |title=New coastal steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19160407.2.47 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> (when the bar was passable),<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 May 1916 |title=The Waipu trade. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19160526.2.10 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> launched on 26 August 1915<ref name=":45">{{Cite web |date=27 August 1915 |title=Launching of the Waipu successfully accomplished. Otago Daily Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ODT19150827.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with 31 berths,<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 June 1916 |title=The Waipu. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19160603.2.6 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> able to take up to 60 passengers and 60 tons, {{Convert|110|ft}} x {{Convert|22|ft}} x {{Convert|5.5|ft}}, with two sets of main engines, powered by a {{Convert|190|psi|abbr=on}} boiler,<ref name=":45" /> {{Convert|250|hp|abbr=on}}, 2 screw,<ref name=":44" /> driving her at up to {{Convert|10|knot}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 May 1916 |title=The Waipu's trials. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19160529.2.55 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> Her completion was delayed by a wartime shortage of pipes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=9 March 1915 |title=Shipping news. Dominion |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/DOM19150309.2.85 |access-date=2025-07-08 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref><ref name=":133">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Waipu |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136865 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084018/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1136865 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> Passenger accommodation was aft, officers' amidships and the crew's forward, with the cargo hold. She had steam steering gear, two large lifeboats and could steam at up to 9¾ knots.<ref name=":45" /> As early as 1917 low tides on the Waipu bar were delaying the ship for days.<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 July 1917 |title=Northern Company's steamers. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19170703.2.6 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> It became even shallower and she was transferred to Hauraki Gulf routes.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 November 1935 |title=New trade. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19351106.2.15 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was replaced by ''Elsie'',<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 August 1917 |title=Shipping. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19170818.2.6 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> though she too grounded on the bar.<ref>{{Cite web |date=13 September 1922 |title=Waipu news. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19220913.2.43 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ''Waipu'' was temporarily laid up in 1921.<ref>{{Cite web |date=27 September 1921 |title=Another steamer idle. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19210927.2.27 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 9 June 1924, coming down the fast flowing, flooded, Waihou, from Kerepehi to Ngatea, to collect butter, despite towing a [[Anchor#grapnel|drag]] astern to steady her, she slowed for the lifting span of the Ngatea Bridge, but the current swung her against the bridge. As she was winched through, the drag caught on telephone lines, cutting off many phones.<ref>{{Cite web |date=September 2007 |title=Ohinemuri - Early Shipping On The Piako River |url=https://ohinemuri.org.nz/journals/journal-51-september-2007/early-shipping-on-the-piako-river |access-date=2025-07-09 |website=ohinemuri.org.nz}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=9 June 1924 |title=Shipping mishaps. Hauraki Plains Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HPGAZ19240609.2.7 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She was laid up in May 1931,<ref>{{Cite web |date=14 August 1935 |title=Steamer Waipu. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19350814.2.72 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> sold to KDV Box Company in 1935,<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 November 1935 |title=Steamship Waipu. Northern Advocate |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NA19351106.2.43 |access-date=2025-07-11 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> converted to diesel and renamed ''[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|MV Hokitika]]'' in 1936.<ref name=":133" /> |- |Waitangi |1889 |1896–1911 |[[Grangemouth Dockyard Company]] |171 |[[File:Waitangi_at_Port_Fitzroy_in_1904.png|thumb|''Waitangi'' at Rarohara Bay (Ungunu Bay), [[Port Fitzroy]] in 1904]]Steamer ''Waitangi'' started running for NSS on 30 September 1896, between Auckland and Thames.<ref>{{Cite web |date=26 September 1896 |title=Ohinemuri Gazette |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/OG18960926.2.21.1 |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> NSS changed her name from ''Banks Peninsula'' on 24 December 1896.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 December 1896 |title=Shipping. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18961224.2.14 |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She had been launched on 22 December 1888, completed in February 1889 for Peninsula and Akaroa Steam Navigation Company Ltd and registered in Lyttelton on 3 June 1889. She was a twin screw, steel, passenger cargo ship, {{Convert|120|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|20|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|7.9|ft|abbr=on}} deep, with 2 × 2 cylinder (13.5, 27 × 18)″, {{Convert|62|hp|abbr=on}}, Hutson & Corbett, Glasgow engines<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer BANKS PENINSULA built by Grangemouth Dockyard Company in 1889 for Peninsula & Akaroa Steam Navigation Co. Ltd., Lyttelton, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?a1PageSize=100&ship_listPage=1&ship_listPageSize=75&a1Order=Sorter_name&a1Dir=DESC&a1Page=394&ref=53181&vessel=BANKS+PENINSULA |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> driving her at {{Convert|12|knot|abbr=on}}. She was divided with 4 watertight bulkheads and rigged as a schooner.<ref>{{Cite web |date=18 May 1889 |title=The Banks' Peninsula. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP18890518.2.7.5 |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1890 she was sold to Union, in 1891 to E.T. Miles, Hobart, in 1896 to NSS and renamed ''Waitangi''.<ref name=":21" /> In 1909, when the Waitara bar silted up too much for her, she was replaced by ''Albatross''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 March 1909 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19090315.2.19 |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1911 she was sold to [[Kauri Timber Company|Kauri Timber Co. Ltd.]], Auckland.<ref name=":21">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Waitangi |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1076071 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083756/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1076071 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-31}}</ref> and to [[Pātea]] Farmers Freezing Company in early 1919 to trade between Wellington and Pātea with their other ship, ''[[Anchor Shipping and Foundry Company#Fleet List|Waverley]]''. The passenger accommodation was removed, and she was insulated for frozen meat. On 5 May 1923 ''Waitangi'' she was wrecked at Pātea River mouth, with 46 tons of chaff for Pātea Farmers Co-op. She hit the Western Breakwater, holing the starboard side and ran aground on the Western Beach. The crew escaped and the cargo was retrieved undamaged. Holes were patched, and she was towed out to sea, but the tow cable had to be cut, leaving her to drift back onto the beach. The masts became gate posts at the entrance to Pātea Freezing Works and part of them later became the [[Aotea Utanganui – Museum of South Taranaki|local museum]]'s donation box.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018 |title=Shipping |url=https://www.pateahistoricalsociety.org.nz/shipping.html |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=PATEA HISTORICAL SOCIETY |language=en}}</ref> Part of the ship is still on the beach.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2018 |title=SS Waitangi Wreck |url=https://www.pateahistoricalsociety.org.nz/ss-waitangi-wreck.html |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=PATEA HISTORICAL SOCIETY |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=6 Beach Rd Patea |url=https://www.google.com/maps/@-39.7722112,174.4888981,3a,15y,226.31h,86.65t/data=!3m7!1e1!3m5!1sI2DLQGfWygxO3WrUK5rEpQ!2e0!5s20131101T000000!7i13312!8i6656?entry=ttu |access-date=2024-01-31 |website=Google Maps |language=en}}</ref> |- |Waiuku |1913 |1913–1931 |[[John I. Thornycroft & Company]], [[Hampshire]] |76 |[[File:Waiuku_about_1917.png|thumb|350x350px|''Waiuku'' at Waiuku about 1917]]Motor barge {{Convert|95|ft|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|17|ft|abbr=on}} wide, {{Convert|5.4|ft|abbr=on}} deep, {{Convert|200|hp|abbr=on}}, built for NSS<ref name=":033" /> as a pre-fabricated hull and assembled by Massey Brothers at Onehunga for the passenger service to Waiuku.<ref name=":3222">{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Waiuku |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133284 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084006/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1133284 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-26}}</ref> She took 2{{frac|1|2}} hours for the {{Convert|28|mi|abbr=on}} journey, with 4 stops,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 November 1913 |title=Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19131105.2.14 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> including [[Āwhitu Peninsula|Āwhitu]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=7 November 1913 |title=Waiuku news. Pukekohe & Waiuku Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/PWT19131107.2.8 |access-date=2024-01-27 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> After bus competition<ref name="PATUMAHOE. FRANKLIN TIMES"/> saw the end of the Waiuku ferry in 1928,<ref name="The Log Book SUN AUCKLAND"/><ref name="ReferenceA"/> she was used for Hauraki Gulf excursions, then dismantled in 1931 at Auckland and broken up in 1945.<ref name=":3222" /> |- |Wakatere |1896 |1896–1929 |[[Napier and Miller#Company history|Napier, Shanks & Bell, Yoker]] |441 |[[File:P.S._Wakatere_at_Auckland.jpg|thumb|''Wakatere'' at Auckland about 1910]]Paddle steamer with electric lighting,<ref name=":422" /> originally designed for [[Isle of Man Steam Packet Company|Isle of Man]] service, ''Wakatere'' was launched on 30 May 1896 for NSS's Auckland-Thames route, to serve the [[Coromandel Gold Rushes#Thames goldfields|goldfields]], and, travelling via Suez,<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 January 1933 |title=Last of the Wakatere. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19330105.2.144 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> left Glasgow on 3 September 1896, arriving at Auckland on 3 December 1896. She was a 1,500-passenger, steel, steamer, {{Convert|210.3|ft|abbr=on}} × {{Convert|26.2|ft|abbr=on}} × {{Convert|10.2|ft|abbr=on}}, with a 2-cylinder (31, 57 - 60)″, {{Convert|140|hp|abbr=on}} engine by Bow, McLachlan & Co. Ltd, Paisley, driving her at up to {{Convert|14|knot|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Paddle Steamer WAKATERE built by Napier, Shanks & Bell in 1896 for Northern Steamship Co Ltd, Auckland NZ, Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?ref=18791 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> A court hearing in 1913 found that a boiler collapse was due to lack of water and suspended the 2nd engineer's certificate.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 July 1913 |title=Wakatere accident. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19130708.2.23 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1914 she collided with a ketch, ''Katie S'', off Ponui Island.<ref>{{Cite web |date=17 December 1914 |title=Collision in the Gulf. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19141217.2.59 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> A court found that the second mate should have kept to starboard of the ketch and charged him costs.<ref>{{Cite web |date=30 January 1915 |title=Sinking of the Katie S. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19150130.2.21 |access-date=2024-04-07 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1921 she was replaced on the Thames route by ''Aupouri,''<ref name=":59" /> though restored, then replaced by ''Rangitoto'' and laid up in 1926.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 July 1926 |title=The Northern Company's steamer Wakatere, the last of Auckland's passenger carrying paddle steamers. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19260705.2.104.1 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1929 she was sold to J.E. Appleton, Auckland to use as a shingle barge<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Wakatere |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102288 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083759/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102288 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-02-22}}</ref> and broken up by Parry Brothers at Freemans Bay in 1933.<ref>{{Cite web |date=4 January 1933 |title=Wakatere's end. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS19330104.2.32 |access-date=2024-02-22 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> The scrap was sent to Japan in 1934, with that from ''Aupouri''.<ref name=":362" /> |- |Wanganui |1902 |1923–1948 |Duinker & Goedhoop, [[Martenshoek]] |351 |[[File:Wanganui_in_1916.jpg|thumb|''Wanganui'' in 1916]]Coal hulk, a former iron sailing ship, used as an oil bunker for new motor ships.<ref name=":26" /> {{Convert|135|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|25.8|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|10.4|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":2522">{{Cite web |date=2014-06-10 |title=The Register of Australian and New Zealand Shipping Corrected 1915 by Australian National Maritime Museum - Issuu |url=https://issuu.com/anmmuseum/docs/the_register_of_australian_and_new__b3148c9798d263 |access-date=2024-04-03 |website=issuu.com |language=en}}</ref> Launched on 13 February 1902 as ''Voorlichter'', a 3-masted schooner, for Maatschappij Zeilschip Voorlichter, [[Amsterdam]], September 1910 to Herman C. Boye, [[Marstal]], renamed ''Carla''.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Stichting Maritiem Historische Data - Schip |url=https://www.marhisdata.nl/schip?id=7087 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=www.marhisdata.nl}}</ref> She had difficulty entering Whanganui,<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 September 1911 |title=Will the anchors hold? Wanganui Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WH19110919.2.61 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> ran aground on 21 September 1911<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 September 1911 |title=The Carla ashore. Wanganui Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WH19110921.2.73 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and was sold for £390 to [[Alexander Hatrick|A. Hatrick]] & Co, who refloated her<ref>{{Cite web |date=10 October 1911 |title=Barque Carla refloated. Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/EP19111010.2.67 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> repaired the limited damage<ref>{{Cite web |date=1911-10-18 |title=Photograph of the wheel-house and deck of the salvaged barquentine Carla |url=https://kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz/digital/collection/photos/id/147477 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=kura.aucklandlibraries.govt.nz |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=16 January 1912 |title=The Carla. Wanganui Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WH19120116.2.56 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> and, in 1912, renamed her ''Wanganui''.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 May 1912 |title=Shipping. Wanganui Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WH19120506.2.13 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In September 1912 the main mast broke.<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 September 1912 |title=Dismasted. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19120924.2.4 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In 1916 she was sold to H. Bleakley, Sydney,<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 June 1916 |title=By Telegraph. Wanganui Chronicle |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WC19160615.2.11.6 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 1918 to W. Archibald, Auckland, 1922 to G.T. Niccol, 1923 to NSS, February 1948 stripped and scuttled outside of [[Cuvier Island]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Wanganui |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1121388 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119084037/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1121388 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> after being towed from Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 February 1948 |title=General News. Press |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/CHP19480216.2.70 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Wave |1904 |1907–1918 |Bailey & Lowe, Freemans Bay |41. 32 from 1909 |[[File:Wave_launch.jpg|thumb|Wave's launch in Auckland in 1904]]Auxiliary scow, ketch, or schooner, launched on 9 January 1904, for Charles Kasper, Matakana.<ref>{{Cite web |date=11 January 1904 |title=Local and general news. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19040111.2.21 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> {{Convert|56|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|18|ft|abbr=on}}, draught {{Convert|6.7|ft|abbr=on}}, with a {{Convert|18|hp|abbr=on}} Union oil engine. In a favourable wind she could average {{Convert|8|knot|abbr=on}}, carrying about 40 tons. In 1905 she passed to John P. Kasper, Auckland, who sold her to NSS in October 1907, as a Matatā-Tauranga feeder vessel, to replace ''Fingal''.<ref name=":51" /> In 1908 her mast was broken by a willow tree near Canaan.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2 March 1908 |title=Mishap to the Wave. Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT19080302.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> By 1914 she was running to [[Thames, New Zealand#Port|Shortland]]. 1915 re-engined and converted to twin screw. 1918 sold to Robert Burns of Auckland.<ref name=":56">{{Cite web |title=Wave |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1118957 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083902/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1118957 |archive-date=2022-01-19 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=nzmaritimeindex.org.nz}}</ref> In May 1919 she was sold by Nelson & Son, Samoa, to Brodniak & Co, Suva.<ref>{{Cite web |date=29 May 1919 |title=Intercolonial and coastal. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH19190529.2.11 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> On 29 November 1923<ref name=":56" /> she was wrecked on Naselai reef, off the south coast of [[Tailevu Province]], with 7 missing.<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 December 1923 |title=Ketch driven on reef. Dominion |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/DOM19231208.2.31 |access-date=2025-07-21 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |- |Weka |1894 |1907–1919 |[[Henry Niccol#Career|William Holmes]], North Shore |127 |[[File:Waiuku-Harbor-1954.jpg|thumb|''Weka'' at Waiuku]]Steamer launched on 19 October 1894 for Waiuku & Onehunga Steam Navigation Co, diagonally built of kauri, {{Convert|95|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|17|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|6.5|ft|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 October 1894 |title=Launch of a steamer. Auckland Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/AS18941019.2.17 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> with 2 compound (9, 18 - 12)″, {{Convert|115|psi|abbr=on}} engines by George Fraser,<ref>{{Cite web |date=3 December 1894 |title=Onehunga and Waiuku SN Company. New Zealand Herald |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/NZH18941203.2.58 |access-date=2025-07-24 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> sold to Manukau S.S. Company in 1903, which was taken over by NSS in 1906. When ''Waiuku'' replaced her, she was transferred to [[Piako River|Piako]]. In 1919 she was sold to [[McCallum Brothers|McCallum Bros]]. to tow shingle barges and renamed ''Kerepeehi''. In 1923 she was sold to Devonport Steam Ferry Company and renamed ''Kiwi''. Further sales were to Bradney & Binns in 1927 and G. Parry, Auckland in 1934. In 1940 she was broken up in Auckland and her hull buried in a harbour reclamation.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-01-19 |title=Northern Steam Ship Company of Auckland - Vessel details - Weka |url=http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102274 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220119083905/http://nzmaritimeindex.org.nz/nssco/nsspage.php?vessel=1102274 |archive-date=19 January 2022 |access-date=2024-01-27}}</ref> |- |Wellington |1863 |1881–1909 |Blackwood & Gordon, Port Glasgow |365<ref>{{Cite web |date=19 Apr 1871 |title=SALE OF STEAMERS. WELLINGTON INDEPENDENT |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/WI18710419.2.14 |access-date=2021-12-18 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> 429 [[Gross tonnage|GT]] |[[File:Wellington_at_Picton.jpg|thumb|''Wellington'' at Picton]]Launched 24 October 1863 for New Zealand Steam Navigation Co, Wellington and transferred to New Zealand Steam Shipping Company (NZSS) in 1871 and on 24 June 1876, when Union took over NZSS, together with ''Lady Bird'', ''Phoebe'', ''Taranaki'' and ''Wellington'' for ''£''47,400.<ref name=":74">{{Cite web |date=24 June 1876 |title=Evening Post |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/EP18760624.2.10 |access-date=2024-09-16 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref>. ''Wellington'' was a 107-passenger,<ref>{{Cite web |date=1 Sep 1863 |title=PORT OF BLUFF HARBOR. SOUTHLAND TIMES |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/ST18630901.2.3.2 |access-date=2021-12-14 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> iron, single-screw steamer, {{Convert|11|knot|abbr=on}}, with horse-stalls, {{Convert|185.5|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|24.6|ft|abbr=on}} x {{Convert|13.2|ft|abbr=on}}, with a 2-cylinder, {{Convert|90|hp|abbr=on}} engine, which arrived on 3 June 1864. In 1881 she was sold to NSS, initially serving Tauranga-Auckland.<ref>{{Cite web |date=21 July 1881 |title=Bay of Plenty Times |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/BOPT18810721.2.4 |access-date=2023-07-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> She ran daily to Whangārei in 1900,<ref name=":422" /> but was laid up about 1905,<ref>{{Cite web |date=24 January 1910 |title=THE OLD S.S. WELLINGTON Hawera & Normanby Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HNS19100124.2.11 |access-date=2023-07-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> after being used on the New Plymouth-Onehunga route.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 March 1905 |title=Hawera & Normanby Star |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HNS19050315.2.3.1 |access-date=2023-07-31 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> In December 1909 she was sold<ref name="ReferenceB"/> to George Turnbull Niccol, who converted her to a hulk at Auckland. In 1913 she was scuttled as a breakwater at [[Moehau Range#'Granite' quarries|Moehau]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=Screw Steamer WELLINGTON built by Blackwood & Gordon in 1863 for New Zealand Steam Navigation Co, Wellington., Passenger / Cargo |url=https://www.clydeships.co.uk/view.php?official_number=&imo=&builder=&builder_eng=&year_built=&launch_after=&launch_before=01/01/1865&role=&type_ref1=&propulsion=&owner=&port=&flag=&disposal=&lost=&ref=2310&vessel=WELLINGTON |access-date=2023-07-31 |website=www.clydeships.co.uk}}</ref> or at [[Whangārei]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=8 March 1913 |title=LOCAL AND GENERAL. Hawke's Bay Tribune |url=https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/newspapers/HBTRIB19130308.2.21 |access-date=2023-08-01 |website=paperspast.natlib.govt.nz}}</ref> |}

[[Tim Brown (American football)|Tim Brown]] – 1987 | AllAmericans = 109 | uniform = [[File:Notre dame football uniforms.png|250px]] | FightSong = [[Notre Dame Victory March]] | Mascot = [[Notre Dame Leprechaun]] | MarchingBand = [[Band of the Fighting Irish]] | PagFreeLabel = Outfitter | PagFreeValue = [[Under Armour]] | PagFreeLabel2 = | PagFreeValue2 = | WebsiteName = FightingIrish.com | WebsiteURL = https://fightingirish.com }} The '''Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team''' represents the [[University of Notre Dame]] in [[American football]]. It plays home games at the 77,622-capacity [[Notre Dame Stadium]], located near [[South Bend, Indiana|South Bend]] in [[Notre Dame, Indiana]]. Notre Dame is one of two schools that competes as an [[NCAA Division I FBS independent schools|independent]] at the [[National Collegiate Athletic Association]] (NCAA) [[NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision|Football Bowl Subdivision]] (FBS) level.{{NoteTag|Notre Dame plays five games a year against opponents from the [[Atlantic Coast Conference]] (ACC), of which it is a member in all other sports except [[Notre Dame Fighting Irish men's ice hockey|ice hockey]]."[http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/041913aad.html] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160327021448/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/041913aad.html|date=March 27, 2016}}"}} The Fighting Irish are one of college football's most prestigious and successful programs. It has won 15 [[College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS|national championships]] since its establishment in 1887, though the school only officially claims 11, including 8 from the major wire-services ([[AP National Championship Trophy|AP Poll]] and/or [[AFCA National Championship Trophy|Coaches' Poll]]).{{cite book | url=http://fs.ncaa.org/Docs/stats/football_records/2017/FBS.pdf | title=2017 NCAA Football Bowl Subdivision Records | publisher=The National Collegiate Athletic Association | date=July 2017 | location=Indianapolis | access-date=October 29, 2020 | pages=110–115 | archive-date=March 20, 2018 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180320073158/http://fs.ncaa.org/Docs/stats/football_records/2017/FBS.pdf | url-status=live }} Seven Notre Dame players have won the [[Heisman Trophy]]. Notre Dame has 962 official [[List of NCAA football teams by wins|victories]], with 21 having been vacated by the NCAA in 2016 for self-reported academic misconduct. The school recognizes 983 total wins in program history.{{Cite web |url=https://www.si.com/college/2018/02/13/notre-dame-fighting-irish-wins-vacated-ncaa-violations |title=Notre Dame forced to vacate wins from 2012, 2013 |date=February 13, 2018 |access-date=August 28, 2023 |archive-date=July 24, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190724213758/https://www.si.com/college-football/2018/02/13/notre-dame-fighting-irish-wins-vacated-ncaa-violations |url-status=live }} Notre Dame has had 22 undefeated seasons including 12 perfect seasons.{{Cite web |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/index.html |title=Notre Dame Fighting Irish College Football History, Stats, Records |access-date=August 28, 2023 |archive-date=December 25, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221225172201/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/index.html |url-status=live }} Notre Dame home games have been televised by [[Notre Dame Football on NBC|NBC]] since 1991.{{cite news|first=Richard|last=Sandomir|title=College Football – Notre Dame Scored a $38 Million Touchdown on Its TV Deal |url=https://query.nytimes.com/gst/fullpage.html?res=9D0CE0D91038F936A1575BC0A967958260|newspaper=The New York Times|date=August 25, 1991|access-date=April 6, 2008}}{{cite web|title=NBC and Notre Dame Extend Football Agreement Through 2010|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/121803aaa.html|website=CSTV.com|date=December 18, 2003|access-date=April 6, 2008|archive-date=May 15, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120515215030/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/121803aaa.html|url-status=dead}} The Fighting Irish have played in blue jerseys with gold helmets and highlights for much of their history. Recognizable team symbols include their fight song, the "[[Notre Dame Victory March]]", and the [[Notre Dame Leprechaun]] mascot. Their [[Notre Dame–USC football rivalry|fiercest and most prominent rivalry]] is with the [[USC Trojans football|USC Trojans]]; it arose from frequent competition for national championships and is one of the best-known in college football.{{cite web|first=Pete|last=Fiutak|title=The Ten Greatest College Football Rivalries|date=November 17, 2006|url=http://cfn.scout.com/2/591649.html|access-date=April 24, 2008|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070309013228/http://cfn.scout.com/2/591649.html|archive-date=March 9, 2007}}{{cite web|first=Dave|last=Revsine|title=Michigan, Ohio State set bar high for other rivalries|date=November 24, 2006|website=[[ESPN]]|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/columns/story?columnist=revsine_dave&id=2674267|access-date=March 24, 2009}}{{cite web|first=Bill|last=Bender|title=CFB 150: Top 10 college football rivalries of all time|website=[[Sporting News]]|date=November 27, 2019|url=https://www.sportingnews.com/us/ncaa-football/news/cfb-150-top-10-college-football-rivalries-of-all-time/1fvt0z837wj011gca5zv7wi8lo|access-date=January 16, 2022}}{{cite web|first=Chris|last=Mueller|title=7 of the Fiercest Rivalries in College Football History|date=September 29, 2021|website=history.com|publisher=[[History Channel]]|url=https://www.history.com/news/biggest-college-football-rivalries-all-time|access-date=January 16, 2022}} ==History== {{Main|History of Notre Dame Fighting Irish football}} {{See also|List of Notre Dame Fighting Irish football seasons}} ===Early history (1887–1917)=== [[File:Notre Dame football team (1887).jpg|thumb|left|The first football team fielded by the University in 1887]] Football did not have an auspicious beginning at the University of Notre Dame. In their inaugural game on November 23, 1887, the Irish lost to [[Michigan Wolverines football|Michigan]] by a score of 8–0.{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/first-game-in-notre-dame-football-history/|title=First game in Notre Dame Football history // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=February 5, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160205085401/http://125.nd.edu/moments/first-game-in-notre-dame-football-history/|url-status=live}} Their first win came in the final game of the [[1888 Notre Dame football team|1888 season]] when the Irish defeated Harvard Prep School of Chicago by a score of 20–0.{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/notre-dames-first-win/|title=Notre Dame's First Win // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=June 10, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170610150922/http://125.nd.edu/moments/notre-dames-first-win/|url-status=live}} At the end of the 1888 season, they had a record of 1–3 with all three losses being at the hands of [[Michigan Wolverines football|Michigan]]. Between 1887 and 1899, Notre Dame compiled a record of 31 wins, 15 losses, and 4 ties against a diverse variety of opponents ranging from local high school teams to other universities.{{cite web |title=Notre Dame's first team. |url=http://www.irishlegends.com/pages/outofbounds/outofbounds1.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210225173416/http://www.irishlegends.com/pages/outofbounds/outofbounds1.html |archive-date=February 25, 2021 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Irishlegends.com}} In 1894, [[James L. Morrison]] was hired as Notre Dame's first head football coach.{{cite web|url=http://buffalonews.com/2004/09/07/irish-coach-could-make-ugly-history/|title=Irish Coach Could Make Ugly History|date=September 7, 2004|website=Buffalonews.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005844/http://buffalonews.com/2004/09/07/irish-coach-could-make-ugly-history/|url-status=live}} Notre Dame took a significant step toward respectability, prominence, and stability when they hired Morrison.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jyCcJuRTOuYC&q=james+l.+morison+notre+dame&pg=PT17|title=The Notre Dame Football Encyclopedia|first=Michael R.|last=Steele|date=January 8, 2013|publisher=Skyhorse Publishing, Inc.|isbn=9781613213742|via=Google Books}} He wrote an acquaintance after his first day on the job: "I arrived here [Notre Dame] this morning and found about as green a set of football players that ever donned a uniform... They want to smoke, and when I told them that they would have to run and get up some wind, they thought I was rubbing it in on them. "One big, strong cuss remarked that it was too much like work. Well, maybe you think I didn't give him hell! I bet you a hundred no one ever makes a remark like that again." [[File:1905 Notre Dame football team.jpg|thumb|left|Notre Dame team of 1905]] In [[1908 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1908]], the win over Franklin saw end [[Fay Wood]] catch the first touchdown pass in Notre Dame history.{{cite web|url=http://www.archives.nd.edu/football/Football-1908s.pdf|title=Notre Dame Scholastic|date=December 1908|website=Archives.nd.edu|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=February 24, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210224133911/http://www.archives.nd.edu/football/Football-1908s.pdf|url-status=live}} Notre Dame continued its success near the turn of the century and achieved their first victory over Michigan in [[1909 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1909]] by the score of 11–3, after which Michigan refused to play Notre Dame again for 33 years. By the end of the [[1912 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1912 season]] the university's team had amassed a record of 108 wins, 31 losses, and 13 ties.{{cite web|url=http://madeira.hccanet.org/project2_spring2013/groenep2/history.html|title=Notre Dame Football - History|website=Madeira.hccanet.org|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060038/http://madeira.hccanet.org/project2_spring2013/groenep2/history.html|url-status=dead}} [[Jesse Harper]] became head coach in 1913, coaching for five years until retiring in 1917.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/harper_jesse00.html|title=Jesse Harper Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171015020835/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/harper_jesse00.html|archive-date=October 15, 2017|url-status=dead}} During his tenure, the Irish began playing only intercollegiate games and posted a record of 34 wins, 5 losses, and one tie.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/jesse-harper-1.html|title=Jesse Harper Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928103044/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/jesse-harper-1.html|url-status=live}} This period would also mark the beginning of the rivalry with [[Army Black Knights football|Army]] and the continuation of rivalry with [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]].{{cite web|url=http://magazine.nd.edu/news/notre-dame-vs-army-the-rivalry-that-shaped-college-football/|title=Echoes: Notre Dame vs. Army: The rivalry that shaped college football // News // Notre Dame Magazine // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR/PAZ // University Communications: Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=Magazine.nd.edu|date=November 11, 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite web|url=https://www.uhnd.com/history/rivalries/rivalries-notre-dame-michigan-state-series/|title=Notre Dame - Michigan State Football Rivalry // UHND.com|date=August 7, 2015|website=Uhnd.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 6, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170906030312/https://www.uhnd.com/history/rivalries/rivalries-notre-dame-michigan-state-series/|url-status=live}} In an effort to gain respect for a regionally successful but small-time Midwestern football program, Harper scheduled games in his first season with national powerhouses [[Texas Longhorns football|Texas]], [[Penn State Nittany Lions football|Penn State]], and [[Army Black Knights football|Army]].{{cite web |title=1913: The Program Changer |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/082713aah.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005831/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/082713aah.html |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Und.com}} That year, Notre Dame burst into the national consciousness and helped to transform the collegiate game in a single contest. [[File:RocknescoringonArmy.jpg|thumb|left|[[Knute Rockne]] running for a touchdown against Army after receiving a [[forward pass]], November 1, 1913]] On November 1, 1913, the Notre Dame squad stunned Army's Black Knights of the Hudson 35–13 in a game played at [[West Point, New York|West Point]].{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/this-day-in-history-the-forward-pass-1913-vs-army/|title=This Day In History: The Forward Pass (1913 vs. Army) // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=November 20, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171120054127/http://125.nd.edu/moments/this-day-in-history-the-forward-pass-1913-vs-army/|url-status=live}} Led by quarterback [[Gus Dorais]] and end [[Knute Rockne]], the Notre Dame team attacked the Cadets with an offense that featured both the expected powerful running game but also long and accurate downfield forward passes from Dorais to Rockne. This game has been miscredited as the invention of the forward pass.{{cite web |title=Notre Dame vs. Army: The game that changed college football 100 years ago |url=http://www.indystar.com/story/sports/college/2013/10/30/notre-dame-vs-army-the-game-that-changed-college-football-100-years-ago/3313083/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160105024352/http://www.indystar.com/story/sports/college/2013/10/30/notre-dame-vs-army-the-game-that-changed-college-football-100-years-ago/3313083/ |archive-date=January 5, 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Indystar.com}} Prior to this contest, receivers would come to a full stop and wait on the ball to come to them, but in this contest, Dorais threw to Rockne in stride, changing the forward pass from a seldom-used play into the dominant ball-moving strategy that it is today. ===Knute Rockne era (1918–1930)=== [[File:Knute Rockne on ship's deck.jpg|160px|thumb|Coach Knute Rockne]] Irish assistant [[Knute Rockne]] became head coach in 1918.{{cite web|url=http://www.rarenewspapers.com/view/633634|title=Knute Rockne named Notre Dame coach...|website=Rarenewspapers.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010124/http://www.rarenewspapers.com/view/633634|url-status=live}} During his 13 years, the Irish won three national championships, had five undefeated seasons, won the [[1925 Rose Bowl|Rose Bowl]] in 1925,{{cite web|url=http://bleacherreport.com/articles/472956-the-1925-rose-bowl-notre-dame-vs-stanford|title=The 1925 Rose Bowl: Notre Dame Vs. Stanford|first=Michael|last=Collins|publisher=[[Bleacher Report]]|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010000/http://bleacherreport.com/articles/472956-the-1925-rose-bowl-notre-dame-vs-stanford|url-status=live}} and produced players such as [[George Gipp]] and the "[[Four Horsemen (football)|Four Horsemen]]".{{cite web |date=9 July 2012 |title=Numbers don't tell story of Knute Rockne |url=https://www.espn.com/blog/notre-dame-football/post/_/id/7403/numbers-dont-tell-story-of-rockne |url-status=live
