|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010014/http://www.espn.com/blog/notre-dame-football/post/_/id/7403/numbers-dont-tell-story-of-rockne |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Espn.com}} Rockne's offenses employed the [[Notre Dame Box]] and his defenses ran a [[7–2–2 defense|7–2–2]] scheme.{{cite web|url=http://www.scout.com/nfl/bears/story/655115-chalk-talk-the-notre-dame-box|title=Chalk Talk: the Notre Dame Box|website=Scout.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LDqZHM-LUtMC&q=knute+rockne+7-2-2+defense&pg=PA56|title=The Notre Dame Football Encyclopedia: The Ultimate Guide to America's Favorite College Team|first1=Keith|last1=Marder|first2=Mark|last2=Spellen|first3=Jim|last3=Donovan|date=September 27, 2017|publisher=Citadel Press|isbn=9780806521084|via=Google Books|access-date=October 5, 2020|archive-date=November 7, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107155849/https://books.google.com/books?id=LDqZHM-LUtMC&q=knute+rockne+7-2-2+defense&pg=PA56#v=snippet&q=knute%20rockne%207-2-2%20defense&f=false|url-status=live}} Rockne took over in the war-torn season of [[1918 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1918]]{{cite web |title=1918 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1918-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055855/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1918-schedule.html |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} He made his coaching debut on September 28, 1918, against [[Case Western Reserve Spartans football|Case Tech]] in [[Cleveland, Ohio]], and earned a 26–6 victory.{{cite web |author=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre Dame |url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/this-day-in-history-rockne-takes-the-reins/ |title=This Day In History: Rockne Takes The Reins // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame |publisher=125.nd.edu |access-date=May 15, 2016 |archive-date=October 3, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151003120516/http://125.nd.edu/moments/this-day-in-history-rockne-takes-the-reins/ |url-status=live }} [[Leonard Bahan]], George Gipp, and [[Curly Lambeau]] were in the backfield.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-fb-a-ros-29.html|title=Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055828/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-fb-a-ros-29.html|archive-date=September 28, 2017|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=http://archives.nd.edu/findaids/ead/xml/ath.xml|format=XML|title=Notre Dame Athletics Collection : 1899-2011|website=Archives.nd.edu|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=November 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171126032412/http://archives.nd.edu/findaids/ead/xml/ath.xml|url-status=live}} With Gipp, Rockne had an ideal handler of the [[forward pass]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_RqQQHqQdAAC&pg=PA98|title=Shaping College Football|isbn=9780815608868|access-date=November 16, 2017|last1=Schmidt|first1=Raymond|date=18 June 2007|publisher=Syracuse University Press|archive-date=November 7, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107155851/https://books.google.com/books?id=_RqQQHqQdAAC&pg=PA98#v=onepage&q&f=false|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-W1JAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA662|title=Leslie's Weekly|website=Google.com|year=1921|access-date=November 16, 2017}} The Irish posted a 3–1–2 record for the season, losing only to the [[1918 Michigan Agricultural Aggies football team|Michigan Agricultural Aggies]]. The [[1919 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1919 team]] had Rockne handle the line and [[Gus Dorais]] handle the backfield.{{cite web |url=http://www.archives.nd.edu/Football/Football-1919.pdf |title=Notre Dame Football Review |access-date=May 15, 2016 |archive-date=September 6, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150906110718/http://www.archives.nd.edu/Football/Football-1919.pdf |url-status=live }} The Irish went undefeated and were one of four teams to be selected for the national championship, although Notre Dame does not claim it.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1919-schedule.html|title=1919 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060256/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1919-schedule.html|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/college-football-national-championship-history|title=College football national championship history|date=May 15, 2017|website=Ncaa.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=December 30, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221230171030/https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/college-football-national-championship-history|url-status=live}} [[File:George gipp football player.jpg|thumb|left|150px|George Gipp, "The Gipper"]] Gipp died at age 25 on December 14, [[1920 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1920]],{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1999-dec-14-sp-43713-story.html|title=Gipp Still an Inspiration Long After His Death|first=EARL|last=GUSTKEY|date=December 14, 1999|via=LA Times|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=July 15, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160715140609/http://articles.latimes.com/1999/dec/14/sports/sp-43713|url-status=live}} just two weeks after Walter Camp elected him as Notre Dame's first All-American.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/allambios/nd-m-footbl-gipp.html|title=Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150910185010/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/allambios/nd-m-footbl-gipp.html|archive-date=September 10, 2015|url-status=dead}} Gipp likely contracted [[Streptococcal pharyngitis|strep throat]] and pneumonia while giving [[Punt (gridiron football)|punting]] lessons after his final game on November 20 against [[Northwestern Wildcats football|Northwestern]]. Since [[antibiotics]] were not available in the 1920s, treatment options for such infections were limited and they could be fatal even to young, healthy individuals.{{cite web|url=https://www.healthychildren.org/English/health-issues/conditions/treatments/Pages/The-History-of-Antibiotics.aspx|title=The History of Antibiotics|website=HealthyChildren.org|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=March 17, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170317130031/https://www.healthychildren.org/English/health-issues/conditions/treatments/Pages/The-History-of-Antibiotics.aspx|url-status=live}} Rockne was speaking to Gipp on his hospital bed when he was purported to have delivered the famous "Win one for the Gipper" line.{{cite web|url=https://parade.com/157772/michaelosacky/what-does-it-mean-to-win-one-for-the-gipper/|title=What Does It Mean to 'Win One For The Gipper?'|first=Michael|last=Osacky|website=Parade.com|date=20 September 2013|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060031/https://parade.com/157772/michaelosacky/what-does-it-mean-to-win-one-for-the-gipper/|url-status=live}}{{Cite book | last = Keyes | first = Ralph | title = The quote verifier: who said what, where, and when | publisher = St. Martin's Press | year = 2006 | url = https://archive.org/details/quoteverifierwho00keye | url-access = registration | page = [https://archive.org/details/quoteverifierwho00keye/page/78 78] | quote = win just one for the gipper. | isbn = 0-312-34004-4 }}{{cite magazine| url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/vault/article/magazine/MAG1082275/index.htm | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121026112339/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/vault/article/magazine/MAG1082275/index.htm | url-status=dead | archive-date=October 26, 2012 | magazine=Sports Illustrated| title=Letter From The Publisher | date=April 14, 1969 | access-date=May 3, 2010}}{{cite magazine| url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/vault/article/magazine/MAG1095375/index.htm | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121026112331/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/vault/article/magazine/MAG1095375/index.htm | url-status=dead | archive-date=October 26, 2012 | magazine=Sports Illustrated | title=Win One For The Gipper | date=September 17, 1979 | access-date=May 3, 2010}} [[File:RogerKiley.jpg|thumb|140px|Roger Kiley]] [[John Mohardt]] led the [[1921 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1921 Notre Dame team]] to a 10–1 record with 781 rushing yards, 995 passing yards, 12 rushing touchdowns, and nine passing touchdowns.{{cite book|author1=Keith Marder |author2=Mark Spellen |author3=Jim Donovan |title=The Notre Dame Football Encyclopedia: The Ultimate Guide to America's Favorite College Team|publisher=Citadel Press|year=2001|isbn=0806521082|page=148}} [[Grantland Rice]] wrote that "Mohardt could throw the ball to within a foot or two of any given space" and noted that the 1921 Notre Dame team "was the first team we know of to build its attack around a forward passing game, rather than use a forward passing game as a mere aid to the running game."{{cite news|title=Where The West Got The Jump: In Addition To Developing Strong Defense and Good Running Game, Has Built Up Forward Pass|author=Grantland Rice|publisher=American Golfer|date=December 3, 1921|url=http://library.la84.org/SportsLibrary/AmericanGolfer/1921/ag2424o.pdf|access-date=August 19, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304062111/http://library.la84.org/SportsLibrary/AmericanGolfer/1921/ag2424o.pdf|archive-date=March 4, 2016|url-status=dead}} Mohardt had both [[Eddie Anderson (American football coach)|Eddie Anderson]] and [[Roger Kiley]] at [[End (American football)|end]] to receive his passes.{{cite web|url=http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1948/10/01/page/49/article/notre-dame-greats-will-pay-tribute-to-slater-today|title=Chicago Tribune - Historical Newspapers|website=Archives.chicagotribune.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=November 7, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107155857/https://www.chicagotribune.com/|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://www.indiana-football.org/?q=node/584|title=Rockne, Knute – Indiana Football Hall of Fame|website=Indiana-football.org|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010141/http://www.indiana-football.org/?q=node/584|url-status=live}} The national champion [[1924 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1924 team]] included the "[[Four Horsemen (football)|Four Horsemen]]" backfield of [[Harry Stuhldreher]], [[Don Miller (American football, born 1902)|Don Miller]], [[Jim Crowley]], and [[Elmer Layden]].{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-fb-a-nattit.html|title=Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151223190232/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-fb-a-nattit.html|archive-date=December 23, 2015|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/october-18-1924-the-four-horsemen-arrive/|title=October 18, 1924: The Four Horsemen Arrive // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=June 2, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170602230753/http://125.nd.edu/moments/october-18-1924-the-four-horsemen-arrive/|url-status=live}} The line was known as the "Seven Mules".{{cite web|url=http://ndfootballhistory.com/category/seven-mules|title=Seven Mules - Forever Irish|website=Ndfootballhistory.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055739/http://ndfootballhistory.com/category/seven-mules|url-status=live}} The Irish capped an undefeated, 10–0 season with a victory over [[1924 Stanford football team|Stanford]] in the [[1925 Rose Bowl|Rose Bowl]]. The [[1926 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1926 team]] beat [[1926 Army Cadets football team|Army]] and was led by [[Christie Flanagan]],{{cite web|url=https://newyorkerstateofmind.com/tag/notre-dame-vs-army/|title=Notre Dame vs. Army – A New Yorker State of Mind|website=Newyorkerstateofmind.com|date=April 25, 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005810/https://newyorkerstateofmind.com/tag/notre-dame-vs-army/|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/vault/1979/09/10/823950/we-know-of-knute-yet-know-him-not-fact-the-real-knute-rockne-was-the-most-successful-of-football-coaches-fancy-the-rockne-of-books-and-film-was-largely-myth|title=We Know of Knute, Yet Know Him Not|first=Coles|last=Phinizy|website=Si.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005932/https://www.si.com/vault/1979/09/10/823950/we-know-of-knute-yet-know-him-not-fact-the-real-knute-rockne-was-the-most-successful-of-football-coaches-fancy-the-rockne-of-books-and-film-was-largely-myth|url-status=live}} but was undone by a loss to [[1926 Carnegie Mellon Tartans football team|Carnegie Tech]] in the penultimate game of the season. In this game, Rockne made what an [[Associated Press]] writer called "one of the greatest coaching blunders in history" by traveling to Chicago for the [[Army–Navy Game]] to "write newspaper articles about it, as well as select an All-America football team." Carnegie Tech used the coach's absence as motivation for a 19–0 win; the upset likely cost the Irish a chance for a national title.{{cite news |author=Robinson, Alan |date=September 9, 2007 |title=Rockne's gaffe remembered |url=http://media.www.dailytexanonline.com/media/storage/paper410/news/2007/09/06/Sports/Rocknes.Gaffe.Remembered-2953261.shtml |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070908073255/http://media.www.dailytexanonline.com/media/storage/paper410/news/2007/09/06/Sports/Rocknes.Gaffe.Remembered-2953261.shtml |archive-date=September 8, 2007 |access-date=September 6, 2007 |work=The Daily Texan |publisher=Texas Student Media |df=mdy}} [[File:Football Notre Dame (South Bend) by South Shore Line - DPLA - 48571238a9311c41362cd1d3bdcb643e.jpg|thumb|A 1926 South Shore Line broadside advertising entertainment such as Notre Dame football games, to encourage use of the railroad between Chicago and South Bend.]] The [[1928 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1928 team]] lost to national champion [[1928 Georgia Tech Golden Tornado football team|Georgia Tech]].{{cite web|url=http://archives.nd.edu/Football/Football-1928.pdf|title=1928 Notre Dame Football|website=Archives.nd.edu|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=March 4, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304045356/http://www.archives.nd.edu/Football/Football-1928.pdf|url-status=live}} "I sat at [[Grant Field]] and saw a magnificent Notre Dame team suddenly recoil before the furious pounding of one man–[[Peter Pund]]," said Rockne. "Nobody could stop him. I counted 20 scoring plays that this man ruined."{{cite web |url=http://www.gshf.org/site/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=254&Itemid=50 |title=Henry R. "Peter" Pund |publisher=Georgia Sports Hall of Fame and Museum |work=Inductees |access-date=August 11, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070928081502/http://www.gshf.org/site/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=254&Itemid=50 |archive-date=September 28, 2007 |url-status=dead |df=mdy }} Among the events that occurred during Rockne's tenure none may be more famous than the Rockne's "Win one for the Gipper" speech.{{cite web|url=http://www.newsday.com/sports/college/college-football/rockne-s-win-one-for-the-gipper-speech-1.2476136|title=Rockne's 'Win one for the Gipper' speech|website=Newsday.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055802/http://www.newsday.com/sports/college/college-football/rockne-s-win-one-for-the-gipper-speech-1.2476136|url-status=live}} Army came into the 1928 matchup undefeated and was the clear favorite.{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/packages/html/sports/year_in_sports/11.10.html|title=The New York Times: This Day In Sports|website=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=January 7, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180107130813/http://www.nytimes.com/packages/html/sports/year_in_sports/11.10.html|url-status=live}} Notre Dame, on the other hand, was having their worst season under Rockne's leadership and entered the game with a 4–2 record. At the end of the half Army was leading and looked to be in command of the game. Rockne entered the locker room and gave his account of Gipp's final words: ''"I've got to go, Rock. It's all right. I'm not afraid. Some time, Rock, when the team is up against it, when things are going wrong and the breaks are beating the boys, tell them to go in there with all they've got and win just one for the Gipper. I don't know where I'll be then, Rock. But I'll know about it, and I'll be happy."''{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/this-day-in-history-win-one-for-the-gipper/|title=This Day In History: Win One For The Gipper // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=May 13, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170513093357/http://125.nd.edu/moments/this-day-in-history-win-one-for-the-gipper/|url-status=live}} The speech inspired the team and they went on to upset Army and win the game 12–6.{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2010/11/17/sports/ncaafootball/17army.html|title=Army-Notre Dame Game Stirs Yankee Stadium's Football Ghosts|first=Bill|last=Pennington|date=November 16, 2010|website=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=August 12, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170812064642/http://www.nytimes.com/2010/11/17/sports/ncaafootball/17army.html|url-status=live}} The [[1929 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1929]] and [[1930 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1930]] teams both went undefeated,{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1929-schedule.html|title=1929 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903121643/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1929-schedule.html|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1930-schedule.html|title=1930 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903122313/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1930-schedule.html|url-status=live}} winning national championships,{{cite web|url=https://www.uhnd.com/history/national-championships/notre-dame-1929-national-championship/|title=Notre Dame's 1929 National Championship - Notre Dame Football History|date=June 24, 2013|website=Uhnd.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903075548/https://www.uhnd.com/history/national-championships/notre-dame-1929-national-championship/|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.uhnd.com/history/national-championships/notre-dame-1930-national-championship/|title=Notre Dame's 1930 National Championship // Notre Dame Football History|date=July 3, 2013|website=Uhnd.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903081508/https://www.uhnd.com/history/national-championships/notre-dame-1930-national-championship/|url-status=live}} and the 1930 team was led by the likes of [[Frank Carideo]], [[Joe Savoldi]], [[Marchmont Schwartz|Marchy Schwartz]] and [[Marty Brill (American football)|Marty Brill]]. It featured the first and only example of all four members of a backfield being named to an All-American team during the same season. The 1929 team played all of its games on the road while the new [[Notre Dame Stadium]] was being built.{{cite web|url=http://archives.nd.edu/Football/Football-1929.pdf|title=1929 Notre Dame Football|website=Archives.nd.edu|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=June 5, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190605164505/http://archives.nd.edu/Football/Football-1929.pdf|url-status=live}} In 1930, "Jumping Joe" Savoldi scored the first Notre Dame touchdown in the new stadium on a 98-yard kickoff return.{{cite web|url=http://www.tiptop25.com/champ1930.html|title=1930 College Football National Championship|website=Tiptop25.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903080003/http://www.tiptop25.com/champ1930.html|url-status=live}} Savoldi is also known as "the first hero in the lore of Notre Dame's Stadium" based on scoring three touchdowns in the official stadium dedication game against Navy the following week.{{cite web|url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1955&dat=19301012&id=sG8hAAAAIBAJ&pg=2617,2207104&hl=en|title=Reading Eagle - Google News Archive Search|website=News.google.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=April 6, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220406234136/https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1955&dat=19301012&id=sG8hAAAAIBAJ&pg=2617,2207104&hl=en|url-status=live}} Rockne coached his last game on December 14, 1930, when he led a group of Notre Dame all-stars against the [[New York Giants]] in New York City.{{cite web|url=http://mmqb.si.com/2014/05/20/nfl-history-in-95-objects-notre-dame-new-york-giants-program|title=Notre Dame vs. New York Giants 1930 Game Program|website=Mmqb.si.com|date=May 20, 2014 |access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=February 2, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170202145507/http://mmqb.si.com/2014/05/20/nfl-history-in-95-objects-notre-dame-new-york-giants-program|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/the-game-that-put-the-nfls-reputation-on-the-line-84128969/|title=The Game that Put the NFL's Reputation on the Line|first=Gilbert|last=King|website=Smithsonianmag.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903074551/http://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/the-game-that-put-the-nfls-reputation-on-the-line-84128969/|url-status=live}} The game raised funds for the Mayor's Relief Committee for the unemployed and needy of the city.{{cite web |title=Rockne's Last Game |url=http://hapmoran.org/giantsnd/Giants_vs_Notre_Dame_page1.htm |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110726140844/http://hapmoran.org/giantsnd/Giants_vs_Notre_Dame_page1.htm |archive-date=July 26, 2011 |access-date=May 24, 2008}} 50,000 fans turned out to see the reunited "Four Horsemen" along with players from Rockne's other championship teams take the field against the pros.New York Times December 15, 1930 On March 31, 1931, Rockne died at age 43 in [[1931 Transcontinental & Western Air Fokker F-10 crash|the crash of a Transcontinental & Western Air airliner]] in [[Kansas]]; he was on his way to help in the production of the film [[The Spirit of Notre Dame]].{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/learning/general/onthisday/bday/0304.html|title=Knute Rocke Dies with Seven Others in Mail Plane Dive|work=The New York Times |access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 15, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170915113939/http://www.nytimes.com/learning/general/onthisday/bday/0304.html|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/movie/review?res=9F0CEEDB123BE23ABC4E52DFB667838A629EDE|title=Movie Review: The Screen – Close-Ups of Gridiron Stars|website=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=December 1, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161201144104/http://www.nytimes.com/movie/review?res=9F0CEEDB123BE23ABC4E52DFB667838A629EDE|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.tcm.com/tcmdb/title/91080/the-spirit-of-notre-dame|title=The Spirit of Notre Dame (1931) - Overview - TCM.com|website=Turner Classic Movies|access-date=June 18, 2017|archive-date=September 15, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170915070409/http://www.tcm.com/tcmdb/title/91080/The-Spirit-of-Notre-Dame/|url-status=live}} The crash site is located in a remote expanse of Kansas known as the [[Flint Hills]] and now features a Rockne Memorial.{{cite web |url=http://www.collegefootball.org/news.php?id=295 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081007022928/http://www.collegefootball.org/news.php?id=295 |url-status=dead |archive-date=October 7, 2008 |publisher=College Football News |title=College Football Hall of Fame's "Road Show" Kansas-Bound for Unveiling of Knute Rockne Memorial |access-date=May 24, 2008 |df=mdy-all }} As Notre Dame's head coach from 1918 to 1930, Rockne posted what has remained for decades the all-time highest winning percentage (.881) for a football coach in the NCAA's flagship FBS division.{{Cite web |last=Scofield |first=Dan |date=October 1, 2009 |title=The True Story Of Knute Rockne, College Football's Most Renowned Coach |url=http://bleacherreport.com/articles/265043-the-true-story-of-knute-rockne-college-footballs-most-renowned-coach |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170915113307/http://bleacherreport.com/articles/265043-the-true-story-of-knute-rockne-college-footballs-most-renowned-coach |archive-date=September 15, 2017 |access-date=June 11, 2017 |publisher=[[Bleacher Report]]}}{{cite web|url=http://www.history.com/this-day-in-history/knute-rockne-born|title=Knute Rockne born - Mar 04, 1888|website=History.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=November 11, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171111094139/http://www.history.com/this-day-in-history/knute-rockne-born|url-status=live}} During his 13-year tenure as head coach of the Fighting Irish, Rockne collected 105 victories, 12 losses, 5 ties and 3 national championships.{{cite web |title=Knute Rockne Coaching Record |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/knute-rockne-1.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160405102645/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/knute-rockne-1.html |archive-date=April 5, 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}}{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/trads/rockne.html|title=UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171116022856/http://www.und.com/trads/rockne.html|archive-date=November 16, 2017|url-status=dead}} Rockne also coached Notre Dame to 5 undefeated and untied seasons. ===Heartley Anderson era (1931–1933)=== Through game broadcasts during the [[Golden Age of Radio]], Notre Dame football gained a nationwide following of "subway alumni", Catholics who became fans whether or not they attended the university.{{cite news | url=http://thequad.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/11/23/for-a-notre-dame-family-a-legacy-of-optimism-with-an-asterisk-or-two/ | title=For a Notre Dame Family, a Legacy of Optimism (With an Asterisk or Two) | work=The New York Times | date=November 23, 2012 | access-date=November 23, 2012 | author=Carter, Bill | archive-date=November 24, 2012 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121124061254/http://thequad.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/11/23/for-a-notre-dame-family-a-legacy-of-optimism-with-an-asterisk-or-two/ | url-status=live }} Former [[Saint Louis Billikens football|Saint Louis]] head coach [[Heartley "Hunk" Anderson]] was promoted from assistant coach and took the helm of the Irish after Knute Rockne's death, leading them to a record of 16 wins, nine losses, and two ties.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/hunk-anderson-1.html|title=Hunk Anderson Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=August 12, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170812102651/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/hunk-anderson-1.html|url-status=live}} Anderson was a former Irish player under Rockne and was serving as an assistant coach at the time of Rockne's death. Notre Dame finished 6–2–1 in [[1931 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1931]].{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1931-schedule.html|title=1931 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=August 12, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170812063834/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1931-schedule.html|url-status=live}} The Irish lost a heartbreaker by a score of 16–14 to [[USC Trojans football|USC]] on November 21 that snapped the program's 26-game unbeaten streak.{{cite web|url=http://www.history.com/this-day-in-history/usc-ends-notre-dame-winning-streak|title=USC ends Notre Dame winning streak - Nov 21, 1931|website=History.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=November 24, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171124124526/http://www.history.com/this-day-in-history/usc-ends-notre-dame-winning-streak|url-status=live}} [[1933 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1933]] was a tough year for the Irish as they finished with a 3–5–2 record, the first losing season for the program since 1888. ND suffered a four-game losing streak, failing to score a point in all four losses to [[Carnegie Mellon Tartans football|Carnegie Tech]], [[Pittsburgh Panthers football|Pittsburgh]], [[Navy Midshipmen football|Navy]], and [[Purdue Boilermakers football|Purdue]].{{cite web |title=1933 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1933-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170812060614/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1933-schedule.html |archive-date=August 12, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} Anderson resigned as Irish head coach after the 1933 season to accept the position of head football coach at [[NC State Wolfpack football|NC State]].{{cite web |date=October 4, 2016 |title=TheWolfpacker.com - NC State football has past ties with Notre Dame |url=https://ncstate.rivals.com/news/nc-state-football-has-past-ties-with-notre-dame |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170812063100/https://ncstate.rivals.com/news/nc-state-football-has-past-ties-with-notre-dame |archive-date=August 12, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Ncstate.rivals.com}} ===Elmer Layden era (1934–1940)=== [[File:1924-Four-Horsemen-Notre-Dame.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Layden (second from left) was a member of Knute Rockne's legendary "Four Horsemen" backfield of 1924.]] Anderson was replaced by [[Elmer Layden]], who was one of Rockne's "Four Horsemen" of 1924. After graduating, Layden played professional football for one year and then began a coaching career.{{cite web |title=Elmer Layden Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/layden_elmer00.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005749/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/layden_elmer00.html |archive-date=2018-08-20 |access-date=2018-08-19}} Layden's [[1935 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1935 squad]] posted one of the greatest wins in school history by rallying to defeat [[1935 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State]] by a score of 18–13 in a game billed as the "[[Game of the Century (college football)|Game of the Century]]".{{cite web|url=http://www.foxsports.com/college-football/story/notre-dame-fighting-irish-ohio-state-buckeyes-all-time-series-shakespeare-010116|title=The Ohio State-Notre Dame series is nothing if not unique - FOX Sports|date=January 1, 2016|access-date=May 29, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042325/http://www.foxsports.com/college-football/story/notre-dame-fighting-irish-ohio-state-buckeyes-all-time-series-shakespeare-010116|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/notre-dames-first-game-of-the-century-1935/|title=Notre Dame's First "Game of the Century," 1935 // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=July 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170726064322/http://125.nd.edu/moments/notre-dames-first-game-of-the-century-1935/|url-status=live}} His [[1938 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1938 team]] finished 8–1, losing only to [[USC Trojans football|USC]] in the season finale.{{cite web |title=1938 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1938-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926144328/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1938-schedule.html |archive-date=September 26, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} This loss cost them a possible consensus national championship, but the team was named national champion by the [[Dickinson System]].{{cite web|url=http://www.collegefootballpoll.com/champions_national.html|title=College Football Poll.com|website=Collegefootballpoll.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110708175404/http://www.collegefootballpoll.com/champions_national.html|archive-date=July 8, 2011|df=mdy-all}} Like Rockne before him, Layden was a goodwill ambassador for Notre Dame during his time as head coach.{{cite web|url=http://stevencwelsh.com/nd/notre-dame-ad-sept-103.php|title=Notre Dame Football :: Depth Chart :: SCW|website=Stevencwelsh.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042644/http://stevencwelsh.com/nd/notre-dame-ad-sept-103.php|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://magazine.nd.edu/news/domers-in-the-news-4/|title=Domers in the news // News // Notre Dame Magazine // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR/PAZ // University Communications: Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=magazine.nd.edu|date=December 4, 2008 }} He was able to schedule a home-and-home series with [[Michigan Wolverines football|Michigan]] after meeting with [[Fielding H. Yost]], healing a rift between the two schools.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zk92GDJuDWIC&q=elmer+layden+fielding+yost&pg=PA120|title=Natural Enemies: Major College Football's Oldest, Fiercest Rivalry-Michigan vs. Notre Dame|first=John|last=Kryk|date=August 20, 2007|publisher=Taylor Trade Publishing|isbn=9781461733737|via=Google Books}} The two teams had not met since 1909, when, after eight straight losses to the Wolverines, the Irish posted their first win.{{cite web|url=http://www.archives.nd.edu/about/news/index.php/2010/nd-vs-michigan/|title=Notre Dame vs. Michigan - Notre Dame Archives News & Notes|website=Archives.nd.edu|date=9 September 2010|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 8, 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190908072804/http://www.archives.nd.edu/about/news/index.php/2010/nd-vs-michigan/|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://bleacherreport.com/articles/1348076-michigan-notre-dame-gridiron-feud-dates-back-to-1909|title=Michigan vs. Notre Dame Gridiron Feud Dates Back to 1909|first=Joel|last=Greer|website=[[Bleacher Report]]|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926041909/http://bleacherreport.com/articles/1348076-michigan-notre-dame-gridiron-feud-dates-back-to-1909|url-status=live}} They were scheduled to meet again in 1910, but Michigan canceled the game and refused to play the Irish again. By the time they met again in 1943, Layden had left Notre Dame and [[Frank Leahy]] had taken his place. The Irish posted a record of 47 wins, 13 losses, and three ties in seven years under Layden,{{cite web |title=Elmer Layden Coaching Record |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/elmer-layden-1.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060234/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/elmer-layden-1.html |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} the most successful record of a Notre Dame coach not to win a national championship.{{cite web |date=July 1, 1973 |title=Elmer Layden Dead |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1973/07/01/archives/elmer-layden-dead-fullback-of-notre-dames-4-horsemen-pierced.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060534/http://www.nytimes.com/1973/07/01/archives/elmer-layden-dead-fullback-of-notre-dames-4-horsemen-pierced.html |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=[[The New York Times]]}} He left Notre Dame after the 1940 season to become [[Commissioner of the National Football League]]. ===Frank Leahy era (1941–1953)=== [[File:Frank Leahy 1949.png|thumb|Frank Leahy]] [[Boston College Eagles football|Boston College]] head coach [[Frank Leahy]] was hired by Notre Dame to take over for Layden in 1941 and was another former Irish player who played during the Rockne era. After graduating from Notre Dame, Leahy held several coaching positions, including line coach of the [[Seven Blocks of Granite]] of [[Fordham Rams football, 1940-1949|Fordham University]] that helped that team win all but two of their games between 1935 and 1937.{{cite web |title=Frank Leahy Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/frank_leahy_94848.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005826/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/frank_leahy_94848.html |archive-date=2018-08-20 |access-date=2018-08-19}} He then coached the [[Boston College Eagles football|Boston College Eagles]] to a win in the [[1941 Sugar Bowl]] and a share of the national championship.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/111715aaa.html|title=Frank Leahy: A Coach For Two Schools|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005933/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/111715aaa.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=https://www.saturdaydownsouth.com/games/1941sugarbowltennvsbc/|title=1941 Sugar Bowl Tennessee vs Boston College|date=August 1, 2014|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010145/https://www.saturdaydownsouth.com/games/1941sugarbowltennvsbc/|url-status=live}} His move to Notre Dame began a new period of gridiron success for the Irish and ensured Leahy's place among the winningest coaches in the history of college football.{{College Football HoF|id=1606|name=Frank "The Master" Leahy |access-date=April 27, 2008}} Leahy coached the team for 11 seasons from 1941 to 1943 and 1946 to 1953. After finishing in the AP top 10 each of his first two seasons, Leahy's [[1943 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1943 team]] went 9–1 and captured Notre Dame's fourth national championship (and first since the advent of the [[AP poll|AP Poll]]). Quarterback [[Angelo Bertelli]] became Notre Dame's first [[Heisman Trophy]] winner. From 1944 to 1945, Leahy served in the [[United States Navy|U.S. Navy]] during [[World War II]] and was honorably discharged as a [[Captain (naval)|captain]].{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/frank-leahy-joins-the-navy/|title=Frank Leahy Joins The Navy // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=June 10, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170610144944/http://125.nd.edu/moments/frank-leahy-joins-the-navy/|url-status=live}} [[Edward McKeever]], Leahy's assistant coach, became interim head coach when Leahy left for the Navy.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/mckeever_ed00.html|title=Ed McKeever Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005945/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/mckeever_ed00.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} During [[1944 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1944]], his one year at the helm, the Irish managed 8 wins and 2 losses.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1944-schedule.html|title=1944 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060317/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1944-schedule.html|url-status=live}} McKeever left Notre Dame in 1945 to take over as head coach of [[Cornell Big Red football|Cornell]].{{cite web|url=https://cdsun.library.cornell.edu/cgi-bin/cornell?a=d&d=CDS19500919-01.2.61|title=The Cornell Daily Sun 19 September 1950 — The Cornell Daily Sun|website=cdsun.library.cornell.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005815/http://cdsun.library.cornell.edu/cgi-bin/cornell?a=d&d=CDS19500919-01.2.61|url-status=live}} For the [[1945 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1945 season]], he was replaced by [[Hugh Devore]], who led the Irish to a 7–2–1 record.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1945-schedule.html|title=1945 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060003/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1945-schedule.html|url-status=live}} Upon Leahy's return in 1946 and due to his status as a veteran himself, the Irish heavily recruited returning veterans who were often much older than incoming freshmen. Largely thanks to this influx of talent, Notre Dame went four consecutive seasons after World War II without losing a game, winning the [[1946 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1946]], [[1947 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1947]], and [[1949 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1949]] national championships in the process.{{Cite web |last=Weinreb |first=Michael |date=February 4, 2015 |title=» How Notre Dame's 1946 Class Forever Altered the Recruiting Landscape |url=https://grantland.com/the-triangle/notre-dame-frank-leahy-1946-football-recruiting-class-signing-day-impact/ |access-date=2024-08-19 |website=Grantland |language=en-US}} The [[1946 Army vs. Notre Dame football game|1946 game against Army]], ending in a 0–0 tie, pitted two of the greatest teams of all time against each other and included three consecutive Heisman Trophy winners ([[1946 Army Cadets football team|Army]]'s [[Doc Blanchard]] and [[Glenn Davis (halfback)|Glenn Davis]] and Notre Dame's [[Johnny Lujack]], who quarterbacked the 1946 and 1947 Irish). The [[1948 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1948 team]] missed out on a national championship due only to a 14–14 tie at [[1948 USC Trojans football team|USC]] in the final game of the season. In 1949, Notre Dame went unbeaten and untied for the second time in three seasons behind Heisman Trophy-winning [[End (gridiron football)|end]] [[Leon Hart]], who would later be selected first overall in the [[1950 NFL draft]]. A fifth national championship was lost because of a 1953 tie against [[1953 Iowa Hawkeyes football team|Iowa]], in a game that featured 1953 [[Heisman Trophy]] winner [[Johnny Lattner]]{{cite web |title=Heisman Trophy |url=http://heisman.com/roster.aspx?rp_id=19 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171204203410/http://heisman.com/roster.aspx?rp_id=19 |archive-date=December 4, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Heisman.com}} that caused a minor scandal at the time, when it appeared that some Irish players had faked injuries to stop the clock, leading some to nickname those players the "Fainting Irish".{{cite web |date=28 January 2014 |title=Historical Debate: The '53 Fainting Irish |url=http://www.everydayshouldbesaturday.com/2014/1/28/5354924/historical-debate-iowa-and-notre-dame |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928145825/https://www.everydayshouldbesaturday.com/2014/1/28/5354924/historical-debate-iowa-and-notre-dame |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=June 11, 2017}}{{cite web |title=Golden Memories |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/110903aaa.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151210220718/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/110903aaa.html |archive-date=2015-12-10 |access-date=2018-08-19}}{{cite web |date=January 4, 2013 |title=Hawkeye Flashback: 1953 Notre Dame |url=http://nealrozendaal.com/2013/01/04/hawkeye-flashback-1953-notre-dame/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010401/http://nealrozendaal.com/2013/01/04/hawkeye-flashback-1953-notre-dame/ |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=August 19, 2018}} Leahy retired in 1954 reportedly due to health issues.{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/vault/1955/10/31/596398/subject-frank-leahy|title=Subject: Frank Leahy|first=Gerald|last=Holland|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055839/https://www.si.com/vault/1955/10/31/596398/subject-frank-leahy|url-status=live}} Perhaps the best example of this occurred during the [[Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football|Georgia Tech]] game in 1953. Leahy fell ill during the game, which led to him collapsing during halftime.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-oyvMRckq0gC&q=frank+leahy+collapse+georgia+tech+1953&pg=PA46|title=A Farewell to Glory: The Rise and Fall of an Epic Football Rivalry Boston College Vs. Holy Cross|first=Wally|last=Carew|date=September 1, 2012|publisher=Xlibris Corporation|isbn=9781479702503|via=Google Books}}{{Self-published inline|certain=yes|date=December 2017}} The situation was so dire that a priest was called in to give Leahy Catholic [[last rites]].{{cite web|url=https://notredame.rivals.com/news/notre-dame-father-hesburgh-a-centennial-remembered|title=BlueAndGold.com - Notre Dame & Father Hesburgh: A Centennial Remembered|date=May 25, 2017|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=July 1, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170701005055/https://notredame.rivals.com/news/notre-dame-father-hesburgh-a-centennial-remembered|url-status=live}} However, Leahy recovered, and the consequent diagnosis was that he was suffering from nervous tension and pancreatitis.Gildea, William; Jennison, Christopher (1976). ''The Fighting Irish: Notre Dame Football Through the Years''. Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice-Hall. {{ISBN|0-13-314641-3}} Leahy has the second highest winning percentage (.864) of any college coach in history. He led the Irish to a record of 87 wins, 11 losses, and nine ties including 39 consecutive games without a loss (37–0–2), four national championships, and six undefeated seasons.{{cite web |title=Frank Leahy Coaching Record |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/frank-leahy-1.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060303/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/frank-leahy-1.html |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} ===Terry Brennan era (1954–1958)=== The departure of Frank Leahy ushered in a downward slope in Notre Dame's performance.{{cite web|url=https://magazine.nd.edu/news/notre-dame-football-the-indisputable-importance-of-saturday/|title=Notre Dame Football: The Indisputable Importance of Saturday // News // Notre Dame Magazine // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR/PAZ // University Communications: Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=magazine.nd.edu|date=May 7, 2005 |access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 11, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170911172830/https://magazine.nd.edu/news/notre-dame-football-the-indisputable-importance-of-saturday/|url-status=live}} 25-year old assistant coach [[Terry Brennan]] was hired as Frank Leahy's successor as the Notre Dame head coach in 1954 and would stay until 1958. Brennan was a former player under Leahy. Before joining the Irish, he had coached the [[Mount Carmel High School (Chicago)|Mount Carmel High School]] team in Chicago.{{cite web |title=Terry Brennan Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/brennan_terry00.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820074607/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/brennan_terry00.html |archive-date=2018-08-20 |access-date=2018-08-19}} His first two seasons the Irish were ranked fourth and ninth respectively in the AP poll, with a loss to [[1954 Purdue Boilermakers football team|Purdue]] the only blemish on the [[1954 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1954 team]]'s schedule.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-footbl-archive.html|title=Notre Dame Football Archives :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171128073549/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-footbl-archive.html|archive-date=November 28, 2017|url-status=dead}} It was the [[1956 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1956 season]] that began to darken Brennan's reputation, for it became one of the most dismal in the team's history and saw them finish the season with a mere two wins, including losses to [[1956 Michigan State Spartans football team|Michigan State]], [[1956 Oklahoma Sooners football team|Oklahoma]], and [[1956 Iowa Hawkeyes football team|Iowa]].{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/notre-dames-fifth-heisman-trophy-paul-hornung-1956/|title=Notre Dame's Fifth Heisman Trophy: Paul Hornung (1956) // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=March 13, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170313064633/http://125.nd.edu/moments/notre-dames-fifth-heisman-trophy-paul-hornung-1956/|url-status=live}} One bright spot in the 1956 season was the awarding of the [[Heisman Trophy]] to [[Paul Hornung]], who would go on to a legendary NFL career with the [[Green Bay Packers]]. Hornung is the only Heisman winner to win the award while playing for a team that had a losing record.{{cite web |title=Heisman Trophy |url=http://heisman.com/roster.aspx?rp_id=22 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171030191941/http://heisman.com/roster.aspx?rp_id=22 |archive-date=October 30, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Heisman.com}} The Irish would recover [[1957 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|the following season]], posting a record of 7–3{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1957-schedule.html|title=1957 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060213/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1957-schedule.html|url-status=live}} and including in their wins a stunning upset of [[Oklahoma Sooners football|Oklahoma]], in [[Norman, Oklahoma]], that ended the Sooners' still-standing record of 47 consecutive wins.{{cite news|url=https://www.espn.com/colleges/oklahoma/football/story/_/id/8552757/reliving-1957-oklahoma-sooners-loss-notre-dame-fighting-irish-snapped-ou-47-game-win-streak|title=Reliving the end of OU's 47-game win streak|newspaper=Espn.com |date=26 October 2012|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010059/http://www.espn.com/colleges/oklahoma/football/story/_/id/8552757/reliving-1957-oklahoma-sooners-loss-notre-dame-fighting-irish-snapped-ou-47-game-win-streak|url-status=live}} In Brennan's [[1958 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|final season]], though, the Irish finished 6–4,{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1958-schedule.html|title=1958 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928103006/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1958-schedule.html|url-status=live}} leading to Brennan's firing in mid-December.{{cite web|url=http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1958/12/22/page/53/article/notre-dame-discharges-terry-brennan|title=Chicago Tribune - Historical Newspapers|access-date=June 11, 2017|archive-date=November 7, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107160936/https://www.chicagotribune.com/|url-status=live}} Brennan's tenure can only be properly framed with the understanding that in a time of zero scholarship limitations in college football, Notre Dame's administration inexplicably began a process of de-emphasizing football, severely cutting scholarships and hindering Brennan from building a roster of any meaningful depth.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eNThHqwUK7kC&q=notre+dame+scholarship+football+cuts+1950s&pg=PA33|title=The USA Today College Football Encyclopedia|first1=Bob|last1=Boyles|first2=Paul|last2=Guido|date=September 27, 2017|publisher=Skyhorse Publishing Inc.|isbn=9781602396777|via=Google Books|access-date=October 5, 2020|archive-date=November 7, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107161013/https://books.google.com/books?id=eNThHqwUK7kC&q=notre+dame+scholarship+football+cuts+1950s&pg=PA33#v=onepage&q=notre%20dame%20scholarship%20football%20cuts%201950s&f=false|url-status=live}} He departed with a total of 32 wins and 18 losses.{{cite web |title=Terry Brennan Coaching Record |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/terry-brennan-1.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055859/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/terry-brennan-1.html |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} ===Joe Kuharich era (1959–1963)=== Former [[San Francisco Dons football|San Francisco]], [[Chicago Cardinals (NFL, 1920–59)|Chicago Cardinals]] and [[Washington Redskins]] head coach [[Joe Kuharich]] took the head coaching position at Notre Dame in 1959, realizing a longtime ambition to return to his alma mater.{{cite web|url=http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1959/10/11/page/276/article/joe-couldnt-say-no-to-notre-dame|title=Chicago Tribune - Historical Newspapers|access-date=June 3, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100055/http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1959/10/11/page/276/article/joe-couldnt-say-no-to-notre-dame/|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/kuharich_joe00.html|title=Joe Kuharich Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820074605/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/kuharich_joe00.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} He had earlier been courted by Notre Dame after the 1956 season, after the Irish finished 2–8, but before he had a chance to accept an offer, [[Terry Brennan]] was given a reprieve.{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1981/01/26/sports/joe-kuharich-dies-coached-eagles-irish.html|title=Joe Kuharich Dies; Coached Eagles, Irish|first=Thomas|last=Rogers|date=September 27, 1981|website=[[The New York Times]]|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100314/http://www.nytimes.com/1981/01/26/sports/joe-kuharich-dies-coached-eagles-irish.html|url-status=live}} Kuharich brought a professional touch to Irish football, putting shamrocks on the players' helmets and shoulder stripes on their jerseys.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/joe-kuharich-1.html|title=Joe Kuharich Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100447/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/joe-kuharich-1.html|url-status=live}} However, his tenure became one of the worst stretches in program history, including a school-record eight-game losing streak in [[1960 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1960]], a year in which the Irish finished 2–8.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1960-schedule.html|title=1960 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100812/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1960-schedule.html|url-status=live}} The consensus opinion was that Kuharich never made the adjustment from pro football to college football, attempting to use complicated pro coaching techniques with collegiate players, and never adapted to the limited substitution rules in effect at the time, having big, immobile linemen playing both ways in an era where smaller, quicker players were preferred. He often said, "You win some and you lose some", and seemed perfectly content finishing 5–5 every year. This did not sit well with the Irish faithful, who expected Notre Dame to beat everybody.{{cite web |title=Chicago Tribune - Historical Newspapers |url=http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1981/01/27/page/50/article/joe-kuharich-a-great-football-mind-is-remembered/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042152/http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1981/01/27/page/50/article/joe-kuharich-a-great-football-mind-is-remembered/ |archive-date=September 26, 2017 |access-date=June 3, 2017}} When the pressure of winning became too much to bear, Kuharich resigned in the spring of 1963 and assumed the post of supervisor of NFL officials. Because it was so late in the spring, the freshman team's coach [[Hugh Devore]] was named head coach for the [[1963 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1963 season]] while the search for a permanent replacement was being conducted.{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/vault/1963/10/28/594317/the-enigma-of-the-interim-coach|title=The enigma of the interim coach|access-date=June 10, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042016/https://www.si.com/vault/1963/10/28/594317/the-enigma-of-the-interim-coach|url-status=live}} Kuharich compiled a 17–23 record over four non-winning seasons and remains to this day the only coach ever to have an overall losing record at Notre Dame. Despite his unsuccessful tenure, Kuharich remains the only Irish coach to post back-to-back shutouts over their greatest rival, the [[USC Trojans football|USC Trojans]], in 1960 (17–0) and 1961 (30–0).{{cite web |title=Chicago Tribune - Historical Newspapers |url=http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1981/01/27/page/50/article/joe-kuharich-a-great-football-mind-is-remembered |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042152/http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1981/01/27/page/50/article/joe-kuharich-a-great-football-mind-is-remembered/ |archive-date=2017-09-26 |access-date=2018-08-19}} ===Ara Parseghian era (1964–1974)=== [[File:Parseghian statue.JPG|140px|thumb|Ara Parseghian Statue, dedicated September 22, 2007]] In 1964, [[Ara Parseghian]] left his job as the [[Northwestern Wildcats football|Northwestern]] head football coach when he was hired to take over the coaching duties at Notre Dame.{{cite web|url=http://newsok.com/article/3887052|title=Ara Parseghian remains a Notre Dame institution|date=September 26, 2013|access-date=June 3, 2017|archive-date=July 29, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170729142451/http://newsok.com/article/3887052|url-status=live}} He immediately brought the team back to a level of success in Irish football history that was comparable only to Rockne and Leahy. These three coaches have an 80% or greater winning percentage while at Notre Dame – Rockne at .881, Leahy at .864, and Parseghian at .836. Parseghian's teams never won fewer than seven nor lost more than three games during the ten-game regular seasons of the era.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/ara-parseghian-1.html|title=Ara Parseghian Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=August 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170803094915/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/ara-parseghian-1.html|url-status=live}} In his [[1964 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|first year]], the Irish improved their record to 9–1 behind [[Heisman Trophy]]-winning quarterback [[John Huarte]], but they lost any hope for a national championship in the last game of the season at [[1964 USC Trojans football team|USC]] when [[Craig Fertig]] connected with a touchdown pass to [[Rod Sherman]].{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1964-schedule.html|title=1964 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928102905/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1964-schedule.html|url-status=live}} Parseghian earned coach of the year honors from the American Football Coaches Association, the Football Writers Association, and ''[[The Sporting News]]'', as well as several others, and a cover story in ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' magazine.{{cite magazine|url=http://content.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,830832,00.html|title=College Football: Ara the Beautiful|date=November 20, 1964|magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=April 11, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160411095952/http://content.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,830832,00.html|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/112812aaa.html|title=Ara Parseghian to Receive Contributions to College Football Award|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055852/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/112812aaa.html|archive-date=September 28, 2017|url-status=dead}} The [[1966 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1966 team]] did finish unbeaten, finishing #1 in the AP Poll and winning the national championship in the process. The Irish had six shutout wins, each by 30 or more points. This includes a season-ending 51-0 win at [[1966 USC Trojans football team|#10 USC]], the largest margin of victory by either side in the rivalry's history. This season is most remembered for a [[1966 Notre Dame vs. Michigan State football game|10–10 tie]] at [[1966 Michigan State Spartans football team|#2 Michigan State]] in the "[[Game of the Century (college football)|Game of the Century]]" in which the injury-plagued Irish played for a tie rather than risking a loss.{{Cite web |date=2016-09-16 |title=Remember that time Notre Dame beat Michigan State, 10-10? |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/17550816/notre-dame-fighting-irish-michigan-state-spartans-play-fifty-years-game-century |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=ESPN.com |language=en}} It was under Parseghian as well that Notre Dame lifted its 40-plus year-old "no bowl games" policy,{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/112014aac.html|title=Changing Course|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005520/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/112014aac.html|archive-date=September 28, 2017|url-status=dead}} beginning with the season of [[1969 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1969]], after which the Irish played the [[1969 Texas Longhorns football team|#1 Texas Longhorns]] in the [[1970 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl Classic]], losing in the final minutes in a closely contested game.{{cite web|url=https://athlonsports.com/college-football/5-greatest-notre-dame-vs-texas-college-football-games-all-time|title=5 Greatest Notre Dame vs. Texas College Football Games of All-Time|website=Athlonsports.com|date=September 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005806/https://athlonsports.com/college-football/5-greatest-notre-dame-vs-texas-college-football-games-all-time|url-status=live}} The [[1970 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|following year]], Parseghian's 9–1 squad{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1970-schedule.html|title=1970 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060248/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1970-schedule.html|url-status=live}} ended Texas' [[Southwest Conference]] record 30-game winning streak in the [[1971 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl]]. In [[1971 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1971]], [[Cliff Brown (American football)|Cliff Brown]] became the first African-American quarterback to start a game for the program.{{cite web |date=18 December 2012 |title=Remembering Cliff Brown |url=https://www.espn.com/blog/notre-dame-football/post/_/id/12557/remembering-cliff-brown |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055824/http://www.espn.com/blog/notre-dame-football/post/_/id/12557/remembering-cliff-brown |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Espn.com}} The Irish returned to glory by winning Parseghian's second national championship in [[1973 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1973]], sweeping through their regular-season schedule 10–0 and defeating [[1973 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|#1 Alabama]] 24-23 in the [[1973 Sugar Bowl|Sugar Bowl]] by taking advantage of a fourth-quarter missed extra point by the Crimson Tide. Due to health issues, Parseghian was forced to retire from coaching after another [[1975 Orange Bowl|bowl win over Alabama]] to conclude the [[1974 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1974 season]].{{cite web|url=http://archives.chicagotribune.com/1974/12/16/page/71/article/parseghian-quits-irish-grid-post|title=Chicago Tribune - Historical Newspapers|website=Archives.chicagotribune.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} During Parseghian's eleven-year career, the Irish amassed a record of 95–17–4 and captured two national championships. The Irish finished in the AP Top 10 nine times and never ranked lower than #14 at the end of a season. Parseghian was also named coach of the year by several selectors in his national championship years of [[1966 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1966]] and [[1973 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1973]] and was inducted into the College Football Hall of Fame in 1980.{{College Football HoF|id=2284|name=Ara Parseghian|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite web |date=28 November 2012 |title=Ara Parseghian to receive award |url=https://www.espn.com/blog/notre-dame-football/post/_/id/12014/ara-parseghian-to-receive-award |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928010112/http://www.espn.com/blog/notre-dame-football/post/_/id/12014/ara-parseghian-to-receive-award |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Espn.com}} ===Dan Devine era (1975–1980)=== [[File:Dan Devine 1965.jpg|thumb|Coach Devine]] [[Dan Devine]] was hired to take over as head coach upon Parseghian's retirement from Notre Dame in 1975.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/050902aaa.html|title=Dan Devine Dies At Age 77|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171130012414/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/050902aaa.html|archive-date=November 30, 2017|url-status=dead}} Devine was already a highly successful coach and had led [[Arizona State Sun Devils football|Arizona State]], [[Missouri Tigers football|Missouri]], and the [[National Football League|NFL]]'s [[Green Bay Packers]]. Devine had been a leading candidate for the head coaching job at Notre Dame in 1964, when Ara Parseghian was hired.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QQCKCgAAQBAJ&q=dan+devine+candidate+notre+dame+parseghian+hired&pg=PT61|title=Ara's Knights: Ara Parseghian and the Golden Era of Notre Dame Football|first1=Frank|last1=Pomarico|first2=Raymond|last2=Serafin|date=September 15, 2015|publisher=Triumph Books|isbn=9781633193277|via=Google Books}} When approached for the job following Parseghian's resignation, Devine accepted immediately, joking that it was probably the shortest job interview in history.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dN3zpS2I7XUC&q=dan+devine+shortest+interview+in+history&pg=PA213|title=Linebacker in the Boardroom: Lessons in Life and Leadership|first=Marvin A.|last=Russell|date=September 27, 2017|publisher=Marvin A. Russell|isbn=9781432776558|via=Google Books}} When he arrived at Notre Dame he already had a college coaching record of 120 wins, 40 losses, and eight ties and had led his teams to victory in four bowl games.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/dan-devine-1.html|title=Dan Devine Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928103048/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/dan-devine-1.html|url-status=live}} At Notre Dame he would lead the Irish to 53 wins, 16 losses, and a tie as well as three bowl victories. His lasting achievement came midway through this run, when the [[1977 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1977 Notre Dame team]] won the national championship, led by junior quarterback [[Joe Montana]].{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/classic/obit/s/2002/0509/1380498.html|title=ESPN Classic - Devine led Notre Dame to national title|website=Espn.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=August 20, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005912/http://www.espn.com/classic/obit/s/2002/0509/1380498.html|url-status=live}} Before the game against [[1977 USC Trojans football team|USC]], played at home on October 22, Devine changed the team's jerseys from navy blue & white to kelly green & gold, later known as the "green jersey game", resulting in a 49–19 victory over the Trojans.{{cite web |title=The Lore Of The Green Jersey |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/092315aam.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171120000721/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/092315aam.html |archive-date=November 20, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Und.com}} The Irish continued to wear green for the rest of Devine's tenure at the school. The championship season climaxed with a 38–10 win in the [[1978 Cotton Bowl Classic]] over previously top-ranked [[1977 Texas Longhorns football team|Texas]], led by Heisman Trophy winner [[Earl Campbell]].{{cite web|url=http://grfx.cstv.com/photos/schools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/1977--22/box_score/stats_20121210aaa.pdf|title=1978 Cotton Bowl: Notre Dame 38 - Texas 10|website=Grfx.cstv.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 6, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150906075159/http://grfx.cstv.com/photos/schools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/1977--22/box_score/stats_20121210aaa.pdf|url-status=dead}} The win vaulted the Irish from fifth to first in the polls. The [[1978 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|following season]], the Irish lost their first two games including a shocking 3–0 loss to [[1978 Missouri Tigers football team|Missouri]] in South Bend, but would recover to finish the regular season 8–3 and earn a berth in the [[1979 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl]] against [[1978 Houston Cougars football team|Houston]]. The Irish used three Montana touchdowns (two passing, one rushing) to recover from a 22-point deficit in the fourth quarter and win 35–34, the largest fourth-quarter comeback in college football history at the time. The game became known as the "Chicken Soup Game" after Montana ate a bowl of chicken soup at halftime while battling the flu.{{Cite news |last=Anderson |first=Dave |date=1994-01-18 |title=Sports of The Times; Joe Cool Has Coped With Cold |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1994/01/18/sports/sports-of-the-times-joe-cool-has-coped-with-cold.html |access-date=2024-08-20 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}} The [[1979 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1979]] and [[1980 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1980]] Irish did not live up to the standards set by earlier Devine teams, although the former season did see [[Vagas Ferguson]] set the single-season program rushing record (1,437 yards) that still stands today. Because he had the unenviable task of following a legend, Devine came under heavy scrutiny while at Notre Dame and it was felt that he was never fully embraced by the Notre Dame community, despite winning a national championship.{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2002-may-10-me-devine10-story.html|title=Dan Devine, 77; Hall of Fame Football Coach|first=Mike|last=Kupper|date=May 10, 2002|via=LA Times|access-date=June 3, 2017|archive-date=December 11, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151211170731/http://articles.latimes.com/2002/may/10/local/me-devine10|url-status=live}} After a 5–2 start in his first season, rumors of incompetence were circulated and that Devine would be dismissed and replaced by [[Don Shula]] or even [[Ara Parseghian]] (who went so far as to say he would not return to Notre Dame under any circumstances).{{cite web|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/sports/1980/11/12/dan-devine-38/aaa07da9-0865-41ca-85e1-4cfa5f0a906e/|title=Dan Devine &|first=John|last=Feinstein|date=November 12, 1980|website=Washingtonpost.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005710/https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/sports/1980/11/12/dan-devine-38/aaa07da9-0865-41ca-85e1-4cfa5f0a906e/|url-status=live}} Even on the day of the 1977 USC game, "Dump Devine" bumper stickers were being sold outside Notre Dame Stadium. On August 15, 1980, Devine announced that he would be leaving Notre Dame at the end of season, saying he wanted to be able to spend more time with his wife. ===Gerry Faust era (1981–1985)=== [[Gerry Faust]] was a surprise choice when hired to replace Devine in 1981.{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2008-nov-24-sp-crowe24-story.html|title=For him, the Notre Dame job was a Faustian bargain|first=Jerry|last=Crowe|date=November 24, 2008|via=LA Times|access-date=June 2, 2017|archive-date=October 23, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121023145106/http://articles.latimes.com/2008/nov/24/sports/sp-crowe24|url-status=live}} Prior to Notre Dame, Faust had been one of the more successful high school football coaches in the country. As coach of [[Moeller High School]] in [[Cincinnati]] he amassed a 174–17–2 record over 19 seasons.{{cite web |last=Jacon-Duffy |first=Marais |date=December 1, 2016 |title=From the Vault: 'The Bold Experiment' with Gerry Faust, Notre Dame and Moeller High School |url=http://www.wcpo.com/news/our-community/from-the-vault/from-the-vault-gerry-faust-puts-moeller-football-on-the-map-leaves-for-notre-dame-after-state-game |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005752/http://www.wcpo.com/news/our-community/from-the-vault/from-the-vault-gerry-faust-puts-moeller-football-on-the-map-leaves-for-notre-dame-after-state-game |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Wcpo.com}} Many of his players had gone on to play for Notre Dame; indeed, when he arrived in South Bend, he was reunited with nine of his former players from Moeller. Despite his success in the high school ranks, Faust's success at Notre Dame was mixed. In his [[1981 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|first season]], the Irish finished 5–6.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1981-schedule.html|title=1981 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060338/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1981-schedule.html|url-status=live}} In Faust's [[1982 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|second season]], Notre Dame improved slightly to 6–4–1.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1982-schedule.html|title=1982 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060106/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1982-schedule.html|url-status=live}} The most successful years under Faust were the [[1983 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1983]] and [[1984 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1984]] campaigns where the Irish finished 7–5 and made trips to the [[1983 Liberty Bowl|Liberty Bowl]] and [[1984 Aloha Bowl|Aloha Bowl]] respectively.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1983-schedule.html|title=1983 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060210/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1983-schedule.html|url-status=live}}{{cite
