web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1984-schedule.html|title=1984 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928103036/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1984-schedule.html|url-status=live}} His final record at Notre Dame was 30–26–1.{{cite web |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/gerry-faust-1.html |title=Gerry Faust Coaching Record |website=Sports-reference.com |date=January 1, 1970 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=June 11, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170611195244/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/gerry-faust-1.html |url-status=live }} To avoid being fired, Faust resigned at the end of the [[1985 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1985 season]].{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/vault/1986/04/21/633826/faust-from-the-gipper-to-the-zippers|title=Faust: From the Gipper to the Zippers|first=Hank|last=Hersch|website=Si.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005759/https://www.si.com/vault/1986/04/21/633826/faust-from-the-gipper-to-the-zippers|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/1989/11/12/even-at-akron-faust-is-faust/|title=Even At Akron, Faust Is Faust|website=Articles.chicagotribune.com|date=November 12, 1989 |access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 28, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928055922/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/1989-11-12/sports/8901300846_1_zips-gerry-faust-football-coach|url-status=live}} He announced his resignation prior to the final game of the year, where Notre Dame suffered a humiliating 58–7 loss at Miami; [[Allen Pinkett]] scored the Irish TD. Faust proceeded to take over as head coach at [[Akron Zips football|Akron]].{{cite news |last=Alfano |first=Peter |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1986/04/04/sports/gerry-faust-starts-over-with-akron.html?pagewanted=all |title=Gerry Faust Starts Over With Akron |website=[[The New York Times]] |date=April 4, 1986 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=September 28, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928060522/http://www.nytimes.com/1986/04/04/sports/gerry-faust-starts-over-with-akron.html?pagewanted=all |url-status=live }} ===Lou Holtz era (1986–1996)=== [[File:Lou Holtz cropped.jpg|thumb|Coach Holtz]] [[Lou Holtz]] had 17 years of head coaching experience by the time he was hired to lead the Irish.{{cite web |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/holtz_lou00.html |title=Lou Holtz Bio :: Notre Dame Football |website=Und.Com |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170618082606/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/holtz_lou00.html |archive-date=June 18, 2017 |url-status=dead }} He had previously been head coach of [[William & Mary Tribe football|William & Mary]], [[NC State Wolfpack football|North Carolina State]], the [[National Football League|NFL]]'s [[New York Jets]], [[Arkansas Razorbacks football|Arkansas]], and [[Minnesota Golden Gophers football|Minnesota]]. In contrast to Faust, Holtz was well known as a master motivator and a strict disciplinarian.{{cite web |title=Follansbee honors legendary coach Lou Holtz {{pipe}} News, Sports, Jobs – The Times Leader |url=http://www.timesleaderonline.com/news/local-news/2017/05/follansbee-honors-legendary-coach-lou-holtz/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501201247/http://www.timesleaderonline.com/news/local-news/2017/05/follansbee-honors-legendary-coach-lou-holtz/ |archive-date=May 1, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=Timesleaderonline.com}}{{cite web |author=Pam Prevatte |title=Woodruff native says ex-Notre Dame coach could turn it all around – News – GoUpstate – Spartanburg, SC |url=http://www.goupstate.com/article/NC/19981125/News/605188904/SJ/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903074127/http://www.goupstate.com/article/NC/19981125/News/605188904/SJ/ |archive-date=September 3, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=GoUpstate.com}} The tone was set with Holtz's first meeting with his team as Irish head coach in 1986, immediately demanding his players sit up straight in their chairs and look him in the eye as he spoke.{{cite book |author1=John Heisler |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aqu3cWMqmpIC&q=lou+holtz+sit+up+in+chairs&pg=PT314 |title=Always Fighting Irish: Players, Coaches, and Fans Share Their Passion for ... |author2=Tim Prister |date=10 August 2012 |publisher=Triumph Books |isbn=9781623680503 |page=314 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107161531/https://books.google.com/books?id=aqu3cWMqmpIC&q=lou+holtz+sit+up+in+chairs&pg=PT314#v=snippet&q=lou%20holtz%20sit%20up%20in%20chairs&f=false |archive-date=November 7, 2023 |url-status=live}} Holtz began in [[1986 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1986]] where his predecessor left off in 1985, finishing with an identical record of 5–6.{{cite web |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1986-schedule.html |title=1986 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results {{pipe}} College Football at |website=Sports-reference.com |date=January 1, 1970 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=June 12, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170612165110/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1986-schedule.html |url-status=live }} However, unlike the 1985 squad, which was generally outcoached and outplayed, Holtz's 1986 edition was competitive in nearly every game, losing five out of those six games by a combined total of 14 points. That would be his only losing season as he posted a record of 95–24–2 over the next ten seasons adding up to a 100–30–2 record overall.{{cite news|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/12-m-footbl-media-guide.html|title=2012 Notre Dame Football Media Guide|publisher=University of Notre Dame|format=PDF|access-date=August 26, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120927033336/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/12-m-footbl-media-guide.html|archive-date=September 27, 2012|url-status=dead}}{{cite web |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/lou-holtz-1.html |title=Lou Holtz Coaching Record {{pipe}} College Football at |website=Sports-reference.com |date=January 1, 1970 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=March 10, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180310045904/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/lou-holtz-1.html |url-status=live }} In [[1987 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1987]], Holtz led the Irish to an 8–4 record.{{cite web |date=January 1, 1970 |title=1987 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results {{pipe}} College Football at |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1987-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170612165234/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1987-schedule.html |archive-date=June 12, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=Sports-reference.com}} Notre Dame's best player was star wide receiver [[Tim Brown (American football)|Tim Brown]], who would win the [[Heisman Trophy]] that season and is Notre Dame's seventh and most recent Heisman winner.{{cite web |url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/tim_brown_112293.html |title=Tim Brown Bio :: Notre Dame Football |website=Und.Com |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903013238/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/tim_brown_112293.html |archive-date=September 3, 2017 |url-status=dead }}{{cite web |url=http://heisman.com/roster.aspx?rp_id=54 |title=Heisman Trophy |website=Heisman.com |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=February 7, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170207064920/http://heisman.com/roster.aspx?rp_id=54 |url-status=live }} The [[1988 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1988]] campaign began with high hopes, as former Notre Dame coaches Rockne, Leahy, Parseghian, and Devine all won their first national championship in their third season with the program. The Irish defeated their first five opponents, ascending to the #4 ranking in the lead-up to a visit from the reigning national champion [[1988 Miami Hurricanes football team|Miami Hurricanes]]. In a game which became known as [[1988 Notre Dame vs. Miami football game|Catholics vs. Convicts]] due to a popular T-shirt design on Notre Dame's campus, the Irish upset the Hurricanes 31–30 when [[Pat Terrell]] knocked down [[Steve Walsh (American football)|Steve Walsh's]] two-point conversion attempt with no time on the clock. When star players [[Ricky Watters]] and [[Tony Brooks (American football)|Tony Brooks]] showed up late for dinner right before the then top-ranked Irish played second-ranked [[1988 USC Trojans football team|USC]] in the final regular season game of 1988,{{cite web |url=http://www.espn.com/abcsports/bcs/s/flashback/notredameusc1988.html |title=Bowl Championship Series – Flashback: Notre Dame-USC 1988 |website=Espn.com |date=December 13, 2002 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=September 3, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903074458/http://www.espn.com/abcsports/bcs/s/flashback/notredameusc1988.html |url-status=dead }} in a controversial move, Holtz took his 10–0 Irish squad to Los Angeles without them.{{cite web|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/1988/12/31/tardy-irish-learn-dont-tick-off-holtz/|title=Tardy Irish Learn: Don't Tick Off Holtz|website=[[Chicago Tribune]] |date=December 31, 1988 |access-date=June 18, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903142439/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/1988-12-31/sports/8802280785_1_coach-holtz-ricky-watters-lou-holtz|url-status=live}} His move was vindicated when the Irish defeated USC anyway.{{cite news |last=Anderson |first=Dave |date=November 27, 1988 |title=Sports of the Times – Lou Holtz of Notre Dame Raps the Gavel Again |url=https://query.nytimes.com/gst/fullpage.html?res=940DE2DB1E3BF934A15752C1A96E948260&sec=&spon=&pagewanted=all |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231107161514/https://www.nytimes.com/1988/11/27/sports/sports-of-the-times-lou-holtz-of-notre-dame-raps-the-gavel-again.html?sec=&spon=&pagewanted=all |archive-date=November 7, 2023 |access-date=April 25, 2008 |website=The New York Times}} Holtz was named national coach of the year, and a win over No. 3 [[1988 West Virginia Mountaineers football team|West Virginia]] in the [[1989 Fiesta Bowl|Fiesta Bowl]] captured the Irish's 11th-all time national championship.{{cite web |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1989-01-03-sp-172-story.html |title=Fiesta Bowl: Notre Dame 34, West Virginia 21 : Dream is Shattered : Lackluster Play, Harris' Injury Keep Mountaineers From Realizing Their Goal |website=Articles.latimes.com |date=October 18, 2003 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=May 28, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160528220844/http://articles.latimes.com/1989-01-03/sports/sp-172_1_notre-dame |url-status=live }} 1988 remains Notre Dame's most recent undefeated season and national championship.{{cite web |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1988-schedule.html |title=1988 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results {{pipe}} College Football at |website=Sports-reference.com |date=January 1, 1970 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=May 31, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170531021605/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1988-schedule.html |url-status=live }}{{cite web|url=http://bleacherreport.com/articles/1441797-notre-dame-football-comparing-1988-championship-team-to-brian-kellys-2012-unit|title=Notre Dame Football: Comparing 1988 Championship Team to Brian Kelly's 2012 Unit|first=Randy|last=Chambers|website=[[Bleacher Report]]|date=December 12, 2012|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903074134/http://bleacherreport.com/articles/1441797-notre-dame-football-comparing-1988-championship-team-to-brian-kellys-2012-unit|url-status=live}}[[File:President Ronald Reagan hosting the NCAA football champion University of Notre Dame football team.jpg|thumb|The 1988 national champion Fighting Irish visited President [[Ronald Reagan]] in the White House in January 1989.]] In [[1989 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1989]], Holtz led the Irish to a 12–1 record. The Irish began the season in the Kickoff Classic game in [[East Rutherford, New Jersey]], against [[1990 Virginia Cavaliers football team|Virginia]].{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1989/01/18/sports/irish-vs-virginia-in-kickoff-classic.html |title=Irish vs. Virginia In Kickoff Classic |website=[[The New York Times]] |date=January 18, 1989 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=September 26, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100226/http://www.nytimes.com/1989/01/18/sports/irish-vs-virginia-in-kickoff-classic.html |url-status=live }} The Irish won by a score of 36–13 and ascended to the #1 spot in the AP Poll.{{cite news |last=Moran |first=Malcolm |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1989/09/14/sports/holtz-with-no-1-team-worries-about-no-2.html |title=Holtz, With No. 1 Team, Worries About No. 2 |website=[[The New York Times]] |date=September 14, 1989 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=September 26, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100713/http://www.nytimes.com/1989/09/14/sports/holtz-with-no-1-team-worries-about-no-2.html |url-status=live }} Next, top-ranked Notre Dame defeated #2 [[1990 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] by a score of 24–19.{{cite news |author=Malcolm Moran |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1989/09/17/sports/college-football-ismail-leads-notre-dame-past-michigan.html |title=College Football – Ismail Leads Notre Dame Past Michigan |website=[[The New York Times]] |date=September 17, 1989 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=September 26, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926100501/http://www.nytimes.com/1989/09/17/sports/college-football-ismail-leads-notre-dame-past-michigan.html |url-status=live }} After nine more consecutive victories to begin the season 11–0, the Irish would lose to #7 [[1990 Miami Hurricanes football team|Miami]] in the final week of the season, ending Notre Dame's 23-game winning streak.{{cite web |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1989-11-26-sp-392-story.html |title=Miami Exacts Its Revenge, 27–10 : Hurricanes: Seventh-ranked squad ends Notre Dame's winning streak and reign as top-ranked team. |website=Articles.latimes.com |date=November 26, 1989 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=September 21, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150921154044/http://articles.latimes.com/1989-11-26/sports/sp-392_1_notre-dame |url-status=live }} Holtz would lead the Irish to a victory in the [[1991 Orange Bowl|Orange Bowl]] over #1 [[1990 Colorado Buffaloes football team|Colorado]] to end the season, finishing #2 in the AP poll behind Miami.{{cite web |date=January 1, 1970 |title=1989 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results {{pipe}} College Football at |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1989-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170520174450/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1989-schedule.html |archive-date=May 20, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=Sports-reference.com}} After beginning the [[1990 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1990 season]] with a #1 ranking and a victory over #4 [[Michigan Wolverines football|Michigan]] by a score of 28–24 and two more wins for a 3–0 start, the Irish took their first loss of the season, losing to [[Stanford Cardinal football|Stanford]] by a score of 36–31. The Irish would ascend back to #1 on the strength of a home win over [[1990 Miami Hurricanes football team|#2 Miami]], a game that saw the end of the series between the two until 2010.{{Cite magazine |last=Jenkins |first=Sally |date=October 29, 1990 |title=NOT SO BITTER END |url=https://vault.si.com/vault/1990/10/29/not-so-bitter-end-notre-dame-beat-miami-29-20-in-a-sweet-finish-to-an-often-rancorous-series |magazine=Sports Illustrated}} However, late-season losses to [[1990 Penn State Nittany Lions football team|Penn State]] and [[1990 Colorado Buffaloes football team|Colorado]] in the [[1991 Orange Bowl|Orange Bowl]] would eliminate the Irish from national championship contention.{{cite web |date=January 1, 1970 |title=1990 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results {{pipe}} College Football at |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1990-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170520181502/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1990-schedule.html |archive-date=May 20, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=Sports-reference.com}} The [[1991 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1991 Fighting Irish]] began the season 8-1, but lost back-to-back games for the first time since 1987 when they again lost to [[1991 Penn State Nittany Lions football team|Penn State]]. The Irish would receive a berth in the [[1992 Sugar Bowl|Sugar Bowl]] in [[New Orleans, Louisiana]], where they defeated [[1991 Florida Gators football team|Florida]] by a score of 39–28.{{cite web|url=https://www.allstatesugarbowl.org/site100.php|title=1992 Game Recap / Allstate Sugar Bowl|website=Allstatesugarbowl.org|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926041100/https://www.allstatesugarbowl.org/site100.php|url-status=dead}}{{cite web |date=January 1, 1970 |title=1991 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1991-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170520183241/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1991-schedule.html |archive-date=May 20, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=Sports-reference.com}} In [[1992 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1992]], Notre Dame finished 10–1–1. After defeating [[1992 Northwestern Wildcats football team|Northwestern]] to start the season,{{cite web |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1992-09-06-sp-420-story.html |title=Notre Dame Rockets Past Northwestern : Notre Dame: Miller, expected to fill role of Ismail, scores on 70-yard pass play in 42–7 victory. |website=Articles.latimes.com |date=September 6, 1992 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=October 24, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151024113715/http://articles.latimes.com/1992-09-06/sports/sp-420_1_notre-dame |url-status=live }} the Fighting Irish tied #5 [[1992 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]], their first tie of the Holtz era.{{cite web |last=Moran |first=Malcolm |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1992/09/13/sports/college-football-notre-dame-and-michigan-sputter-at-the-end-to-a-17-17-tie.html |title=College Football – Notre Dame and Michigan Sputter at the End to a 17–17 Tie |website=[[The New York Times]] |date=September 13, 1992 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |archive-date=August 22, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170822120637/http://www.nytimes.com/1992/09/13/sports/college-football-notre-dame-and-michigan-sputter-at-the-end-to-a-17-17-tie.html |url-status=live }} This season saw the Irish's largest win in history against [[1992 Purdue Boilermakers football team|Purdue]], a 48–0 win at [[Notre Dame Stadium]], and a 28–3 [[1993 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl]] against previously unbeaten #4 [[1992 Texas A&M Aggies football team|Texas A&M]].{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aqu3cWMqmpIC&q=notre+dame+vs+texas+a%26m+cotton+bowl+1993+28-3&pg=PT169 |title=Always Fighting Irish: Players, Coaches, and Fans Share Their Passion for ... |author1=John Heisler |author2=Tim Prister |page=169 |date=January 1, 1992 |publisher=Triumph Books |isbn=9781623680503 |access-date=May 28, 2017}}{{cite web |date=January 1, 1970 |title=1992 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1992-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170520185614/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1992-schedule.html |archive-date=May 20, 2017 |access-date=May 28, 2017 |website=Sports-reference.com}} The Irish enjoyed another successful season in [[1993 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1993]], finishing the season at 11–1. After scoring 27 points in wins over [[1993 Northwestern Wildcats football team|Northwestern]] and #2 [[1993 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] to start the season, the Irish defeated their next six opponents to enter a matchup with undefeated #1 [[1993 Florida State Seminoles football team|Florida State]] 8–0 and ranked just behind the Seminoles at #2. For the first time, ESPN's [[College GameDay (football TV program)|College GameDay]] decided to make a visit to campus to host the show live on Saturday morning.{{cite news |last=Fendrich |first=Howard |date=September 2, 2000 |title=ESPN' "College GameDay" a huge hit |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=hmRWAAAAIBAJ&sjid=PvIDAAAAIBAJ&pg=6808%2C813561 |work=Spokesman-Review |location=(Spokane, Washington) |page=C2 |agency=Associated Press}} In a classic, the Irish defeated the Seminoles [[1993 Florida State vs. Notre Dame football game|31–24]], ascending to the #1 ranking a day later. However, a loss to #17 [[1993 Boston College Eagles football team|Boston College]] on a game-winning field goal as time expired by a score of 41–39 ended the Irish's national championship aspirations.{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1993/11/21/sports/college-football-boston-college-knocks-the-smile-off-the-irish.html|title=Boston College Knocks the Smile Off the Irish|date=November 21, 1993|website=The New York Times}} The Irish would face a rematch with #6 [[1993 Texas A&M Aggies football team|Texas A&M]] in the [[1994 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl]] to finish the season, a game the Irish won by a score of 24–21.{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1994-01-02-sp-7819-story.html|title=Notre Dame Rallies for Victory, Then Awaits Its Fate in the Polls : Cotton Bowl: Holtz says Irish have rightful claim to the national title after they beat Texas A&M, 24–21, on a field goal by Pendergast with 2:17 to play.|first=Randy|last=Harvey|date=January 2, 1994|via=LA Times}}{{cite web |title=1993 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1993-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} Controversially, Notre Dame finished #2 in the AP poll behind a team in Florida State it had beaten. In [[1994 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1994]], Holtz led Notre Dame to a 6–5–1 record, the Irish's worst record since Holtz's first season in 1986.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1994-schedule.html|title=1994 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} The team lost three of four games midseason to drop out of the rankings for the first time since 1986. The Irish would improve to 9–3 in [[1995 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1995]], but lost to #8 [[1995 Florida State Seminoles football team|Florida State]] in the [[1996 Orange Bowl (January)|Orange Bowl]].{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/abcsports/bcs/orange/s/1996.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170928005908/http://www.espn.com/abcsports/bcs/orange/s/1996.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 28, 2017|title=Bowl Championship Series - 1996 - Florida St. 31, Notre Dame 26|website=Espn.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite web |title=1995 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1995-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} Lou Holtz's final season at Notre Dame in [[1996 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1996]] resulted in an 8–3 record. The season notably saw Notre Dame's first participation in the [[Emerald Isle Classic]] in [[Republic of Ireland|Ireland]], a 54–27 win over [[1996 Navy Midshipmen football team|Navy]], and an overtime loss to [[1996 USC Trojans football team|USC]], snapping the Irish's 13-game non-losing streak against the Trojans.{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/abcsports/s/notredameuscgreatestgames.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170906102014/http://www.espn.com/abcsports/s/notredameuscgreatestgames.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 6, 2017|title=ABC Sports - Greatest games of the Notre Dame-USC rivarly(sic)|website=Espn.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite web |title=1996 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/1996-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} Holtz's option offense, which helped catapult Notre Dame to many victories in the late 1980s and early 1990s, also helped rack up impressive recruiting classes. During the 1989 season, Holtz had the following future NFL players on offense: QB [[Rick Mirer]], RB [[Ricky Watters]], RB [[Anthony Johnson (running back)|Anthony Johnson]], RB [[Rodney Culver]] RB [[Dorsey Levens]], and WR [[Raghib Ismail]]. In 1990, he added RB [[Jeff Burris]] (who would later move to safety), FB [[Jerome Bettis]] and TE [[Irv Smith Sr.|Irv Smith]]. 1991 saw the additions of RB [[Reggie Brooks]] and FB [[Ray Zellars]]. 1992 saw the addition of WR [[Derrick Mayes]]. For 1993, he added FB [[Marc Edwards (American football)|Marc Edwards]]. In 1995, he added RB [[Autry Denson]]. From the 1987–1995 [[NFL draft]]s, there were a total of 65 Notre Dame players selected.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-fb-a-nfl2.html|title=Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926041655/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/archive/nd-m-fb-a-nfl2.html|archive-date=September 26, 2017|url-status=dead}} Overall, Holtz took Notre Dame to one undefeated season, nine consecutive New Year's Day bowl games, and top 10 finishes in the AP poll in five seasons,{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/auto_pdf/p_hotos/s_chools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/07fbguidehistory|title=Notre Dame Media Guide:History and Records|publisher=University of Notre Dame|format=PDF|access-date=December 17, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120515215139/http://www.und.com/auto_pdf/p_hotos/s_chools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/07fbguidehistory|archive-date=May 15, 2012|url-status=dead}} as well as overseeing the majority of the longest winning streak against [[USC Trojans football|USC]] in program history (11 games). Holtz retired from Notre Dame following the 1996 season,{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1996-11-29-sp-3969-story.html|title=A Case of the Bends : Lou Holtz Loved Notre Dame's Stage, but Roller Coaster Season May Have Been Too Much for Him|first=Jim|last=Hodges|date=November 29, 1996|via=LA Times}} but would unretire in 1999 to accept the head coaching position at [[South Carolina Gamecocks football|South Carolina]] where he would serve until the completion of the 2004 season.{{cite web|url=https://www.saturdaydownsouth.com/coaches/lou-holtz/|title=Lou Holtz|date=August 14, 2014}}{{cite web|url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/sports/college/football/sec/2004-11-22-scarolina-holtz-retirement_x.htm|title=South Carolina's Holtz announces retirement|website=Usatoday30.usatoday.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} ===Bob Davie era (1997–2001)=== [[File:MountainWestMD-2016-0726-BobDavie.png|thumb|Coach Davie]] [[Bob Davie (coach)|Bob Davie]], who had been Holtz's defensive coordinator from 1994 to 1996, was promoted to head coach when Holtz retired.{{cite news| url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/1996/12/07/davies-hiring-good-judgment-call-by-irish/ | work=Chicago Tribune | first=Bob | last=Verdi | title=Davie's Hiring Good Judgment Call By Irish | date=December 7, 1996}} Davie, who turned down a head coaching offer from [[Purdue Boilermakers football|Purdue]] to accept the Irish's head coaching position,{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/vault/1997/05/05/226566/this-old-house-as-he-rebuilds-at-notre-dame-bob-davie-plans-to-leave-the-dome-golden-the-rest-is-up-for-grabs#|title=This Old House As He Rebuilds at Notre Dame, Bob Davie Plans to Leave the Dome Golden. The Rest Is Up For Grabs|first=Tim |last=Layden|website=Si.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} was a well-respected defensive mind who had also served as defensive coordinator at [[Tulane Green Wave football|Tulane]] and [[Texas A&M Aggies football|Texas A&M]].{{cite web|url=http://www.golobos.com/coaches.aspx?rc=77|title=The University of New Mexico Lobos – 2015 Football Coaching Staff|website=Golobos.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 26, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926095559/http://www.golobos.com/coaches.aspx?rc=77|url-status=dead}} Davie had also filled in as head coach for one game during the 1995 season when Lou Holtz was dealing with a health issue.{{cite news |date=September 17, 1995 |title=College Football: Notre Dame Doesn't Miss Holtz : Nonconference: With coach watching at home, the Irish rout Vanderbilt, 41-0, behind Kinder |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1995-09-17-sp-46972-story.html |newspaper=LA Times |agency=Associated Press}} One of his first major decisions was to fire long-time offensive line coach [[Joe Moore (American football coach)|Joe Moore]], who then successfully sued the university for age discrimination.Lieberman, Richard. ''Personal Foul: Coach Joe Moore vs. The University of Notre Dame''. Academy Chicago Publishers, 2001. On Davie's watch, the team suffered three bowl game losses ([[1997 Independence Bowl]],{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1997-dec-29-sp-3271-story.html|title=Notre Dame Loses Rematch to LSU|agency=Associated Press|date=December 29, 1997|via=LA Times}} [[1999 Gator Bowl]],{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/1999/01/02/sports/college-football-bowl-roundup-gator-bowl-irish-come-up-short-against-georgia.html|title=Irish Come Up Short Against Georgia Tech|date=January 2, 1999|website=AP|via=The New York Times}} and [[2001 Fiesta Bowl]]){{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/abcsports/bcs/s/recap/fiestabowl.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926095810/http://www.espn.com/abcsports/bcs/s/recap/fiestabowl.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 26, 2017|title=Bowl Championship Series – Oregon State 41, Notre Dame 9|website=Espn.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} and it failed to qualify for a bowl game in two others (1999 and 2001). The highlight of Davie's tenure was a 36–20 upset win in [[1998 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1998]] over #5 [[Michigan Wolverines football|Michigan]], the defending national champions.{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/irish-upset-national-champs-1998-vs-michigan/|title=Irish Upset National Champs (1998 vs. Michigan) // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu}}{{cite web|url=http://www.herloyalsons.com/blog/2017/03/31/5-best-notre-dame-teams-post-holtz-era/|title=5 Best Notre Dame Teams of the Post-Holtz Era - Her Loyal Sons|date=March 31, 2017|access-date=June 12, 2017|archive-date=August 20, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820074553/http://www.herloyalsons.com/blog/2017/03/31/5-best-notre-dame-teams-post-holtz-era/|url-status=dead}} On December 17, 1999, Notre Dame was placed on [[NCAA probation|probation]] by the NCAA. The association's Committee on Infractions found two series of violations. ''[[The New York Times]]'' reported "the main one involved the actions of a booster, Kimberly Dunbar, who lavished gifts on football players with money she later pleaded guilty to [[embezzling]]." In the second series of events, a football player was accused of trying to sell several complimentary game tickets and of using others as repayment of a loan. The player was also said "to have been romantically involved with a woman (not Dunbar), a part-time tutor at the university, who wrote a term paper for another player for a small fee and provided players with meals, lodging and gifts."{{cite web |last=Litsky |first=Frank |date=December 18, 1999 |title=Football – N.C.A.A. Puts Notre Dame Football on Probation |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1999/12/18/sports/football-ncaa-puts-notre-dame-football-on-probation.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=[[The New York Times]]}} The Dunbar violation began while Lou Holtz was head coach: "According to the NCAA committee report, Dunbar, the woman at the center of the more serious violations, had become romantically involved with several Notre Dame football players from June 1995 to January 1998 and had a child with one, Jarvis Edison." Notre Dame was placed on probation for two years and lost one of its 85 football scholarships each year in what the ''Times'' termed "minor" penalties.{{cite web |title=Irish Are Assessed Penalties |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-srv/WPcap/1999-12/18/037r-121899-idx.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Washingtonpost.com}} The [[2001 Fiesta Bowl]] was Notre Dame's first invitation to the [[Bowl Championship Series]]. The Irish lost by 32 points to [[2000 Oregon State Beavers football team|Oregon State]]{{cite web |title=Oregon State blasts Irish in Fiesta |url=https://usatoday30.usatoday.com/sports/football/sfc/00bowls/game24.htm |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=Usatoday30.usatoday.com}} but would finish No. 15 in the AP Poll, Davie's highest final ranking as head coach. Despite Davie's rocky tenure, new athletic director [[Kevin White (athletic director)|Kevin White]] gave the coach a contract extension following the 2000 season,{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/080599aaa.html|title=Fighting Irish Football Coach Signs Contract Extension|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005909/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/080599aaa.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} then saw the team start 0–3 in [[2001 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2001]] – the first such start in school history.{{cite web|url=http://todayinndhistory.com/pages/events/?id=344|title=Today in ND History: The first 0–3 start|website=Todayinndhistory.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Disappointed by the on-field results, coupled with the Joe Moore and Kim Dunbar scandals, the administration decided to dismiss Davie after the 2001 season.{{cite web|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2001-dec-03-sp-10906-story.html|title=Davie Fired By Irish|first=Chris|last=Dufresne|date=December 3, 2001|via=LA Times}} His final record at Notre Dame was 35–25.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/bob-davie-1.html|title=Bob Davie Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} ====George O'Leary controversy==== On December 9, 2001, Notre Dame hired [[George O'Leary]], the head coach at [[Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football|Georgia Tech]], to replace Davie.{{cite web|url=http://www.upi.com/Sports_News/2001/12/09/Notre-Dame-hires-George-OLeary/UPI-21411007934537/|title=Notre Dame hires George O'Leary|work=UPI|access-date=September 6, 2015}} However, while researching a story on O'Leary, [[New Hampshire Union Leader]] reporter Jim Fennell uncovered misrepresentations in O'Leary's resume that had influenced the administration's decision to hire him.{{cite news |author=Haugh, David|url=http://old.irishsports.com/stories/subscription/2002/12/19/isr_football.20021222-isr-TRIB-SS28-By_George_reporter_.fff.sto|title=By George, reporter carries on |publisher=Irish Sports Report|date=December 19, 2002|access-date=September 7, 2007 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070928092556/http://old.irishsports.com/stories/subscription/2002/12/19/isr_football.20021222-isr-TRIB-SS28-By_George_reporter_.fff.sto |archive-date = September 28, 2007}} The resulting media scandal embarrassed Notre Dame officials and tainted O'Leary; he resigned five days later before coaching a single practice, recruiting a single player, or hiring a single assistant coach.{{cite news| url=https://www.nytimes.com/2001/12/15/sports/notre-dame-coach-resigns-after-5-days-and-a-few-lies.html | work=The New York Times | first=John W. | last=Fountain | title=Notre Dame Coach Resigns After 5 Days and a Few Lies | date=December 15, 2001}} O'Leary's tenure is the shortest of any head coach in FBS history.{{cite news| url=http://www.nydailynews.com/sports/shortest-coaching-tenures-gallery-1.58158?pmSlide=1.68123 | location=New York | work=Daily News | title=Over and out! Shortest coaching tenures – slide 8}} O'Leary would go on to become the head football coach at the [[UCF Knights football|University of Central Florida]].{{cite web |agency=Associated Press |url=http://onlineathens.com/stories/120903/dog_20031209032.shtml |title=UCF hires O'Leary as new coach |publisher=Online Athens |date=December 9, 2003 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131225164239/http://onlineathens.com/stories/120903/dog_20031209032.shtml |archive-date=December 25, 2013 |url-status=dead |df=mdy-all }} ===Tyrone Willingham era (2002–2004)=== Once again in need of a new head coach, the school turned to [[Tyrone Willingham]], the head coach at [[Stanford Cardinal football|Stanford]].{{cite news |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/college/news/2001/12/31/notre_dame_willingham_ap/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20020103071050/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/football/college/news/2001/12/31/notre_dame_willingham_ap/ |url-status=dead |archive-date=January 3, 2002 |work=CNN |title=Notre Dame gives Stanford coach six-year deal |df=mdy-all }} Willingham's hiring made him the first [[African American]] head coach in Notre Dame football history.{{Cite web |title=ESPN Classic - Willingham brings untiring determination to ND |url=https://www.espn.com/classic/biography/s/Willingham_Tyrone.html |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=[[ESPN]]}} Bringing a feeling of change and excitement to campus, Willingham led the [[2002 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2002 squad]] to a 10–2 regular season record, including an 8–0 start with wins over #7 [[2002 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] and #11 [[2002 Florida State Seminoles football team|Florida State]] and a #4 ranking. This great early start, however, would be the lone highlight of Willingham's tenure, as Notre Dame finished the year with a loss to [[2002 Boston College Eagles football team|Boston College]], then lopsided losses to [[2002 USC Trojans football team|USC]] and [[2002 NC State Wolfpack football team|North Carolina State]] in the [[2003 Gator Bowl|Gator Bowl]].{{cite web |title=2002 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2002-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} The program faltered over the next two seasons under Willingham, compiling an 11–12 record.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/coaches/tyrone-willingham-1.html|title=Tyrone Willingham Coaching Record|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} During this time, Notre Dame lost a game by at least 30 points on five occasions. Furthermore, Willingham's 2004 recruiting class was judged by analysts to be the worst at Notre Dame in more than two decades.{{cite news|url=http://www.wthr.com/article/notre-dame-facing-bevy-of-problems|title=Notre Dame facing bevy of problems|publisher=WTHR|date=September 25, 2007|access-date=November 28, 2007|archive-date=December 20, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220031033/http://www.wthr.com/article/notre-dame-facing-bevy-of-problems|url-status=dead}} Citing Notre Dame's third consecutive four-touchdown loss to arch-rival [[2004 USC Trojans football team|USC]]{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=243320030|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042049/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=243320030|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 26, 2017|title=Notre Dame vs. USC – Game Recap – November 27, 2004 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} compounded by another year of subpar recruiting efforts, the Willingham era ended on November 30, 2004 after the conclusion of the season when the university chose to terminate him and pay out the remainder of Willingham's six-year contract.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=1935138|title=Willingham fired by Notre Dame after three years|date=November 30, 2004|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} ===Charlie Weis era (2005–2009)=== [[File:Charlie-Weis-2008CoachesTour-5-29-08.jpg|thumb|Coach Weis]] After Willingham's firing, Notre Dame initially pursued [[Utah Utes football|Utah]] head coach [[Urban Meyer]], who had been an Irish assistant from 1996 to 2000 and had a clause in his Utah contract that stated he could take the Notre Dame head coaching job without paying a buyout.{{Cite web |date=2004-12-02 |title=Notre Dame officials meet with Meyer in Utah |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=1936781 |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN.com |language=en}}{{cite web|url=https://www.orlandosentinel.com/2004/12/04/heres-the-truth-behind-the-meyer-hiring/|title=Here's The Truth Behind The Meyer Hiring|date=December 4, 2004 }} After Meyer accepted the [[Florida Gators football|Florida]] head coaching position and turned down the Irish,{{cite web|url=https://usatoday30.usatoday.com/sports/college/football/sec/2004-12-03-florida-meyer_x.htm|title=Urban Meyer jilts Notre Dame, heads to Florida|website=Usatoday30.usatoday.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} [[Charlie Weis]] left the [[National Football League|NFL]]'s [[New England Patriots]], where he won three [[Super Bowl]]s as offensive coordinator,{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/17379571/former-notre-dame-kansas-football-coach-charlie-weis-wants-new-gig|title=How to define Charlie Weis|website=Espn.com|date=24 August 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017}} to become head football coach for the Irish beginning with the [[2005 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2005 season]].{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=1943710|title=Weis to be introduced as Irish coach Monday|work=ESPN.com|date=13 December 2004 |access-date=September 6, 2015}} A first-time head coach when he was hired by Notre Dame,{{Cite web |title=Weis close to agreement with Notre Dame {{!}} The Spokesman-Review |url=https://www.spokesman.com/stories/2004/dec/12/weis-close-to-agreement-with-notre-dame/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=www.spokesman.com|date=December 12, 2004 }} Weis was officially introduced on December 12, 2004.{{Cite news |last=Schlabach |first=Mark |date=2004-12-12 |title=Patriots' Weis To Coach Notre Dame |language=en-US |newspaper=Washington Post |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/sports/2004/12/12/patriots-weis-to-coach-notre-dame/cfc5c8ce-4a27-493d-b590-dcda9e508c45/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |issn=0190-8286}} Weis' hiring as the Irish's 30th head football coach made him the first Notre Dame graduate to hold the football head coaching position on a full-fledged basis since [[Joe Kuharich]].{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=1943710|title=Weis to be introduced as Irish coach Monday|publisher=ESPN|date=December 13, 2004|access-date=November 13, 2007}} Weis signed a six-year contract worth about $2 million annually excluding incentives.{{Cite web |last= |date=2004-12-13 |title=Weis signs with Notre Dame |url=https://www.deseret.com/2004/12/13/19866495/weis-signs-with-notre-dame |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=Deseret News |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=Weis to Notre Dame; deal is officially done |url=https://www.capecodtimes.com/story/sports/2004/12/13/weis-to-notre-dame-deal/50915035007/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=Cape Cod Times |language=en-US}}{{Cite web |title=Weis gets six-year deal |url=https://www.milforddailynews.com/story/sports/2004/12/12/weis-gets-six-year-deal/41179797007/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=Milford Daily News |language=en-US}} To kick off his [[2005 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|inaugural season]], Weis led the Irish to back-to-back road wins over ranked teams, the first time that had been done by a Notre Dame coach since [[Knute Rockne]]. On September 25, Weis and the Irish traveled to [[Seattle, Washington|Seattle]] to face [[2005 Washington Huskies football team|Washington]] and former head coach [[Tyrone Willingham]], who was hired by the Huskies to be their head coach two weeks after getting fired at Notre Dame.{{cite web|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2004/12/13/uw-hires-willingham/|title=UW hires Willingham|website=[[Chicago Tribune]] |date=December 13, 2004 }} The Irish won by a score of 36–17.{{cite web |title=Notre Dame vs. Washington - Game Recap - September 24, 2005 - ESPN |url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=252670264 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926041500/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=252670264 |archive-date=September 26, 2017 |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=ESPN.com}} Entering the rivalry game with [[2005 USC Trojans football team|USC]], defending national champions and owners of a 27-game winning streak, the Irish had risen to #9 in the AP Poll. In one of the most famous games in [[Notre Dame–USC football rivalry|the rivalry]]'s history, USC defeated Notre Dame [[2005 USC vs. Notre Dame football game|34–31]]. The game concluded with Trojan running back and eventual Heisman winner [[Reggie Bush]], who had already scored three touchdowns that day, illegally pushing quarterback [[Matt Leinart]] into the end zone on the final play of the game in a play now known as the "Bush Push". The Irish won their remaining five games to earn an appearance in the [[2006 Fiesta Bowl|Fiesta Bowl]]. They were defeated there by the [[2005 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State Buckeyes]] 34–20{{cite web |date=December 13, 2015 |title=Flashback: Ohio State Last Met Notre Dame in the 2006 Fiesta Bowl |url=http://www.elevenwarriors.com/ohio-state-football/2015/12/64992/flashback-ohio-state-last-met-notre-dame-in-the-2006-fiesta-bowl}} to finish the season 9–3 and #9 in the AP poll, their highest final ranking since 1993. Quarterback [[Brady Quinn]] broke numerous team passing records that season and became a top [[Heisman Trophy]] contender.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/brady_quinn_235722.html|title=Brady Quinn Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005947/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/brady_quinn_235722.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} Wide receiver [[Jeff Samardzija]] was the team's leading receiver and also broke Notre Dame's single-season records for receiving yards and touchdowns.{{cite web|url=http://bleacherreport.com/articles/2130477-notre-dame-football-what-if-jeff-samardzija-had-kept-playing-football|title=Notre Dame Football: What If Jeff Samardzija Had Kept Playing Football?|first=Keith|last=Arnold|website=[[Bleacher Report]] }} During the 2005 season, Notre Dame signed Weis to a big raise and ten-year contract extension that was set to keep the coach at the university through the 2015 season.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=2207478|title=ND's Weis gets contract extension worth $30–40 million|date=October 29, 2005|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite web |title=2005 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2005-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} Weis and the Irish went into the [[2006 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2006 season]] with a No. 2 preseason ranking in the ESPN/Coaches Poll.{{cite web|url=http://www.uhnd.com/football/2006-season/notre-dame-opens-2-in-ap/|title=Notre Dame Opens 2006 Season #2 in AP Poll // UHND.com|date=August 20, 2006}} They finished the regular season with a 10–2 record, losing only to [[2006 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] and [[2006 USC Trojans football team|USC]]. Notre Dame accepted a bid to the [[2007 Sugar Bowl]], losing to [[2006 LSU Tigers football team|LSU]] 41–14. This marked their ninth consecutive postseason loss, the longest drought in NCAA history.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/bowls06/columns/story?columnist=schlabach_mark&id=2719490|title=Schlabach: Notre Dame shows it doesn't belong in BCS bowls|date=January 4, 2007|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} As a result, Notre Dame dropped to #17 in the final rankings.{{cite web|url=http://www.nationalchamps.net/NCAA/2006_final_polls.htm|title=2006 College Football Final Polls|website=Nationalchamps.net|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{cite web |title=2006 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2006-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} In the wake of a graduating class that sent eleven players to the NFL,{{cite news|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/043007aac.html|title=Seven Notre Dame Football Players Drafted into NFL; Four Others Sign Free Agent Contracts|publisher=und.cstv.com|date=April 30, 2007|access-date=November 28, 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150123225443/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/043007aac.html|archive-date=January 23, 2015|url-status=dead}} the [[2007 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2007 season]] included various negative milestones: the most losses in a single year (9),{{cite web|url=http://football.stassen.com/cgi-bin/records/fetch-team.pl?team=Notre_Dame|title=All-Time Records for Notre Dame|work=stassen.com|access-date=September 6, 2015|archive-date=September 12, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150912094500/http://football.stassen.com/cgi-bin/records/fetch-team.pl?team=Notre_Dame|url-status=dead}} two of the ten worst losses in program history (38–0 losses to both [[2007 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]]{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=272580130|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926041654/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=272580130|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 26, 2017|title=Notre Dame vs. Michigan – Game Recap – September 15, 2007 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} and [[2007 USC Trojans football team|USC]]),{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap/_/id/272930087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042307/http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap/_/id/272930087|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 26, 2017|title=USC vs. Notre Dame – Game Recap – October 20, 2007 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} and the first 6-game losing streak for home games in ND history.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/090514aab.html|title=Notre Dame-Michigan: To The End|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005815/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/090514aab.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} The [[2007 Navy Midshipmen football team|Naval Academy]] recorded [[2007 Navy vs. Notre Dame football game|their first win over the Irish since 1963]], breaking Notre Dame's NCAA-record 43-game win streak.{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=273070087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170111005630/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=273070087|url-status=dead|archive-date=January 11, 2017|title=Navy vs. Notre Dame – Game Recap – November 3, 2007 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} In [[2008 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2008]], the Irish started 4–1 but completed the regular season with a 6–6 record,{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2008-schedule.html|title=2008 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} including a 24–23 home loss to [[Syracuse Orange football|Syracuse]], the first time that Notre Dame had fallen to an eight-loss team.{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=283270087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926042322/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=283270087|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 26, 2017|title=Syracuse vs. Notre Dame – Game Recap – November 22, 2008 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Quarterback [[Jimmy Clausen]] was the team's star player, completing over 60% of his passes.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/clausen_jimmy00.html|title=Jimmy Clausen Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150906084054/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/clausen_jimmy00.html|archive-date=2015-09-06|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=http://bleacherreport.com/articles/428451-jimmy-clausen-the-notre-dame-legend-that-never-was|title=Jimmy Clausen: The Notre Dame Legend That Never Was|first=Dan|last=Scofield|website=[[Bleacher Report]] }} Despite speculation the university might fire Weis, the university announced that he would remain head coach.{{cite news|url=https://sports.yahoo.com/ncaa/football/news?slug=ap-notredame-weis&prov=ap&type=lgns|title=Notre Dame keeps Weis, though season 'fell short'|publisher=und.cstv.com|date=December 3, 2008|access-date=December 3, 2008}} Weis's Notre Dame squad ended the season breaking the Irish's NCAA bowl losing streak by beating [[2008 Hawaii Warriors football team|Hawaii]] 49–21 in the [[2008 Hawaii Bowl|Hawaii Bowl]].{{cite news|url= http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap?gameId=283590087|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20161220032747/http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap?gameId=283590087|url-status= dead|archive-date= December 20, 2016|title=Clausen sets ND records with 401 yards passing, 5 TDs in romp |work=[[ESPN]] |date=December 24, 2008|access-date=December 24, 2008}} Weis entered the 2009 season with the expectation from the Notre Dame administration that his team would be in position to compete for a [[Bowl Championship Series|BCS Bowl]] berth.{{cite news|first=George|last=Smith|url= https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=4670853 |title=Swarbrick: Didn't see Navy loss coming |work=[[ESPN]]|date=November 19, 2008|access-date=November 27, 2008}} Notre Dame started the first part of the season 4–2, with close losses to [[2009 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] and [[2009 USC Trojans football team|USC]]. Sitting at 6–2, however, Notre Dame lost to [[2009 Navy Midshipmen football team|Navy]] for the second time in three years,{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap/_/id/293110087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170226214202/http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap/_/id/293110087|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 26, 2017|title=Navy vs. Notre Dame - Game Recap - November 7, 2009 - ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} which became the first loss in a four-game losing streak to finish the season. Quarterback [[Jimmy Clausen]] and wide receiver [[Golden Tate]] would forgo their senior seasons and enter the [[NFL draft]].{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/nfl/draft10/news/story?id=4721703|title=Notre Dame's Clausen decides to enter draft|date=December 7, 2009|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Weis was fired on November 30, 2009.{{cite web | url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=4700891 | title=Notre Dame fires Weis after five seasons | date=November 30, 2009 }} According to his buyout provision, Weis was to be paid $6 million first and then $2.05 million annually until the contract ran out in December 2015 for a total of about $19 million.{{cite web|url=http://www.indystar.com/story/sports/college/notre-dame/2017/05/15/notre-dames-charlie-weis-buyout-gift-keeps-giving/322298001/|title=Notre Dame paid Charlie Weis nearly $19 million to go away}} During that time, Weis made more money annually not to coach the Irish than his successor [[Brian Kelly (American football coach)|Brian Kelly]] earned to coach the team.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/15566256/tax-returns-notre-dame-paid-charlie-weis-more-2014-15-brian-kelly|title=Weis still earning more cash from ND than Kelly|date=16 May 2016 }} ===Brian Kelly era (2010–2021)=== [[File:Brian Kelly.jpeg|thumb|Coach Kelly]] On December 10, 2009, [[Cincinnati Bearcats football|Cincinnati]] head coach [[Brian Kelly (American football coach)|Brian Kelly]] became the 31st head coach of the Fighting Irish after leading the [[2009 Cincinnati Bearcats football team|Bearcats]] to a 12–0 record and BCS bowl-game berth, but he left the team before the bowl game.{{cite web | url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=4734086 | title=Kelly: Notre Dame will 'go to work right away' | date=December 11, 2009 }}{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=4734086|title=Kelly: Notre Dame will 'go to work right away'|date=December 11, 2009|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Kelly's hiring surprised very few as he was a northern Irish Catholic who was considered one of college football's rising stars.{{cite news | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/12/08/sports/ncaafootball/08irish.html | title=Kelly to Interview with Notre Dame | work=The New York Times | date=December 7, 2009 | last1=Thamel | first1=Pete }} In his [[2010 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|first season]], Kelly led the Fighting Irish to an 8–5 record.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2010-schedule.html|title=2010 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Tragedy struck early in the season when Declan Sullivan, a junior working for the athletic department, died while filming a practice on a scissor lift in dangerously high winds.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/news/story?id=5734494|title=Notre Dame student taping football practice dies|date=October 27, 2010|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} [[Dayne Crist]] started the season at quarterback but was injured for a second consecutive year, this time in the [[2010 Tulsa Golden Hurricane football team|Tulsa]] game, which the Irish lost.{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=303030087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160915052419/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=303030087|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 15, 2016|title=Tulsa vs. Notre Dame - Game Recap - October 30, 2010 - ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Kelly turned to freshman quarterback [[Tommy Rees (quarterback)|Tommy Rees]], who led the Irish to victories in the last three games against #15 [[Utah Utes football|Utah]], [[Army Black Knights football|Army]] in [[Yankee Stadium]], and breaking an eight-year losing streak to USC in the [[Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum|LA Coliseum]]. Kelly guided the Irish to a 33–17 win over [[2010 Miami Hurricanes football team|Miami (FL)]] in the [[2010 Sun Bowl]] to finish 2010 with an 8–5 record.{{cite web|url=http://www.scout.com/college/notre-dame/story/802274-2010-season-preview-notre-dame|title=Notre Dame Fighting Irish Football, Basketball, and Recruiting Front Page|work=scout.com|access-date=September 6, 2015}} With wideout [[Michael Floyd]] returning for his senior season and an outstanding recruiting class that included several highly touted defensive linemen,{{cite web|url=http://www.news-sentinel.com/sports/ndu/Looking-back-on-the-2011-Notre-Dame-recruiting-class--Part-I|title=Looking back on the 2011 Notre Dame recruiting class, Part I|website=News-Sentinel.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}}{{dead link|date=February 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} Kelly and the Irish looked to improve on their 8–5 record from the prior year. However, an early season upset to a [[Skip Holtz]]-led [[2011 South Florida Bulls football team|South Florida team]] and a last-second loss to [[2011 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] in [[Ann Arbor, Michigan|Ann Arbor]], games in which the Irish committed 10 total turnovers, left the Irish at 0–2 to start the season. The Irish bounced back to beat #15 [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]] and had two 4-game winning streaks, with the only loss during that stretch coming at the hands of [[2011 USC Trojans football team|USC]].{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=312950087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170926041233/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=312950087|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 26, 2017|title=USC vs. Notre Dame – Game Recap – October 22, 2011 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} The Irish also broke Navy's 2-game winning streak over Notre Dame. Notre Dame lost 18–14 to [[2011 Florida State Seminoles football team|Florida State]] in the [[2011 Champs Sports Bowl]], concluding the 2011 campaign with an 8–5 record overall, identical to the 2010 season.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2011-schedule.html|title=2011 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} In the team's losses, multiple turnovers from the quarterback position were often the culprit, and as a whole turnovers at critical times in the game often derailed potential Irish comebacks. On September 12, 2012, during the football program's 125th season, Notre Dame announced that it would leave the [[Big East Conference (1979–2013)|Big East Conference]] for the [[Atlantic Coast Conference]], excluding the football and hockey programs.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-sports/story/_/id/8369070/notre-dame-sports-football-hockey-acc|title=Notre Dame sports, except football, joins ACC|date=12 September 2012 }} This move became official on July 1, 2013. While the Fighting Irish football team remained an FBS independent, it agreed to play five games per season against ACC teams starting with the 2014 football season. In return, Notre Dame became eligible to participate in the ACC's sub-BCS level bowl arrangements.{{cite press release|url=http://www.theacc.com/genrel/091212aaa.html |title=ACC Accepts Notre Dame as New Member |publisher=Atlantic Coast Conference |date=September 12, 2012 |access-date=September 12, 2012 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120913011751/http://www.theacc.com/genrel/091212aaa.html |archive-date=September 13, 2012 |df=mdy }} The [[2012 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2012 season]] was another entry in historic third seasons for Notre Dame head coaches. The Irish began the season 7–0, including wins over ranked rivals in [[2012 Michigan State Spartans football team|Michigan State]], [[2012 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]], and [[2012 Stanford Cardinal football team|Stanford]]. The game against the Cardinal required a [[Goal line stand|goal-line stand]] against running back [[Stepfan Taylor]] in overtime to preserve the win for the Irish.{{Cite web |title=Notre Dame 20-13 Stanford (Oct 13, 2012) Game Recap |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/322870087 |access-date=2024-08-20 |website=ESPN |language=en}} The season's signature win came at [[2012 Oklahoma Sooners football team|#8 Oklahoma]] with ESPN's [[College GameDay (football TV program)|College GameDay]] in town. On November 18, after a loss by [[2012 Oregon Ducks football team|#1 Oregon]], Notre Dame became ranked #1 in the nation in both the AP and Coaches' polls after reaching 11–0 during the regular season for the first time since 1993, also ranking #1 in the [[Bowl Championship Series|BCS standings]] for the first time in the 14-year history of the selection system.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/kelly_brian00.html|title=Brian Kelly Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005935/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/kelly_brian00.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} After defeating [[2012 USC Trojans football team|USC]] on November 24,{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=323290030|title=Notre Dame vs. USC – Game Recap – November 24, 2012 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Notre Dame concluded its first 12–0 regular season, and the Irish were soon formally named to appear in the [[BCS National Championship Game]] for the first time. In that game, on January 7, 2013, the Irish lost to [[2012 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|Alabama]] [[2013 BCS National Championship Game|42–14]].{{cite news| url=https://www.foxnews.com/sports/alabama-beats-notre-dame-to-win-bcs-championship-game | work=Fox News | title=Alabama beats Notre Dame to win BCS championship game | date=January 8, 2013}} The strength of the 2012 Fighting Irish was its defense, captained by senior leader [[Manti Te'o]], who broke the school record for interceptions by a linebacker with 7 and finished second to [[Johnny Manziel]] in Heisman Trophy voting. Coming off the previous year's national title game appearance, the Fighting Irish were dealt a blow when 2012 starting quarterback, [[Everett Golson]], was suspended from the University due to an academic violation.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/9899850/notre-dame-suspension-due-poor-judgment-test|title=Golson: Suspension for poor judgment on test|date=29 October 2013 }} Senior Tommy Rees then took over. Notre Dame's [[2013 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2013 season]] ended with a record of 9–4,{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2013-schedule.html|title=2013 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} a victory over [[2013 Rutgers Scarlet Knights football team|Rutgers]] in the [[2013 Pinstripe Bowl|Pinstripe Bowl]], and a #20 AP poll ranking.{{cite web|url=http://chicago.cbslocal.com/2013/12/28/notre-dame-beats-rutgers-29-16-in-pinstripe-bowl/|title=Notre Dame Beats Rutgers 29–16 in Pinstripe Bowl|work=cbslocal.com|date=December 28, 2013 |access-date=September 6, 2015}}{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/rankings/_/year/2013/|title=2013 NCAA College Football Polls and Rankings for Week 1 – ESPN|work=ESPN.com|access-date=September 6, 2015}} The [[2014 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2014 season]] started off with 6 straight victories and a #5 national ranking heading into a showdown with #2 [[2014 Florida State Seminoles football team|Florida State]] in [[Tallahassee, Florida]]. FSU won that game 31–27, on a controversial offensive [[pass interference]] call that brought back a last-second Notre Dame touchdown. The Fighting Irish bounced back with a win against [[2014 Navy Midshipmen football team|Navy]] before dropping their final 4 games of the season. They did win the [[2014 Music City Bowl|Music City Bowl]] by defeating the [[2014 LSU Tigers football team|LSU Tigers]] and finished the season at an 8–5 record.{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2014-schedule.html|title=2014 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results - College Football at Sports-Reference.com|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161226221830/http://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2014-schedule.html|archive-date=December 26, 2016|df=mdy-all}} The [[2015 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2015 Fighting Irish]] began its season with another new offensive coordinator, [[Mike Sanford Jr.]]{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/mike_sanford_957996.html|title=Mike Sanford Bio :: Notre Dame Football :: UND.COM :: The Official Site of Notre Dame Athletics|access-date=2018-08-19|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820005902/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/mtt/mike_sanford_957996.html|archive-date=2018-08-20|url-status=dead}} Led for most of the year by sophomore quarterback [[DeShone Kizer]], the Irish had fourteen plays of over 50 yards during the season, which was a school record, including two touchdowns of over 90 yards (a 91-yard touchdown run by [[C. J. Prosise]] and a 98-yard touchdown run by [[Josh Adams (American football)|Josh Adams]]). The Irish only had two in the previous 126 years of Notre Dame football. They finished the regular season averaging 34 points per game, including a 62-point effort against UMass, the most points in a game since 1996. The Irish began the season 10–1 and were in position for a [[College Football Playoff]] appearance, but lost a last-second game to [[2015 Stanford Cardinal football team|Stanford]] to finish with a 10–2 record and a berth in the [[2016 Fiesta Bowl (January)|Fiesta Bowl]].{{cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2015-schedule.html|title=2015 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Star linebacker [[Jaylon Smith]] was injured early in the game which became a loss to [[2015 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State]].{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=400852736|title=Notre Dame vs. Ohio State – Game Recap – January 1, 2016 – ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} The [[2016 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2016 season]] ended with a 4–8 record, Brian Kelly's worst win–loss record at Notre Dame. The tone for the season was set early, with a heartbreaking double overtime loss to [[2016 Texas Longhorns football team|Texas]] in the season opener.{{cite web|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/game?gameId=400868946|title=Notre Dame vs. Texas - Game Summary - September 4, 2016 - ESPN|website=ESPN.com|access-date=November 16, 2017}} Just 4 games into the season, Brian Kelly fired defensive coordinator [[Brian VanGorder]].{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/college-football/2016/09/25/notre-dame-fighting-irish-brain-vangorder-fired|title=VanGorder firing won't fix Notre Dame's real issue|first=Brian|last=Hamilton|date=September 25, 2016 }} At the end of the season, starting quarterback [[DeShone Kizer]] declared for the NFL draft{{cite web|url=http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/college/ct-notre-dame-deshone-kizer-nfl-draft-20161212-story.html|title=Notre Dame QB DeShone Kizer declares for NFL draft|first=Paul|last=Skrbina|website=[[Chicago Tribune]] |date=December 12, 2016 }} and backup quarterback [[Malik Zaire]] announced he would be transferring in the winter after graduation.{{cite web|url=http://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/college/ct-malik-zaire-choices-notre-dame-football-spt-1130-20161129-story.html|title=Malik Zaire to get release from Notre Dame; could end up at Wisconsin|first=Paul|last=Skrbina|website=[[Chicago Tribune]] |date=November 29, 2016 }}{{cite web |title=2016 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2016-schedule.html |access-date=November 16, 2017 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} Amidst speculation that Kelly's job was in jeopardy and that Kelly was looking to leave Notre Dame, athletics director [[Jack Swarbrick]] announced that Kelly would return for the 2017 season.{{cite web|url=https://www.si.com/college-football/2016/10/21/brian-kelly-notre-dame-return-2017-jack-swarbrick|title=Notre Dame AD says Brian Kelly to return in 2017|website=Si.com|date=October 21, 2016 |access-date=November 16, 2017}} The [[2017 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2017 season]] was one of Kelly's strongest seasons at ND. An early one-point loss to [[2017 Georgia Bulldogs football team|#2 Georgia]] was the only blemish on an 8-1 start, keyed by a 49-14 blowout over rival [[2017 USC Trojans football team|USC]]. The tough running of running back Josh Adams behind an experienced and talented offensive line, which included future top-10 picks [[Quenton Nelson]] and [[Mike McGlinchey (offensive lineman)|Mike McGlinchey]] and won the [[Joe Moore Award]] as the best offensive line in that nation, allowed Notre Dame to string together 6 consecutive 20+ point victories. However, when #3 Notre Dame visited [[2017 Miami Hurricanes football team|#7 Miami]] in South Florida on November 7, an embarrassing 41-8 loss effectively ended the Irish hopes of a playoff run. They ended the season with a win over [[2017 LSU Tigers football team|LSU]] in the [[2017 Music City Bowl|Music City Bowl]].{{Cite web |title=2017 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results {{!}} College Football at Sports-Reference.com |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2017-schedule.html |access-date=2018-04-11 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en}} The Irish opened the 2018 season at home against [[2018 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]] and won, 24–17.{{Cite web |title=Michigan at Notre Dame Box Score, September 1, 2018 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2018-09-01-notre-dame.html |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en |archive-date=February 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230221191330/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2018-09-01-notre-dame.html |url-status=live }} After benching quarterback [[Brandon Wimbush]] in favor of [[Ian Book]], the Irish then won the remainder of their regular season games, including victories over #7 [[2018 Stanford Cardinal football team|Stanford]], #24 [[2018 Virginia Tech Hokies football team|Virginia Tech]], #12 [[2018 Syracuse Orange football team|Syracuse]], and [[2018 Northwestern Wildcats football team|Northwestern]].{{Cite web |title=2018 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2018-schedule.html |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en |archive-date=March 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321203705/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2018-schedule.html |url-status=live }} This led to Notre Dame's first undefeated regular season since 2012.{{Cite web |date=November 2, 2018 |title=Notre Dame Football: Let's Compare the 2012 & 2018 Offenses |url=https://www.onefootdown.com/2018/11/2/18057818/notre-dame-football-irish-college-2012-2018-offense-undefeated-everett-golson-ian-book-rees-wimbush |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=One Foot Down |language=en |archive-date=August 9, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220809054338/https://www.onefootdown.com/2018/11/2/18057818/notre-dame-football-irish-college-2012-2018-offense-undefeated-everett-golson-ian-book-rees-wimbush |url-status=live }} They were ranked #3 in the nation by the College Football Playoff committee and selected to play in the [[2018 College Football Playoff|College Football Playoff]] semifinal at the [[2018 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton Bowl]] against [[2018 Clemson Tigers football team|Clemson]].{{Cite web |last=Staats |first=Wayne |date=December 19, 2018 |title=College Football Playoff: Alabama, Clemson, Notre Dame and Oklahoma in top four |url=https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2018-12-02/college-football-playoff-alabama-clemson-notre-dame-and-oklahoma |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=NCAA.com |language=en |archive-date=August 13, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220813152944/https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2018-12-02/college-football-playoff-alabama-clemson-notre-dame-and-oklahoma |url-status=live }} Notre Dame's undefeated streak came to an end after losing to the eventual national champions 30–3 to finish the season at 12–1.{{Cite web |title=Cotton Bowl - Notre Dame vs Clemson Box Score, December 29, 2018 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2018-12-29-clemson.html |access-date=March 21, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en |archive-date=March 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321203810/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2018-12-29-clemson.html |url-status=live }} Kelly was named AP Coach of the Year and Home Depot Coach of the Year for the 2018 season.{{Cite web |last=Russo |first=Ralph D. |date=December 17, 2018 |title=Notre Dame's Brian Kelly wins second AP Coach of the Year award |url=https://www.indystar.com/story/sports/college/notre-dame/2018/12/17/notre-dames-brian-kelly-wins-second-ap-coach-year-award-fighting-irish/2337103002/ |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=The Indianapolis Star |language=en-US |archive-date=August 20, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220820051956/https://www.indystar.com/story/sports/college/notre-dame/2018/12/17/notre-dames-brian-kelly-wins-second-ap-coach-year-award-fighting-irish/2337103002/ |url-status=live }}{{Cite web |title=Home Depot Coach of the Year Award Winners |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/home-depot-coy.html |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en |archive-date=December 27, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221227201841/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/awards/home-depot-coy.html |url-status=live }} Kelly and the Fighting Irish started off the [[2019 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2019 season]] with a #9 ranking in the AP Poll. Notre Dame won their first two games before dropping a 23–17 result to #3 [[2019 Georgia Bulldogs football team|Georgia]]. Notre Dame won their next three games before dropping a 45–14 game to #19 [[2019 Michigan Wolverines football team|Michigan]].
