Notre Dame closed out the regular season with victories in their last five games, followed by a win in the [[2019 Camping World Bowl|Camping World Bowl]] 33–9 over [[2019 Iowa State Cyclones football team|Iowa State]].{{Cite web |title=Camping World Bowl - Notre Dame vs Iowa State Box Score, December 28, 2019 |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2019-12-28-iowa-state.html |access-date=March 21, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en |archive-date=March 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321203720/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/boxscores/2019-12-28-iowa-state.html |url-status=live }} In response to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]] and the [[Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on gridiron football|cancellation of regular season scheduled games]], Notre Dame joined the Atlantic Coast Conference (ACC) in football for a single season in [[2020 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2020]] and played a full slate of conference matches.{{Cite web |last=Martinelli |first=Michelle R. |date=July 30, 2020 |title=Answering 8 questions about Notre Dame football joining the ACC in 2020 |url=https://ftw.usatoday.com/2020/07/college-football-notre-dame-acc-explain-conference-championship |access-date=March 21, 2023 |website=For The Win |language=en-US |archive-date=March 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321203619/https://ftw.usatoday.com/2020/07/college-football-notre-dame-acc-explain-conference-championship |url-status=live }} The Irish won all ten of their games, including a mid-season contest against perennial conference power and top-ranked [[2020 Clemson Tigers football team|Clemson]]. The Fighting Irish defeated the Tigers 47–40 in double overtime. However, Clemson defeated the Irish in a rematch in the [[2020 ACC Championship Game|ACC Championship Game]] 34–10. Notre Dame qualified for the [[2020 NCAA Division I FBS football season#College Football Playoff|College Football Playoff]] as the #4 seed.{{Cite web |last=Staats |first=Wayne |date=December 20, 2020 |title=College Football Playoff: Alabama, Clemson, Ohio State and Notre Dame lead rankings |url=https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2020-12-20/college-football-playoff-alabama-clemson-ohio-state-and-notre-dame-make-semifinals |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=NCAA.com |language=en |archive-date=March 13, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230313183944/https://www.ncaa.com/news/football/article/2020-12-20/college-football-playoff-alabama-clemson-ohio-state-and-notre-dame-make-semifinals |url-status=live }} In the College Football Playoff Semifinals against [[2020 Alabama Crimson Tide football team|Alabama]] at the [[2021 Rose Bowl|Rose Bowl]], Notre Dame lost 31–14 to finish with a 10–2 record.{{Cite web |title=2020 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2020-schedule.html |access-date=April 2, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en |archive-date=March 21, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321203648/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2020-schedule.html |url-status=live }} In what would become Kelly's final season as head coach in [[2021 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2021]], the Fighting Irish were ranked #9 to begin the season, but struggled in their first two games, beating [[2021 Florida State Seminoles football team|Florida State]] 41–38 and [[2021 Toledo Rockets football team|Toledo]] 32–29. Following a 24–13 loss to #7 [[2021 Cincinnati Bearcats football team|Cincinnati]], Kelly helped lead the Fighting Irish to a seven-game winning streak to finish the regular season 11–1 and earn a berth in the [[Fiesta Bowl]].{{Cite web |title=2021 Notre Dame Fighting Irish Schedule and Results |url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2021-schedule.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321203029/https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/2021-schedule.html |archive-date=March 21, 2023 |access-date=March 21, 2023 |website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com |language=en}} On November 29, 2021, Kelly resigned as Notre Dame head coach to accept a ten-year, $95 million contract offer to become head coach at [[LSU Tigers football|LSU]].{{Cite web |date=2021-11-30 |title=Kelly leaves ND for LSU, gets 10-year, $95M deal |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/32748893/lsu-set-hire-notre-dame-brian-kelly-next-head-football-coach-sources-say |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN.com |language=en}} In so doing, Kelly became the first Irish head coach since Thomas A. Barry in 1907 to leave Notre Dame for another coaching job.{{Cite web |last= |date=2021-11-30 |title=Brian Kelly leaves Notre Dame as he predicted, though the unexpected rush overshadows his successes |url=https://www.nbcsports.com/college-football/news/brian-kelly-leaves-notre-dame-as-he-predicted-though-the-unexpected-rush-overshadows-his-successes |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=NBC Sports |language=en-US}} Kelly left the Irish as the program's all-time winningest head coach.{{Cite web |date=2021-09-25 |title=Notre Dame's Brian Kelly passes Knute Rockne as winningest coach in Fighting Irish history |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/notre-dames-brian-kelly-passes-knute-rockne-as-winningest-coach-in-fighting-irish-history/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=CBSSports.com |language=en}}{{Citation |title=Brian Kelly Notre Dame's All-Time Winningest Coach |date=2021-09-26 |url=https://fightingirish.com/brian-kelly-notre-dames-all-time-winningest-coach/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |publisher= |language=en-US}} ===Marcus Freeman era (2021–present)=== [[File:90th Annual Tony the Tiger Sun Bowl game (8189157).jpg|thumb|right|Notre Dame in the [[2023 Sun Bowl]]]] On December 3, 2021, Notre Dame's defensive coordinator [[Marcus Freeman]] was promoted to the head coaching position to replace Brian Kelly, becoming the 32nd head coach in program history.{{Cite web |date=2021-12-03 |title=Notre Dame elevates DC Freeman to head coach |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/story/_/id/32777349/sources-notre-dame-fighting-irish-expected-announce-hiring-marcus-freeman-head-football-coach-friday-morning |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN.com |language=en}}{{cite news |first=Shehan |last=Jeyarajah |date=December 2, 2021 |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/notre-dame-hires-marcus-freeman-as-next-coach-irish-listen-to-players-also-retain-tommy-rees-to-lead-offense/ |title=Notre Dame hires Marcus Freeman as next coach: Irish listen to players, also retain Tommy Rees to lead offense |work=CBS Sports}} The second [[African American]] head coach in the history of the Irish football program, Freeman signed a five-year contract worth $4 million annually excluding incentives.{{Cite news |title=BREAKING: Notre Dame tabbing Marcus Freeman as head coach |language=en |work=Footballscoop |url=https://footballscoop.com/news/notre-dame-marcus-freeman-lsu-brian-kelly-ohio-state |access-date=2023-08-22}}{{Cite news |last=Blinder |first=Alan |date=2022-09-02 |title=Marcus Freeman is 36. He's Also in Charge at Notre Dame. |language=en-US |work=The New York Times |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/09/02/sports/ncaafootball/marcus-freeman-notre-dame.html |access-date=2023-08-22 |issn=0362-4331}}{{Cite magazine |title=Notre Dame hires Marcus Freeman as next head coach - Sports Illustrated |language=en-us |magazine=Sports Illustrated |url=https://www.si.com/college/2021/12/03/notre-dame-hires-marcus-freeman-head-coach |access-date=2023-08-22}} Freeman took control immediately, coaching the Irish in their [[2022 Fiesta Bowl (January)|Fiesta Bowl]] loss to [[2021 Oklahoma State Cowboys football team|Oklahoma State]].{{Cite web |title=Oklahoma State storms back to hand Notre Dame another big-game loss in Fiesta Bowl |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/ncaaf/2022/01/01/oklahoma-state-epic-comeback-beat-notre-dame-fiesta-bowl/9069434002/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=USA TODAY |language=en-US}}{{Cite web |title=Oklahoma State 37-35 Notre Dame (Jan 1, 2022) Final Score |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/game/_/gameId/401331238 |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN |language=en}} Freeman opened the [[2022 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2022 season]] with losses to [[2022 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State]] (his alma mater) and [[2022 Marshall Thundering Herd football team|Marshall]], thus becoming first head coach in Notre Dame history to start his tenure with three losses.{{cite web | url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/news/now-0-3-notre-dame-coach-marcus-freeman-must-dig-himself-out-of-the-worst-start-in-program-history/ | title=Now 0-3, Notre Dame coach Marcus Freeman must dig himself out of the worst start in program history | date=10 September 2022 }} He collected his first win the following week against [[2022 California Golden Bears football team|California]].{{Cite web |title=California 17-24 Notre Dame (Sep 17, 2022) Game Recap |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/401403987 |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN |language=en}}{{Cite web |date=2022-09-17 |title=Pyne throws for 2 TDs as Notre Dame beats Cal 24-17 - CBS San Francisco |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/sanfrancisco/news/pyne-throws-2-tds-notre-dame-beats-cal-24-17/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=www.cbsnews.com |language=en-US}} Freeman's Irish would go on to finish the regular season ranked 19th with a record of 8-4, including an upset win over #5 [[2022 Clemson Tigers football team|Clemson]].{{Cite web |title=Clemson 14-35 Notre Dame (Nov 5, 2022) Game Recap |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/401404130 |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN |language=en}}{{Cite web |last= |date=2022-11-05 |title=Notre Dame dominates No. 4 Clemson in all phases in a stunning 35-14 upset |url=https://www.nbcsports.com/college-football/news/notre-dame-dominates-no-4-clemson-in-all-phases-in-a-stunning-35-14-upset |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=NBC Sports |language=en-US}} They were awarded a berth in the [[2022 Gator Bowl|Gator Bowl]] where they defeated [[2022 South Carolina Gamecocks football team|South Carolina]] 45–38.{{Cite web |title=Notre Dame 45-38 South Carolina (Dec 30, 2022) Game Recap |url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap/_/gameId/401442036 |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=ESPN |language=en}}{{Cite web |title=Notre Dame Fighting Irish vs. South Carolina Gamecocks Live Score and Stats - December 30, 2022 Gametracker |url=https://www.cbssports.com/college-football/gametracker/recap/NCAAF_20221230_ND@SC/ |access-date=2023-08-22 |website=CBSSports.com |language=en}} In [[2023 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2023]], Freeman guided Notre Dame to a 10-win season. Safety [[Xavier Watts]] received the [[Bronko Nagurski Trophy]] for college football's best defensive player and the team earned victories over [[2023 Duke Blue Devils football team|#17 Duke]] (and future Notre Dame quarterback [[Riley Leonard]]), [[2023 USC Trojans football team|#10 USC]], and [[2023 Oregon State Beavers football team|#19 Oregon State]] in the [[2023 Sun Bowl|Sun Bowl]]. Notre Dame finished the year ranked #14 in the final AP Top 25. Freeman and the Fighting Irish played their first home game of the [[2024 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|2024 season]] against [[2024 Northern Illinois Huskies football team|Northern Illinois]] as the #5 ranked team in the AP Top 25 and as a 28.5-point favorite to win. They fell to the Huskies 16-14, with quarterback Riley Leonard throwing two interceptions and the team giving up two blocked field goals. It was the second early-season home loss for the Irish against a heavy underdog in three years following the 2022 loss to Marshall, the first-ever win by a [[Mid-American Conference]] football program against a top 5 team, the first-ever Huskie win against a top 10 team, and one of the largest point-spread upsets in modern college football.{{cite news |last= bromberg |first= nick |date= September 7, 2024 |title= Northern Illinois stuns No. 5 Notre Dame for first-ever win over a top-10 team |url= https://sports.yahoo.com/northern-illinois-stuns-no-5-notre-dame-for-first-ever-win-over-a-top-10-team-224835461.html |location= Yahoo! Sports}} Despite the upset, the Irish won every remaining game to complete the regular season 11-1 and hosted a home game in the first year of the expanded college football playoffs in which they beat #10 [[2024 Indiana Hoosiers football team|Indiana]] 27-17. They next beat the [[2024 Southeastern Conference football season|SEC]] champion #2 [[2024 Georgia Bulldogs football team|Georgia Bulldogs]] in the [[2025 Sugar Bowl|Sugar Bowl]] by a score of 23-10, earning the program's first major bowl win since the [[1994 Cotton Bowl Classic|1993]] season and followed it with a 27-24 win over #6 [[2024 Penn State Nittany Lions football team|Penn State]] in the [[2025 Orange Bowl|Orange Bowl]] to advance to the [[2025 College Football Playoff National Championship|2025 National Championship Game]], their first appearance in the title game since 2012. The Irish fell short of winning a national championship as they were defeated by [[2024 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State]] by a score of 34-23.{{Cite web |last=White |first=Brian |title=Ohio State tops Notre Dame in CFP final to win 7th football national championship |url=https://www.dispatch.com/story/sports/college/football/2025/01/20/ohio-state-notre-dame-college-football-playoff-championship/77745136007/ |access-date=2025-01-21 |website=The Columbus Dispatch |language=en-US}} Watts was voted a consensus All-American at safety for the second consecutive year,{{Cite web |date=2024-12-19 |title=Watts Earns Consensus All-America Honors, First Two-Time Irish Consensus Honoree since 1993 |url=https://fightingirish.com/watts-earns-consensus-all-america-honors-first-two-time-irish-consensus-honoree-since-1993/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Notre Dame Fighting Irish - Official Athletics Website |language=en-US}} while Freeman won the Dodd {{Cite web |date=2024-12-31 |title=Freeman Wins Dodd Trophy for Coach of the Year |url=https://fightingirish.com/freeman-wins-dodd-trophy-for-coach-of-the-year/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Notre Dame Fighting Irish - Official Athletics Website |language=en-US}} and the Bear Bryant{{Cite web |title=Marcus Freeman Named 2024 Paul "Bear" Bryant Coach of the Year |url=https://newsroom.heart.org/news/marcus-freeman-named-2024-paul-bear-bryant-coach-of-the-year |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=American Heart Association |language=en-us}} trophies for national coach of the year. ==Personnel== ===Coaching staff=== {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:90%;" |- | colspan=4 style="{{CollegePrimaryStyle|Notre Dame Fighting Irish|border=2}}; text-align: center"| '''Notre Dame Fighting Irish''' |- align="center"; ! |- | colspan="4" style="font-size:8pt; text-align:center;"|'''Reference:'''{{cite web|title=Staff Directory|url=https://und.com/staff-directory/sports/football/|website=und.com}} |} ==Championships and distinctions== ===National championships=== [[File:Rissman Trophy 1929.jpg|thumb|Jack F. Rissman Trophy awarded to the team ranked No. 1 by the [[Dickinson System]]. Notre Dame gained permanent possession of the trophy by winning it three times.]] [[File:Erskine Trophy Presentation 1929 Notre Dame.jpg|thumb|[[1929 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|Notre Dame]] receiving the [[Albert Russel Erskine Trophy]] following the [[1929 college football season|1929]] season.]] *Notre Dame was ranked No. 1 by the [[Dickinson System]] in 1924, 1929, and 1930 and awarded the [[Rissman Trophy]]. [[Knute Rockne]]'s teams gained permanent possession of this national championship trophy by winning it three times in ten years.{{cite book |last=Dickinson |first=Frank G. |author-link=Dickinson System |date=February 1941 |title=Dickinson's Football Ratings — from Grange to Harmon |url= |location=Omaha, Nebraska |publisher=What's What Publishing Company}} *Notre Dame has won eight [[wire service]] [[College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS|national championships]] awarded by the [[AP Trophy|AP Poll]] or [[Coaches' Trophy|Coaches Poll]], second most in the post-1936 poll era.{{cite web |url= https://247sports.com/college/notre-dame/article/notre-dame-alabama-national-titles-for-the-record-104803/|title= ND-Alabama Titles: For The Record|last1= Somogyi|first1= Lou|date= December 3, 2012|work= Blue & Gold Illustrated|access-date=December 20, 2012}} *The three Dickinson System and eight wire service national championships make a total of 11, but Notre Dame is often credited with additional national championships. The 1938, 1953, and 1964 seasons are part of the reason for the discrepancy. In the [[1938 college football season|1938 season]], [[1938 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|8–1 Notre Dame]] was awarded the [[Dickinson System#Rockne Trophy|Knute Rockne Memorial Trophy]] and the national championship by the [[Dickinson System]], while [[1938 TCU Horned Frogs football team|TCU]] (which finished 11–0) was awarded the championship by the Associated Press. In the [[1953 college football season|1953 season]], an [[1953 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|undefeated Notre Dame team]] (9–0–1) was named national champion by almost every major selector except the AP and UPI (coaches') polls, where the Irish finished second in both to 10–1 [[Maryland Terrapins football|Maryland]]. In [[1964 NCAA University Division football season|1964]] Notre Dame was named national champions by the [[National Football Foundation]] and awarded the [[MacArthur Bowl]]. As Notre Dame has a policy of only recognizing AP and Coaches' Poll national championships post-1936, the school does not officially recognize the 1938, 1953, or 1964 national championships.{{cite book | url=http://issuu.com/bhardin2/docs/2014_football_media_guide | last2=Masters | first2=Chris | last3=Torbin | first3=Leigh | last1=Bertsch | first1=Michael | title=Notre Dame Football 2014 Media Guide | year=2014 | pages=2,157 | publisher=University of Notre Dame | access-date=May 5, 2015 | archive-date=April 30, 2015 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150430072134/http://issuu.com/bhardin2/docs/2014_football_media_guide | url-status=live }} *Beyond their eleven claimed national championships, Notre Dame has been named national champion by at least one NCAA-designated "major selector" in an additional eleven seasons: 1919, 1920, 1927, 1938, 1953, 1964, 1967, 1970, 1989, 1993, and 2012.{{cite web |url=https://www.ncaa.com/history/football/fbs|title=Championship History-FBS|access-date=December 20, 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/gameon/2013/01/11/bcs-computers-rank-notre-dame-ahead-of-alabama/1826755/|title= Notre Dame is still No. 1, according to one BCS computer|website= [[USA Today]]|access-date=January 3, 2018}} Notre Dame's 11 claimed national championships are: {| class="wikitable" |- {{CollegePrimaryHeader|team=Notre Dame Fighting Irish|Year|Coach|Selector|Record|Bowl|Final AP|Final Coaches}} |- | [[1924 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1924]] || rowspan="3"|[[Knute Rockne]] || rowspan="3"| [[Dickinson System]] || 10–0 || Won [[1925 Rose Bowl|Rose]] || – || – |- | [[1929 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1929]] || 9–0 || – || – || – |- | [[1930 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1930]] || 10–0 || – || – || – |- | [[1943 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1943]] || rowspan="4"|[[Frank Leahy]] || rowspan="4"|[[AP Poll|AP]] || 9–1 || – || rowspan="8"|'''No. 1''' || – |- | [[1946 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1946]] || 8–0–1 || – || – |- | [[1947 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1947]] || 9–0 || – || – |- | [[1949 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1949]] || 10–0 || – || – |- | [[1966 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1966]] || rowspan="2"|[[Ara Parseghian]] || AP, [[Coaches Poll|Coaches]] || 9–0–1 || – || '''No. 1''' |- | [[1973 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1973]] || AP || 11–0 || Won [[1973 Sugar Bowl|Sugar]] || No. 4 |- | [[1977 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1977]] || [[Dan Devine]] || rowspan="2"|AP, Coaches || 11–1 || Won [[1978 Cotton Bowl Classic|Cotton]] || rowspan="2"|'''No. 1''' |- | [[1988 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1988]] || [[Lou Holtz]] || 12–0 || Won [[1989 Fiesta Bowl|Fiesta]] |- style="{{NCAA color cell|Notre Dame Fighting Irish}}" | colspan="3"| '''National Championships''' | colspan="4"| '''11''' |} Following its appearance in the 1925 Rose Bowl, Notre Dame did not play in a post-season game for 44 consecutive years (1925–1968), including after many of the team's most successful seasons from the Rockne era in the 1920s and the Leahy era in the 1940s. The university's stated reasons for its self-imposed hiatus were that bowl games were "glorified exhibitions" that played no part in national championship selections and that they extended the season too far to the detriment of players' academic progress.{{cite web |url=https://247sports.com/college/notre-dame/Article/Notre-Dame-The-Bowl-Games-33639014/ |title= Notre Dame & The Bowl Games |last= Somogyi |first= Lou|date= December 7, 2014|website= Irish Illustrated|access-date= June 26, 2021}} ===Appearances in the final Associated Press poll=== Notre Dame has made 874 appearances in the Associated Press poll over 71 seasons. Notre Dame has spent 591 weeks in the Top 10, 318 weeks in the Top 5, and 98 weeks at No. 1.{{cite web|url=http://www.collegepollarchive.com/football/ap/teams/summary.cfm?teamid=7|title=Notre Dame AP Football Poll Summary – College Poll Archive – Historical College Football and Basketball Polls and Rankings|work=collegepollarchive.com|access-date=September 6, 2015}} Notre Dame has finished the year ranked in the final Associated Press poll of the season 56 times: {{col-begin|width=auto}} {{col-break}} {| class="wikitable" |- {{CollegePrimaryHeader|team=Notre Dame Fighting College football ALL-TIME RECORD (WINNING %) (*minimum 100 games) rankings|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/ranking/all-time-record|access-date=2021-04-07|website=Winsipedia|language=en}} *As of 2023, Notre Dame has produced the most players drafted into the [[National Football League]] of any program in the country. As of the [[2023 NFL draft]], 532 players have been drafted.{{Cite web|title=Winsipedia – College football NFL draft picks rankings|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/ranking/nfl-draft-picks|access-date=2021-04-07|website=Winsipedia|language=en}} *As of 2023, Notre Dame is fourth in wins among Division I-A/FBS programs (948), trailing Ohio State (964), Alabama (965), and Michigan (1004).{{Cite web|title=Winsipedia – College football all-time wins rankings|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/ranking/all-time-wins|access-date=2021-04-07|website=Winsipedia|language=en}} *As of 2023, Notre Dame has the second-fewest losses (338) of any NCAA Division I program that has been playing football for more than 100 years, behind Alabama (337). *As of 2023, Notre Dame has had 112 winning seasons in 137 years of football and only 15 losing seasons (including the record for the 2012-2013 season, whose wins were retroactively stripped by the NCAA).{{cite web|url=http://cfbdatawarehouse.com/data/active/n/notre_dame/yearly_totals.php|title=Notre Dame Yearly Totals|website=cfbdatawarehouse.com|access-date=December 25, 2008|df=mdy-all|archive-date=March 11, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150311183536/http://cfbdatawarehouse.com/data/active/n/notre_dame/yearly_totals.php|url-status=dead}} *One of three football programs with at least seven individual Heisman Trophy winners (shares the distinction with Oklahoma (seven), and USC (eight); Ohio State has seven Heisman Trophies that were won by six players). *As of 2014, Notre Dame has produced more 1st Team All-Americans (188), consensus All-Americans (81 players on 99 selections) and unanimous All-Americans (33) in football than any other college program.{{cite news|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/12-m-footbl-media-guide.html|title=2012 Notre Dame Football Media Guide|publisher=University of Notre Dame|format=PDF|access-date=August 26, 2012|access-date=November 19, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130306210945/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/121412aac.html|archive-date=March 6, 2013|url-status=dead}} *Notre Football Hall of Fame]], tied with USC. *Helped by its status as a highly regarded academic institution (ranked 19th by [[U.S. News & World Report]]),{{cite web|url=http://colleges.usnews.rankingsandreviews.com/best-colleges/notre-dame-1840|title=University of Notre Dame – Best College – Education – US News|access-date=August 25, 2010|archive-date=February 14, 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110214181400/http://colleges.usnews.rankingsandreviews.com/best-colleges/notre-dame-1840|url-status=dead}} Notre Dame is second only to Nebraska in Academic All-Americans (66). *Notre Dame holds the [[NCAA]] record for [[Most consecutive wins over one opponent]], beating the US Naval Academy (USNA) 43 times in a row before falling to them in 2007.{{cite news|url=http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap?gameId=273070087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161220030840/http://www.espn.com/ncf/recap?gameId=273070087|url-status=dead|archive-date=December 20, 2016|title=Notre Dame's NCAA-record 43-game win streak over Navy ends |work=[[ESPN]]|access-date=April 24, 2008}} *The football program is also known for ending the Oklahoma Sooners' NCAA record winning streak of 47 games in 1957. Coincidentally, Oklahoma's 28–21 loss to Notre Dame to open the 1953 season was the last loss before the beginning of the streak.{{cite web|url=http://www.history.com/this-day-in-history.do?action=Article&id=57496|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090226083014/http://www.history.com/this-day-in-history.do?action=Article&id=57496|url-status=dead|archive-date=February 26, 2009|title=This Day in History 1957: Notre Dame ends Oklahoma record winning streak|website=history.com|access-date=April 20, 2008}} *Notre Dame has had 13 undefeated seasons and 11 others with at most one loss or tie. * Notre Dame is 3–3–1 in games where the national title winners from the previous two years have met in a regular season game. There have only been 13 of these games played in college football history. Notre Dame has played in 7 of the 13 games: **1945 – Army def. Notre Dame 48–0 **1947 – Notre Dame def. Army 27–7 **1968 – Notre Dame tied USC 21–21 **1974 – USC def. Notre Dame 55–24 **1978 – Notre Dame def. Pitt 26–17 **1989 – Miami def. Notre Dame 27–10 **1990 – Notre Dame def. Miami 29–20 *Notre Dame is one of only two Catholic universities that field a team in the Football Bowl Subdivision, the other being Boston College. This distinction has resulted in what some have termed the [[Holy War (Boston College-Notre Dame)|Holy War]] rivalry. ===No. 1 vs. No. 2=== Notre Dame has participated in eleven "No. 1 vs No. 2" matchups since the AP poll began in 1936.{{cite web |url=http://www.vegasinsider.com/college-football/story.cfm/story/521675|title=No. 1 vs. No. 2 |access-date=April 21, 2008}} They have a record of 5–3–2 in such games, with a 4–1–1 record as the No. 1 team in such matchups. ‡ Kent Baer served as interim head coach for one game at the 2004 [[Insight Bowl]] after Tyrone Willingham was fired. ===Bowl games=== Notre Dame has made 45 bowl game appearances, winning 23 and losing 21, with one game vacated.{{Cite web|url=https://www.sports-reference.com/cfb/schools/notre-dame/|title=Notre Dame Fighting Irish Bowls|website=College Football at Sports-Reference.com}} After an initial appearance in a postseason contest in the [[1925 Rose Bowl]], the Fighting Irish refused to participate in bowl games for more than four decades;{{cite magazine|last1= Jenkins|first1= Dan|year= 1969|title= Knute Rockne Would Have Agreed, Ara|magazine= Sports Illustrated|volume= 31|issue= 26|page= 26|publisher= Time-Life, Inc.|url= http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/vault/article/magazine/MAG1083174/index.htm|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20120326070443/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/vault/article/magazine/MAG1083174/index.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date= March 26, 2012|access-date= June 14, 2012|df= mdy-all}} Sports Illustrated's [[Dan Jenkins]] speculated that Notre Dame might have gone to as many as twenty bowl games during the self-imposed forty-five-year hiatus. From 1994 to the 2006 football seasons, Notre Dame lost 9 consecutive bowl games, tied with Northwestern for the most in NCAA history. That streak ended with a 49–21 win over Hawaii in the [[2008 Hawaii Bowl]]. In the process, Notre Dame scored its highest point total in postseason play. The record of 9 consecutive bowl losses was later tied by Northwestern in 2011, then that streak was snapped [[2013 Gator Bowl|a year later]]. The Fighting Irish suffered in a multi-decade skid with an 0-8 record in BCS/Playoff games and an 0-10 record in major bowl games between [[1994 Cotton Bowl Classic|1994]] and 2025. The losing streak would come to an end when Notre Dame beat Georgia in the [[2025 Sugar Bowl]]. As of 2024, Notre Dame has said they will not list CFP first round games under their bowl games. They will “have two sub-categories: bowl record and playoff record, with the latter including CFP first-round games. Bowl games played in the quarterfinals and semifinals of the CFP would count toward both records.”{{Cite web |last=Leuzzi |first=John |title=Will CFP first-round games count as bowl games in record book? Depends on who you ask |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/ncaaf/2024/12/20/college-football-playoff-cfp-first-round-games-bowl-game-record/77072585007/ |access-date=2025-08-27 |website=USA 20, 2025 || [[2025 College Football Playoff National Championship|CFP National Championship]]|| No. 6 [[2024 Ohio State Buckeyes football team|Ohio State]] || L 23–34 |- |} ===Shamrock Series=== Between 2009 and 2016, and resuming again in 2018, Notre Dame has hosted an annual off-site home football game known as the '''Shamrock Series'''. The series promotes Notre Dame's athletic and academic brand, and has brought the Fighting Irish to [[San Antonio]], [[New York (state)|New York]], [[Greater Washington, D.C.]], [[Chicago]], [[Indianapolis]], [[Boston]] and the [[Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex]]. Each game in the series also features a unique alternate uniform for the Notre Dame team. Notre Dame has yet to lose a game in the Shamrock Series with a record of 11–0 (including the later-vacated win over [[2013 Arizona State Sun Devils football team|Arizona State]] in the 2013 season). Prior to the 2012 season, Notre Dame Athletic Director Jack Swarbrick announced at a news conference of plans to continue the series through 2016. He confirmed after his news conference that New York is expected to fall in that rotation and then continue to be the one consistently repeating venue for Shamrock Series games. While the Shamrock Series was not held in 2017 or 2023, Notre Dame still featured a unique alternate uniform at one game each of those years, both times against [[Navy Midshipmen football|Navy]] (at Notre Dame in 2017 and in [[Dublin|Dublin, Ireland]] in 2023).{{cite web |url=https://fightingirish.com/first-look-notre-dame-football-reveals-rockne-heritage-uniform/ |title=First Look: Notre Dame Football reveals Rockne Heritage Uniform |publisher=Notre Dame Fighting Irish |date=August 18, 2017 |access-date=January 13, 2025}}{{cite web |url=https://www.uniswag.com/blog/notre-dame-and-navy-unveil-alternate-uniforms-for-2023-ireland-opener |title=Notre Dame and Navy Unveil Alternate Uniforms for 2023 Ireland Opener |website=uniswag.com |date=August 23, 2023 |access-date=January 13, 2025}} One of the unique aspects of the Shamrock Series is its inclusion of academic and other non-football activities in the area of that year's host city the days and hours leading up to the game, which include pep rallies, drummers' circles, and academic lectures.{{cite web|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/sports/ct-xpm-2012-08-16-chi-with-chicago-next-notre-dame-shamrock-series-games-here-to-stay-20120816-story.html|title=With Chicago next, Notre Dame 'Shamrock Series' games here to stay|work=Chicago Tribune|date=August 16, 2012 |access-date=September 6, 2015}}{{cite web|url=http://www.ndsmcobserver.com/viewpoint/reevaluate-the-shamrock-series-1.2920173|title=Reevaluate the Shamrock Series |work=The Observer |publisher=University of Notre Dame and Saint Mary's Dame athletes#American football|label 2=Fighting Irish football players|Notre Dame Fighting Irish football statistical leaders}} ===Heisman Trophy=== {{see also|Heisman Trophy}} Seven Notre Dame football players have won the Heisman Trophy, which ties them with the [[Oklahoma Sooners football|University of Oklahoma]] for the second most by any university. ([[Ohio State Buckeyes football|Ohio State]] also has seven trophies that were won by only six players, while [[USC Trojans football|USC]] has the most winners with eight). {| class="wikitable" ! web|url=https://footballfoundation.org/hof_search.aspx|title=Hall of Famers: Select 'Group by School'|access-date=December 17, 2007}} Notre Dame leads all universities in players inducted. {| Salmon]] || [[Fullback (gridiron football)|FB]] ||1971 |- | [[Marchmont Schwartz]] || [[Halfback (American football)|HB]] ||1974 |- | [[William Shakespeare (American football)|William Shakespeare]] || [[Halfback (American football)|HB]] ||1983 |- | [[Emil Sitko]] || [[Halfback (American football)|HB]]/[[Fullback (gridiron football)|FB]] ||1984 |- | [[John "Clipper" Smith]] || [[Guard (American football)|G]] ||1975 |- | [[Mike Stonebreaker]] || [[Linebacker|LB]] ||2023 |- | [[Harry Stuhldreher]] || [[Quarterback|QB]] ||1958 |- | [[Aaron Taylor (American football, born 1972)|Aaron Taylor]] || [[Tackle (gridiron football position)|T]] ||2021 |- | [[Joe Theismann]] || [[Quarterback|QB]] ||2003 |- | [[Adam Walsh (American football)|Adam Walsh]] || [[Center (American football)|C]] ||1968 |- | [[Bob Williams (quarterback)|Bob Williams]] || [[Quarterback|QB]] ||1988 |- | [[Tommy Yarr]] || [[Center (American football)|C]] ||1987 |- | [[Chris Zorich]] || [[Defensive tackle|DT]] ||2007 |} ==Uniforms== [[File:Notre Dame vs. Stanford (2006).jpg|thumb|240px|The team in home uniforms from 2005-09]] Notre Dame's home jersey is navy blue with white numerals, gold outlining, and a small interlocking "ND" logo on each sleeve. The away jersey is white with navy numerals, gold outlining, and the interlocking "ND" on the sleeves. These uniforms were introduced by Lou Holtz in 1986 and retained during the tenures of Bob Davie (1997-2001) and Tyrone Willingham (2002–04). In 2005, Charlie Weis removed the interlocking "ND" from the sleeves, replacing it with the player's number, the first time the Irish had worn "TV numbers" since 1986, when they were located on the shoulders. These jerseys remained throughout Weis' tenure. When Brian Kelly succeeded Weis in 2010, the interlocking "ND" returned to the sleeves, and it has remained since. The Irish traditionally have not worn player names on their jerseys during regular season games, but they were included during the coaching tenures of Dan Devine (1975–80) and Gerry Faust (1981-85). When Notre Dame appeared in the [[1970 Cotton Bowl Classic]], its first bowl game in 45 years, Ara Parseghian had player names placed on the backs of the jerseys, a tradition which was retained for four other bowl appearances under Parseghian. Holtz, Davie, Willingham and Weis (in the [[2006 Fiesta Bowl]] and [[2007 Sugar Bowl]]) did not adhere to this tradition for bowl games they coached, but it returned under Weis for the [[2008 Hawaii Bowl]] and has been continued by Kelly and Marcus Freeman. [[File:Maillots ND déplacement.jpg|thumb|left|100px|Former quarterback Brady Quinn in the away jersey worn from 2005-09]] Notre Dame's helmets are solid gold with gray facemasks, the gold being emblematic of the University's administration building's "Golden Dome". Notre Dame's tradition for the team's student managers to spray-paint the team's helmets prior to each game ended in 2011 when the football equipment staff, along with Notre Dame Athletics Director Jack Swarbrick and head coach Brian Kelly outsourced the painting process to Hydro Graphics Inc. During Gerry Faust's tenure (1981–85), Notre Dame's blue jerseys switched from the traditional navy (although Notre Dame wore green jerseys full-time between the 1977 game vs. USC and the end of the [[1980 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1980 season]]) to royal blue with gold and white stripes on the sleeves. The navy blue jerseys returned for the [[1984 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team|1984 season]] and have continued through the 2024 season. No uniform numbers have been retired by Notre Dame. Upon being issued a number, each player is given a card that lists some of the more famous players who have worn that particular number. An examples is number 3, which was worn by [[Ralph Guglielmi]], [[George Izo]], [[Daryle Lamonica]], [[Coley O'Brien]], [[Joe Montana]], [[Michael Floyd]], [[Rick Mirer]] and [[Ron Powlus]], among others. Number 5 is also notable, as it is the only number to be worn by one of the so-called "Four Horsemen" (Elmer Layden), a Heisman Trophy winner ([[Paul Hornung]]), and a national championship-winning quarterback ([[Terry Hanratty]]).{{cite web |title=ND Archives: All-Time Roster |url=http://www.und.com/auto_pdf/p_hotos/s_chools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/FBRecSuppAll-TimeRoster |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120509194759/http://www.und.com/auto_pdf/p_hotos/s_chools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/FBRecSuppAll-TimeRoster |archive-date=May 9, 2012 |access-date=May 6, 2008 |publisher=und.cstv.com}} Number 7 has been worn by such Irish greats as 1964 Heisman Trophy winner [[John Huarte]], 1970 Heisman runner-up [[Joe Theismann]], Steve Beuerlein, Jimmy Clausen, and Jarious Jackson. In 2011, both Michigan and Notre Dame wore throwback uniforms in their game against each other. For each Shamrock Series game, Notre Dame wears specially designed helmets, jerseys, and pants. [[Champion (sportswear)|Champion]] supplied football jerseys for Notre Dame for over 50 years until the Irish switched to [[Adidas]] in 2001. On July 1, 2014, the Notre Dame athletic department began wearing uniforms and footwear supplied by [[Under Armour]]. === Green jerseys === Over the years, Notre Dame has occasionally worn green instead of blue as its home jersey, sometimes adopting the jersey for an entire season – or more – at a time. Notre Dame is confirmed to have worn green as far back as 1926 against [[Penn State Nittany Lions football|Penn State]], and in 1942 they officially became an alternate color for the team.{{Cite web |title=Notre Dame wears green to continue a proud uniform tradition vs. Ohio State |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/ncaaf/2023/09/23/iconic-green-jerseys-a-part-of-notre-dame-history/70926002007/ |access-date=2023-09-23 |website=USA TODAY |language=en-US}} Currently, Notre Dame reserves its green jerseys for special occasions. Sometimes on such occasions, the Irish will take the field for warmups dressed in blue, only to switch to green when they go back to the locker room before kickoff. This tradition was started by Dan Devine in 1977 before the USC game. Notre Dame has also been known to switch jerseys at halftime, as during the 1985 USC game and in the loss to [[Nile Kinnick]]-led Iowa in 1939, although this was to help avoid confusion between their navy uniforms and Iowa's black ones. The current design of the jersey is kelly green with gold numbers and white outlining. For the 2006 Army game, Coach Charlie Weis broke out the green jerseys as a reward to his senior players and in an attempt to end the string of losses by the Irish when wearing green. Notre Dame wore throwback green jerseys in 2007 against USC in honor of the 30th anniversary of the 1977 National Championship team but lost the game 38-0.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/061107aab.html|title=Notre Dame Football Team to Wear Throwback Uniforms Against USC|work=cstv.com|access-date=September 6, 2015|archive-date=January 9, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160109175057/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/061107aab.html|url-status=dead}} As of 2023, Notre Dame's record in green is 42-15-1, going back to 1975. On October 4, 2025, the Irish paired green jerseys with green pants, defeating Boise State. On at least one occasion (1992 Sugar Bowl) Notre Dame has worn an away variant of the jersey, a white jersey with green numbers. ==Facilities== ===Notre Dame Stadium=== [[File:Notredamestadiumgamedayband.jpg|thumb|right|320px|Notre Dame Stadium on game day, with student section and band]] {{main|Notre Dame Stadium}} Notre Dame Stadium is the home football stadium for the University of Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team. Located on the southeast part of the university's campus in Notre Dame, Indiana and with a seating capacity of 77,622, Notre Dame Stadium is one of the most renowned venues in college football.{{cite web|title=Notre Dame Stadium|url=http://www.und.com/facilities/notre-dame-stadium.html|publisher=Notre Dame Fighting Irish|access-date=November 24, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171130172136/http://www.und.com/facilities/notre-dame-stadium.html|archive-date=November 30, 2017|url-status=dead}} The ''[[Sporting News]]'' ranks Notre Dame Stadium as #2 on its list of "College Football Cathedrals".{{cite web|url=https://www.msnbc.com/modules/sports/collegefootballstadiums/Default.asp?cp1=1|title=Sporting News College Football Cathedrals|work=MSNBC|access-date=January 30, 2007 |archive-url = http://webarchive.loc.gov/all/20010912195116/http://msnbc.com/modules/sports/collegefootballstadiums/default.asp?cp1=1 |archive-date = September 12, 2001}} and the stadium experience evokes a more traditional feel. Notre Dame Stadium is used for football-related activities and for Commencement (since 2010). Notre Dame Stadium had no permanent lighting until the expansion project in 1997. NBC paid for the lighting as they have televised all Notre Dame home football games since 1991. On April 12, 2014, it was announced during the annual Blue-Gold Spring Game that a FieldTurf synthetic surface would replace the grass field after the 2014 Commencement Weekend. In November 2014 the University of Notre Dame embarked on a $400 million stadium expansion called the Crossroads Campus Project, which was completed in time for the 2017 season. ===Cartier Field=== {{main|Cartier Field}} Cartier Field was the original playing field of the Fighting Irish. In 1930, it was replaced by Notre Dame Stadium, due to the growing popularity of ND football. ===Guglielmino Athletics Complex=== Known by fans as "The Gug" (pronounced "goog"), The Guglielmino Athletics Complex is Notre Dame's football headquarters. The Gug houses the offices for all team staff members, an auditorium, a weight room, a training room, the equipment room, the practice locker room, a brand new napping room, and various other spaces for the football team to utilize. The complex was underwritten by Don F. Guglielmino and his family. ===Irish Athletic Center=== The indoor practice facility has a synthetic turf field and a video board at midfield for replay and graphics. On the second level of the facility, there is a 1,600-square-foot recruiting lounge. In all, the facility totals 111,400 square feet, and the ceiling is 95 feet at its peak. ==Rivalries== {{main|Notre Dame Fighting Irish football rivalries}} [[File:Jeweledshillelagh.jpg|thumb|250px|The first [[Notre Dame–USC football rivalry#Trophy: Jeweled Shillelagh|Jeweled Shillelagh]], awarded to the winner of the annual USC vs. Notre Dame game]] Notre Dame has rivalries with several universities. Although the Fighting Irish competes as an Independent, they play a national schedule that includes annual or otherwise regularly scheduled rivalry games. [[Army Black Knights football|Army]], [[Boston College Eagles football|Boston College]], [[Michigan Wolverines football|Michigan]], [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]], [[Navy Midshipmen football|Navy]], [[Northwestern Wildcats football|Northwestern]], [[Pittsburgh Panthers football|Pittsburgh]], [[Purdue Boilermakers football|Purdue]], [[Stanford Cardinal football|Stanford]], and [[USC Trojans football|USC]] are among Notre Dame's rivals. ===USC=== {{main|Notre Dame–USC football rivalry}} [[File:Notre Dame at USC 554 (5215052676).jpg|thumb|Notre Dame at USC (2010)]] USC is Notre Dame's primary rival, and the schools compete annually for the [[Notre Dame–USC football rivalry#Trophy: Jeweled Shillelagh|Jeweled Shillelagh]]. The rivalry has produced 39 [[College football national championships in NCAA Division I FBS#Total championship selections from major selectors by school|national championships]],{{cite book|url=http://fs.ncaa.org/Docs/stats/football_records/2021/FBS.pdf|title=2021 NCAA Football Bowl Subdivision Records| publisher=The National Collegiate Athletic Association|date=July 2021|location=Indianapolis|access-date=January 16, 2022}}{{rp|113–118}} 15 [[Heisman Trophy]] winners,{{cite web|url=https://www.heisman.com/heisman-winners/|title=Heisman Trophy Winners List|publisher=Heisman.com |access-date=January 16, 2022}} and 184 [[College Football All-America Team#Consensus All-Americans|Consensus All-Americans]] through the 2021 season.{{cite web|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/ranking/all-americans|title=Winsipedia – Consensus All Americans|website=Winsipedia|access-date=January 16, 2022}} It is considered one of the most prominent rivalries in college football, and has been called the greatest rivalry not dictated by [[List of college athletic conferences|conference]] affiliation or geography.{{cite news |last=Walters |first=John |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/2005/writers/john_walters/10/12/inside.cfb/index.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060509113608/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/2005/writers/john_walters/10/12/inside.cfb/index.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=May 9, 2006 |title=Does it get any better than this? |work=si.com |date=October 13, 2005 |access-date=April 24, 2008 |df=mdy-all }} The teams have played each other annually since 1926, except from 1943 to 1945 and 2020. Notable games in the series include the 1977 "Green Jersey" game and the [[2005 USC vs. Notre Dame football game|2005 "Bush Push" game]]. Through the 2024 season, Notre Dame leads the all-time series 50–37–5 and the trophy series 35–30–3.{{cite web|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/notre-dame/vs/usc|title=Winsipedia – Notre Dame Fighting Irish vs. USC Trojans football series history|website=Winsipedia}} ===Miami (FL)=== {{See also|1988 Notre Dame vs. Miami football game|Catholics vs. Convicts (film)}} [[File:Michael Floyd Sun Bowl.jpg|thumb|[[Michael Floyd]] battles a University of Miami defensive back for position during the [[2010 Sun Bowl]]]] Notre Dame and the [[Miami Hurricanes football|Miami Hurricanes]] first met during the [[1955 college football season]].{{Cite web |title=Winsipedia – Miami (FL) Hurricanes vs. Notre Dame Fighting Irish football series history |url=http://www.winsipedia.com/miami-fl/vs/notre-dame |access-date=2022-05-20 |website=Winsipedia |language=en}} They met three times during the 1960s (1960, 1965, and 1967) and proceeded to play each other annually from 1971 to 1990 (except in 1986). Notre Dame consistently dominated the series in the 1970s, but in the 1980s, Miami began to dominate as the once docile rivalry intensified significantly. Both teams were national contenders in the later part of the decade, and both teams cost each other at least one national championship. Hostilities were fueled when the Hurricanes routed the Fighting Irish in the 1985 season finale 58–7, with Miami widely accused of [[running up the score]] in the second half. The rivalry gained national attention and both teams played their most famous games from 1988 to 1990. The game known as [[1988 Notre Dame vs. Miami football game|Catholics vs. Convicts]] in Indiana was won by the Fighting Irish 31–30. The following year, Miami ended Notre Dame's 23-game winning streak with a 27–10 victory. The rivalry ended after the Fighting Irish dashed #2 Miami's hopes for a repeat national championship with a 29–20 victory in Indiana. The two teams met in the 2025 season opener at [[Hard Rock Stadium]], with Miami winning 27-24.https://www.espn.com/college-football/game/_/gameId/401754522/notre-dame-miami They also will play each other in yet to be scheduled dates in the 2031, 2032, 2034, and 2037 seasons.[https://fbschedules.com/2025-miami-football-schedule/ FB Schedules 2025] ===Michigan=== {{main|Michigan–Notre Dame football rivalry}} The first meeting between Notre Dame and Michigan was in 1887, the first official football game in Notre Dame's history.{{cite web|url=http://125.nd.edu/moments/first-game-in-notre-dame-football-history/|title=First game in Notre Dame Football history // Moments // 125 Football // University of Notre Dame|first=ENR // MarComm:Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=125.nd.edu}} The two schools have met 44 times in total, the latest matchup in 2019. Through the 2021 season, the two schools are both in the top five in all-time Division I winning percentage, with Notre Dame fourth and Michigan third. Michigan leads the series 25–18–1. ===Michigan State=== {{main|Michigan State–Notre Dame football rivalry}} Meeting for the first time in 1897, Notre Dame and [[Michigan State Spartans football|Michigan State]] play for the Megaphone Trophy. The two schools played in 64 out of 70 seasons from 1948 to 2017, but have not met since. Their next scheduled game is in 2026.{{cite web|first=Chris|last=Solari|url=http://www.freep.com/story/sports/college/michigan-state/spartans/2017/09/22/michigan-state-notre-dame-rivalry-history/691141001/|title=Michigan State-Notre Dame rivalry hits longest break in about 70 years|date=September 22, 2017|website=Detroit Free Press|access-date=January 16, 2022}} Notable games in the series include the [[1966 Notre Dame vs. Michigan State football game|1966 "Game of the Century"]]. Through the 2023 season, Notre Dame leads the all-time series 48–28–1 and the trophy series 33–27–1.{{cite web|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/michigan-state/vs/notre-dame|title=Winsipedia – Michigan State Spartans vs. Notre Dame Fighting Irish football series history|website=Winsipedia}} ===Navy=== {{main|Navy–Notre Dame football rivalry}} Navy and Notre Dame played 93 games without interruption from 1927 to 2019. Notre Dame had a 43-game win streak during this time frame, the longest streak by one team over another in Division I-A football, which ended in 2007.{{cite web|title=Notre Dame's NCAA-record 43-game win streak over Navy ends|date=November 3, 2007|website=ESPN|url=https://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=273070087|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170111005630/http://www.espn.com/college-football/recap?gameId=273070087|url-status=dead|archive-date=January 11, 2017|access-date=January 16, 2022}} While the 2020 contest was cancelled due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the series resumed in 2021. Through the 2024 season, Notre Dame leads the series 83–13–1.{{cite web|url=http://www.winsipedia.com/navy/vs/notre-dame|title=Winsipedia – Navy Midshipmen vs. Notre Dame Fighting Irish football series history|website=Winsipedia}} ==Gameday traditions== {{see also|Band of the Fighting Irish}} [[File:NDBandBondHall.png|thumb|240px|The band playing in a [[pre-game ceremony]] before a football game]] Due to its long history, Fighting Irish football has a number of traditions unique to Notre Dame. Some of these are: * The tradition of having 23.9 karat gold in the helmet paint continues, with the painting process provided by Hydro Graphics, Inc. and no longer by student managers. The gold particles that are used on the helmet were collected from the re-gilding on the Notre Dame dome in 2007. During the 2011 season, however, a new helmet paint scheme was introduced. While retaining the basic gold helmet and grey facemask look, the new gold is much more reflective than the old; there have already been several variations of this new "gold chrome" look, including brick and fish-scale texturing. *Formerly, prior to the start of the game, the team attended Mass in semi-formal attire at the [[Basilica of the Sacred Heart, Indiana|Sacred Heart Basilica]]. At the conclusion of Mass, fans formed a line that the team walked through from the chapel to the stadium.{{cite web |url= http://www.und.com/auto_pdf/p_hotos/s_chools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/07fbguideintro1 |title= 2007 Notre Dame Media Guide: Introduction Part I (Page 10 – Football Weekend at ND) |publisher= und.cstv.com |access-date= May 6, 2008 |archive-date= May 9, 2012 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20120509194808/http://www.und.com/auto_pdf/p_hotos/s_chools/nd/sports/m-footbl/auto_pdf/07fbguideintro1 |url-status= dead }} However, in 2011 the team changed its movements prior the game, instead taking buses back to Guglielmino Athletics Complex for final meetings.{{Cite web|url=https://www.uhnd.com/traditions/irish-traditions-notre-dame-player-walk/|title=Irish Traditions: Notre Dame Player Walk|website=UHND.com - Notre Dame Football, Basketball, & Recruiting}} In 2013, Mass was moved to Friday night; as a result, the walk now originates from the Athletics complex.{{Cite web|url=https://news.nd.edu/news/father-thesing-to-serve-as-football-team-chaplain/|title=Father Thesing to serve as Notre Dame football team chaplain|first=Marketing Communications: Web // University of Notre|last=Dame|website=Notre Dame News|date=August 22, 2013 }} In 2022, Coach Freeman announced that the tradition of Mass before home games would return.{{Cite web|url=https://247sports.com/college/notre-dame/Article/Marcus-Freeman-Resurrects-Game-Day-Mass-185657182/|title=Marcus Freeman Resurrects Game-Day Mass|first=Tim|last=Prister|website=Irish Illustrated}} [[File:Notredamepostgamehelmetraise.jpg|thumb|left|240px|Team raising their helmets to the student section]] * Earlier on game day, the [[Band of the Fighting Irish]] trumpet section plays [[Notre Dame, Our Mother]] & [[Victory March (fight song)|the Notre Dame victory march]] under the [[University of Notre Dame#Buildings and architecture|Golden Dome]], with visitors standing at the bottom of the [[Rotunda (architecture)|rotunda]], and behind the band members on each upper floor.{{cite web |url=https://www.sbnation.com/ad/18069400/notre-dame-band-trumpets-under-the-dome-football |title=How America's oldest college band preps for game day |publisher=Vox Media |website=SB Nation |date=8 Nov 2018 |access-date=8 January 2020}} *Coming out of the locker room, players slap the famous "Play Like a Champion Today" sign. This sign originated in 1986 when head coach Lou Holtz stumbled across a black and white photo of a similar sign and wanted one for his own players.{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/espn/story/_/id/8765862/notre-dame-play-champion-today-sign |title=Notre Dame PLAY LIKE A CHAMPION TODAY sign |date=December 21, 2012 }} * Prior to the start of the game, with between 15 and 30 seconds left on the pregame clock, a clip of [[Cathy Richardson]] singing "Here Come The Irish" is played, followed up by [[I'm Shipping Up To Boston]], which continues to play until the football is [[Kickoff (gridiron football)|kicked off]].{{cite web |url=https://slapthesign.com/2019/06/29/notre-dame-footballs-fan-experience-best-college-football/ |title=Notre Dame Football's fan experience is the best in college football |first=Ben |last=Belden |publisher=FanSided |website=Slap The Sign |date=29 June 2019 |access-date=8 January 2020}} *Between the third and fourth quarters of home games, the Notre Dame Marching Band plays the finale to the [[1812 Overture]], as the crowd reacts with a synchronized waving of arms, with their fingers in the shape of the current coach's first initial. As of 2023, this is an "F" for [[Marcus Freeman]]. *Starting in 1960, Sergeant Tim McCarthy of the Indiana State Police read a driving safety announcement to the crowd during the fourth quarter. When Sergeant McCarthy began his announcement, the crowd went silent to hear his message, which invariably ended with a pun.{{cite web |last=Katzmann |first=Kristy |url=http://www.nd.edu/~ndmag/copau00.htm |title=Tim McCarthy adds puns to football announcements:Football announcer punches it up |publisher=Notre Dame Magazine |date=Fall 2000 |access-date=July 19, 2008 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080725135310/http://www.nd.edu/~ndmag/copau00.htm |archive-date=July 25, 2008 |df=mdy-all }}{{cite book|first1=Mike|last1=Collins|first2=Tim|last2=McCarthy|title=May I Have Your Attention Please...: Wit & Wisdom From the Notre Dame Pressbox|year=2009|publisher=Corby Books|location=[[Notre Dame, Indiana]]|isbn=978-0981960531}} Sergeant McCarthy retired from this duty in the 2015 season after 55 years of fan-favorite messages. He was honored on the field on September 26, 2015, to deliver his last in-person address in which he repeated the same announcement he did on his first gameday.{{cite web|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/092315aac.html|title=Tim McCarthy to Deliver One Final Live Safety Message At Notre Dame-Massachusetts Football Game Saturday|website=Und.com|access-date=November 16, 2017|archive-date=September 3, 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170903073437/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/092315aac.html|url-status=dead}} He died on October 1, 2020.{{cite news|first=John|last=Fineran|title=May (we) have your attention please? ND Stadium legend Tim McCarthy dies at 89|url=https://www.southbendtribune.com/news/local/may-we-have-your-attention-please-nd-stadium-legend-tim-mccarthy-dies-at-89/article_224bfac0-04fe-11eb-99d1-efcb52d43876.html|work=[[South Bend Tribune]]|date=October 3, 2020|access-date=October 3, 2020}} *At the conclusion of every home game, the team turns to the student section to salute them by raising their helmets in the air. They do this after a win or after a loss. Then, the band plays the Alma Mater, "Notre Dame, Our Mother". Those who stay link arms and sing the lyrics. ==Irish in the NFL== Since the NFL began drafting players in 1936, 495 Notre Dame football players have been selected by NFL teams. Additionally, Notre Dame has had 65 players selected in the first round of the NFL draft, including five overall number one picks. Of the 46 Super Bowls competed, as of 2012, only 14 teams have won the event without an Irish player on the roster.{{cite news|url=http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/020612aaa.html|title=Justin Tuck Stars in Super Bowl Again As Giants Top Patriots For Second Time in Four Years|publisher=UND.cstv.com|date=February 6, 2012|access-date=August 26, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121119010853/http://www.und.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/020612aaa.html|archive-date=November 19, 2012|url-status=dead}} Looking at both participating team rosters, there have only been five Super Bowls that did not feature at least one former Notre Dame player on either team's roster – Denver vs. Atlanta, 1999; Dallas vs. Buffalo, 1994; Washington vs. Denver, 1988; Dallas vs. Denver, 1978; and Baltimore vs. Dallas, 1971. Eleven former players have won multiple Super Bowls: Mark Bavaro, Rocky Bleier, Nick Buoniconti, Eric Dorsey, Dave Duerson, David Givens, Terry Hanratty, Bob Kuechenberg, Joe Montana, Steve Sylvester and Justin Tuck. ===Pro Football Hall of Fame=== {{main|Pro Football Hall of Fame}} 13 former Notre Dame players, plus 1 non-player alumnus, have been inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame, tying it for first with [[USC Trojans football|USC]] among all college football programs.{{cite web |url=http://www.foxsports.com/college-football/story/notre-dame-now-ranks-first-in-most-pro-football-hall-of-famers-020716| title= Hall of Famers by College| work=profootballhof.com | access-date=April 23, 2008 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20080418093119/http://www.profootballhof.com/hof/colleges.html |archive-date = April 18, 2008}}{{cite web |url=http://collegefootballtalk.nbcsports.com/2015/01/31/notre-dame-catches-usc-in-pro-football-hall-of-famers/| title= Notre Dame catches USC in Pro Football Hall of Famers|work=collegefootballtalk.nbcsports.com | date= February 2015| access-date=February 1, 2015 }} *1963: [[Curly Lambeau]] – [[Green Bay Packers]] (1919–1949), [[Chicago Cardinals]] (1950–1951), [[Washington Commanders|Washington Redskins]] (1952–1953). *1963: [[Johnny "Blood" McNally]]* – [[Milwaukee Badgers]] (1925–1926), [[Duluth Eskimos]] (1926–1927), [[Pottsville Maroons]] (1928), [[Green Bay Packers]] (1929–1933, 1935–1936), [[Pittsburgh Steelers|Pittsburgh Pirates]] (1934, 1937–1938). *1964: [[George Trafton]] – [[Chicago Bears|Decatur Staleys / Chicago Staleys / Chicago Bears]] (1920–1921, 1923–1932). *1968: [[Wayne Millner]] – [[Washington Commanders|Boston / Washington Redskins]] (1936–1941, 1945). *1975: [[George Connor (American football)|George Connor]] – [[Chicago Bears]] (1948–1955). *1986: [[Paul Hornung]] – [[Green Bay Packers]] (1957–1962, 1964–1966). *1988: [[Alan Page]] – [[Minnesota Vikings]] (1967–1978), [[Chicago Bears]] (1978–1981). *2000: [[Joe Montana]] – [[San Francisco 49ers]] (1979–1992), [[Kansas City Chiefs]] (1993–1994). *2001: [[Nick Buoniconti]] – [[New England Patriots|Boston Patriots]] (1962–1968), [[Miami Dolphins]] (1969–1974, 1976). *2002: [[Dave Casper]] – [[Oakland Raiders]] (1974–1980), [[Houston Oilers]] (1980–1983), [[Minnesota Vikings]] (1983), [[Los Angeles Raiders]] (1984). *2015: [[Jerome Bettis]] – [[Los Angeles Rams|Los Angeles / St. Louis Rams]] (1993–1995), [[Pittsburgh Steelers]] (1996–2005). *2015: [[Tim Brown (American football)|Tim Brown]] – [[Los Angeles Raiders|Los Angeles / Oakland Raiders]] (1988–2003), [[Tampa Bay Buccaneers]] (2004). *2016: [[Edward J. DeBartolo Jr.]] – [[San Francisco 49ers]] (1977–2000), received B.A. from Notre Dame, inducted into Pro Hall of Fame in 2016 as a Contributor. *2021: [[Bryant Young]] – [[San Francisco 49ers]] [[Running back|RB]] || [[Los Angeles Rams]] |- | [[Brock Wright]] | 2021 || [[Tight end|TE]] || [[Detroit Lions]] |- ! colspan="4" style="text-align:center; font-size:9pt;" |This list is complete and up-to-date through October 27, 2025.{{cite web|title=Notre Dame Fighting Irish in the NFL|date=December 29, 2021|website=ourlads.com|url=https://www.ourlads.com/ncaa-football-depth-charts/active-nfl-players-by-college/notre-dame/91487|access-date=January 17, 2022}} |} ==Media== The Fighting Irish are the only team, professional or collegiate, to have all their games broadcast nationally on the radio and their home games on television.{{cite web|title=Air Force Football Travels to Notre Dame|url=http://www.goairforcefalcons.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/100311aae.html|website=goairforcefalcons.com|access-date=April 20, 2015|archive-date=September 24, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924022737/http://www.goairforcefalcons.com/sports/m-footbl/spec-rel/100311aae.html|url-status=dead}} Notre Dame famously left the [[College Football Association]], a consortium that administered television broadcast rights on behalf of over 64 schools, in 1990, in order to establish its own broadcasting deal with [[NBC]]. From 1968 to 2007, [[Westwood One (1976–2011)|Westwood One]] served as the official radio partner for the Irish, broadcasting their games for 40 consecutive years.{{cite news |url=http://chicagosports.chicagotribune.com/sports/columnists/cs-070627greenstein,1,5244401.column?coll=cs-home-utility|archive-url=https://archive.today/20120707123345/http://chicagosports.chicagotribune.com/sports/columnists/cs-070627greenstein,1,5244401.column?coll=cs-home-utility|url-status=dead|archive-date=July 7, 2012|title=If Roberts has anything to say, he'll rejoin Irish|work=[[Chicago Tribune]]|access-date=June 28, 2007}} Until the 2006 Air Force game, Notre Dame had a record 169 consecutive games broadcast nationally on either [[NBC]], ABC, ESPN, or [[CBS]].{{cite web |url=http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/15593455/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070428031855/http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/15593455/ |url-status=dead |archive-date=April 28, 2007 |title=N.D.-Air Force not on major network – Notre Dame Central – MSNBC.com |publisher=MSNBC |df=mdy-all }} The 2006 ND vs. Air Force game was broadcast on [[CBS Sports Network|CSTV]], a college sports channel owned by CBS who had an exclusive contract with the [[Mountain West Conference]], of which Air Force is a member. ===Radio=== Radio rights to the Fighting Irish are held by Skyview Networks, who began distributing the broadcasts as part of a multi-sport arrangement in 2020.{{Cite web|date=2020-07-30|title=Notre Dame Global Partnerships Announces Multi-Year Partnership with Skyview Networks for Full Suite of Broadcast Solutions, Beginning Fall 2020|url=https://skyviewnetworks.com/news/press-releases/notre-dame-global-partnerships/|access-date=2021-09-01|website=Skyview Networks|language=en-US}} ====Current broadcast team==== * [[Paul Burmeister]] (play-by-play) 2018–present * [[Ryan Harris (American football)|Ryan Harris]] (color commentator) 2018–present * Jack Nolan (network studio host) 2020–present ====Former commentators==== * [[Tony Roberts (sportscaster)|Tony Roberts]] (play-by-play) – 1980–2005 * Tom Pagna (color analyst) * [[Pat Haden]] * [[Ralph Guglielmi]] * [[Lindsey Nelson]] * [[Al Wester]] * [[Don Criqui]] (play-by-play) – 1974–1976, 2006–2017 * [[Allen Pinkett]] (color analyst) – 2002–2017 * [[Tom Hammond]] * [[Mike Mayock]] * [[Tony Dungy]] ===Television=== {{Main|Notre Dame Football on NBC}} NBC has been televising Notre Dame home regular season football games since the 1991 season. In addition to TV broadcasts, NBC also maintains several dedicated websites to ND football and Notre Dame Central,{{cite web |url=http://nbcsports.msnbc.com/id/25889627/ns/sports-notre_dame_central/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100916195630/http://nbcsports.msnbc.com/id/25889627/ns/sports-notre_dame_central/ |url-status=dead |archive-date=September 16, 2010 |title=MSNBC – Notre Dame Central – Stay Current with Notre Dame Football Scores, Schedule, Match-ups & Breaking News – MSNBC.com Front Page |publisher=Nbcsports.msnbc.com |df=mdy-all }} which provides complete coverage, full game replays and commentary of the Notre Dame team. NBC's television contract with Notre Dame was renewed in April 2013 and was set to continue through the 2025 football season,{{cite web|last=Arnold|first=Keith|title=Notre Dame and NBC Sports renew partnership|date=April 17, 2013|url=http://irish.nbcsports.com/2013/04/17/offensive-line-rotation-coming-into-focus/#/2013/04/18/notre-dame-nbc-sports-renew-partnership/|publisher=nbcsports.com|access-date=April 19, 2013}} before another contract renewal was made in 2023. NBC is now set to be the home of Notre Dame football through the 2029 season. It also aired football games on NBC's streaming service [[Peacock (streaming service)|Peacock]] started in the 2021 season, with the home opener on September 11, 2021, against [[Toledo Rockets football|Toledo]] aired exclusively on the streaming service.{{Cite web|url=https://und.com/notre-dame-football-home-opener-to-air-exclusively-on-peacock/|title=Notre Dame Football Home Opener to Air Exclusively on Peacock|date=August 4, 2021}}{{Cite web|url = https://thespun.com/more/top-stories/notre-dame-news-television-first-game|title = College Football Fans React to Notre Dame Television News|date = August 8, 2021|access-date = August 10, 2021|archive-date = August 10, 2021|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210810034001/https://thespun.com/more/top-stories/notre-dame-news-television-first-game|url-status = dead}} South Bend NBC affiliate [[WNDU-TV]] also produces its own pregame show, ''Countdown to Kickoff'', which airs prior to every home game broadcast on NBC. Internationally [[Sky Sports]] has been covering Notre Dame Football home games in the [[United Kingdom]], [[Republic of Ireland]], [[Italy]] and [[Germany]] since 2018 as a result of [[Comcast]]'s takeover of [[Sky Group|Sky]]. ====Current broadcast team==== * [[Dan Hicks (sportscaster)|Dan Hicks]] (lead [[play-by-play]]) * [[Jason Garrett]] (lead [[color commentator]]) * Zora Stephenson (lead [[sideline reporter]]) * [[Noah Eagle]] (primetime [[play-by-play]]) * [[Todd Blackledge]] (primetime [[color commentator]]) * [[Kathryn Tappen]] (primetime [[sideline reporter]]) ==Future schedules== Although the Notre Dame football program is not a member of the Atlantic Coast Conference (ACC), it has an agreement to play an average of five ACC schools per season. In return Notre Dame has access to the non-College Football Playoff ACC bowl line-up.{{cite web|last=Schwab|first=Frank|title=Notre Dame to the ACC, football not included... but football will have ACC feel to it|date=September 12, 2012 |url=https://sports.yahoo.com/blogs/ncaaf-dr-saturday/notre-dame-acc-football-not-included-football-acc-141820042--ncaaf.html|publisher=Yahoo! sports|access-date=September 28, 2012}}{{cite web|last=Hansen|first=Eric|title=Swarbrick offers updates on hot topics|url=http://articles.southbendtribune.com/2012-10-10/sports/34369074_1_football-series-shamrock-series-notre-dame-stadium|publisher=South Bend Tribune|access-date=November 8, 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130416004347/http://articles.southbendtribune.com/2012-10-10/sports/34369074_1_football-series-shamrock-series-notre-dame-stadium|archive-date=April 16, 2013|url-status=dead}} Notre Dame utilizes a format of six home games in South Bend, five away games, and one major metropolitan area neutral site "Shamrock Series" home game for scheduling. This includes preserving traditional yearly rivalries with USC, Stanford, and Navy,{{cite news |last=Lazerus |first=Mark |title=Notre Dame drops Michigan from football schedule |url=http://www.suntimes.com/sports/colleges/15375852-419/notre-dame-drops-michigan-from-football-schedule.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121005024543/http://www.suntimes.com/sports/colleges/15375852-419/notre-dame-drops-michigan-from-football-schedule.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=October 5, 2012 |newspaper=suntimes.com |access-date=November 16, 2012 |df=mdy-all }} five rotating ACC opponents (two away ACC games will coincide in even years with away games at Navy and three away ACC games will coincide in odd years with home games against Navy),{{cite news|last=Joseph|first=Allen|title=Observer Exclusive: Swarbrick discusses eventful fall {{Short description|South Korean TV program}} {{Use dmy dates|date=March 2018}} {{Infobox television | image = Omniscient Interfering View.jpg | alt_name = ''The Manager'' | native_name = {{Infobox Korean television name/auto|hangul=^전지적 참견 시점|hanja=全知的 參見 視點}} | genre = {{ubl|[[Reality television]]
Observational [[Talk show]]}} | country = South Korea | language = Korean | num_episodes = 369 + 2 pilot ([[#Episodes|list of episodes]]) | location = {{nowrap|South Korea, Japan, Canada, Singapore}} | camera = [[Multiple-camera setup|Multi-camera]] | runtime = 80–90 minutes | company = [[Munhwa Broadcasting Corporation|MBC]] | network = [[Munhwa Broadcasting Corporation|MBC]] | first_aired = {{start date|2018|3|10|df=y}} | last_aired = present }} '''''Omniscient Interfering View''''' ({{korean|hangul=전지적 참견 시점}}) is a South Korean [[Television comedy|television entertainment program]], distributed and syndicated by [[Munhwa Broadcasting Corporation|MBC]] on Saturdays at 11:05 pm ([[Time in South Korea|KST]]) since 10 March 2018. ==Synopsis== ''Omniscient Interfering View'' is an observational entertainment show that uses a documentary style techniques to observe the life of the cast members, invited guests and their managers.{{cite web|url=http://program.imbc.com/Concept/omniscient|title=Program Introduction|website=[[Munhwa Broadcasting Corporation]]}} The show follows another program, ''[[I Live Alone (TV series)|I Live Alone]]'' of a similar format, which airs on the same channel on Fridays at the same time slot. Similar program, [[On & Off (TV series)|On & Off]], produced by tvN is a show of personal documentary, where celebrities share their "On" (busy working daily life) and/or "Off" (private/at home) moments of their lives. ==History== Between episode 1 and 10, the cast members, a behavioral analysis expert, and occasionally guests, watch the videos in a studio setting and provide commentary.{{cite web|url=http://program.imbc.com/Cast/omniscient|title=Introduction to Cast Member|work=[[Munhwa Broadcasting Corporation]]}} From episode 11 onward, the format has changed slightly to invite guests with their managers. There are no restrictions on the guest's occupation. The cast members and invited guests watch the videos as they observe and comment on the life of the invited guests and their managers. Occasionally the cast members and their managers are also observed and commented. ==Airtime== {|class=wikitable style=text-align:center |- !Airdate!!colspan=2|Broadcast Start Time (KST) |- |rowspan=2|10 March 2018 – 26 June 2021||style=background:Lightgray|Part 1||Saturdays 11:05 pm |- |style=background:Lightgray|Part 2||Sundays 12:05 am |- |3 July 2021 – present||colspan=2|Saturdays 11:10 pm |- |} ==Cast== ===Pilot episode=== For the two pilot episodes aired on 29 and 30 November 2017, the cast consisted of [[Jun Hyun-moo]], [[Lee Young-ja (comedian)|Lee Young-ja]], [[Song Eun-i]], [[Yang Se-hyung]], Seo Min (parasite doctor), [[Lee Jai-jin (musician, born 1979)|Lee Jai-jin]] and Yang Jae-woong (psychiatrist).{{cite web|url=https://program.imbc.com/Enews/Detail/omniscient?idx=221169|script-title=ko:<전지적 참견 시점> 이영자&이재진, 매니저 '특급 폭로'에 당황!|language=ko|work=[[MBC News]]|date=2017-11-24}} Seo Min and Lee Jai-jin were replaced by the time the show started its proper run. ===Current cast/interferer=== {|class=wikitable style="width:95%;text-align:center" |- !style=width:15%|Name !style=width:10%|Duration !style=width:12%|Manager !style=width:58%|Notes |- |{{CMain|[[Jun Hyun-moo]]}}
