|- |{{ill|Shin Gi-ru|ko|신기루 (희극인)}}, [[Lee Guk-joo]], {{ill|Poongja|ko|풍자 (유튜버)}}||{{nom}} |} ==Controversies== ===2018=== ====Kim Saeng-min==== On 2 April, Korean media outlet Dispatch broke a story about [[Kim Saeng-min]] harassing two female staff members at a party in 2008. One of the afflicted staff members received an apology in 2008, whilst the other spoke about trying to raise the issue, but being slowly pushed out of the program by other staff members, before she eventually quit her job as a staff member on the program.{{cite web|script-title=ko:'성추행 의혹' 김생민 "10년전 일 죄송" 방송 거취 노코멘트|url=http://entertain.naver.com/now/read?oid=382&aid=0000635860|language=ko|website=Sports Donga|via=[[Naver]]|date=2018-04-02|access-date=2018-05-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190719074954/https://entertain.naver.com/now/read?oid=382&aid=0000635860|archive-date=2019-07-19|url-status=live}}{{cite web|script-title=ko:김생민, 방송 스태프 성추행…피해자 만나 10년 만에 사과|url=https://www.dispatch.co.kr/1227425|website=[[Dispatch (South Korea)|Dispatch]]|language=ko|date=2018-04-02|access-date=2018-05-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190719080647/https://www.dispatch.co.kr/1227425|archive-date=2019-07-19|url-status=live}} Upon being contacted by Dispatch regarding the story, Kim Saeng-min apologized to the former staff member, and later released an official apology through his agency. He then withdrew from all of his current TV and radio programs, including ''Omniscient Interfering View''.{{cite web|script-title=ko:팟캐스트 '김생민의 영수증' 측 "폐지 결정..콘텐츠 모두 삭제"|url=http://entertain.naver.com/read?oid=076&aid=0003236670|language=ko|website=[[Sports Chosun]]|via=[[Naver]]|date=2018-04-03|access-date=2018-05-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180716111735/https://entertain.naver.com/read?oid=076&aid=0003236670|archive-date=2018-07-16|url-status=live}} ====Fishcake controversy==== During the 5 May broadcast, the show used a censored clip from MBC News coverage of the [[Sinking of MV Sewol|Sewol Ferry disaster]] with the headline "Lee Young-ja makes shocking confession while eating fish cakes". This sparked a controversy, as users of the controversial online community [[Ilbe Storehouse|Ilbe]] had used the term "fish cakes" to refer to the victims of the Ferry disaster. Both the production staff and the MBC president immediately apologized, and launched an internal investigation of the issue.{{cite web|script-title=ko:'전지적 참견 시점' 측 "이영자 어묵 장면, 세월호 피해자 가족에 사과" |language=ko|url=http://entertain.naver.com/read?oid=144&aid=0000551346|website=[[Sports Kyunghyang]]|via=[[Naver]]|date=2018-05-09|access-date=2018-05-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180701164959/https://entertain.naver.com/read?oid=144&aid=0000551346|archive-date=2018-07-01|url-status=live}} The program was temporarily halted, and went off-air for 7 weeks before returning to air on 30 June 2018.{{cite web|title=MBC 측 "'전참시' 2주 결방…진상조사위 활동 착수"|url=http://entertain.naver.com/now/read?oid=076&aid=0003254396|language=ko|website=[[Sports Chosun]]|via=[[Naver]]|date=2018-05-10|access-date=2018-05-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190719081055/https://entertain.naver.com/now/read?oid=076&aid=0003254396|archive-date=2019-07-19|url-status=live}} ===2019=== ====Fraud case==== During the 20 July broadcast, the show displayed a subtitle notice to alert the audiences that "the production team does not ask for money on the pretext of filming". This was due to an incident of a scammer who had been masquerading as a member of the production crew and attempted to solicit large amounts of money from shopkeepers and business owners with the promise of advertising their goods or services.{{cite web|script-title=ko:'전참시' 측, 제작진 사칭 사기 피해 당부 "촬영 빌미로 금품 요구 안해"[공식입장]|url=http://www.osen.co.kr/article/G1111189932|website=[[OSEN (newspaper)|OSEN]]|language=ko|date=2019-07-21|access-date=2019-07-21|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190721015859/http://www.osen.co.kr/article/G1111189932|archive-date=2019-07-21|url-status=live}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=note}} {{notelist}} ==See also== *[[I Live Alone (TV series)|I Live Alone]] *[[On & Off (TV series)|On & Off]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{official website|http://www.imbc.com/broad/tv/ent/omniscient/}} {{in lang|ko}} {{MBC TV (South Korean TV channel) current original programming}} [[Category:2020s South Korean television series]] [[Category:Korean-language television shows]] [[Category:MBC TV original programming]] [[Category:South Korean variety television shows]] [[Category:South Korean reality television series]] [[Category:2018 South Korean television series debuts]]

{{Short description|Fossil flora and fauna in the Posidonia Shale, Germany}} {{Use dmy dates|date=March 2021}} [[File:Seironcrinus subangularis Miller, die größte fossile Seelilien-Kolonie (18 x 6 m).jpg|thumb|380px|right|18x6 m fossilized floating wood (''[[Agathoxylon]]''), with Crinoids attached (''[[Pentacrinites]]'' & ''[[Seirocrinus]]''). It is one of the most emblematic fossils of the formation, where the anoxic seas of the lower toarcian lead to an exquisite preservation.]] {{Paleontology}} The '''[[Sachrang Formation]]''' or '''"Posidonienschiefer" Formation''' (common name the "Posidonia Shale") is a [[geological formation]] of southwestern [[Germany]], northern [[Switzerland]], northwestern [[Austria]], southeast [[Luxembourg]] and the [[Netherlands]], that spans about 3 million years during the [[Early Jurassic]] period (early [[Toarcian]] stage). It is known for its detailed [[fossil]]s, especially marine biota, listed below.<ref name=PosidoABB>{{cite book |last1=Hess |first1=H. |title=Fossil Crinoids |chapter=Lower Jurassic Posidonia Shale of Southern Germany |date=1999 |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=183–196 |doi=10.1017/CBO9780511626159.025 |isbn=978-0-521-45024-9 |url=http://www.fossilcrinoids.com/literature/9.1_Hess%20(1999).pdf |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> Composed mostly of black [[shale]], the formation is a [[Lagerstätte]], where fossils show exceptional preservation (including exquisite soft tissues), with a thickness that varies from about 1 m to about 40 m on the Rhine level, being on the main quarry at [[Holzmaden]] between 5 and 14 m.<ref name=PosidoABB/> Some of the preserved material has been transformed into the fossil hydrocarbon [[Jet (gemstone)|jet]] which, especially jet derived from wood remains, is used for jewelry.<ref name=soup>{{cite journal |last1=Martill |first1=D. M. |title=Soupy substrates: a medium for the exceptional preservation of ichthyosaurs of the Posidonia Shale (Lower Jurassic) of Germany |journal=Kaupia |date=1993 |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=77–97 |url=https://pubs.geoscienceworld.org/sepm/palaios/article-pdf/10/1/96/2834888/96.pdf |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> The exceptional preservation seen in the Posidonia Shale has been studied since the late 1800s, finding that a cocktail of chemical and environmental factors led to such an impressive preservation of the marine fauna.<ref name=soup/> The most common theory is that changes in the oxygen level, where the different anoxic events of the Toarcian left oxygen-depleted bottom waters, stopped scavengers from consuming the dead bodies.<ref name=GiantAmmoni>{{cite journal |last1=Schmid–Röhl |first1=A. |last2=Röhl |first2=H. J. |title=Overgrowth on ammonite conchs: environmental implications for the Lower Toarcian Posidonia Shale |journal=Palaeontology |date=2003 |volume=46 |issue=2 |pages=339–352 |doi=10.1111/1475-4983.00302 |bibcode=2003Palgy..46..339S |s2cid=128413601 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==Biological interactions== [[File:Seirocrinus subsingularis, view 1, Jurassic, Hlzmaden Black Shale Formation, Holzmaden, Germany - Houston Museum of Natural Science - DSC01836.JPG|thumb|right|Seirocrinus subsingularis stems over a branch]] *The ''"Monotis"''-''[[Dactylioceras]]'' bed shows an accumulation of the bivalves ''[[Meleagrinella]] substriata'' and the [[ammonite]] ''[[Dactylioceras]]'', that were the most abundant representatives of its group on the Altdorf region, and were probably washed to near epicontinental waters by a rapid event, or as result of a large succession of events.<ref name=MonotisD/> This assemblage has been compared with modern Brazilian coastal mangroves and also linked to tsunami events.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Birzer |first1=F. |last2=Joos |first2=O. |title=Die Monotisbank in den Posidonienschiefern, besonders Frankens: Zur Geologie der Ehrenbürg (Walberla) bei Forchheim. Bayer |journal=Oberbergamt |date=1936 |volume=35 |issue=2 |pages=1–46 |url=https://www.lfu.bayern.de/geologie/geo_karten_schriften/historische_schriften/doc/1936_26.pdf |access-date=2 April 2022}}</ref> [[File:Hybodus hauffianus 2.JPG|thumb|right|Hybodus hauffianus with skin and Belemnnite traces]] *Several empty ammonite shells from Holzmaden have been found with associated decapods inside.<ref name="FraayeA"/> This includes a possible member of the genus ''[[Paleastacus]]'' inside a chamber of a ''[[Harpoceras]]''.<ref name="FraayeA"/> This decapod is related to the family [[Erymidae]], which are considered to be possible bottom-dweller carnivores or carrion feeders.<ref name="FraayeA" /> The associated fossil has several spherical structures that had been interpreted as decapod [[coprolite]]s, implying that the animal lived for a long period within the shell.<ref name="FraayeA" /> More recent studies had recovered new data about the [[inquilinism]] of decapods inside ammonites, this time, however, recovering three [[Eryonoidea|eryonoids]] together within a body chamber.<ref name="Klompmaker" /> The eryonoids most likely used the ammonoid as some kind of shelter, possibly due to the shell being an ideal location to molt, protection against predators, a source of food or that the shell was used as a long-term shelter.<ref name="Klompmaker" /> One key aspect found was that the muddy bottom was not suitable for burrowing, implying that the decapods inhabited a different shelter due to being unable to make their own.<ref name="Klompmaker" /> Other biota are found related to the decayed ammonite shells, such as serpulid annelids and bivalves, creating what was denominated as "benthic islands".<ref name="FraayeA"/> *Beyond trace fossils, several vertebrate specimens show associations with crustacean exoskeletal remains such as GPIT-PV-31586 and SMNS 58389 (''[[Pachycormus (fish)|Pachycormus]] macropterus'') with [[necrophagous]] interaction as well SMNS 55934 (''[[Stenopterygius]] quadriscissus'') or SMNS 95401 (''[[Metopacanthus]] sp.'').<ref name=MainInteractions/>
*The genus ''[[Clarkeiteuthis]]'' and its predatory behaviour, found associated with fishes of the genus ''[[Leptolepis]]''.<ref name="ClarkeiteuthisA">{{cite journal |last1=Jenny |first1=D. |last2=Fuchs |first2=D. |last3=Arkhipkin |first3=A.I. |date=2019 |title=Predatory behaviour and taphonomy of a Jurassic belemnoid coleoid (Diplobelida, Cephalopoda) |journal=Sci Rep |volume=9 |issue=7944 |pages=65–77 |bibcode=2019NatSR...9.7944J |doi=10.1038/s41598-019-44260-w |pmc=6538661 |pmid=31138838}}</ref> Based on the position of prey and predator, it was suggested that the cephalopods caught and killed the fishes while the schools were still in well-oxygenated waters and then descended into oxygen-depleted water layers where the cephalopod suffocated and died attached to its prey.<ref name="ClarkeiteuthisA"/> The cephalopods' arms were contracted over the fish, likely killing it quickly by cutting its spine.<ref name="ClarkeiteuthisA"/> *Several ''[[Geotheutis]]'' have been reported with eumelanin preserved along with their ink sacs.<ref name=melanin/> *A specimen of ''[[Jeletzkyteuthis]]'' found in Ohmden has appeared predating a ''[[Parabelopeltis]]''. The association of these 2 genera shows the predatory behaviour of this group when its members lived in epicontinental seas, which is rather different than extant Vampyromorphs.<ref name="Predatios">{{cite journal |last1=Klug |first1=C. |last2=Schweigert |first2=G. |last3=Fuchs |first3=D. |date=2021 |title=Distraction sinking and fossilized coleoid predatory behaviour from the German Early Jurassic |journal=Swiss J Palaeontol |volume=140 |issue=7 |pages=17–31 |bibcode=2021SwJP..140....7K |doi=10.1186/s13358-021-00218-y |pmc=7965854 |pmid=33815267 |doi-access=free}}</ref> *A [[pabulite]] (fossilized meal which never entered the digestive tract) was recovered from Holzmaden, being composed of an associated ''[[Passaloteuthis]] laevigata'' with its arms embracing an exuvia of a crustacean.<ref name=Pabu>{{cite journal |last1=Klug |first1=C. |last2=Schweigert |first2=G. |last3=Hoffmann |first3=R. |title=Fossilized leftover falls as sources of palaeoecological data: a "pabulite" comprising a crustacean, a belemnite and a vertebrate from the Early Jurassic Posidonia Shale |journal=Swiss J Palaeontol |date=2021 |volume=140 |issue=10 |pages=18–31 |doi=10.1186/s13358-021-00225-z |pmid=34721282 |pmc=8549986 |s2cid=233450993 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2021SwJP..140...10K }}</ref> The belemnnite itself can be seen as the remnant of a failed predation attempt from a ''[[Hybodus]]'', corroborating a possible tropic chain.<ref name=Pabu/> [[File:Stenopterygius quadriscissus.jpg|thumb|right|''Stenopterygius quadriscissus'', mother with embryo]] *One of the most complex organism interactions on the Posidonia Shale were the crinoid megarafts that grouped a wide variety of animals, creating large floating ecosystems.<ref name="CrinoidMegaraftA"/> The largest megaraft found measured {{convert|18|m|ft}}, and is based on an ''[[Agathoxylon]]'' trunk, where different animals were attached.<ref name="CrinoidMegaraftA"/> The first attached animals would have been the growing community of oysters, bivalves and crinoids, that would add a small weight to the raft (about {{convert|800|kg|lb}}).<ref name="CrinoidMegaraftA"/> The presence of these megarafts were in part possible due to the absence of marine wood worms which destroy tree logs in less than 3 years along with the absence of modern raft wood predators (that appeared on the [[Bathonian]]). These rafts could last up to 5 years, which is the main reason the crinoids attached were able to reach huge sizes.<ref name="CrinoidMegaraftA"/> These rafts were likely also essential to distribute animals along sea basins.<ref name="CrinoidMegaraftA"/> ''[[Seirocrinus]]'' & ''[[Pentacrinites]]'' were some of the main crinoid colonizers of the floating rafts.<ref name="Crino0"/> ''[[Seirocrinus]]'' is the main representative of the pelagic crinoids, being among the longest animals known, reaching 26 m long in the largest documented specimen.<ref name="Crino0"/> The ecology of the genus is widely known, with it being known that the smallest stems were among the first animals to colonize the rafts, with at least 2 generations of crinoids found per raft, where the hydrodynamic changes of the log influenced the settlement of the crinoids.<ref name="Crino0"/> It is believed that ''[[Seirocrinus]]'' underwent seasonal reproduction linked to monsoonal conditions that sent new logs to the sea.<ref name="Crino0"/> The large crinoids would have feed on pelagic micronutrients, and after the log fell to the bottom, all of the colony would have died.<ref name="Crino0"/> *Thoracic barnacles of the genus ''[[Toarcolepas]]'' became the oldest epiplanktonic barnacle known in the fossil record, probably motivated by the appearance of the giant crinoid rafts. It has been found in situ associated with fossil wood.<ref name=Toarcole/> [[File:Clarkeiteuthis with Leptolepis fossil.png|thumb|Fossil of ''Clarkeiteuthis'' preying on ''Leptolepis'']] *The shark ''[[Hybodus]]'' includes specimens with gastric contents full of belemnnite fragments.<ref name="HybodusA">{{cite journal |last1=Schmidt |first1=M. |title=Hybodus hauffianus und die Belemnitenschlachtfelder |journal=Jahreshefte des Vereins für vaterländische Naturkunde in Württemberg |date=1921 |volume=77 |issue=1 |pages=103–107}}</ref> This implies active predatory behaviour by the genus towards several kinds of belemnnites, such as ''[[Youngibelus]]''.<ref name="HybodusA"/> *A speiballen (a regurgitated mass composed of indigestible stomach contents) had been found in the Holzmaden quarry.<ref name="HybodusB"/> The speiballen measures 285 mm in length with a diameter of 160 mm, and consists of 4 members of the genus ''[[Dapedium]]'' ([[Dapediidae]]) and a jaw identified as ''[[Lepidotes]]'' ([[Semionotidae]]).<ref name="HybodusB"/> The maker of this speiballen has been suggested to be a shark like ''Hybodus'', an actinopterygian or a marine reptile.<ref name="HybodusB"/> *A specimen of ''[[Pachycormus (fish)|Pachycormus]]'' has been found with stomach contents that include hooks similar to the ones found on genera like ''[[Clarkeiteuthis]]''.<ref name=Predatios0/> *Another specimen of ''Pachycormus macropterus'' preserves an ammonite inside its gut, likely swallowed by accident and directly responsible for the fish's death.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cooper |first1=S. L. |last2=Maxwell |first2=E. E. |title=Death by ammonite: fatal ingestion of an ammonoid shell by an Early Jurassic bony fish |journal=Geological Magazine |date=2023 |volume=160 |issue=7 |pages=1254–1261 |doi=10.1017/S0016756823000456 |bibcode=2023GeoM..160.1254C |doi-access=free }}</ref> *SMNS 51144 (''[[Saurostomus]] esocinus'') was found with ''[[Chondrites]] sp.'' burrows in the abdominal cavity, what indicates a possible opportunistic scavenger. Other ''[[Chondrites]] sp.'' includes SMNS 17500 and MHH 1981/25 (''[[Stenopterygius]] uniter'') that either suggest the ichthyosaurs were preserved immediately below one such bioturbation horizon or scavenger association.<ref name=MainInteractions/> * The known specimens of ''[[Toarcocephalus]]'' are evidence of successful predation events, as the head of one was isolated, likely as product of a decapitation, with another preserved within a regurgitated mass.<ref name=":0" /> *One of the most emblematic finds of the formation is that of a mother ''[[Stenopterygius]]'' giving birth to live young. While specimens have been found with embryos, the bones of these embryos are scattered partly beyond the body limits of the mother.<ref name="IchthyosaurembryosB">{{cite journal |last1=van Loon |first1=A. J. |title=Ichthyosaur embryos outside the mother body: not due to carcass explosion but to carcass implosion |journal=Palaeobiodiversity and Palaeoenvironments |date=2013 |volume=93 |issue=1 |pages=103–109 |doi=10.1007/s12549-012-0112-6 |s2cid=199406163 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2013PdPe...93..103V }}</ref> There have been various theories about this scenario: either the bones of embryos had been deposited before the body of the adult went to the sea floor, or in the ichthyosaurs' last moments where it sank to the bottom and may have given untimely birth to some of the foetuses, and finally another option follows post mortem hydrostatic pressure being too high to be prevented by the body, exploding or expelling its embryos.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reisdorf |first1=A. G. |last2=Anderson |first2=G. S. |last3=Bell |first3=L. S. |last4=Klug |first4=C. |last5=Schmid-Röhl |first5=A. |last6=Röhl |first6=H. J. |last7=Wyler |first7=D. |title=Reply to "Ichthyosaur embryos outside the mother body: not due to carcass explosion but to carcass implosion" by van Loon (2013) |journal=Palaeobiodiversity and Palaeoenvironments |date=2014 |volume=94 |issue=3 |pages=487–494 |doi=10.1007/s12549-014-0162-z |bibcode=2014PdPe...94..487R |s2cid=129387302 |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12549-014-0162-z |access-date=2 April 2022|url-access=subscription }}</ref> *The specimen SMNS 53363 (''[[Eurhinosaurus]]?'') from [[Aichelberg]] was found with two encrusted large oysters (''[[Liostrea]]'') on the right pterygoid, considered to be part of a reef stage over bones.<ref name=MainInteractions>{{cite journal |last1=Maxwell |first1=Erin E. |last2=Cooper |first2=Samuel L. A. |last3=Mujal |first3=Eudald |last4=Miedema |first4=Feiko |last5=Serafini |first5=Giovanni |last6=Schweigert |first6=Günter |title=Evaluating the Existence of Vertebrate Deadfall Communities from the Early Jurassic Posidonienschiefer Formation |journal=Geosciences |date=2022 |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=158–176 |doi=10.3390/geosciences12040158 |bibcode=2022Geosc..12..158M |doi-access=free }}</ref> *SMNS 80234 (''[[Stenopterygius]] quadriscissus'') represents another female with embryos, yet also shows ribs broken perimortem that may represent either intraspecific aggression or a predation attempt. This specimen has several taxa associated: ammonite aptychi, two ophiuroids (''[[Sinosura]] brodiei'') and an articulated echinoid (''[[Diademopsis]] crinifera'') indicate a short-lived deadfall community.<ref name=MainInteractions/> *SMNS 81841 (''[[Stenopterygius]] quadriscissus'') represents one of the clearest examples of deadfall communities described in the formation: the skeleton is associated with serpulids surrounded by a mass of disarticulated ophiuroid remains, indeterminate echinoid tests, an isolated crinoid ossicle, the byssate bivalve ''[[Oxytoma]] inaequivalvis'', the pectinid ''[[Propeamussium]] pumilus'', ''[[Eopecten]] strionatis'', ''[[Plagiostoma (bivalve)|Plagiostoma]] sp.'', ''[[Meleagrinella]] sp.'', ''"[[Cucullaea]]" muensteri'', with the genera ''[[Parainoceramya]] dubia'' and ''Liostrea'' associated with the carcass.<ref name=deadfallA>{{cite journal |last1=Dick |first1=D.G. |title=An Ichthyosaur Carcass-Fall Community from the Posidonia Shale (Toarcian) of Germany |journal=PALAIOS |date=2015 |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=353–361 |doi=10.2110/palo.2014.095 |bibcode=2015Palai..30..353D |s2cid=129077450 |url=https://pubs.geoscienceworld.org/sepm/palaios/article-abstract/30/5/353/331203 |access-date=2 April 2022|url-access=subscription }}</ref> As many of these bivalves show overgrowth the community likely persisted for some time.<ref name=deadfallA/> Fossil traces of ''[[Gastrochaenolites]] isp.'' attributed to mechanical bivalve borers are abundant implicating prolonged exposure of the skeleton on the seafloor.<ref name=deadfallA/> *SMNS 81719 (''[[Stenopterygius]] uniter'') includes ''[[Liostrea]]'', ''[[Propeamussium]] pumilus'', ''[[Plagiostoma (bivalve)|Plagiostoma]] sp.'' and ''[[Parainoceramya]] dubia'', with other invertebrates found (?) not being part of the deadfall community, such as several ammonites and ''Parainoceramya'' valves, being stratigraphically below the specimen.<ref name=MainInteractions/> This specimen includes also traces of scavenging activity, possibly by crustaceans.<ref name=MainInteractions/> *SMNS 80113, (''Stenopterygius triscissus'') was found populated by ''Parainoceramya'', a specimen of ''Eopecten strionatis'' and an unexpected specimen of the small infaunal lucinid ''[[Mesomiltha]] pumila'', equivocal evidence for the sulfophilic stage.<ref name=MainInteractions/> *Local ichthyosaur soft tissues include skin enough well preserved to infer coloration and appearance on the living animal, as well evidence for homeothermy and crypsis.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Eriksson |first1=M. E. |last2=De La Garza |first2=R. |last3=Horn |first3=E. |last4=Lindgren |first4=J. |title=A review of ichthyosaur (Reptilia, Ichthyopterygia) soft tissues with implications for life reconstructions |journal=Earth-Science Reviews |date=2022 |volume=226 |issue=1 |pages=103–124 |doi=10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965 |bibcode=2022ESRv..22603965E |s2cid=246846785 |doi-access=free }}</ref> *Gut contents of the local pterosaurs are known: ''[[Campylognathoides]]'' preserves hooks of the coleoid ''[[Clarkeiteuthis]]'' (therefore being one of the few teuthophagous pterosaurs), while ''[[Dorygnathus]]'' preserves remains of ''[[Leptolepis]]''.<ref name=squidnom>{{Cite journal |last1=Cooper |first1=Samuel. L. A. |last2=Smith |first2=R. E. |last3=Martill |first3=D. M. |date=2024 |title=Dietary tendencies of the Early Jurassic pterosaurs Campylognathoides Strand, 1928, and Dorygnathus Wagner, 1860, with additional evidence for teuthophagy in Pterosauria |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|volume=44 |issue=2 |article-number=e2403577 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2024.2403577 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2024JVPal..44E3577C }}</ref>
== Microbial activity == Non-fenestrate [[stromatolite]] crusts formed in [[aphotic]] deep-water environments during intervals of very low sedimentation are recovered in places such as Teufelsgraben, Hetzles.<ref name=structu>{{cite journal |last1=Böhm |first1=F. |last2=Brachert |first2=T. C. |title=Deep-water stromatolites and Frutexites Maslov from the early and Middle Jurassic of S-Germany and Austria |journal=Facies |date=1993 |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=145–168 |doi=10.1007/bf02539734 |bibcode=1993Faci...28..145B |s2cid=129365360 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/251225434 |access-date=2 March 2022}}</ref> The stromatolites of this region have evidence of live on a deeper shelf environment with a quietwater deposit which suffered repeated phases of stagnant bottom waters, where a depth water habitat developed, probably at more than 100 meters depth.<ref name=structu/> There is a thin, southern widespread stromatolite crust on the top of the Sachrang Formation, called "Wittelshofener Bank", that has made researchers rethink the depth of the major southern basin of the formation, where the absence of phototrophic calcareous benthic organisms (probably due to the lack of light) shows the depth of the basin.<ref name=structu/> On the "Wittelshofener Bank" there is also the only occurrence of [[ooid]]s, presumably formed in the same deep-water environment.<ref name=structu/> {{paleobiota-key-compact}} {| class = "wikitable" |- ! Genus ! Species ! Location ! Material ! Notes ! Images |- |style="background:#FEF6E4;" | ''[[Frutexites]]''<ref name=structu/> |style="background:#FEF6E4;" | *''F. arboriformis'' |style="background:#FEF6E4;" | * Teufelsgraben, Hetzles |style="background:#FEF6E4;" | Possible traces of Microbial Activity |style="background:#FEF6E4;" | Probably related with [[archaea]]l activity.<ref name=structu/> Although ''Frutexites'' is a cryptic microfossil and an important element of many deep water stromatolites, with an inorganic origin proposed it was interpreted as dendritic shrubs of purely inorganic growth of [[Aragonite|aragonitic]] crystals, but it also resembles shrubs of the cyanobacteria [[Angulocellularia]].<ref name=structu/> In the Posidonia Shale a cryptoendopelitic mode of life is assumed, being only possible for [[heterotroph]]ic bacteria or [[fungi]].<ref name=structu/> As seen in the stromatolites of the Posidonia Shale, ''Frutexites'' acted mainly as a dweller or secondary binder of the deep-water stromatolites, not as their major constructor.<ref name=structu/> |style="background:#FEF6E4;" | |- |}
== Cyanobacteria == {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! Genus ! Species ! Location ! Material ! Notes |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Girvanella]]''<ref name=BlackFo/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''G. minuta'' *''G. staminea'' *''G. tucci'' *''G.'' spp. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Chalhac * Reutlingen * Dotternhausen * Mössingen * Gomaringen * Reutlingen * Ohmenhausen * Aselfingen * Schandelah * Hondelange |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Crypt laminites |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A cyanobacteria, member of the family [[Oscillatoriales]]. ''Girvanella'' is almost rock-forming in the lower and upper levels, and is very common, but can only rarely be detected in the bituminous clay marl slate due to preservation reasons.<ref name=BlackFo/> |- |}
== Rhizaria == === Foraminifera === {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! Genus ! Species ! Location ! Material ! Notes ! Images |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Ammodiscus]]''<ref name=Tirol/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''A. siliceus'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Unken |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Shells |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A benthic [[foraminifera]]n, type member of the family [[Ammodiscinae]] inside [[Ammodiscina]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Annulina]]''<ref name=Tirol/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''A. metemis'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Unken |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Shells |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A benthic foraminiferan, member of [[Psammosphaerinae]] inside the family [[Psammosphaeridae]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Astacolus]]''<ref name=BlackFo/><ref name=Tirol/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''A. bochardi'' *''A. primus'' *''A. varians'' *''A. matutina'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Buttenheim * Unken |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Shells |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A benthic foraminiferan, member of [[Vaginulinidae]] inside the family [[Vaginulinida]] ([[Lagenina]]). |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | [[File:Cristellaria crepidula.jpg|thumb|center|195px|Drawing of an ''Astacolus'' shell]] |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Citharina]]''<ref name=Tirol/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''C. gradata'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Unken |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Shells |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A benthic foraminiferan, member of [[Vaginulinidae]] inside the family [[Vaginulinida]] ([[Lagenina]]). |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Cornuspira]]''<ref name=BlackFo/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''C. involvens'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Buttenheim |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Shells |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A benthic foraminiferan, type member of [[Cornuspiridae]] inside the family [[Cornuspirida]] ([[Lagenina]]). Round-spiral shell morphology |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Cyclogyra]]''<ref name=Tirol/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''C. orbicula'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Unken |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Shells |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A benthic foraminiferan, member of the family [[Cornuspirinae]] inside [[Cornuspiridae]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Dentalina]]''<ref name=BlackFo/><ref name="Riegraf1"/><ref name=Tirol>{{cite journal |last1=Ebli |first1=O. |title=Foraminiferen und Coccolithen aus den Lias-Epsilon-Schiefern der Unkener Mulde (Tirolikum, Nördliche Kalkalpen) |journal=Mitt. == Dinoflagellata == === Dinoflagellate cysts === The evolutionary burst of the [[Toarcian]] dinoflagellates led the first appearance and rapid radiation of the [[Phallocystaceae]] (''[[Susainium]]'', ''[[Parvocysta]]'', ''[[Phallocysta]]'', ''[[Moesiodinium]]'' and related forms).<ref name=Dinocy>{{cite journal |last1=Feist-Burkhardt |first1=S. |last2=Wille |first2=W. |title=Jurassic palynology in southwest Germany–state of the art |journal=Cahiers de Micropaléontologie |date=1992 |issue=2 |pages=13–16 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/237081251 |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> This occurred at the time of a widespread Lower Toarcian bituminous anoxia-derived shale, which is recovered from the Posidonienschiefer, [[Pozzale]], [[Italy]], [[Asturias]], [[Spain]], [[Bornholm]], [[Denmark]], the [[Lusitanian Basin]] of [[Portugal]], the [[Jet Rock Formation]] in [[Yorkshire]] and to the "Schistes Carton" in northern [[France]]. Whether there is a causal connection in this co-occurrence of [[Phallocystaceae]] and bituminous facies is a problem still to be resolved. This family has its acme in diversity and quantity in the latest Toarcian and became less important in the [[Aalenian]].<ref name=Dinocy/>

== Algae == The Posidonia Shale preserves an abundant variety of algae, such as the genus of colonial green algae [[Botryococcus]], or the unicellular algal bodies [[Tasmanites]], and other small examples. Algae are a good reference for changes on the oxygen conditions along the Toarcian.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schouten |first1=S. |last2=van Kaam-Peters |first2=H. M. |last3=Rijpstra |first3=W. I. C. |last4=Schoell |first4=M. |last5=Damste |first5=J. S. S. |title=Effects of an oceanic anoxic event on the stable carbon isotopic composition of early Toarcian carbon |journal=American Journal of Science |date=2000 |volume=300 |issue=1 |pages=1–22 |doi=10.2475/ajs.300.1.1 |bibcode=2000AmJS..300....1S |hdl=1874/4290 |url=https://www.ajsonline.org/content/300/1/1 |access-date=3 March 2022 |hdl-access=free |archive-date=3 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220303223234/https://www.ajsonline.org/content/300/1/1 }}</ref>

== Fungi == Fungal spores, hyphae and indeterminate remains are a rare element of the otherwise open marine deposits of the Posidonienschiefer formation, but were recovered at Dormettingen.<ref name=AdvaPal>{{cite journal |last1=Galasso |first1=F. |last2=Schmid-Röhl |first2=A. |last3=Feist-Burkhardt |first3=S. |last4=Bernasconi |first4=S. M. |last5=Schneebeli-Hermann |first5=E. |title=Changes in organic matter composition during the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event (T-OAE) in the Posidonia Shale Formation from Dormettingen (SW-Germany) |journal=Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology |date=2021 |volume=569 |issue=1 |pages=675–689 |doi=10.1016/j.palaeo.2021.110327 |bibcode=2021PPP...56910327G |s2cid=233550926 |doi-access=free |hdl=20.500.11850/474401 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> These fungal remains are composed mostly of indeterminate spores and indicate oxygenated environments and suitable transportation by rivers.<ref name=AdvaPal/>
=== Incertae sedis === {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! Genus ! Species ! Location ! Material ! Notes ! Images |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Ostracoblabe]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''O. sp.'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Holzmanden * Dotternhausen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Fungal patches in ammonite shells and belemnite rostra |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A marine parasitoid fungus of uncertain relationship, linked with shells of marine invertebrates. The extant ''Ostracoblabe implexa'' is usually found associated with bivalve shells as an external parasitoid. Beyond this genus, other fungal remains include indeterminate endolithic fungi linked with microbial mats. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |}
==Ichnofossils== The major ichnological analyses of the Posidonian Shale come from Dotternhausen/Dormettingen, where the ichnogenus ''[[Phymatoderma]]'' formed the so-called Tafelfleins and Seegrasschiefer.<ref name=Ich>{{cite journal |last1=Rodríguez-Tovar |first1=F. J. |title=Ichnology of the Toarcian Oceanic Anoxic Event: An {{sic|nolink=y|reason=error in source|understimated}} tool to assess palaeoenvironmental interpretations |journal=Earth-Science Reviews |date=2021 |volume=216 |issue=1 |pages=122–146 |doi=10.1016/j.earscirev.2021.103579 |bibcode=2021ESRv..21603579R |s2cid=233849558 |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0012825221000787 |access-date=3 March 2022|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The Tafelflein bed was deposited under anoxic bottom and pore water, where a recover of oxygen allow the Phymatoderma-producers return.<ref name=Ich/> The two organic-rich layers (Tafelfleins and Seegrasschiefer) are characterized by the dense occurrence of trace fossils such as ''[[Chondrites]]'' and ''[[Phymatoderma]]'', done episodically due to the fall of the oxygen levels.<ref name=Ich/> The coeval more nearshore Swiss deposits referred Posidonian Shale ([[Rietheim Member]]) hosted similar trace fossils to those recovered on SW Germany.<ref name=Ich/> Ichnofossils in this setting apparently evolved faster to more oxic-to-dysoxic bottom waters.<ref name=Ich/> At Unken, laminated deposits of red limestone suggest well oxygenated active waters (as they lack shale), where high amounts of ''[[Chondrites]]'' are found.<ref name=EBLIA/>

==Invertebrata== ===Porifera=== In the non-bituminous facies located on Obereggenen im Breisgau (Shore of the Black Forest High), especially the lower semicelatum subzone, pyritized individual needles of silica sponges ([[Demospongiae]] and [[Hexactinellida]]) are found, rarely on pelagic layers to very often on the low depth marine deposits.<ref name=BlackFo/> They are usually associated with radiolarian stone cores. In [[Dusslingen]] and [[Reutlingen]], these sponge needles are sometimes barytized in phosphorites of the Haskerense subzone and are much more common here than in any other zone of the Lower Toarcian. These needles are absent in the bituminous horizons of the entire Lower Toarcian.<ref name=BlackFo/> Increased amounts of sponge needles (dominated by [[Hexactinellida]]) are also found on the arenaceous facies of the nearshore unit that is the Unken member, being the only section if its region hosts them, probably due to be an active and well oxygenated bottom.<ref name=EBLIA/> The location of this member as a possible bay on the south of the vindelician land probably allow to the development of more pre-Toarcian AOE conditions, hence the presence of biota otherwise rare on bituminous layers.<ref name=EBLIA/>

Images |- | ''[[Gibbirynchia]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/><ref name=PosidoSwi/> | *''G. amalthei'' | * Holzmaden * Aselfingen | Shells | A [[Pennospiriferinidae|pennospiriferinid]] [[rhynchonellata]]n.<ref name=biology/> | |- | ''[[Lingula (brachiopod)|Lingula]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''L. posidoniae'' | * Hondelange * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhaussen | Shells | A [[Lingulidae]] [[rhynchonellata]]n. Associations of bioturbating infauna are dominated in certain sections by ''[[Palaeonucula]]''/Lingula aggregations, developed under longer-term oxygenated conditions within the substrate and bottom waters.<ref name=biology/> | |- | ''[[Orbiculoidea]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''O. papyracea'' | * Hondelange * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhaussen | Shells | A [[Discinidae]] [[rhynchonellata]]n. This genus was found to have a planktotrophic larval stage that adapted while growing to the local redox boundary. When this fluctuated near the sediment–water interface and oxygen availability prevailed, it allowed benthic colonization. It is found in associations with ''[[Grammatodon]]'' and ''[[Pseudomytiloides]]''.<ref name=biology/> | |- | ''[[Rhynchonella]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''R.amalthei'' | * Holzmaden * Dotternhaussen | Shells | A [[Rhynchonellidae]] [[rhynchonellata]]n. Found associated with ''[[Plicatula]]'' in long-term well-oxygenated conditions within the substrate and bottom waters.<ref name=biology/> | |- | ''[[Spiriferina]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''S. villosa'' | * Holzmaden * Dotternhaussen | Shells | A [[Spiriferinidae]] [[rhynchonellata]]n. | |- | ''[[Waldheimia]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''W. subdigona'' | * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhaussen | Shells | A [[Terebratellidae]] [[rhynchonellata]]n. | |- |}
=== Mollusca === ====Bivalvia==== {| class = "wikitable" |- ! Genus ! Species ! Location ! Material ! Notes ! Images |- | ''[[Antiquilima]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''A.'' sp. | Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Limidae|Limidoid]] file [[clam]]. | |- | ''[[Bositra]]''<ref name="PosidoniaBo">{{cite journal |last1=Jefferies |first1=R. P. S. |last2=Minton |first2=P. |title=The mode of life of two Jurassic species of Posidonia (Bivalvia) |journal=Palaeontology |date=1965 |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=156–185 |url=https://www.palass.org/publications/palaeontology-journal/archive/8/1/article_pp156-185 |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> | *''B. buchii'' *''B. radiata'' | All the Formation | Shells | A [[Posidoniidae|posidoniid]] [[ostreoida]]n. It is the type fossil of the Sachrang Formation. Originally it was named "''Posidonia bronni''", thought to be a new genus, and the strata were denominated the Posidonia layers after it. Years later it turned out to be a junior synonym of ''Bositra'', and thus it was reassigned. However, the name of the layers was retained. The habitat and mode of life of ''Bositra'' has been debated for more than a century. There have been different interpretations, such as a pseudoplanktonic organism,<ref name= hauff>{{cite journal |last1=Hauff |first1=B. |title=Untersuchung der Fossilfundstätten von Holzmaden im Posidonienschiefer des Oberen Lias Württembergs |journal=Palaeontographica |date=1921 |volume=64 |issue=1 |pages=1–42 |url=https://www.schweizerbart.de/papers/palae/detail/64/67083/Untersuchung_der_Fossilfundstatten_von_Holzmaden_im_Posidonienschiefer_des_oberen_Lias_Wurttembergs |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> a benthic organism living on the open marine floor, where it was the main inhabitant of the basinal settings, and a hybrid mode, where it had a life cycle with holopelagic reproduction controlled by changes in oxygen levels, and even a chemosymbiotic lifestyle with the large crinoid rafts being the main "safe havens" to evade anoxic events. Various hypotheses along the years led to a large study in 1998, where the size/frequency distribution, the density of growth through lines related to the shell size and the position of the redox boundary by total organic carbon diagrams revealed that ''Bositra'' probably had a benthic mode of life.<ref name=Rohl>{{cite journal |last1=Röhl |first1=H. J. |title=Hochauflösende palökologische und sedimentologische Untersuchungen im Posidonienschiefer (Lias e)[epsilon)] von SW-Deutschland |journal=Tübinger Geowissenschaftliche Arbeiten, Reihe A |date=1998 |volume=48 |issue=1 |pages=1–189 |url=https://lgrbwissen.lgrb-bw.de/hochaufloesende-geochemische-untersuchungen-im-posidonienschiefer-lias-epsilon-sw-deutschland |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> | [[File:Bositra buchi 45.jpg|thumb|center|155px|Thousands of specimens in one matrix]] |- | ''[[Camptonectes]]''<ref name=Oxs>{{cite journal |last1=Arp |first1=G. |last2=Gropengießer |first2=S. |last3=Schulbert |first3=C. |last4=Jung |first4=D. |last5=Reimer |first5=A. |title=Biostratigraphy and sequence stratigraphy of the Toarcian Ludwigskanal section (Franconian Alb, Southern Germany) |journal=Zitteliana |date=2021 |volume=95 |issue=1 |pages=95–57 |doi=10.3897/zitteliana.95.56222 |s2cid=237384737 |url=https://zitteliana.pensoft.net/article/56222/ |access-date=3 March 2022 |doi-access=free }}</ref> | *''C. subulatus'' | *Altdorf *Dörlbach | Shells | A [[Pectinoidae|pectinoid]] [[scallop]]. The presence of this genus along endobenthic and epibenthic bivalves, which are absent farther up the section, suggest a delayed overstepping of anoxic bottom waters on the Altdorf High.<ref name=Oxs/> | |- | ''[[Chlamys]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''C. priscus'' *''C. sp.'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Dörlbach * Mistelgau * Hondelange * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Pectinoidae|pectinoid]] [[scallop]]. | [[File:Chlamys varia 20191231.jpg|thumb|center|155px|Single specimen]] |- | ''[[Cucullaea]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/><ref name=PosidoSwi/> | *''C. (Idonearca) muensteri'' *''C. sp.'' | * Holzmaden * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Cucullaeidae|cucullaeid]] [[clam]]. | |- | ''[[Eopecten]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/><ref name=PosidoSwi/> | *''E. strionatis'' *''E. tumidus'' *''E. sp.'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Mistelgau * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen * Aselfingen | Shells | A [[Pectinoidae|pectinoid]] [[scallop]]. | |- | ''[[Exogyra]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''E. berthaudi'' | * Holzmaden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Gryphaeidae|gryphaeid]] mud [[oyster]]. | |- | ''[[Gervillella]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/><ref name=PosidoSwi/> | *''G. lanceolata'' | * Holzmaden * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Bakevelliidae|bakevelliid]] mud [[oyster]]. | |- | ''[[Goniomya]]''<ref name=MonotisD/> | *''G. rhombifera'' | * Altdorf * Dörlbach * Hirschbühler Bach | Shells | A [[Pholadomyidae|Pholadomyid]] [[clam]] | |- | ''[[Grammatodon]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''G. taylori'' *''G. jurianus'' *''G. sp.'' | * Banz * Ludwig Canal * Hondelange * Pferdsfeld * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Grammatodontinae]] [[clam]]. Being the second most common genus of bivalve in the Formation, it has been subject to several studies in regards to its ecological niche, similar to ''Bositra''. Several opinions include a pseudoplanktonic-only organism able to live in the open sea, or a benthic-only organism. Within the 1998 evaluation with ''Bositra'', was found that this genus probably had a benthic juvenile stage that transitioned to a faculatively pseudoplanktonic adult.<ref name=Rohl/> | [[File:Pseudomytiloides dubius.jpg|thumb|center|155px|Single specimen]] |- | ''[[Pteria (bivalve)|Pteria]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''P.'' sp. | * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Pteriidae]]oid wing-[[oyster]]. | |- | ''[[Solemya]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''S. bollensis'' *''S. voltzi'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Mistelgau * Hondelange * Aichelberg * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen | Shells | A [[Clam]], type member of the family [[Solemyidae]] inside [[Solemyida]]. | [[File:Solemya voltzi.jpg|thumb|center|155px|Single specimen]] |- | ''[[Steinmannia]]''<ref name=MAJORFos/> | *''S. bronni'' *''S. radiata'' | * Hondelange * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen | Shells | A "[[Posidoniidae|posidoniid]]" [[ostreoida]]n. === Echinodermata === Echinoderm debris is relatively abundant in the shale-free Unken and Salzburg members, including crinoid and brittle star skeletal elements; sea urchins take their place later in the formation, with them having especially diversified at that time, leading to [[pedicellaria]] being observed very often.<ref name=EBLIA/>

B. |title=Pentacrinites quenstedti (Oppel) aus dem oberen Untertoarcium (Lias Epsilon) von Ohmden bei Holzmaden (SW-Deutschland) |journal=Paläontologische Zeitschrift |date=1984 |volume=58 |issue=1 |pages=255–263 |doi=10.1007/BF02986064 |s2cid=128699904 |url=https://www.buchfreund.de/de/d/p/78428853/pentacrinites-quenstedti-oppel-aus-dem-oberen |access-date=20 February 2022|url-access=subscription }}</ref> | * Chalhac * Häufig in Obereggenen * Aselfingen * Gomaringen * Reutlingen * Dotternhausen * Mössingen * Ohmenhausen * Altdorf * Oedhof * Mistelgau * Banz * Irlbach * Kerkhofen * Heiningen * Reichenbach * Wasseralfingen * Unterstürmig * Schandelah * Hondelange |Various complete and nearly complete specimens, some associated with [[raft]]s. | Type genus of the family [[Pentacrinitidae]]. Like ''Seirocrinus'', ''Pentacrinites'' formed colonies in rafting wood. Was a small genus, with multiple specimens no more than 1 meter long, usually measuring 40–70 cm. | [[File:Pentacrinites dichotomus.JPG|thumb|center|155px|Close view in one specimen from the Posidonienschiefer]] |- | ''[[Praetetracrinus]]''<ref name="Crino1">{{cite journal |last1=Jäger |first1=M. |title=Echinodermata aus dem Ober-Toarcium und Aalenium Deutschlands I. Crinoidea: Cyrtocrinina und Millericrinina |journal=Stuttgarter Beiträge zur Naturkunde, Serie B (Geologie und Paläontologie) |date=1995 |volume=226 |issue=1 |pages=1–51 |url=https://www.zobodat.at/pdf/Stuttgarter-Beitraege-Naturkunde_226_B_0001-0051.pdf |access-date=20 February 2022}}</ref> | *''P. kutscheri'' | * Schömberg | Isolated Stems | A [[crinoid]] in the family [[Plicatocrinidae]]. | |- | ''[[Procomaster]]''<ref name="SeaStars"/> | *''P. pentadactylus'' | * Aichelberg | Exceptionally well preserved individual with the arms, pinnules and cirri largely intact | A [[crinoid]] in the family [[Isocrinida]]. This benthic crinoid clearly represents an exotic element in the typical Posidonia fauna, likely moved from coastal settings. | |- | ''[[Seirocrinus]]''<ref name="Crino0"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Seilacher |first1=A. |last2=Drozdzewski |first2=G. |last3=Haude |first3=R. |title=Form and function in the stem in a pseudoplanktonic crinoid (Seirocrinus) |journal=Palaeontology |date=1968 |volume=11 |issue=2 |pages=275–282 |url=http://www.fossilcrinoids.com/literature/9.1b_Seilacher.pdf |access-date=20 February 2022}}</ref> | *''S. subangularis'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Mistelgau * Aichelberg * Schandelah * Dotternhausen * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Maurach |Various complete and nearly complete specimens, some associated with [[raft]]s | The largest known [[crinoid]], from the family [[Pentacrinitidae]]. ''Seirocrinus'' consists of fossils in colonies along large wood trunks, with specimens up to 14 m long and the largest reaching 26 m, which makes it among the tallest known Mesozoic organisms, one of the largest invertebrates known in the fossil record and one if the tallest known animals.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Simms |title=Contrasting lifestyles in lower Jurassic crinoids: A comparison in benthic and pseudopelagic Isocrinida |journal=Palaeontology |date=1989 |volume=29 |issue=2 |pages=475–493 |url=https://www.palass.org/publications/palaeontology-journal/archive/29/3/article_pp475-493 |access-date=20 February 2022}}</ref> It was an open ocean organism that lived on rafting wood, probably filtering food and serving as a refuge for other animals, such as ammonites. The crinoids had a large colonization process, based on their fossils in the wood.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Haude |first1=R. |last2=Jangoux |first2=M. |title=Echinoderms: Present and Past |chapter=Constructional morphology of the stems of Pentacrinitidae, and mode of life of Seirocrinus |date=1980 |volume=1 |issue=2 |pages=17–23 |doi=10.1201/9781003078913-3 |isbn=978-1-003-07891-3 |s2cid=225515238 |chapter-url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.1201/9781003078913-3/constructional-morphology-stems-pentacrinitidae-mode-life-seirocrinus-haude?context=ubx&refId=3c73c390-2737-47b5-b86c-a8d93ee370be |access-date=20 February 2022|chapter-url-access=subscription }}</ref> The large rafts were the home for a high variety of marine organisms, such as barnacles, ammonites and others. It has been estimated that without the presence of modern raft wood predators (that appeared in the [[Bathonian]]) these rafts could have lasted up to 5 years, being the main reason that the crinoids were able to reach such huge sizes. The large rafts were also probably essential to distribute animals along the Early Jurassic seas.<ref name="CrinoidMegaraftA">{{cite journal |last1=Hunter |first1=A. W. |last2=Mitchell |first2=E. G. |last3=Casenove |first3=D. |last4=Mayers |first4=C. |title=Reconstructing the ecology of a Jurassic pseudoplanktonic megaraft colony |journal=Royal Society Open Science |date=2019 |volume=7 |issue=7 |pages=12–31 |doi=10.1098/rsos.200142 |pmid=32874621 |pmc=7428219 }}</ref> | [[File:Seirocrinus subangularis (fossil crinoid) (Posidonia Shale, Lower Jurassic; Holzmaden area, Germany) 1 (35674916591).jpg|thumb|center|155px|Close view of one specimen from the Posidonienschiefer]] |- |}

E. |title=New Metrics To Differentiate Species of Stenopterygius (Reptilia: Ichthyosauria) from the Lower Jurassic of Southwestern Germany |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=2012 |volume=86 |issue=1 |pages=105–115 |doi=10.1666/11-038.1 |s2cid=130298530 |jstor=41409134 }}</ref> | *''S. quadriscissus'' *''S. triscissus'' *''S. uniter'' *''S.'' sp. | * Banz * Altdorf * Mistelgau * Schlierbach * Hondelange * Grassel * Beienrode * Schandelah * Aichelberg * Staffelstein * Pferdsfeld * Oedhof * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Gomaringen * Dotternhausen * Dörnten * Langenbrücken * Bascharange * Klein Lehmhagen | Various complete and nearly complete specimens | Type genus of the family [[Stenopterygiidae]]. A common Toarcian ichthyosaur, present in multiple layers. The rather exquisite level of preservation has led to even the coloration being preserved. This shows a clear countershading, with an upper part being darker than the lower, similar to modern [[killer whale]]s, the [[Heaviside's dolphin]] or the [[Dall's porpoise]]. There is also evidence of changes in color with ontogenetic changes, going from dark juveniles to countershaded adults. The skin was flexible & scaleless, as in dolphins.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lindgren |first1=J |last2=Sjövall |first2=P |last3=Thiel |first3=V |last4=Zheng |first4=W |last5=Ito |first5=S |last6=Wakamatsu |first6=K |last7=Hauff |first7=R |last8=Kear |first8=BP |last9=Engdahl |first9=A |last10=Alwmark |first10=C |last11=Eriksson |first11=ME |last12=Jarenmark |first12=M |last13=Sachs |first13=S |last14=Ahlberg |first14=PE |last15=Marone |first15=F |last16=Kuriyama |first16=T |last17=Gustafsson |first17=O |last18=Malmberg |first18=P |last19=Thomen |first19=A |last20=Rodriguez-Meizoso |first20=I |last21=Uvdal |first21=P |last22=Ojika |first22=M |last23=Schweitzer |first23=MH |title=Soft-tissue evidence for homeothermy and crypsis in a Jurassic ichthyosaur |journal=Nature |date=2018 |volume=564 |issue=1 |pages=359–365 |doi=10.1038/s41586-018-0775-x |pmid=30518862 |bibcode=2018Natur.564..359L |s2cid=54458324 |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-018-0775-x |access-date=10 February 2022|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The study of several specimens has revelated that ''Stenopterygius quadriscissus'' underwent a size-related trophic niche shift through ontogeny, shifting from a piscivorous diet to a teuthophagous diet, known thanks to exquisitely preserved stomach contents.<ref name=Niche>{{cite journal |last1=Dick |first1=D. B"'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Mistelgau * Schlierbach * Hondelange * Grassel * Schandelah * Dörnten * Langenbrücken * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhausen * Klein Lehmhagen | Various complete and nearly complete specimens | Type genus of the family [[Temnodontosauridae]]. A large macroraptorial ichthyosaur, the [[apex predator]] of its environment. It ranges between 9 and 12 m, being one of the largest known ichthyosaurs, characterised by skulls and jaws over 1 m in length, with the largest being over 1.9 m long. It has been found with fragments of young ichthyosaurs in its stomach.<ref name="HybodusB">{{cite journal |last1=Thies |first1=D. |last2=Hauff |first2=R. B. |title=A Speiballen from the lower jurassic posidonia shale of South Germany |journal=Neues Jahrbuch für Geologie und Paläontologie - Abhandlungen |date=2013 |volume=267 |issue=1 |pages=117–124 |doi=10.1127/0077-7749/2012/0301 |bibcode=2013NJGPA.267..117T |url=https://www.urweltmuseum.de/wp-content/uploads/2013/02/Speiballen-0301_Thies_117_124_cmyk_wm.pdf |access-date=10 February 2022 |archive-date=3 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220303130400/https://www.urweltmuseum.de/wp-content/uploads/2013/02/Speiballen-0301_Thies_117_124_cmyk_wm.pdf }}</ref> | [[File:Temnodontosaurus trigonodon 2.JPG|thumb|200px|''T. trigonodon'' ]] |- |}

Was considered synonymous with ''[[Steneosaurus]]'' until in 2020 the latter was recovered as invalid. It reached large sizes, with specimens exceeding 5 m, being a generalist predator.<ref name="ThalaA">{{cite journal |last1=Johnson |first1=Michela M. |last2=Young |first2=Mark T. |last3=Brusatte |first3=Stephen L. |date=2020 |title=The phylogenetics of Teleosauroidea (Crocodylomorpha, Thalattosuchia) and implications for their ecology and evolution |journal=PeerJ |volume=8 |article-number=e9808 |doi=10.7717/peerj.9808 |pmc=7548081 |pmid=33083104 |doi-access=free}}</ref> | [[File:Steneosaurus bollensis Tubingen.JPG|thumb|center|155px|''[[Macrospondylus|M]]. bollensis'']] |- | ''[[Mystriosaurus]]''<ref name=ThalaA/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Heller |first1=F. |title=Ein Mystriosaurus-Fund im Lias epsilon von Mistelgau |journal=Geologische Blätter für Nordost-Bayern und angrenzende Gebiete |date=1953 |volume=6 |issue=2 |pages=146–148}}</ref><ref name=Mys>{{cite journal |last1=Sachs |first1=S |last2=Johnson |first2=M.M. |last3=Young |first3=M.T. |last4=Abel |first4=P. |title=The mystery of Mystriosaurus: Redescribing the poorly known Early Jurassic teleosauroid thalattosuchians Mystriosaurus laurillardi and Steneosaurus brevior |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |date=2019 |volume=64 |issue=3 |pages=565–579 |doi=10.4202/app.00557.2018 |s2cid=202892925 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/335814033 |access-date=10 February 2022|doi-access=free |hdl=20.500.11820/fa362d74-7f12-4513-b1f9-cc4eadbb0d67 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> | *''M. laurillardi'' *''M. sp.'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Mistelgau * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhausen * Schandelah<ref name=Schadel/> * Klein Lehmhagen | Various complete and nearly complete specimens | A mesorostrine [[thalattosuchia]]n of the family [[Teleosauridae]]. A marine crocodylomorph with a diet probably composed of fish. Was considered synonymous with ''[[Steneosaurus]]'' until recently.<ref name=Mys/> Due to the unusual placement of the external nares, ''Mystriosaurus'' was likely more terrestrial, or spent a greater amount of time on land, than other teleosauroids. This would explain its greater presence in zones of the formation closer to the emerged landmasses. Its morphology suggest it was a mesorostrine generalist.<ref name=Mys/> | [[File:Palais de Rumine (Lausanne)-05.JPG|thumb|center|155px|''[[Mystriosaurus]]'']] |- | ''[[Pelagosaurus]]''<ref name=Pel>{{cite journal |last1=Pierce |first1=S. E. |last2=Williams |first2=M. |last3=Benson |first3=R. B. |title=Virtual reconstruction of the endocranial anatomy of the early Jurassic marine crocodylomorph Pelagosaurus typus (Thalattosuchia) |journal=PeerJ |date=2017 |volume=3225 |issue=1 |pages=1–32 |url=https://peerj.com/articles/3225/ |access-date=10 February 2022}}</ref> | *''P. typus'' *''P. sp.'' | * Banz * Altdorf * Ohmden * Holzmaden * Dotternhausen * Dudelange-Bettembourg | Various complete and nearly complete specimens | A [[thalattosuchia]]n with a complex designation, probably the basalmost [[metriorhynchoid]]. ''Pelagosaurus typus'' was a small-bodied thalattosuchian (~1 m in length) considered to be an adept aquatic pursuit predator, with a long streamlined snout ideal for snapping at fast moving prey (one specimen was found with ''[[Leptolepis]]'' fishes inside) and large, anterolaterally placed orbits for increased visual acuity.<ref name=Pel/> | [[File:Pelagosaurus typus.JPG|thumb|center|155px|''[[Pelagosaurus]]'']] |- | ''[[Plagiophthalmosuchus]]''<ref name=ThalaA/> | *''P. gracilirostris'' | * Dudelange-Bettembourg | Various complete and nearly complete specimens | A longirostrine [[thalattosuchia]]n, the most basal known. Was considered synonymous with ''[[Steneosaurus]]''. Longirostrine specialist, probably an active fish hunter. | |- | ''[[Platysuchus]]''<ref name=ThalaA/> | *''P. multiscrobiculatus'' | * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhausen * Dudelange-Bettembourg | Various complete and nearly complete specimens | A longirostrine [[thalattosuchia]]n of the family [[Teleosauridae]]. ''Platysuchus'' was slightly more robust than its contemporaneous relatives, being probably adapted to hunt larger fish. It was a heavily armoured, semi-terrestrial longirostrine generalist form, indicated by the extensive and tightly packed rows of dorsal osteoderms.<ref name=ThalaA/> | [[File:Platysuchus.png|thumb|center|155px|''[[Platysuchus]]'']] |- |}

== Plantae == [[File:Phragmites australis kz01.jpg|thumb|205px|left|The Flora is dominated by horsetails, which may be in a similar ecological niche to the modern genus ''[[Phragmites]]'', able to resist saline conditions. Storms or floods may be the major events that transported this flora to the sea.]] The macroflora of the Posidonia Slate can be described as extremely poor in species.<ref name="Wilde">{{cite journal |last1=Wilde |first1=V. |title=Die Landpflanzen-Taphozönose aus dem Posidonienschiefer des Unteren Jura (Schwarzer Jura [Epsilon], Unter-Toarcium) in Deutschland und ihre Deutung |journal=Staatliches Museum für Naturkunde |date=2001 |volume=304 |issue=2 |pages=1–12 |url=https://silo.tips/download/stuttgarter-beitrge-zur-naturkunde-5 |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> Apart from the remains of [[horsetail]]s, it is without exception the remains of coarse branches and fronds from gymnosperms, which can be assumed were transport resistant. Remains of [[fern]]s are completely missing, except for tall arboreal ferns ([[Peltaspermales]]).<ref name="Vogellehner82">{{cite journal |last1=Vogellehner |first1=D. |title=Zur Anatomie und Systematik von "Treibhölzern" aus dem Posidonienschiefer von Holzmaden (Schwäb. Alb) |journal=Cour. Forsch.-Inst. Senckenberg |date=1982 |volume=56 |issue=1 |pages=15–22}}</ref> Most of the flora was reported from the area of [[Braunschweig]].<ref name="Wilde"/> The major explanation for this flora could be that the plants in question are mono-or oligotypic stands on the edge of the waters that flowed into the Posidonienschiefer sea, probably torn away in flood events, easily fragmented during transport and from waves, especially in the occasional storm events postulated.<ref name=Gagat>{{cite journal |last1=Seilacher |first1=A. |title=Die Holzmadener Posidonienschiefer. Entstehung der Fossillagerstätte und eines Erdölmuttergesteines |journal=Klassische Fundstellen der Paläontologie |date=1990 |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=107–131 |url=https://www.leo-bw.de/web/guest/detail/-/Detail/details/DOKUMENT/wlbblb_labi/1266663/Die+Holzmadener+Posidonienschiefer++Entstehung+der+Fossillagerst%C3%A4tte+und+eines+Erd%C3%B6lmuttergesteins |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> In terms of taphonomy, this would result in a comparison with today's reed ''[[Phragmites]]'', which can form extensive stocks on the edge of shallow and slowly flowing waters.<ref name="Wilde"/> The [[wood]] remnants clearly indicate higher diversity of [[conifer]]ous flora in the delivery area than remains of leafy branches.<ref name="Wilde"/> This fact is likely to be proportionate, similar to the frequent occurrence of charcoalized trunks, most of them are believed to be "driftwoods" which spent a long time drifting, and this also suggests frequent settlement with mussels and full-grown [[crinoid]]s.<ref name="Wilde"/><ref name=Gagat/> The deposition settings are a large distance from the nearest coastline (for southern Germany about 100 kilometers), making only plants resistant to transportation able to survive long enough to get deposited.<ref name="AMMON">{{cite book |last1=Ammon |first1=V.L. |title=Die Jura Ablagerungen zwischen Regensburg und Passau |date=1875 |publisher=Theodor Ackermann |location=München |url=https://www.iberlibro.com/primera-edicion/Jura-Ablagerungen-zwischen-Regensburg-Passau-Ammon-Ludwig/22915905934/bd |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref><ref name="kerkhof">{{cite journal |last1=Brachert |first1=T.H. |title=Makrofossilführung der "Siemensi-Geoden" (Mittlerer Lias Epsilon, Unteres Toarcium) von Kerkhofen/Oberpfalz (Bayern): Neue Insekten- und Pflanzenfunde |journal=Geologische Blätter NO-Bayern |date=1987 |volume=37 |issue=4 |pages=217–240}}</ref> At Irlbach and Kheleim, NE of Regensburg, where the Posidonienschiefer has a near mainland deposit with abundant sand, a rich deposit filled with plant remains of different kinds (Seeds, reproductive organs, leaves, stems, cuticles and wood) with traces of coal was recovered, however, it was never studied in depth.<ref name=Regensbur/> Of all the plant material excavated only a few bennettite leaves and two conifer branches with leaves were cited and none studied.<ref name=Regensbur/> In the Austrian sites the Sachrang Member was developed in the basinal area, while the Unken Member, sandwiched between red, often condensed limestones, represents the marginal facies.<ref name=EBLIA/> Due to being more marginal and connected with the southern Vindelician land, the most diverse palynological assemblages of the formation are found transported from zones with moldanuvian granites as proven by the feldspar accumulations.<ref name=EBLIA/>
{{clear}}
===Phytoclasts=== [[Phytoclast]]s have been recovered from several sections on the formation, but only studied in depth from the Dotternhausen and specially Dormettingen.<ref name=AdvaPal/> Here two kinds of phytoclasts were recovered, opaque phytoclasts (charcoal, indicator of wildfire activity on nearby landmasses, indicator of seasonal alterations of the water column) and translucent phytoclasts (indicator of proximal landmasses with high availability of wood and other plant material, as well as transport conditions).<ref name=AdvaPal/> On the lowermost part of the section opaque phytoclasts are low (15% of the total organic matter) while translucent are incredibly abundant (40%), lowering to around 20-10% on the next section.<ref name=AdvaPal/> The Exaratum subzone is the only one with an inverse trend and more abundance of opaque phytoclasts. On the Bifrons level, both types reach between a 15% and a 30%, showing a rapid increase, then decreasing at the end of the section to values of less than 10%.<ref name=AdvaPal/> Opaque Phytoclasts, for a supposed marine deposit are relatively abundant on some sections, while their decreasing levels suggest (along with increasing levels of Kaolinite) an increased delivery of land plant material by rivers, from areas with wetter climate and less frequent fires, while its rise suggests the opposite, a nearby continental setting with dry climate and continuous wildfire activity.<ref name=AdvaPal/>

Hornwort spores. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | [[File:Notothylas orbicularis (Anthocerotophyta (hornwort)).png|thumb|155px|Example of extant ''[[Notothylas]]'' specimens]] |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Quadraeculina]]''<ref name=PalyDor/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''Q. anaellaeformis'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Dormettingen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Pollen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Podocarpaceae]] and [[Pinaceae]] inside [[Coniferophyta]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Retitriletes]]''<ref name=PalyDor/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''R.austroclavatidites'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Dormettingen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Spores |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Lycopodiaceae]] inside [[Lycopsida]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Spheripollenites]]''<ref name=PraussSouth/><ref name=":1" /><ref name=Madler3/><ref name=AdvaPal/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''S.subgranulatus'' *''S.'' sp. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Bisingen/Zimmern borehole * Klein Lehmhagen pit, Grimmen * Dormettingen * Salem Borehole |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Pollen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Cheirolepidiaceae]] inside [[Pinaceae]]. Abundant in the Lower Jurassic of NW Europe. ''[[Spheripollenites]]'' co-occurs on the coeval [[Sorthat Formation]] with cuticles of ''[[Dactyletrophyllum]] ramonensis'', and after further study a highly significant correlation was found, which may suggest that the species ''S. psilatus'' was produced by the conifer genus ''Dactyletrophyllum''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wade-Murphy |first1=J. |last2=Kuerschner |first2=W. M |title=A new technique to infer the botanical affinity of palynomorphs, and its application on Spheripollenites psilatus from the Toarcian of Bornholm, Denmark |journal=In 7 Th European Palaeobotany Palynology Conference |date=2006 |issue=1–2 |pages=153–154 |url=https://mi-pub.cen.uni-hamburg.de/fileadmin/files/forschung/theomet/docs/pdf/2006-eppc_programme.pdf |access-date=13 October 2021 |archive-date=22 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211222152614/https://mi-pub.cen.uni-hamburg.de/fileadmin/files/forschung/theomet/docs/pdf/2006-eppc_programme.pdf }}</ref> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Stereisporites]]''<ref name=PalyDor/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''S. psilatus'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Dormettingen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Spores |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Sphagnaceae]] inside [[Sphagnopsida]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Striatella]]''<ref name=PalyDor/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''S. seebergensis'' *''S. sp.'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Dormettingen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Spores |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Polypodiaceae]] inside [[Filicopsida]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Todisporites]]''<ref name=PalyDor/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''T. major'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Dormettingen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Spores |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Osmundaceae]] inside [[Filicopsida]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |}

=== Ginkgoales === {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! Genus ! Species ! Location ! Material ! Notes ! Images |- |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Ginkgoites]]''<ref name="Wilde"/><ref name=Land-Pflanzen/><ref name=RemainsA/><ref name="Vogellehner82"/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''G. digitata'' *''G. sp.'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Schandelah<ref name=Schadel/> * Ohmden |style="background:#D1FFCF;" |Leaf compressions |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Ginkgoaceae]] inside [[Ginkgoales]]. In Germany, there are regular remains of coal, which are initially reminiscent of small ginkgo leaves. The leaves are hard to identify, more or less regularly concentric structures, with them sometimes appearing like the coarse fruiting bodies of wood-dwelling fungi, such as the genus ''[[Trametes]]''. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | [[File:Ginkgoites digitata.jpg|thumb|center|155px|''[[Ginkgoites]] digitata'' specimens]] |- |}

''Pagiophyllum araucarinum'' predominates among the types of leafy coniferous branches found locally. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | [[File:Koniferenblatt Pagiophyllum kurri.jpg|thumb|center|155px|''[[Pagiophyllum]]'' kurri specimen from Banz]] |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Widdringtonites]]''<ref name="Wilde"/><ref name=windri/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''W. liasicus'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Irlbach |style="background:#D1FFCF;" |Branched Shoots |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | A possible ancestral member of the [[Callitroideae]] inside [[Cupressaceae]], or a member of [[Cheirolepidiaceae]]. === Fossil wood === Fossil wood amount increases in the marginal Unken Member, with great amounts of logs and fragments more than 1 m long. Surface studies suggest relationships with the wood genera identified on the coeval [[Úrkút Manganese Ore Formation]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tollmann |first1=A. |title=Analyse des klassischen nordalpinen Mesozoikums |journal=Stratigraphie, Fauna und Fazies der Nördlichen Kalkalpen |date=1976 |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=123–141 |oclc=3710249 }}</ref> {| class="wikitable sortable" |- ! R. |title=Evolutionary trends in gymnospermous wood structures during Mesozoic-Protopinaceous woods in the German Jurassic |journal=Palaeobotanist |date=1986 |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=233–235 |url=http://14.139.63.228:8080/pbrep/bitstream/123456789/1265/1/PbV35N3_233.pdf |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''P.'' sp. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Hondelange * Beienrode * Schandelah * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhausen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Wood |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Wood of the Protopinaceae, a possible "morpho-group" of the family [[Cheirolepidiaceae]].<ref name=Prto>{{cite journal |last1=Bamford |first1=M. K. |last2=Philippe |first2=M. |last3=Thévenard |first3=F. |title=Long overdue extinction of the Protopinaceae |journal=Review of Palaeobotany and Palynology |date=2016 |volume=234 |issue=1 |pages=25–30 |doi=10.1016/j.revpalbo.2016.06.006 |bibcode=2016RPaPa.234...25B |url=https://daneshyari.com/article/preview/4750023.pdf |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Phyllocladoxylon]]''<ref name="Wilde"/><ref name="Vogellehner82"/> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''P. sp.'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Hondelange * Hattorf * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Altdorf |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Wood |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Podocarpaceae]] inside [[Pinales]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Taxodioxylon]]''<ref name="Wilde"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Müller-Stoll |first1=W.R. |last2=Schultze-Motel |first2=J. |title=Gymnospermen-Hölzer des Deutschen Jura. Teil 3: Abietoid (modern) getüpfelte Hölzer |journal=Zeitschrift der deutschen geologischen Gesellschaft |date=1990 |volume=141 |issue=2 |pages=61–77|doi=10.1127/zdgg/141/1990/61 }}</ref> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''cf. T. sp.'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Altdorf * Korbach * Hondelange * Holzmaden * Ohmden * Dotternhausen |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Wood |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with the [[Cupressaceae]] inside [[Pinales]]. | style="background:#D1FFCF;" | [[File:Cooks Lake (5494812678).jpg|thumb|center|155px|''[[Taxodium distichum]]'' specimen, whose role was probably shared by the makers of the ''Taxodioxylon'' woods.]] |- |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | ''[[Xenoxylon]]''<ref name="Wilde"/><ref name=Xenox/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Philippe |first1=M. |last2=Thevenard |first2=F. |title=Distribution and palaeoecology of the Mesozoic wood genus Xenoxylon: palaeoclimatological implications for the Jurassic of Western Europe |journal=Review of Palaeobotany and Palynology |date=1996 |volume=91 |issue=4 |pages=353–370 |doi=10.1016/0034-6667(95)00067-4 |bibcode=1996RPaPa..91..353P |url=https://www.academia.edu/15318003 |access-date=3 March 2022}}</ref> |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | *''X. phyllocladoides'' *''X. cf. barberi'' *''X. ellipticum'' |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | * Braunschweig area * Taught near Hanover * Dörnten not far from Liebenburg, district of Goslar |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Wood |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | Affinities with [[Pinophyta]], likely close to the [[Podocarpaceae]], [[Cupressaceae]] and in a lesser extent to the [[Cheirolepidiaceae]]. Finally it may be a member of the extinct family [[Miroviaceae]]. |style="background:#D1FFCF;" | |- |}
