revision.comment: Undid revision [[Special:Diff/1316751261|1316751261]] by [[Special:Contributions/185.104.136.132|185.104.136.132]] ([[User talk:185.104.136.132|talk]]) - no need for pipe
revision.text:
{{Short description|British political scandal}} {{About|the political scandal in the United Kingdom|the Hong Kong political scandal|Witman Hung partygate}} {{Very long|date=June 2023|words=16,000}} {{Use British English|date=February 2022}} {{Use dmy dates|date=December 2022}} [[File:Sue Gray report Fig 7.jpg|alt=Prime Minister Boris Johnson at a Partygate party|thumb|Prime Minister [[Boris Johnson]] at one of the gatherings in which some attendees breached COVID-19 regulations. The other participants were made unidentifiable by [[Sue Gray, Baroness Gray of Tottenham|Sue Gray]] in this image from [[Sue Gray report|her report]].]] {{Boris Johnson sidebar}} '''Partygate'''{{efn|For etymology of the "-gate" suffix, see [[List of -gate scandals and controversies]]}} was a [[political scandal]] in the United Kingdom about gatherings of [[Government of the United Kingdom|government]] and [[Conservative Party (UK)|Conservative Party]] staff during the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom|COVID-19 pandemic]] in 2020 and 2021, when [[British government response to the COVID-19 pandemic|public health restrictions]] prohibited most gatherings. The scandal contributed to [[Boris Johnson]]'s downfall as Prime Minister and his resignation as an MP.
While several [[COVID-19 lockdown in the United Kingdom|lockdowns]] were in place, gatherings took place at [[10 Downing Street]], [[Garden of 10 and 11 Downing Street|its garden]] and other government and Conservative Party buildings. Reports of these events later attracted media attention, public backlash and political controversy. In January 2022, twelve gatherings came under investigation by the [[Metropolitan Police]], including at least three attended by Johnson, the prime minister. The police issued 126 [[fixed penalty notice]]s to 83 individuals, including Johnson, [[Spouse of the prime minister of the United Kingdom|his wife]] [[Carrie Johnson|Carrie]] and [[Rishi Sunak]] (then [[Chancellor of the Exchequer]], who also subsequently became prime minister), who all apologised and paid the penalties.
The first reporting was on 30 November 2021 by the ''[[Daily Mirror]]'' of 10 Downing Street staff gatherings during the 2020 [[Christmas season]]. Johnson said rules had been followed, and Downing Street denied that a party took place. A week later, video of a mock press conference in 10 Downing Street was broadcast in which joking comments about a party having taken place were made. [[Allegra Stratton]] was featured in the video during her role as [[Downing Street Press Secretary]]. She resigned her subsequent government position after the video surfaced. [[Shaun Bailey, Baron Bailey of Paddington|Shaun Bailey]] resigned as chair of the [[London Assembly]]'s Police and Crime Committee after evidence emerged that he had attended a gathering, where it was alleged that Covid regulations had been broken on 14 December 2020 with Conservative Party staff. In January 2022, reports emerged of an event with drinks on 20 May 2020 in the garden of 10 Downing Street during the [[Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (England) Regulations 2020|first national lockdown]]. Johnson said that he attended and apologised for doing so. Downing Street apologised to [[Queen Elizabeth II]] for two events on 16 April 2021, the day before [[Prince Philip's funeral]], during a third lockdown across England. Reports followed of a gathering celebrating Johnson's birthday in June 2020.
After the mock press conference video leaked, on 8 December 2021, Johnson announced a [[Cabinet Office]] inquiry, eventually undertaken by civil servant [[Sue Gray, Baroness Gray of Tottenham|Sue Gray]]. In January 2022, the Metropolitan Police opened its own investigation into potential breaches of COVID-19 regulations, which delayed Gray's report. An update on Gray's investigation was published on 31 January 2022. Gray's final report in May 2022 described multiple events, including excessive drinking and a lack of respect shown to cleaning and security staff. She concluded that senior political and civil service leadership "must bear responsibility for this culture".
Public disquiet over the events led to a decline in public support for Johnson, the government and the Conservatives, and contributed to the party's loss of the [[2021 North Shropshire by-election]] and poor performance in the [[2022 United Kingdom local elections|2022 local elections]]. In early 2022, a number of opposition, and a few Conservative politicians called for Johnson's resignation or a [[2022 vote of confidence in the Conservative Party leadership of Boris Johnson|confidence vote]]. The scandal led to the resignation of five senior Downing Street staff in February, and that of [[Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Justice]] [[David Wolfson, Baron Wolfson of Tredegar|David Wolfson]] in April. On 21 April, MPs referred the allegations that Johnson misled Parliament over events to the [[Commons Privileges Committee investigation into Boris Johnson|Parliamentary Privileges Committee]].
On 9 June 2023, Johnson resigned as an MP after having received the committee's draft report. The committee's final report, published six days later, concluded Johnson had deliberately and repeatedly misled Parliament and impugned and intimidated the committee, would have recommended a 90-day suspension had he not resigned, and recommended that Johnson not be given a courtesy access pass to Parliament otherwise given to former MPs. On 19 June 2023, MPs voted 354 to 7 to accept the results of the privileges committee report, which included Johnson having his privilege to access parliament removed.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2023/jun/19/boris-johnson-faces-loss-of-westminster-pass-as-mps-back-partygate-report|title=Tories round on Boris Johnson as MPs vote to approve Partygate report|first=Aubrey|last=Allegretti|work=The Guardian|date=19 June 2023|access-date=19 June 2023|archive-date=19 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230619205831/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2023/jun/19/boris-johnson-faces-loss-of-westminster-pass-as-mps-back-partygate-report|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=revoked>{{cite news|url=https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-denied-special-access-to-parliament-as-mps-endorse-report-which-said-he-lied-12905498|title=Boris Johnson denied special access to parliament as MPs endorse report which said he lied|first=Alexandra|last=Rogers|publisher=Sky News|date=20 June 2023|access-date=20 June 2023|archive-date=19 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230619210022/https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-denied-special-access-to-parliament-as-mps-endorse-report-which-said-he-lied-12905498|url-status=live}}</ref>
==Background== === COVID-19 lockdowns in the United Kingdom === {{Main|British government response to the COVID-19 pandemic|COVID-19 lockdown in the United Kingdom}}
In response to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom]], a [[COVID-19 lockdown in the United Kingdom|UK-wide lockdown]] began on 23 March 2020 under [[Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (England) Regulations 2020|a new statutory instrument]]. This was a [[stay-at-home order]] that prohibited all non-essential travel and social gatherings.<ref name="auto4" /> Some rules were incrementally relaxed in the following months in England; starting from 13 May, "two people from separate households were permitted to meet outside in a public place".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Grierson |first=Jamie |date=10 January 2022 |title=20 May 2020: what was UK doing while No 10 aide organised a party? |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/jan/10/20-may-2020-what-was-uk-doing-while-no-10-aide-organised-a-party |access-date=11 January 2022 |archive-date=21 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621141422/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/jan/10/20-may-2020-what-was-uk-doing-while-no-10-aide-organised-a-party |url-status=live }}</ref> Six people were allowed to socialise outdoors by June, and indoor social gatherings were permitted from 4 July (only between members of two households).<ref>{{Cite news |last=Grierson |first=Jamie |date=24 January 2022 |title=What were the Covid rules at the time of Boris Johnson's 2020 birthday? |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/24/what-were-the-covid-rules-at-the-time-of-boris-johnsons-2020-birthday |access-date=26 January 2022 |archive-date=21 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621084318/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/24/what-were-the-covid-rules-at-the-time-of-boris-johnsons-2020-birthday |url-status=live }}</ref>
[[File:Hands, Face, Space (Johnson press conference).png|thumb|Prime Minister Boris Johnson at a press conference on 16 December 2020, when he announced London would be moved to "Tier 3" rules.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Prime Minister's statement on coronavirus (COVID-19)|last=Johnson|first=Boris|date=16 December 2020|url=https://www.gov.uk/government/speeches/prime-ministers-statement-on-coronavirus-covid-19-16-december-2020|website=Prime Minister's Office|access-date=18 December 2021|archive-date=21 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621141418/https://www.gov.uk/government/speeches/prime-ministers-statement-on-coronavirus-covid-19-16-december-2020|url-status=live}}</ref>]] With the second wave of the COVID-19 pandemic in much of England, a [[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (England) (No. 4) Regulations 2020|second national lockdown]] started on 5 November 2020. A [[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020|regional tiered lockdown system]] replaced this on 2 December.<ref name=ComLib /> [[COVID-19 pandemic in London|London]] was initially placed in "Tier 2", was moved to the highest level "Tier 3" on 16 December, and finally placed under a newly introduced stay-at-home order, "Tier 4", on 19 December. Socialising between households or outside of [[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020#Linked households|support bubbles]] was not allowed throughout this period.<ref name="BBC_59577129">{{cite news |author=Edgington |first=Tom |date=15 December 2021 |title=Downing Street Christmas party: What were the Covid rules at the time? |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59577129 |access-date=15 December 2021 |archive-date=9 October 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231009230253/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59577129 |url-status=live }}</ref> Household mixing and socialising for [[Christmas]] itself was also restricted to a small number of households and only permitted on 25 December across much of the UK, and in London was cancelled altogether.<ref>{{Cite news|date=23 December 2020|title=Christmas rules 2020: What are the new rules on mixing?|work=[[BBC News]]|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/explainers-55056375|access-date=18 December 2021|archive-date=21 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621085815/https://www.bbc.com/news/explainers-55056375|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 5 January 2021, a third lockdown began across the whole of England. This was gradually lifted in [[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (Steps) (England) Regulations 2021|a series of steps beginning 29 March]], with social contact limited to groups of six from no more than two households and outdoors, into April.<ref name=":2">{{Cite news|date=14 January 2022|title=Lockdown parties in No 10 on eve of Prince Philip's funeral|work=[[BBC News]]|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59989946|access-date=14 January 2022|archive-date=7 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230607153906/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59989946|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite news |last=Campbell |first=Lucy |date=14 January 2022 |title=Renewed calls for PM to resign over parties on eve of Philip funeral |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/14/renewed-calls-for-boris-johnson-to-resign-over-parties-eve-of-philip-funeral |access-date=14 January 2022 |archive-date=21 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621184338/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/14/renewed-calls-for-boris-johnson-to-resign-over-parties-eve-of-philip-funeral |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== Westminster === [[10 Downing Street]] is a government building in the [[City of Westminster]], [[central London]], used by some staff in the [[Cabinet Office]]. It also contains a personal flat designated for the [[Prime Minister of the United Kingdom|prime minister]], though [[Boris Johnson]] used the larger flat in the adjoining [[11 Downing Street]] during his premiership. The Cabinet Office is based at [[70 Whitehall]], connected to 10 and 11 Downing Street.
Social drinks at the end of the week, known as "wine time Friday", were a standing tradition in the Number 10 press office before the pandemic.<ref name="podcast1">{{cite podcast |title=Partygate: The Inside Story - new revelations and our whistleblowers in their own words: 1. Get the party started... |website=ITV News |date=12 January 2023 |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |access-date=3 May 2024 |archive-date=6 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240406105915/https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |url-status=live }}</ref>
ITV News quoted anonymous staff saying that, unlike at other Government departments, there was little attention paid to social distancing or wearing face masks in 10 Downing Street.<ref name=podcast1 />
==Timeline of reporting and reaction== {{Main|Timeline of Partygate}}
===2021=== [[File:Downing Street (18423529009).jpg|thumb|[[Downing Street]], the location of some gatherings.]]
==== Up to 6 December ==== [[Pippa Crerar]], then the political editor at the ''[[Daily Mirror]]'', said that she was first told of breaches of COVID-19 regulations involving the Conservative Party in January 2021, but was unable to get the story "over the line". In October 2021, she received evidence from a contact.<ref name=podcast1 /> As a result, on 30 November 2021, the ''Daily Mirror'' reported allegations that some Downing Street staff had held three gatherings in November and December 2020, when London was under [[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020|COVID-19 tier 3 lockdown restrictions]].<ref name="mirrorfirst">{{Cite news|last=Crerar|first=Pippa|date=30 November 2021|orig-date=updated 1 December 2021|title=Boris Johnson 'broke Covid lockdown rules' with Downing Street parties at Xmas|work=Mirror|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-broke-covid-lockdown-25585238|access-date=2 February 2022|archive-date=11 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220711154159/https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-broke-covid-lockdown-25585238|url-status=live}}</ref> Crerar has subsequently said that, at that time, she was aware of further allegations, but that they were not sufficiently confirmed to be included.<ref name=podcast3>{{cite podcast |title=Partygate: The Inside Story - new revelations and our whistleblowers in their own words: 3. BYOB |website=ITV News |date=12 January 2023 |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |access-date=3 May 2024 |archive-date=6 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240406105915/https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |url-status=live }}</ref>
The restrictions in November and December 2020 prohibited indoor gatherings of more than six people, with exceptions for certain work-related activities.<ref name="ComLib">{{Cite journal|last1=Baker|first1=Carl|last2=Kirk-Wade|first2=Esme|last3=Brown|first3=Jennifer|last4=Barber|first4=Sarah|date=12 July 2021|title=Coronavirus: A history of English lockdown laws|url=https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-9068/|website=UK Parliament: House of Commons Library|access-date=9 December 2021|archive-date=4 May 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240504005254/https://commonslibrary.parliament.uk/research-briefings/cbp-9068/|url-status=live}}</ref> A leaving party for an aide was reported to have been held on 27 November 2020 and attended by Johnson. A Christmas party on 18 December was reported, and a smaller gathering on 13 November "where they were all getting totally plastered".<ref name=mirrorfirst/><ref name="Allegretti">{{cite news |last1=Allegretti |first1=Aubrey |title=The list so far of alleged Covid rule-breaking parties at Westminster The second was |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/09/a-look-at-alleged-covid-rule-breaking-parties-across-westminster |access-date=9 December 2021 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=9 December 2021 |archive-date=9 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211209200138/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/09/a-look-at-alleged-covid-rule-breaking-parties-across-westminster |url-status=live }}</ref> Downing Street denied that a party had taken place.<ref name="auto1">{{cite news|last1=Stone|first1=Jon|date=7 December 2021|title=Boris Johnson says 'rules were followed' at No 10 party as Sunak denies attending|work=[[The Independent|Independent]]|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-rishi-sunak-downing-street-party-b1971373.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-rishi-sunak-downing-street-party-b1971373.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="auto">{{cite news |date=7 December 2021 |title=Cheese and wine? UK PM's staff joked about alleged lockdown party |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/covid-rules-were-not-broken-downing-street-party-uks-johnson-says-2021-12-07/ |url-status=live |url-access=registration |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220112185850/https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/covid-rules-were-not-broken-downing-street-party-uks-johnson-says-2021-12-07/ |archive-date=12 January 2022}}</ref> The official response to the ''Daily Mirror'' report was that "Covid rules have been followed at all times".<ref name="Barradale">{{cite news |last1=Barradale |first1=Greg |title=The Downing Street Christmas party explained: A timeline of the scandal |url=https://www.bigissue.com/news/politics/number-10-christmas-downing-street-party-explained/ |access-date=10 December 2021 |work=[[The Big Issue]] |date=8 December 2021 |ref=Barradale |archive-date=10 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211210145453/https://www.bigissue.com/news/politics/number-10-christmas-downing-street-party-explained/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
The following day, other media outlets reported further details of the event on 18 December, with BBC News reporting it involved "drinks, nibbles, and games" and a source told the ''[[Financial Times]]'' that parties were vital for Downing Street staff to relieve stress. Downing Street responded by saying: "We don't recognise these accounts". At [[Prime Minister's Questions]], Johnson told the Commons: "All guidance was followed completely [in] Number 10".<ref>{{cite web|date=1 December 2021|title=Engagements Volume 704: debated on Wednesday 1 December 2021|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2021-12-01/debates/A0E282CF-039D-4F26-8F16-946B8C6E2ABC/Engagements|access-date=16 January 2022|website=UK Parliament: Hansard|archive-date=16 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220116084111/https://hansard.parliament.uk/commons/2021-12-01/debates/A0E282CF-039D-4F26-8F16-946B8C6E2ABC/Engagements|url-status=live}}</ref> Some Downing Street staff were angered by the denials.<ref name=podcast1 />
On 3 December, Labour MP [[Barry Gardiner]] wrote to the [[Metropolitan Police]] asking them to investigate, but they responded saying that they do not normally investigate "retrospective breaches of the Covid-19 regulations". On 5 December, the [[Secretary of State for Justice|Justice Secretary]] [[Dominic Raab]] told [[Andrew Marr]] that if a "formal party" had taken place "then of course it would be wrong" but that the reports were based on "unsubstantiated, anonymous claims" which is why Downing Street did not respond more directly.<ref name="Barradale" /> Raab also stated "the police don't normally look back and investigate things that have taken place a year ago", about which a [[Full Fact]] investigation concluded "Police often investigate alleged offences which took place years before. This is less clear cut in the context of breaches of Covid-19 regulations, which police say they do not routinely investigate retrospectively".<ref>{{cite web |last1=Turnnidge |first1=Sarah |title=Do police normally investigate things that happened a year ago? |url=https://fullfact.org/law/dominic-raab-police-investigate-downing-street-party/ |website=[[Full Fact]] |access-date=16 December 2021 |date=8 December 2021 |archive-date=15 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211215170207/https://fullfact.org/law/dominic-raab-police-investigate-downing-street-party/ |url-status=live }}</ref> On 6 December, former government adviser [[Dominic Cummings]] alleged that unnamed journalists attended the reported party and that it was "very unwise for No 10 to lie" about the events.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Mason |first1=Rowena |title=Dominic Cummings: 'very unwise for No 10 to lie' about Christmas parties |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/06/dominic-cummings-very-unwise-for-no-10-to-lie-about-christmas-parties |access-date=10 December 2021 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=6 December 2021 |archive-date=10 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211210014437/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/06/dominic-cummings-very-unwise-for-no-10-to-lie-about-christmas-parties |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Barradale" /> The prime minister's spokesperson reiterated: "There was not a party and Covid rules have been followed at all times".<ref name="Barradale" />
==== Stratton video (7–8 December) ==== [[File:Allegra Stratton at Policy Exchange.jpg|alt=Allegra Stratton at a press event.|thumb|Then-[[Downing Street Press Secretary]] [[Allegra Stratton]], who appeared in a leaked video that was part of the controversy, and subsequently resigned.]] [[ITV News]] had been contacted by a Downing Street staff member with a video clip, that had been widely circulated at Number 10, before the ''Daily Mirror'' first broke the Partygate story. That source, frustrated by the government's denials to the ''Mirror'''s story, agreed to let ITV News air the video, which they did on 7 December 2021.<ref name=podcast1 /> In the video, then-[[Downing Street Press Secretary]] [[Allegra Stratton]] and other Downing Street staff – during a mock [[press conference]] on 22 December 2020 – made joking references to a Christmas gathering in 10 Downing Street four days earlier on 18 December 2020.<ref>{{Cite news|date=7 December 2021|title=No 10 staff joke in leaked recording about Christmas party they later denied|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2021-12-07/no-10-staff-joke-in-leaked-recording-about-christmas-party-they-later-denied|access-date=7 December 2021|work=[[ITV News]]|archive-date=7 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211207182129/https://www.itv.com/news/2021-12-07/no-10-staff-joke-in-leaked-recording-about-christmas-party-they-later-denied|url-status=live}}</ref> The leaked 47-second clip<ref>{{cite web|date=7 December 2021|title=Leaked video shows Downing Street staff joking about Christmas party they later denied|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4Ofkm48nv_k|access-date=7 December 2021|publisher=ITV News|archive-date=7 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211207185338/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4Ofkm48nv_k&gl=US&hl=en|url-status=live}}</ref> began with media advisor Ed Oldfield playing the role of a journalist and asking Stratton: "I've just seen reports on Twitter that there was a Downing Street Christmas party on Friday night, do you recognise those reports?" In response, Stratton and other Downing Street staff joked about the "fictional party" being just "[[cheese and wine]]" and a "business meeting", with "no social distancing".<ref name="walker">{{cite news |last1=Walker |first1=Peter |last2=Allegretti |first2=Aubrey |last3=Grierson |first3=Jamie |title=PM accused of lying after No 10 officials caught joking about Christmas party |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/07/leaked-video-shows-no-10-officials-joking-about-holding-christmas-party |access-date=10 December 2021 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=7 December 2021 |archive-date=9 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211209002840/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/07/leaked-video-shows-no-10-officials-joking-about-holding-christmas-party |url-status=live }}</ref>
''[[The Guardian]]'' said that the video gave "the strong impression that a staff-based party took place on 18 December 2020 and that No 10 officials realised that they were likely to have broken rules".<ref name="walker" /> [[BBC News]] reported that the event had "several dozen" attendees, and that "party games were played, food and drink were served, and the party went on past midnight".<ref name="BBC_59505975">{{Cite news |last=Scott |first=Jennifer |date=2 December 2021 |title=Covid bereaved families 'sickened' over No 10 Christmas party |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59505975 |access-date=7 December 2021 |archive-date=6 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211206214205/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59505975 |url-status=live }}</ref> ''[[The Times]]'' reported allegations that the party was organised via [[WhatsApp]] with staff requested to bring [[Secret Santa]] gifts.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Wright|first1=Oliver|date=7 December 2021|title='Downing Street Christmas party was hosted by officials'|work=[[The Times]]|url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/healthcare/article/downing-street-christmas-party-hosted-by-officials-kcqz6kmhp|access-date=9 December 2021|archive-date=8 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211208043848/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/downing-street-christmas-party-hosted-by-officials-kcqz6kmhp|url-status=live}}</ref>
On 8 December, Johnson addressed the [[House of Commons of the United Kingdom|house]] and said he was "furious" to see that video, and that he "apologises unreservedly" for the offence and impression given by it. He said it "shocked" him to see it, as he had had repeated assurances that no party had taken place.<ref name=":20" />
Later on 8 December, during [[Prime Minister's Questions]], Labour MP [[Catherine West]] asked: "Will the Prime Minister tell the House whether there was a party in Downing Street on 13 November?" Johnson replied: "No, but I am sure that whatever happened, the guidance was followed and the rules were followed at all times."<ref name="What Covid rules were broken?">{{cite web |author= |date=12 May 2022 |title=Downing Street parties: What Covid rules were broken? |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59577129 |access-date=4 July 2022 |website=BBC News |archive-date=10 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220710185513/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59577129 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Hansard 8 Dec 21">{{cite web|date=8 December 2021|title=Engagements: Volume 705: debated on Wednesday 8 December 2021|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2021-12-08/debates/45C3B261-14F7-4DDC-A7D8-EB8A76097CFB/Engagements|access-date=4 July 2022|website=UK Parliament: Hansard|archive-date=5 July 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220705190943/https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2021-12-08/debates/45C3B261-14F7-4DDC-A7D8-EB8A76097CFB/Engagements|url-status=live}}</ref> Three hours later, Stratton resigned from her position as government spokesperson for the COP26 summit and apologised for her remarks which she said she would regret "for the rest of [her] days".<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/08/allegra-stratton-leaves-cop26-role-after-no-10-christmas-party-video|work=The Guardian|title=Allegra Stratton resigns after No 10 Christmas party video|last=Walker|first=Peter|date=8 December 2021|access-date=8 December 2021|archive-date=8 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211208154713/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/08/allegra-stratton-leaves-cop26-role-after-no-10-christmas-party-video|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=PMQs: Boris Johnson sorry for offence caused by aides joking about lockdown party |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59580640 |work=BBC News |access-date=8 December 2021 |date=8 December 2021 |archive-date=8 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211208120909/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59580640 |url-status=live }}</ref> ITV News reported in January 2023 that, at the time, staff in Downing Street were "shocked" at the reporting and Johnson's denials. Staff also said that Stratton herself never attended any of the parties. ITV News quoted one staff member as saying Downing Street initially thought that, after Stratton's resignation, the story would be gone within a week.<ref name="podcast2">{{cite podcast |title=Partygate: The Inside Story - new revelations and our whistleblowers in their own words: 2. Gobsmacked |website=ITV News |date=12 January 2023 |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |access-date=3 May 2024 |archive-date=6 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240406105915/https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 8 December, the Metropolitan Police said they had "received a significant amount of correspondence relating to allegations reported in the media" and that this had been "considered by detectives in detail, as well as footage published by ITV News". They concluded that the "correspondence and footage does not provide evidence of a breach of the Health Protection Regulations […] Based on the absence of evidence and in line with our policy not to investigate retrospective breaches of such regulations, the Met will not commence an investigation at this time".<ref>{{Cite news|last=Dodd|first=Vikram|date=8 December 2021|title=Met police say they will not investigate Downing Street Christmas party|work=[[The Guardian]]|url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2021/dec/08/met-police-say-they-will-not-investigate-downing-street-christmas-party|access-date=14 December 2021|archive-date=10 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211210233225/https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2021/dec/08/met-police-say-they-will-not-investigate-downing-street-christmas-party|url-status=live}}</ref>
==== 9 December onwards ==== BBC News reported on 9 December 2021 that [[Jack Doyle (journalist)|Jack Doyle]], the deputy [[Downing Street Director of Communications]] at the time, attended the event on 18 December, gave a speech, and handed out awards at the function.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59601447 |title=Boris Johnson's press chief spoke at No 10 party last year |work=BBC News |date=9 December 2021 |access-date=9 December 2021 |archive-date=9 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211209204223/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59601447 |url-status=live }}</ref> On 10 December, the government's [[Chief Whip]] [[Mark Spencer (British politician)|Mark Spencer]] said that the event had been a "meeting", rather than a social gathering.<ref>{{cite news |last=Parkinson |first=Justin |date=10 December 2021 |title=Downing Street Christmas party cancelled amid fury over 2020 event |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59606793 |access-date=10 December 2021 |archive-date=10 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211210133629/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59606793 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 11 December, reports emerged that two dozen [[HM Treasury]] staff gathered for drinks on 25 November 2020 to celebrate Chancellor [[Rishi Sunak]]'s Autumn Spending Review.<ref>{{cite news |last=McShane |first=Asher |date=11 December 2021 |title=Treasury staff 'had drinks to celebrate spending review during lockdown' |work=LBC |url=https://www.lbc.co.uk/hot-topics/coronavirus/treasury-staff-had-drinks-to-celebrate-spending-review-during-lockdown/ |access-date=12 December 2021 |archive-date=4 May 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240504005242/https://www.lbc.co.uk/hot-topics/covid/treasury-staff-had-drinks-to-celebrate-spending-review-during-lockdown/ |url-status=live }}</ref> On 12 December, Downing Street confirmed that Johnson "briefly" took part in a virtual Christmas quiz held at the venue on 15 December 2020 after the ''[[Sunday Mirror]]'' published a picture of him participating via his computer in the event, in a room with two other people. Downing Street said he was there to thank staff for their work during the pandemic and that the event was a "virtual" one, while Johnson himself responded that he "certainly broke no rules".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59625432 |first=Dulcie |last=Lee |title=PM Boris Johnson took part in No 10 Christmas quiz last year |work=[[BBC News]] |date=12 December 2021 |access-date=12 December 2021 |archive-date=12 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211212000534/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59625432 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Morris |first=Sophie |date=13 December 2021 |title=COVID-19: PM says he 'certainly broke no rules' as pressure over Downing Street parties mounts |work=Sky News |url=https://news.sky.com/story/covid-19-pm-says-he-certainly-broke-no-rules-as-pressure-over-downing-street-parties-mounts-12494642 |access-date=13 December 2021 |archive-date=13 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211213131633/http://news.sky.com/story/covid-19-pm-says-he-certainly-broke-no-rules-as-pressure-over-downing-street-parties-mounts-12494642 |url-status=live }}</ref>
The [[Department for Work and Pensions]] (DWP) also confirmed that staff working past normal employment hours drank alcohol and ate takeaway "late into the night" on a number of occasions during COVID restrictions after this was reported by the ''Sunday Mirror''.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.itv.com/news/anglia/2021-12-12/mp-therese-coffeys-staff-stayed-late-and-drank-alcohol-and-ordered-takeaways | title=MP Therese Coffey's staff stayed late and 'drank alcohol and ordered takeaways' | date=13 December 2021 | access-date=1 September 2022 | archive-date=1 September 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220901114921/https://www.itv.com/news/anglia/2021-12-12/mp-therese-coffeys-staff-stayed-late-and-drank-alcohol-and-ordered-takeaways | url-status=live }}</ref>
[[Northern Ireland First Minister]], [[Paul Givan]], and his deputy [[Michelle O'Neill]], said the controversy had damaged the [[public health]] message in Northern Ireland.<ref name=":0">{{cite news |last1=Young |first1=David |title=Downing Street party row damaging public health message in NI – Stormont leaders |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/downing-street-stormont-first-minister-covid-prime-minister-b1972901.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/downing-street-stormont-first-minister-covid-prime-minister-b1972901.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |work=[[The Independent|Independent]] |date=9 December 2021}}</ref> On 12 December, ''[[The Independent]]'' reported that senior police officers feared people would be less likely to comply with any new restrictions because of the controversy. The news website said they had gathered anecdotal evidence of terse exchanges between police officers and members of the public in early December 2020.<ref>{{Cite news|last= Dearden|first=Lizzie|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/home-news/downing-street-party-covid-law-compliance-b1973651.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/home-news/downing-street-party-covid-law-compliance-b1973651.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Police warning over Covid compliance after Downing Street Christmas party scandal|date=12 December 2021|work=[[The Independent|Independent]]|access-date=12 December 2021}}</ref>
On 14 December, Conservative politician [[Shaun Bailey, Baron Bailey of Paddington|Shaun Bailey]], a member of the [[London Assembly]] and a former candidate for [[Mayor of London]], resigned as chair of the Police and Crime Committee of the Assembly after his attendance at a gathering that was alleged to have breached COVID-19 regulations.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mortimer |first=Josiah |date=14 December 2021 |title=Top London Tory Shaun Bailey quits police role after Christmas party which 'broke Covid rules' |work=MyLondon |url=https://www.mylondon.news/news/zone-1-news/london-covid-top-london-tory-22462463 |access-date=14 November 2022 |archive-date=14 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221114160743/https://www.mylondon.news/news/zone-1-news/london-covid-top-london-tory-22462463 |url-status=live }}</ref> Shortly after his resignation, photographs were published of him and over 20 members of staff at a gathering with drinks and a buffet held in the basement of [[Conservative Campaign Headquarters]] on 14 December 2020, when he was the party's [[2021 London mayoral election|mayoral candidate]]. Four aides on secondment from the party who attended the gathering had been disciplined by the party the week before his resignation, for holding a "raucous" event.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Talora |first=Joe |date=11 January 2022 |title=Shaun Bailey steps down from second Assembly role amid 'partygate' scandal |work=Evening Standard |url=https://uk.news.yahoo.com/shaun-bailey-steps-down-second-145039610.html |access-date=13 November 2022 |via=Yahoo!News |archive-date=12 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221112100251/https://uk.news.yahoo.com/shaun-bailey-steps-down-second-145039610.html |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Cabinet of the United Kingdom|Cabinet]] member [[Grant Shapps]] stated that the event was "absolutely unacceptable", and not authorised by the Conservative Party.<ref name=":22">{{Cite news|last=Sabin|first=Lamiat|url= https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/shaun-bailey-tory-christmas-party-quits-b1976193.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/shaun-bailey-tory-christmas-party-quits-b1976193.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Shaun Bailey quits London Assembly role as photo shows dozens at Tory HQ party |date=15 December 2021|work=[[The Independent|Independent]]|access-date=15 December 2021}}</ref><ref name="bbcbailey">{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-london-59657361|title=Shaun Bailey: Ex London mayor candidate quits over Christmas party|date=15 December 2021|work=BBC News|access-date=15 December 2021|archive-date=14 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211214220615/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-london-59657361|url-status=live}}</ref> The Metropolitan Police later said they would contact two people who were at the party over possible breaches of the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) Regulations.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/london/shaun-bailey-tory-party-hq-lockdown-party-metropolitan-police-b972477.html|title=Police want to interview two people over Shaun Bailey's Tory HQ party|first=John|last=Dunne|date=16 December 2021|work=[[Evening Standard]]|access-date=16 December 2021|archive-date=16 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211216224734/https://www.standard.co.uk/news/london/shaun-bailey-tory-party-hq-lockdown-party-metropolitan-police-b972477.html|url-status=live}}</ref> On 11 November 2022, the police said they would be taking no action over the matter as "the investigation reviewed all the material thoroughly and after careful consideration, it was determined that there was insufficient evidence to disprove the version of events provided by attendees to a standard that would meet the threshold required".<ref name=":23">{{Cite news |date=11 November 2022 |title=Covid lockdown: No police action over Shaun Bailey Tory event |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-63596886 |access-date=11 November 2022 |archive-date=11 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221111143216/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-63596886 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 16 December, a joint investigation by ''The Guardian'' and ''The Independent'' reported allegations that Johnson attended a party in Downing Street on 15 May 2020, during the first national lockdown. Downing Street issued a statement, saying: "On 15 May 2020 the prime minister held a series of meetings throughout the afternoon, including briefly with the then health and care secretary and his team in the garden following a press conference. The prime minister went to his residence shortly after 7pm. A small number of staff required to be in work remained in the Downing Street garden for part of the afternoon and evening".<ref name="auto3">{{Cite news|last1=Walker|first1=Peter|last2=Mason|first2=Rowena|date=16 December 2021|title=Boris Johnson joined No 10 party during May 2020 lockdown, say sources|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2021/dec/16/boris-johnson-joined-no-10-party-during-may-2020-lockdown-say-sources|access-date=16 December 2021|work=[[The Guardian]]|archive-date=16 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211216160404/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2021/dec/16/boris-johnson-joined-no-10-party-during-may-2020-lockdown-say-sources|url-status=live}}</ref> On 19 December, ''The Guardian'' published a photograph of the event showing 19 people drinking wine and noting "there are no laptops, files or notepads to take minutes on show". Johnson was shown sitting next to his then-fiancée, [[Carrie Symonds]], who was holding their newborn son. Downing Street insisted the photograph showed a work meeting.<ref name="guard_20211209">{{cite news |last1=Walker |first1=Peter |title=Chatting over cheese and wine: anatomy of Downing Street lockdown gathering |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/19/chatting-over-cheese-and-wine-anatomy-downing-street-lockdown-gathering-picture |access-date=20 December 2021 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=19 December 2021 |archive-date=11 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220111211855/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/19/chatting-over-cheese-and-wine-anatomy-downing-street-lockdown-gathering-picture |url-status=live }}</ref>
The Metropolitan Police referred itself and their role in the policing and security of the buildings to the [[Independent Office for Police Conduct]] (IOPC), the police watchdog, on 17 December, following a complaint by [[Green Party of England and Wales|Green Party]] peer [[Jenny Jones, Baroness Jones of Moulsecoomb|Jenny Jones]], who said that there was a "conflict of interest and a potential cover up" in relation to the police declining to investigate.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Lyons|first=Izzy|date=21 December 2021|title=Met refers itself to police watchdog over handling of Downing Street Christmas party allegations|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2021/12/21/met-refers-police-watchdog-handling-downing-street-christmas/|url-access=subscription|access-date=23 December 2021|archive-date=23 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211223120217/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2021/12/21/met-refers-police-watchdog-handling-downing-street-christmas/|url-status=live}}</ref>
===2022=== ====Up to 19 January==== In a blog post on 7 January, [[Dominic Cummings]] argued that the photo from 15 May 2020 in the garden of 10 Downing Street did not show a party.<ref name=bbc_cum/> He described having a work meeting with [[Boris Johnson]] and the Prime Minister's [[Principal Private Secretary]] [[Martin Reynolds (civil servant)|Martin Reynolds]], with [[Carrie Symonds]] subsequently joining them.<ref name=guard_cum/><ref name="auto8" /> Meanwhile, according to his account, various other groups of people were also meeting in the garden, and staff had been advised to meet outside in the garden where there was less risk of COVID-19 transmission.<ref name=bbc_cum/>
Cummings said that there was a "socially distanced drinks" gathering in the garden on 20 May 2020 that he and another [[Special adviser (UK)|special adviser]] cautioned against.<ref name=guard_cum>{{Cite news|last=Mason|first=Rowena|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/07/dominic-cummings-says-another-party-was-held-in-no-10-garden-in-lockdown|title=Dominic Cummings makes new claim of party in No 10 garden in lockdown|date=7 January 2022|work=The Guardian|access-date=17 January 2022|archive-date=14 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220114095411/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/07/dominic-cummings-says-another-party-was-held-in-no-10-garden-in-lockdown|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="auto8">{{Cite web|last=Cummings|first=Dominic|date=7 January 2022|title=Parties, photos, trolleys, variants|url=https://dominiccummings.substack.com/p/parties-photos-trolleys-variants|website=Dominic Cummings substack|access-date=10 January 2022|archive-date=10 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220110193408/https://dominiccummings.substack.com/p/parties-photos-trolleys-variants|url-status=live}}</ref> On 10 January, ITV News showed a 20 May 2020 email sent on behalf of Reynolds to over one hundred 10 Downing Street staff, inviting them "to make the most of the lovely weather and have some socially distanced drinks in the No 10 garden this evening (...) [[bring your own booze]]!" Various news organisations reported that around 30–40 people were present that evening, eating picnic food and drinking, including Johnson and Symonds.<ref name="auto7">{{Cite news |last=Brand |first=Paul |date=10 January 2022 |title=Email proves Downing Street staff held drinks party at height of lockdown |work=ITV News |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-10/email-proves-downing-street-staff-held-drinks-party-at-height-of-lockdown |access-date=10 January 2022 |archive-date=20 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220120084744/https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-10/email-proves-downing-street-staff-held-drinks-party-at-height-of-lockdown |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=":21">{{Cite news|url = https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59930733|title = '100 invited' to Downing Street garden lockdown drinks|work = [[BBC News]]|date = 10 January 2022|access-date = 10 January 2022|archive-date = 10 January 2022|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20220110225035/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59930733|url-status = live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|first1=Rowena|last1=Mason|first2=Elgot|last2=Jessica|first3=Robert|last3=Booth|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/10/email-shows-boris-johnsons-official-invited-no-10-staff-to-lockdown-byob-party|title=Email shows Boris Johnson aide invited No 10 staff to lockdown 'BYOB' party|date=10 January 2022|work=The Guardian|access-date=14 January 2022|archive-date=13 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220113091041/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/10/email-shows-boris-johnsons-official-invited-no-10-staff-to-lockdown-byob-party|url-status=live}}</ref> In his 15 January 2022 column in ''The Times'', [[Tim Shipman]] relayed accounts of a meeting of Johnson's team which was held in Downing Street on 11 January, the day after ITV News showed the Reynolds email. He said it was obvious that Johnson was furious with them, and had left them in no doubt that he thought they had let him down. Also that Johnson's view seemed to be "that he is not to blame, that everyone else is to blame" and had asked: "How has all this been allowed to happen? How has it come to this? How haven't you sorted this out?" Shipman wrote that sources present said senior staff "studied the floor". Shipman added that "insiders said Reynolds, his deputy, Stuart Glassborow, Dan Rosenfield, ... and some members of the communications team are likely to be out of a job when a report by the mandarin Sue Gray is published". Shipman added that an MP likened it to [[Harold Macmillan]]'s 1962 [[Night of the Long Knives (1962)|Night of the Long Knives]] (when he sacked a third of his cabinet), "Boris is preparing to lay down the lives of his staff to save his own. It will be the Night of the Long Scapegoats".<ref>{{Cite web|last=Shipman|first=Tim|date=15 January 2022|title=Is Boris Johnson finally sunk?|url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/is-boris-johnson-finally-sunk-t9ndpfwks|website=The Times|access-date=4 February 2022|archive-date=2 February 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220202144514/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/is-boris-johnson-finally-sunk-t9ndpfwks|url-status=live}}</ref> Johnson initially declined to comment on whether he was present or not.<ref>{{Cite news|url = https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/11/tories-ridicule-no-10s-wall-silence-whether-boris-johnson-attended/|title = Tories ridicule No 10's wall of silence on whether Boris Johnson attended lockdown garden party|work = [[The Daily Telegraph]]|date = 11 January 2022|last1 = Riley-Smith|first1 = Ben|last2 = Yorke|first2 = Harry|last3 = Boycott-Owen|first3 = Mason|access-date = 11 January 2022|archive-date = 11 January 2022|archive-url = the prime minister said he still had confidence in Reynolds.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/politics/martin-reynolds-no10-boris-johnson-full-confidence-leaked-email-party-lockdown-b976058.html|title=No10 says it has 'full confidence' in Martin Reynolds despite email|first=David|last=Bond|date=11 January 2022|work=[[Evening Standard]]|access-date=14 January 2022|archive-date=13 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220113020421/https://www.standard.co.uk/news/politics/martin-reynolds-no10-boris-johnson-full-confidence-leaked-email-party-lockdown-b976058.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Campaigners, including the [[Covid-19 Bereaved Families for Justice]] group, called for Reynolds to be dismissed.<ref>{{Cite news|last=McShane|first=Asher|date=11 January 2022|title=Partygate: Calls grow for PM's top aide Martin Reynolds to be sacked|url=https://www.lbc.co.uk/news/partygate-calls-grow-for-pms-top-aide-martin-reynolds-to-be-sacked/|work=[[LBC]]}}</ref>
On 11 January, the Metropolitan Police said it was in contact with the government over "widespread reporting relating to alleged breaches" of COVID rules.<ref name=":21" /> There was additional reporting of a party in Downing Street on 18 December, with Reynolds said to have been present.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/11/pressure-grows-boris-johnsons-private-secretary-amid-claims/|title=Pressure grows on Boris Johnson's private secretary amid claims of another boozy lockdown gathering|first1=Ben|last1=Riley-Smith|first2=Tony|last2=Diver|first3=Harry|last3=Yorke|date=11 January 2022|access-date=14 January 2022|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|archive-date=12 Shaun Bailey resigned as chair of a second London Assembly committee, the economy committee, in addition to his resignation from the police and crime committee in December.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-london-59950101|title=Conservative Christmas party: Shaun Bailey quits second role|work=[[BBC News]]|date=11 January 2022|access-date=12 January 2022}}</ref> Opposition party leaders [[Ed Davey]]<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-59958872|title=As it happened: Boris Johnson apologises for lockdown drinks party|work=[[BBC News]]|date=12 January 2022|access-date=14 January 2022|archive-date=13 January |last=Howarth |first=Angus |url=https://www.scotsman.com/news/politics/nicola-sturgeon-calls-on-boris-johnson-to-resign-over-downing-street-party-3523363 |title=Nicola Sturgeon calls on Boris Johnson On 12 January, Johnson, speaking at [[PMQs]] in the [[House of Commons of the United Kingdom|House of Commons]], said that he had attended the gathering and apologised for doing so.<ref>{{Cite news|date=12 January 2022|title=In full: Boris Johnson's apology over lockdown drinks party|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59969631|access-date=13 January 2022|work=[[BBC News]]}}</ref> He said that he was present for about 25 minutes and that he thought it was an allowed work meeting. He said: "I should have recognised that even if it (the gathering) could be said technically to fall within the guidance, there would be millions and millions of people who simply would not see it that way".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Hughes |first=David |date=12 January 2022 |title=Watch and read Boris Johnson's PMQs apology over the Downing Street party in full |work=i |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/boris-johnson-downing-street-party-what-say-pmqs-prime-minister-apology-full-video-1396434 |access-date=14 January 2022 |archive-date=12 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220112231450/https://inews.co.uk/news/boris-johnson-downing-street-party-what-say-pmqs-prime-minister-apology-full-video-1396434 |url-status=live }}</ref> In response, the [[Leader of the Opposition (United Kingdom)|leader of the opposition]], [[Keir Starmer]], asked if Johnson would now "do the decent thing and resign". Other MPs also called on Johnson to resign,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59967930 |title=PMQs: Boris Johnson faces calls to quit after lockdown party apology |work=[[BBC]] |date=12 January 2022 |access-date=12 January 2022 |archive-date=12 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220112133915/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59967930 Johnson: Scottish Conservative leader Douglas Ross calls for prime minister to resign over Number 10 party row |work=[[Sky News]] |url=https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-scottish-conservative-leader-douglas-ross-calls-for-prime-minister-to-resign-over-number-10-party-row-12514413 |access-date=12 January 2022 |url-status=live }}</ref> On 13 January 2022, 27 of the 31 Conservative Scottish MSPs came out publicly against Johnson.<ref name="msps27">{{Cite news |title=Every Tory MSP who has called for Boris Johnson to quit as party civil war rages |last=Davidson |first=Peter |work=Daily Record |date=13 January 2022 |url=https://www.dailyrecord.co.uk/news/politics/every-tory-msp-who-called-25932498 On 13 January, it was reported that two separate parties were held in 10 Downing Street on 16 April 2021. These were leaving events for [[James Slack]], Johnson's director of communications, and a photographer. The date was the eve of the [[Death and funeral of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh|funeral of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh]], when the UK was observing a period of [[national mourning]], and England was in step two of its lockdown roadmap, with indoor mixing banned. Johnson was out of London at the time.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59989946 |title=Lockdown parties in No 10 on eve of Prince Philip's funeral |work=[[BBC News]] |date=14 January 2022 |access-date=16 January 2022 |archive-date=15 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220115231955/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59989946 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/downing-street-parties-what-were-covid-rules-april-2021-no-10-event-prince-philip-funeral-explained-1400618 |last=Walker |first=Amy |title=Downing Street parties: What the Covid rules were in April 2021 when the latest No 10 event took place |work=inews |date=14 January 2022 |access-date=16 January 2022 |archive-date=4 May 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240504005227/https://inews.co.uk/news/downing-street-parties-what-were-covid-rules-april-2021-no-10-event-prince-philip-funeral-explained-1400618 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="auto9">{{cite news |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/13/two-parties-held-downing-street-queen-country-mourned-death/ |title=Exclusive: Two parties photographer's party reportedly involved loud music, a DJ and a staff member sent to the [[Co-op Food|Co-op]] store on the [[Strand, London|Strand]] to fill a suitcase with bottles of wine.<ref>{{cite news |last=Merrick|first=Rob|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/no10-party-prince-philip-funeral-apology-b1993192.html |access-date=16 January 2022}}</ref> The next day, Slack and Downing Street confirmed there was an event, with Slack apologising for what happened.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/jan/14/boris-johnsons-former-aide-james-slack-apologises-downing-street-party-covid-omicron |title=Boris Johnson's former aide James Slack |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 14 January ''The Independent'' reported that Johnson had drawn up a plan to retain his premiership.<ref name=":24" /> ''The Guardian'' said it was his allies who did the planning.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Sparrow |first=Andrew |date=25 November 2022 |title=Minister becomes eighth Tory MP to announce they are stepping down at next election – as it happened |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/nov/25/steve-barclay-nurses-strike-keir-starmer-nicola-sturgeon-uk-politics-live?page=with:block-6380cbf38f08a40520c817c9 |access-date=5 January 2023 |archive-date=5 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230105111422/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/nov/25/steve-barclay-nurses-strike-keir-starmer-nicola-sturgeon-uk-politics-live?page=with:block-6380cbf38f08a40520c817c9 |url-status=live }}</ref> The plan was said by ''The Independent'' to include a list of officials who should resign over the parties controversy, a communications strategy for cabinet ministers, as well as "sounding out support" for leadership rivals from backbenchers.<ref name=":24">{{cite news |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-downing-street-partygate-b1993433.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-downing-street-partygate-b1993433.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |first=Anna |last=Isaac |title=Operation Save Big Dog: Boris Johnson draws up plan for officials to quit over partygate so he can keep job |work=[[The Independent|Independent]] |date=14 January 2022 |access-date=14 January 2022}}</ref> The ''Daily Mirror'' reported that Downing Street staff had regular Friday evening events with wine, for which a dedicated fridge was bought, being delivered on 11 December 2020. What were called "Wine time Fridays" were scheduled into the diaries of about 50 staff from 4–7pm. Johnson was reported to visit some of these and to be aware of their existence.<ref>{{Cite news |last=James |first=Liam |date=14 January 2022 |title=Downing Street staff 'held regular wine-time Friday' drinks during lockdown |work=[[The Independent|Independent]] |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-downing-street-party-b1993596.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-downing-street-party-b1993596.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref> 10 Downing Street did not deny the allegations.<ref name="auto10">{{Cite news |last=Heffer |first=Greg |date=17 January 2022 |title=Downing Street parties: Number 10 doesn't deny staff held 'wine-time Fridays' throughout COVID restrictions |work=[[Sky News]] |url=https://news.sky.com/story/number-10-doesnt-deny-staff-held-wine-time-fridays-throughout-covid-restrictions-12516198 |access-date=15 January 2022 |archive-date=15 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220115113556/https://news.sky.com/story/number-10-doesnt-deny-staff-held-wine-time-fridays-throughout-covid-restrictions-12516198 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 16 January, in a column in ''The Times'', [[Dominic Lawson]] said a former Downing Street official had told him of at least two people warning Johnson that the 20 May 2020 event should be cancelled and that Johnson said they were "overreacting".<ref>{{Cite news |last=Lawson |first=Dominic |date=15 January 2022 |title=Live by the pint, die by the pint |work=The Times |url=https://www.thetimes.com/business-money/economics/article/live-by-the-pint-die-by-the-pint-dh0dqzftl |url-status=live |url-access=subscription
