news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59577129|title=Downing Street Christmas party: What were the Covid rules at the time?|work=[[BBC News]]|date=18 December 2021|access-date=19 December 2021|archive-date=19 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211219003315/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59577129|url-status=live}}</ref> Covered by Gray investigation; not investigated by the Metropolitan Police.<ref name="grayupdate" /><ref name="bbcpoliceemail" />
=== With London in Tier 2 === [[File:High COVID-19 Alert Level Poster.jpg|thumb|upright=0.75|alt=Poster outlining COVID-19 restrictions|Government poster describing "[[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020|Tier 2]]" social distancing rules, that were in place in London between 2 and 15 December 2020.]] *10 December 2020: Christmas party at the [[Department for Education]]'s café hosted by the [[Secretary of State for Education|Education Secretary]], [[Gavin Williamson]] and attended by the Permanent Secretary, [[Susan Acland-Hood]]; a department spokesman said that "a gathering of colleagues who were already present at the office" had taken place.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Sabin |first1=Lamiat |title=Department for Education admits holding Christmas 'gathering' last December |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/department-education-party-lockdown-covid-b1971685.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/department-education-party-lockdown-covid-b1971685.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=20 December 2021 |work=[[The Independent|Independent]] |date=8 December 2021}}</ref> A spokesperson confirmed that Williamson gave a speech and that "drinks and canapés" were served.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Duncan |first1=Pamela |last2=Gutiérrez |first2=Pablo |last3=Kirk |first3=Ashley |last4=Probyn |first4=Miles |last5=Thomas |first5=Tobi |date=12 April 2022 |title=How No 10's alleged parties took place as UK Covid death toll rose – interactive |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/ng-interactive/2022/jan/14/how-no-10s-alleged-parties-took-place-as-uk-covid-death-toll-rose-interactive |access-date=17 January 2022 |archive-date=17 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220117094100/https://www.theguardian.com/world/ng-interactive/2022/jan/14/how-no-10s-alleged-parties-took-place-as-uk-covid-death-toll-rose-interactive |url-status=live }}</ref> Covered by Gray investigation; not under investigation by the Metropolitan Police as of 10 February 2022.<ref name="grayupdate" /><ref name="bbcpoliceemail" /> *14 December 2020: a gathering of about 23 staff members took place in the basement of the [[Conservative Campaign Headquarters]] in Westminster. A year later, following the publication of allegations that Covid regulations were breached at the gathering, Conservative politician [[Shaun Bailey (AM)|Shaun Bailey]] resigned as chair of a police and crime committee of the London Assembly, with a spokesperson saying it was to prevent the "unauthorised social gathering" from distracting from the committee's work. The Conservative Party took disciplinary action against four of their employees for holding a "raucous" event,<ref name=":22" /><ref name="bbcbailey" /> The police decided in November 2022 that there was not sufficient evidence of the allegation to take any further action over it.<ref name=":23" /> Not covered by Gray investigation. Others present included [[Ben Mallett]] (a close friend of Carrie Johnson and ran [[Zac Goldsmith]]'s 2016 London mayoral campaign), [[Nick Candy]] (a donor to the Conservative party), Jack Smith (parliamentary aide to the Conservative energy minister [[Graham Stuart (politician)|Graham Stuart]]) and Malin Bogue (worked on Johnson's 2019 leadership campaign).<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2023/jun/18/who-was-at-conservative-hq-christmas-party-during-covid-lockdown-partygate-video | title=Who was at Conservative HQ Christmas party during Covid lockdown? | newspaper=The Guardian | date=18 June 2023 | last1=Adu | first1=Aletha | access-date=19 June 2023 | archive-date=19 June 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230619072739/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2023/jun/18/who-was-at-conservative-hq-christmas-party-during-covid-lockdown-partygate-video | url-status=live }}</ref> *15 December 2020: a Christmas quiz took place for 10 Downing Street staff; Johnson took part over a remote connection.<ref name="auto4" /> A Downing Street spokesperson said the event was all held virtually; other sources said teams were sitting together in a room.<ref name="auto5" /><ref name="auto2" /> Covered by Gray investigation; not initially under investigation by the Metropolitan Police (as of 31 January 2022),<ref name="grayupdate" /> but the Metropolitan Police were reviewing this decision in February 2022.<ref name="bbcpoliceemail" />
=== With London in Tier 3 === [[File:Very High COVID-19 Alert Level Poster.jpg|thumb|upright=0.75|alt=Poster outlining COVID-19 restrictions|Government poster describing "[[The Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020|Tier 3]]" social distancing rules, that were in place in London between 16 and 19 December 2020.]] *16 December 2020: Christmas party by staff working for Grant Shapps at the Department of Transport; Shapps, who had not been present, apologised for what happened.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/shapps-transport-christmas-party-lockdown-b1977686.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/shapps-transport-christmas-party-lockdown-b1977686.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Grant Shapps' department admits staff held party during lockdown curbs|date=16 December 2021|work=[[The Independent|Independent]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.leicestermercury.co.uk/news/uk-world-news/grant-shapps-staff-boozed-danced-6363566|title=Grant Shapp's staff 'boozed and danced' during 'lockdown-breaking' office party|first1=Rachel|last1=Wearmouth|first2=Matt|last2=Jackson|date=16 December 2021|work=[[Hull Daily Mail]]|access-date=16 December 2021|archive-date=16 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211216230311/https://www.leicestermercury.co.uk/news/uk-world-news/grant-shapps-staff-boozed-danced-6363566|url-status=live}}</ref> Not covered by Gray investigation. *17 December 2020: [[Simon Case]]'s team had a Christmas party at 5.30pm in the Cabinet Office, 70 Whitehall.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Zeffman |first1=Henry |last2=Grylls |first2=George |date=17 December 2022 |title=Simon Case: Official investigating No 10 parties hosted Christmas drinks |work=[[The Times]] |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/police-question-aides-seen-at-tory-hq-lockdown-party-s3c9zxl0t |access-date=17 December 2021 |archive-date=17 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211217163710/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/police-question-aides-seen-at-tory-hq-lockdown-party-s3c9zxl0t |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="auto4" /><ref name="auto5" /> Sky News reported the event was described as a "Christmas party", that it was an hour long, and involved a quiz with the six people who were already in the office and around six more attending virtually. Case did not take part in the event, but walked through the room.<ref name="skypartylist" /> Covered by Gray investigation.<ref name="grayupdate" /> *17 December 2020: Cabinet Office staff gathered with drinks to mark the departure of Kate Josephs, Director General of the [[Vaccine Taskforce|COVID Taskforce]].<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/14/former-government-head-responsible-covid-rules-held-boozy-leaving/|title=Exclusive: Woman who 'wrote government Covid rules' held 'boozy' party during Christmas restrictions|first=Harry|last=Yorke|date=14 January 2022|work=The Telegraph|access-date=14 January 2022|archive-date=14 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220114162001/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/14/former-government-head-responsible-covid-rules-held-boozy-leaving/|url-status=live}}</ref> Josephs apologised for the event.<ref>{{Cite tweet |last=Josephs|first=Kate|author-link= |user=Katejosephs |number=1482010169693413383 |date=14 January 2022 |title=I am today making a statement. I am truly sorry.|link=}}</ref> [[Paul Scriven]], a former Leader of [[Sheffield City Council]], called for Josephs' resignation from her role as chief executive of Sheffield Council.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-south-yorkshire-60009287 |title=Kate Josephs: Sheffield Council boss 'must stand down' |work=[[BBC News]] |access-date=15 January 2022 |archive-date=15 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220115175004/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-south-yorkshire-60009287 |url-status=live }}</ref> Josephs went on paid leave while her involvement was investigated by a cross-party council committee; their investigation was continuing as of 25 May 2022.<ref name=glive25 /> Covered by Gray investigation.<ref name="grayupdate" /> *17 December 2020: an additional gathering at 10 Downing Street is mentioned in Gray's interim update. The Telegraph reported that this was the leaving party for Boris Johnson's defence adviser [[Captain (Royal Navy)|Captain]] Steve Higham that had been previously reported as occurring in December. Boris Johnson reportedly attended and spoke.<ref name="sky_12530611" /><ref name="Ind_b1994291" /> FPNs were issued for breaches of Paragraph 1 of Schedule 3 to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering in the Tier 3 area consisting of two or more people.<ref name="Partygate live" /> *18 December 2020: Christmas gathering at 10 Downing Street,<ref name="three" /> with music.<ref name=podcast1 /> Sky News described allegations of this being a "cheese and wine night", with sources saying around 40 people were present in an enclosed space,<ref name=podcast1 /> and that it continued until 2am.<ref name="skypartylist" /> During the event, a security alarm was accidentally pressed, leading to a Downing Street custodian arriving, who expressed unhappiness with what was going on. A Metropolitan Police officer subsequently also arrived to investigate the alarm.<ref name=podcast4>{{cite podcast |title=Partygate: The Inside Story - new revelations and our whistleblowers in their own words: 4. The Cover-Up |website=ITV News |date=12 January 2023 |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |access-date=3 May 2024 |archive-date=6 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240406105915/https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |url-status=live }}</ref> Gathering covered by Gray investigation.<ref name="grayupdate" /> FPNs were issued for breaches of Paragraph 1 of Schedule 3 to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering in the Tier 3 area consisting of two or more people.<ref name="Partygate live" />
===During third lockdown in England=== *14 January 2021: A gathering in 10 Downing Street on the departure of two private secretaries. ''The Guardian'' reported sources saying some attending drank [[prosecco]], and that Boris Johnson gave a speech and stayed for about 5 minutes.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Allegretti |first1=Aubrey |last2=Mason |first2=Rowena |last3=Walker |first3=Peter |date=2022-02-01 |title=Boris Johnson attended leaving do during strict January lockdown |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/01/boris-johnson-attended-leaving-do-during-strict-january-lockdown |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077 |archive-date=1 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220201224210/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/01/boris-johnson-attended-leaving-do-during-strict-january-lockdown |url-status=live }}</ref> Downing Street declined to comment on the matter.<ref name="sky_12530611" /> Covered by Gray investigation.<ref name="grayupdate" /> FPNs were issued for breaches of Paragraph 3 of Schedule 3A to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering in the Tier 4 area consisting of two or more people.<ref name="Partygate live" /> *16 April 2021: there were two leaving events (one for James Slack, Boris Johnson's director of communications; one for a personal photographer to Johnson) in different parts of Downing Street, which later merged into one, with about 30 people present. Johnson was not in London at the time.<ref name="auto9" /> Slack (now deputy editor of ''[[The Sun (United Kingdom)|The Sun]]'') apologised for the event on 14 January 2022.<ref>{{Cite news|title=Covid: Ex No 10 staff member sorry for lockdown leaving party|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-59983239|access-date=14 January 2022|work=BBC News|archive-date=14 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220114080133/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-59983239|url-status=live}}</ref> Both parties took place on the evening before the [[funeral of Prince Philip]] on 17 April 2021, and featured alcohol and one of them featured loud music.<ref name="auto9" /><ref>{{Cite news|last1=Walker|first1=Peter|last2=Taylor|first2=Harry|date=13 January 2022|title=Two Downing Street parties held evening before Prince Philip's funeral|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/13/two-downing-street-parties-held-evening-before-prince-philips-funeral-reports|access-date=14 January 2022|work=The Guardian|archive-date=13 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220113235137/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/13/two-downing-street-parties-held-evening-before-prince-philips-funeral-reports|url-status=live}}</ref> ''The Daily Telegraph'' reported partying continued from 6pm until 1am. A child's swing in the garden was broken.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/21/exclusive-taking-turns-wilfs-slide-spilling-wine-office-printer/|title=Exclusive: Taking turns on Wilf's slide and spilling wine on the office printer - new details of Downing Street party revealed|first1=Ben|last1=Riley-Smith|first2=Tony|last2=Diver|work=The Telegraph|date=21 January 2022|access-date=21 January 2022|archive-date=21 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220121165423/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/21/exclusive-taking-turns-wilfs-slide-spilling-wine-office-printer/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=podcast3 /> ITV News reported dancing and at least two couples "getting it on with each other" and "touching each other up", while two other members of staff who were not openly a couple previously were "all over each other".<ref name=itv23jan /><ref name=podcast3 /> Both gatherings were covered by the Cabinet Office investigation.<ref name="grayupdate" /> FPNs were issued for breaches of Paragraph 2 of Schedule 2 to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (Steps) (England) Regulations 2021 – Restriction on participating in an outdoor gathering in the Step 2 area consisting of more than six people.<ref name="Partygate live" />
==Investigations==
=== Cabinet Office inquiry === ====Simon Case==== At Prime Minister's Questions on 8 December, Prime Minister [[Boris Johnson]] apologised for the video of the mock press conference, describing himself as "furious" about it, but maintained that, as far as he knew from senior staff, a party had not taken place. He also said that he had commissioned an investigation into whether any rules had been broken, to be undertaken by [[Cabinet Secretary (United Kingdom)|Cabinet Secretary]] Simon Case.<ref name=":20">{{cite news|last1=Walker|first1=Peter|last2=Mason|first2=Rowena|date=8 December 2021|title=Johnson 'apologises unreservedly' over No 10 Christmas party video|work=[[The Guardian]]|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/08/boris-johnson-apologises-unreservedly-over-no-10-christmas-party-video|access-date=8 December 2021|archive-date=8 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211208121917/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/08/boris-johnson-apologises-unreservedly-over-no-10-christmas-party-video|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|date=8 December 2021|title=Allegra Stratton resigns over No 10 Christmas party video|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59584736|access-date=8 December 2021|work=[[BBC News]]}}</ref> On 9 December, it was announced that the inquiry led by Simon Case would focus on three events, two at 10 Downing Street on 27 November and 18 December 2020, and one at the Department for Education on 10 December 2020.<ref name="three">{{Cite web |last=Parkinson |first=Justin |date=2021-12-09 |title=Three government staff parties to be investigated |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59591610 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=BBC News |language=en-GB |archive-date=27 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230327064253/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59591610 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 17 December 2021, it was announced that Case would no longer lead the inquiry following reports that a party had been held in his own office on 17 December 2020.
====Sue Gray==== [[File:Sue Gray (civil servant).png|thumb|[[Sue Gray, Baroness Gray of Tottenham|Sue Gray]], the civil servant who undertook the inquiry]] The inquiry was taken over by senior civil servant Sue Gray, Second Permanent Secretary in the Cabinet Office.<ref>{{Cite news|date=17 December 2021|title=Top civil servant Simon Case quits No 10 party probe amid rule breach claims|work=[[BBC News]]|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59701369|access-date=17 December 2021|archive-date=17 December 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211217164132/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59701369|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=":1" /> [[Lord Barwell]], former Conservative MP and chief of staff for [[Theresa May]] when she was the prime minister, said, "he could not think of a better person for the role". Opposition politicians, Labour MP [[Chris Bryant]] and SNP MP [[Ian Blackford]], called for the inquiry to be led by a person independent of the government and civil service.<ref name=":1">{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59709414 |title=Downing Street parties inquiry: Opposition MPs call for independent figure to lead probe |work=[[BBC News]] |date=18 December 2021 |access-date=18 December 2021 |archive-date=18 December 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211218102742/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59709414 |url-status=live }}</ref> Alex Thomas, the civil service programme director at the [[Institute for Government]], said that Gray, as a civil servant, is not independent of government.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Frei |first=Matt |date=14 January 2022 |title=Partygate: Sue Gray 'not independent of government', says Institute for Government's Alex Thomas |work=4 News |url=https://www.channel4.com/news/partygate-sue-gray-not-independent-of-government-says-institute-for-governments-alex-thomas |access-date=16 January 2022 |archive-date=16 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220116124857/https://www.channel4.com/news/partygate-sue-gray-not-independent-of-government-says-institute-for-governments-alex-thomas |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Hayward |first=Freddie |date=2022-05-24 |title=Sue Gray: the civil servant who could bring down Boris Johnson |url=https://www.newstatesman.com/politics/2022/05/who-sue-gray-report-could-bring-down-boris-johnson |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=New Statesman |language=en-US |archive-date=27 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230327071250/https://www.newstatesman.com/politics/2022/05/who-sue-gray-report-could-bring-down-boris-johnson |url-status=live }}</ref>
In January 2022, ITV reported that Gray's investigation would also cover the 15 May 2020 photo and Dominic Cummings' allegations of parties on 20 May and 13 November 2020.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web|url = https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-07/dominic-cummings-claim-of-another-rule-breaking-no10-party-to-be-investigated|title = Dominic Cummings' claim of another rule-breaking No10 party to be investigated|date = 7 January 2022|access-date = 10 January 2022|archive-date = 4 May 2024|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20240504005219/https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-07/dominic-cummings-claim-of-another-rule-breaking-no10-party-to-be-investigated|url-status = live}}</ref>
Gray interviewed over 70 individuals.<ref name=grayfindings>{{cite news |title=Sue Gray party report: What are the findings? |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60201752 |work=BBC News |date=31 January 2022 |access-date=1 February 2022 |archive-date=1 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220201062430/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60201752 |url-status=live }}</ref> Gray had reportedly questioned Johnson, by 17 January, about events<ref>{{Cite news |last=Osborne |first=Samuel |date=17 January 2022 |title=Boris Johnson questioned by Sue Gray as part of investigation into partygate allegations - report |work=[[Sky News]] |url=https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-questioned-by-sue-gray-as-part-of-investigation-into-partygate-allegations-report-12518089 |access-date=17 January 2022 |archive-date=17 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220117091150/https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-questioned-by-sue-gray-as-part-of-investigation-into-partygate-allegations-report-12518089 |url-status=live }}</ref> and also Cummings, who insisted on only answering in written form.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mathers |first=Matt |date=23 January 2022 |title=PM will 'come back' from partygate, Raab insists – follow live |work=Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-latest-party-resign-b1998824.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-latest-party-resign-b1998824.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref> Gray also had access to [[swipe card]] and other security data on people's movements in and out of 10 Downing Street,<ref name="auto11">{{Cite news |last=Helm |first=Toby |date=22 January 2022 |title=No 10 staff have swipe card data logged in probe of 'partygate' |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/jan/22/partygate-inquiry-given-access-to-log-of-staff-movements-in-and-out-of-building |access-date=23 January 2022 |archive-date=23 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220123094834/https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/jan/22/partygate-inquiry-given-access-to-log-of-staff-movements-in-and-out-of-building |url-status=live }}</ref> and has talked to police officers who were on duty, whose evidence was described by one source as "extremely damning".<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/23/number-10-police-questioned-sue-gray-downing-street-parties/|title=No 10 police give 'extremely damning' evidence to Sue Gray over 'partygate'|first1=Martin|last1=Evans|first2=Lucy|last2=Fisher|date=23 January 2022|work=The Telegraph|access-date=24 January 2022|archive-date=24 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220124101809/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/23/number-10-police-questioned-sue-gray-downing-street-parties/|url-status=live}}</ref> Sky News reported that Gray had received photos showing Johnson attending events with alcohol.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Boris Johnson: Sue Gray has received photos of parties in Downing Street which show the PM next to wine bottles, Sky News understands |last=Morris |first=Sophie |website=Sky News |date=26 January 2022 |url=https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-sue-gray-has-received-photos-of-parties-in-downing-street-which-show-the-pm-next-to-wine-bottles-sky-news-understands-12525259 |access-date=25 January 2022 |archive-date=25 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220125184621/https://news.sky.com/story/boris-johnson-sue-gray-has-received-photos-of-parties-in-downing-street-which-show-the-pm-next-to-wine-bottles-sky-news-understands-12525259 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Gray's report was initially expected in the week beginning 24 January 2022.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-20/sue-gray-finds-email-warning-against-no10-drinks-party-in-may-2020|title=Sue Gray finds email warning against No10 drinks party in May 2020|date=20 January 2022|work=ITV News|access-date=21 January 2022|archive-date=21 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220121072428/https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-20/sue-gray-finds-email-warning-against-no10-drinks-party-in-may-2020|url-status=live}}</ref> Labour and the [[Liberal Democrats (UK)|Liberal Democrats]] asked for all accompanying evidence, including emails and witness accounts, to be published.<ref name="guardian22/1/2022" /> Deputy Prime Minister [[Dominic Raab]] would not confirm that Gray's report would be published in full, and said that Johnson would decide how much detail would be released publicly.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Walker |first1=Peter |last2=Stewart |first2=Heather |date=2022-01-23 |title=Dominic Raab refuses to confirm full publication of Sue Gray partygate report |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/23/sue-gray-partygate-report-dominic-raab |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077 |archive-date=23 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220123140452/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/23/sue-gray-partygate-report-dominic-raab |url-status=live }}</ref>
====Investigation update==== {{wikisource|Investigation into alleged gatherings on government premises during Covid restrictions: Update}} The Metropolitan Police asked Gray to omit key details from her report of events they were investigating, to avoid any prejudice to their investigation. They later explained that this was so they could collect independent accounts from individuals questioned.<ref name=":25" /> As a result, there was uncertainty over when, or even whether, Gray's report would be published.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-01-28 |title=Downing Street parties: Calls grow for Sue Gray report to be published |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60166997 |access-date=2023-03-27 |archive-date=27 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230327071238/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60166997 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-28/police-asked-sue-gray-to-remove-details-of-parties-under-criminal-investigation|title=Police ask Sue Gray to remove details of parties under criminal investigation|date=28 January 2022|work=ITV News|access-date=28 January 2022|archive-date=28 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220128121214/https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-28/police-asked-sue-gray-to-remove-details-of-parties-under-criminal-investigation|url-status=live}}</ref> Instead of publishing her report, an eleven-page update<ref name="theguardian Read Gray's report">{{cite news |title=Read Sue Gray's report into Downing Street parties |url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/jan/31/sue-gray-report-into-downing-street-parties |access-date=6 February 2022 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=31 January 2022 |archive-date=5 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220205230236/https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/jan/31/sue-gray-report-into-downing-street-parties |url-status=live }}[https://assets.documentcloud.org/documents/21193251/investigation_into_alleged_gatherings_on_government_premises_during_covid_restrictions_-_update.pdf PDF] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240503232702/https://assets.documentcloud.org/documents/21193251/investigation_into_alleged_gatherings_on_government_premises_during_covid_restrictions_-_update.pdf |date=3 May 2024 }}</ref> was delivered first to Johnson and then published later on 31 January 2022.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-01-31 |title=Sue Gray: Initial findings of Downing Street lockdown party probe published |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60195620 |access-date=2023-03-27 |archive-date=27 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230327071250/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60195620 |url-status=live }}</ref> Johnson then made a statement to the Commons.<ref name=grayfindings/>
The update provided a list of 16 gatherings, including some that had not been previously reported in the media,<ref name=":25" /> and said that 12 were under investigation by the Metropolitan Police. Of the 12 under investigation, Gray said that she was "extremely limited in what I can say about those events and it is not possible at present to provide a meaningful report setting out and analysing the extensive factual information I have been able to gather". She had decided not to describe the other four dates, writing, "I do not feel that I am able to do so without detriment to the overall balance of the findings".<ref name="whatsuegray" /><ref name="grayupdate" />
The update concluded that at least some of the gatherings examined did not meet the standards expected of the public at the time. She wrote, "A number of these gatherings should not have been allowed to take place or to develop in the way that they did".<ref name="whatsuegray" /> It was critical of No 10 and the Cabinet Office,<ref name=":25" /><ref name="whatsuegray">{{Cite news |title=What Sue Gray's edited report into alleged lockdown parties says |last=Walker |first=Peter |work=The Guardian |date=31 January 2022 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/31/what-sue-gray-edited-report-into-alleged-lockdown-parties-says |access-date=31 January 2022 |archive-date=31 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220131190601/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/31/what-sue-gray-edited-report-into-alleged-lockdown-parties-says |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=grayfindings/> including over the consumption of alcohol and how some staff felt unable to raise concerns.<ref name="whatsuegray" /> Gray recommended the government address learnings of the update "immediately", rather than wait for the police investigation to conclude.<ref name=grayfindings/>
{{Quote box|align=right|quote='''General findings'''
i. Against the backdrop of the pandemic, when the Government was asking citizens to accept far-reaching restrictions on their lives, some of the behaviour surrounding these gatherings is difficult to justify.
ii. At least some of the gatherings in question represent a serious failure to observe not just the high standards expected of those working at the heart of Government but also of the standards expected of the entire British population at the time.
iii. At times it seems there was too little thought given to what was happening across the country in considering the appropriateness of some of these gatherings, the risks they presented to public health and how they might appear to the public. There were failures of leadership and judgment by different parts of No 10 and the Cabinet Office at different times. Some of the events should not have been allowed to take place. Other events should not have been allowed to develop as they did.
iv. The excessive consumption of alcohol is not appropriate in a professional workplace at any time. Steps must be taken to ensure that every Government Department has a clear and robust policy in place covering the consumption of alcohol in the workplace.
v. The use of the garden at No 10 Downing Street should be primarily for the Prime Minister and the private residents of No 10 and No 11 Downing Street. During the pandemic it was often used as an extension of the workplace as a more covid secure means of holding group meetings in a ventilated space. This was a sensible measure that staff appreciated, but the garden was also used for gatherings without clear authorisation or oversight. This was not appropriate. Any official access to the space, including for meetings, should be by invitation only and in a controlled environment.
vi. Some staff wanted to raise concerns about behaviours they witnessed at work but at times felt unable to do so. No member of staff should feel unable to report or challenge poor conduct where they witness it. There should be easier ways for staff to raise such concerns informally, outside of the line management chain.
vii. The number of staff working in No 10 Downing Street has steadily increased in recent years. In terms of size, scale and range of responsibility it is now more akin to a small Government Department than purely a dedicated Prime Minister's office. The structures that support the smooth operation of Downing Street, however, have not evolved sufficiently to meet the demands of this expansion. The leadership structures are fragmented and complicated and this has sometimes led to the blurring of lines of accountability. Too much responsibility and expectation is placed on the senior official whose principal function is the direct support of the Prime Minister. This should be addressed as a matter of priority. |source=Sue Gray, "Investigation into alleged gatherings on government premises during Covid restrictions: Update"<ref name=grayupdate>{{Cite web|title=Investigation into alleged gatherings on government premises during Covid restrictions: Update|url=https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/investigation-into-alleged-gatherings-on-government-premises-during-covid-restrictions-update|date=2022-01-31|publisher=UK Government|access-date=31 January 2022|archive-date=7 June 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220607094205/https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/investigation-into-alleged-gatherings-on-government-premises-during-covid-restrictions-update|url-status=live}} Text available under [http://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/version/3/ Open Government Licence v3.0] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170628175632/https://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/version/3/ |date=28 June 2017 }}</ref>}} [[File:Official portrait of Rt Hon Ian Blackford MP crop 2.jpg|thumb|281x281px|SNP MP [[Ian Blackford]] was ordered to withdraw from the House of Commons after he repeatedly stated that Johnson had misled the House.]] In response, Johnson said he would create a new [[Prime Minister's Office (United Kingdom)|Office of the Prime Minister]], review the [[Civil Service (United Kingdom)#Civil Service Code|Civil Service code of conduct]], and bring in other measures.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-01-31 |title=Sue Gray: Failure of leadership over Downing Street lockdown parties |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60203287 |access-date=2023-03-27 |archive-date=27 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230327071253/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60203287 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the ensuing Commons debate, SNP MP [[Ian Blackford]] was [[Suspension from the UK parliament|ordered to withdraw from the House of Commons]] for the remainder of the sitting day, by the [[Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)|Speaker]], [[Lindsay Hoyle]], after he repeatedly stated that Johnson had misled the House and refused to qualify his remarks to state that the misleading was "inadvertent".<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/ian-blackford-ejected-commons-boris-b2004309.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/ian-blackford-ejected-commons-boris-b2004309.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=SNP's Ian Blackford ejected from parliament for saying Boris Johnson misled MPs|last=Stone|first=Jon|work=Independent|date=31 January 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.thenational.scot/news/19888008.sue-gray-report-ian-blackford-kicked-house-commons-boris-johnson-criticism/|title=Ian Blackford KICKED OUT of House of Commons after Boris Johnson criticism|last=Webster|first=Laura|date=31 January 2022|work=The National|access-date=31 January 2022|archive-date=31 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220131161158/https://www.thenational.scot/news/19888008.sue-gray-report-ian-blackford-kicked-house-commons-boris-johnson-criticism/|url-status=live}}</ref>
==== Alleged lobbying before the final report was published ==== On 20 May 2022, Sky News reported that, some time before 21 April, Gray had a meeting with Johnson.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Coates |first=Sam |date=21 May 2022 |title=Boris Johnson under pressure to 'urgently explain' why he privately met Sue Gray to discuss handling of partygate report |url=https://news.sky.com/story/sue-gray-and-boris-johnson-had-private-meeting-to-discuss-handling-of-partygate-report-sky-news-understands-12617829 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=Sky News |language=en |archive-date=21 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220521125251/https://news.sky.com/story/sue-gray-and-boris-johnson-had-private-meeting-to-discuss-handling-of-partygate-report-sky-news-understands-12617829 |url-status=live }}</ref> There was initial confusion as to who asked for the meeting, with Conservative sources saying Gray called the meeting and Gray's sources saying Downing Street had. Later, BBC News said that the original idea came from Downing Street, and Gray then sent the calendar invite for the meeting.<ref name=":11">{{Cite news |last=Cooney |first=Christy |date=22 May 2022 |title=PM urged to explain Sue Gray partygate meeting |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61531971 |access-date=21 May 2022 |archive-date=21 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220521174706/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61531971 |url-status=live }}</ref> It is not clear what was discussed. It was reported that a senior government source said they had talked about whether to include photographs in the report; this was disputed by a spokesperson for Gray.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Coates |first=Sam |date=21 May 2022 |title=Partygate: Sue Gray's team reject claim she initiated meeting with Boris Johnson over report |url=https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-sue-grays-team-reject-claim-she-initiated-meeting-with-pm-over-report-12618116 |website=Sky News |quote=The briefing war will deepen suspicions about the purpose of this the meeting, revealed days before the final report is due to be published. |access-date=21 May 2022 |archive-date=21 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220521125251/https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-sue-grays-team-reject-claim-she-initiated-meeting-with-pm-over-report-12618116 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Cooney |first=Christy |date=21 May 2022 |title=PM urged to explain Sue Gray partygate meeting |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-61531971 |access-date=21 May 2022 |archive-date=21 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220521174724/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-61531971 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=":11" /> The ''Times'' reported two Whitehall sources saying Johnson suggested that Gray abandon plans to publish her report given the police investigation into events.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Swinford |first1=Steven |last2=Wright |first2=Oliver |date=24 May 2022 |title=PM called Sue Gray to meeting and suggested she abandon report |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/pm-called-sue-gray-to-meeting-and-suggested-she-abandon-report-p830t7szs |access-date=24 May 2022 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20220523233104/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/pm-called-sue-gray-to-meeting-and-suggested-she-abandon-report-p830t7szs |archive-date=23 May 2022 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Downing Street also said that Johnson and Gray had had a previous meeting around the time her interim report was released.<ref name="downingstrequested">{{cite news |last1=Rodger |first1=Hannah |date=23 May 2022 |title=Downing Street requested Johnson-Gray meeting but will not publish minutes |work=The Herald |url=https://www.heraldscotland.com/politics/20158732.downing-street-requested-johnson-gray-meeting-will-not-publish-minutes/ |access-date=24 May 2022 |archive-date=23 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220523221145/https://www.heraldscotland.com/politics/20158732.downing-street-requested-johnson-gray-meeting-will-not-publish-minutes/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
The ''Guardian'' reported allegations that senior civil servants lobbied Gray to leave out some names and some details of the report and that Gray insisted she would not do so unless issued with an instruction.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Badshah |first=Nadeem |date=2022-05-28 |title=Downing Street accused of trying to 'dilute' Sue Gray's Partygate report |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/may/29/downing-street-reportedly-tried-to-get-partygate-report-diluted |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077 |archive-date=29 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529091048/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/may/29/downing-street-reportedly-tried-to-get-partygate-report-diluted |url-status=live }}</ref>
''The Times'' said that partial drafts of the final report were circulating at Number 10 the day before publication and that three senior civil servants, including Case, lobbied for changes. One alleged change reported by ''The Times'' was the removal of details of the 13 November 2020 gathering in the Johnsons' flat. Downing Street denied this.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/muzzled-how-senior-officials-fought-to-water-down-sue-gray-report-vrw5xk0t6 |title=Muzzled? How senior officials fought to water down Sue Gray report |first1=Tim |last1=Shipman |first2=Caroline |last2=Wheeler |first3=Harry |last3=Yorke |work=The Times |access-date=29 May 2022 |archive-date=29 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529071632/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/muzzled-how-senior-officials-fought-to-water-down-sue-gray-report-vrw5xk0t6 |url-status=live }}</ref> Asked whether Number 10 had in any way influenced the report, [[Northern Ireland Secretary]] [[Brandon Lewis]] said he was "absolutely confident" that was not the case.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Craig |first1=Jon |last2=Culbertson |first2=Alix |date=29 May 2022 |title=Partygate: Minister 'absolutely confident' nobody in No 10 pressured Sue Gray to change report |url=https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-boris-johnson-faces-despot-charge-over-cover-up-claims-12623349 |access-date=29 May 2022 |website=Sky News |archive-date=28 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220528224750/https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-boris-johnson-faces-despot-charge-over-cover-up-claims-12623349 |url-status=live }}</ref>
====Final report==== {{wikisource|Findings of Second Permanent Secretary's Investigation Into Alleged Gatherings on Government Premises During Covid Restrictions}} The police announced that their investigation had been completed on 19 May 2022, and Gray's final report was expected to follow soon after.<ref name=":9" /><ref name="Partygate live"/> Individuals named in the report were given the opportunity to raise an objection, with the deadline to do that set as 5 pm on 22 May.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Cooney |first1=Christy |last2=Parkinson |first2=Justin |date=22 May 2022 |title=Deadline passes for officials set to be named by Sue Gray |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61539326 |access-date=25 May 2022 |archive-date=24 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220524015934/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61539326 |url-status=live }}</ref>
The final report was published and delivered to the prime minister on 25 May 2022.<ref>{{Cite news |date=25 May 2022 |title=Partygate: No 10 braced as Sue Gray's report into lockdown parties is delivered |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61573531 |access-date=30 May 2022 |archive-date=30 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220530083836/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61573531 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=grayfinal /> The 60-page report of the investigation covered 16 different events which occurred on 12 different dates between May 2020 and April 2021.<ref name=":14">{{Cite news |last=Walker |first=Peter |date=25 May 2022 |title=Sue Gray report: full breakdown of findings about No 10 parties |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/may/25/sue-gray-report-full-breakdown-findings-no-10-parties |access-date=30 May 2022 |archive-date=30 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220530194636/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/may/25/sue-gray-report-full-breakdown-findings-no-10-parties |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name=":15">{{Cite news |date=26 May 2022 |title=Sue Gray partygate report: The key findings at a glance |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61578618 |access-date=25 May 2022 |archive-date=25 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220525164315/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61578618 |url-status=live }}</ref> Each of these events occurred whilst Covid lockdown regulations were in force and involved gatherings of people. Eight of the events were attended by the prime minister.<ref name=":14" /> The investigation found that many of the events breached Covid regulations,<ref name=":14" /><ref name=":17">{{Cite news |last=Gutteridge |first=Nick |date=26 May 2022 |title=Key points: Sue Gray's final report on Boris Johnson and partygate |work=The Telegraph |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/25/sue-gray-report-key-findings-points-summary-partygate-boris/ |access-date=30 May 2022 |archive-date=30 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220530205024/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/25/sue-gray-report-key-findings-points-summary-partygate-boris/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and that multiple breaches of the regulations had occurred.<ref name=":15" /> It was also found that there were repeated instances of staff "flouting" the regulations and the investigation gave an extremely critical assessment of the drinking culture in Downing Street,<ref name=":17" /> and that several of the events appeared notably drunken and rowdy,<ref name=":14" /> and that some went on into the early hours.<ref name=":18">{{Cite news |last=Wright |first=Oliver |date=25 May 2022 |title=What are the key findings from Sue Gray's partygate report? |work=The Times |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/sue-gray-report-summary-meaning-key-points-tkzhqzbk9 |url-access=subscription |access-date=30 May 2022 |archive-date=31 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220531003205/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/sue-gray-report-summary-meaning-key-points-tkzhqzbk9 |url-status=live }}</ref> Gray said she was disappointed that some of the events may never have been known about if they had not been reported by the media.<ref name=":16">{{Cite news |last=Keay |first=Lara |date=26 May 2022 |title=Sue Gray report key findings: Karaoke machine, drunkenness and panic button triggered |work=Sky News |url=https://news.sky.com/story/sue-gray-report-all-the-key-criticisms-of-the-government-12621059 |access-date=30 May 2022 |archive-date=26 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220526222346/https://news.sky.com/story/sue-gray-report-all-the-key-criticisms-of-the-government-12621059 |url-status=live }}</ref>
The culture and leadership in Downing Street was criticised,<ref name=":15" /> and it said senior leadership must bear responsibility for it.<ref name=":16" /> There was said to be a failure of leadership and judgment in No 10 and the Cabinet Office, with some of the events being attended by leaders, and many of which should not have been allowed to happen.<ref name=":14" /> It said that the officials organising events knew that what they were doing was wrong,<ref name=":18" /> and that they renamed events to make them sound less like parties.<ref name=":18" /> Staff emails and messages were analysed and showed regular planning of social events,<ref name=":14" /> some officials trying to warn that events were a bad idea,<ref name=":14" /> a warning sent that bring-your-own-booze garden event could be a "comms risk",<ref name=":16" /> and a Reynold's email that said he "got away" with the bring-your-own-booze event.<ref name=":16" />
The lack of respect for security staff and cleaners was also a concern as they were often treated in an unacceptable way,<ref name=":14" /> with Downing Street staff routinely being rude to them.<ref name=":18" /> Some said they were fearful of raising concerns,<ref name=":16" /> and junior staff thought it was reasonable to attend events as bosses seemed to be condoning them.<ref name=":17" /> It said too that Gray was "encouraged" that there had already been changes made to simplify the process of raising directly with the permanent secretary.<ref name=":17" />
{{Quote box | align = center | quote = '''Conclusions''' The general findings set out in my update of 31st January 2022 still stand.
Whatever the initial intent, what took place at many of these gatherings and the way in which they developed was not in line with Covid guidance at the time. Even allowing for the extraordinary pressures officials and advisers were under, the factual findings of this report illustrate some attitudes and behaviours inconsistent with that guidance. It is also clear, from the outcome of the police investigation, that a large number of individuals (83) who attended these events breached Covid regulations and therefore Covid guidance.
I have already commented in my update on what I found to be failures of leadership and judgment in No 10 and the Cabinet Office. The events that I investigated were attended by leaders in government. Many of these events should not have been allowed to happen. It is also the case that some of the more junior civil servants believed that their involvement in some of these events was permitted given the attendance of senior leaders. The senior leadership at the centre, both political and official, must bear responsibility for this culture.
In my update I made a number of general limited findings, I am pleased progress is being made in addressing the issues I raised. I commented on the fragmentary and complicated leadership structures in No 10. Since my update there have been changes to the organisation and management of Downing Street and the Cabinet Office with the aim of creating clearer lines of leadership and accountability and now these need the chance and time to bed in.
I found that some staff had witnessed or been subjected to behaviours at work which they had felt concerned about but at times felt unable to raise properly. I was made aware of multiple examples of a lack of respect and poor treatment of security and cleaning staff. This was unacceptable. I am reassured to see that steps have since been taken to introduce more easily accessible means by which to raise concerns electronically, in person or online, including directly with the Permanent Secretary in No 10. I hope that this will truly embed a culture that welcomes and creates opportunities for challenge and speaking up at all levels.
I also made a recommendation that steps should be taken to ensure that every Government Department has a clear and robust policy in place covering the consumption of alcohol in the workplace. Since then guidance has been issued to all Government Departments.
The matter of what disciplinary action should now take place is outside of the scope of this report and is for others to consider. Nothing set out in this report can be taken as constituting a disciplinary investigation or findings of fact appropriate for such a purpose. However, I do offer a reflection: while there is no excuse for some of the behaviour set out here it is important to acknowledge that those in the most junior positions attended gatherings at which their seniors were present, or indeed organised. I have no doubt that they will have taken the learning from this experience and, while this is not a matter for me, I hope this will be taken into account in considering any disciplinary action.
Many will be dismayed that behaviour of this kind took place on this scale at the heart of Government. The public have a right to expect the very highest standards of behaviour in such places and clearly what happened fell well short of this. It is my firm belief, however, that these events did not reflect the prevailing culture in Government and the Civil Service at the time. Many thousands of people up and down the country worked tirelessly to deliver in unprecedented times. I remain immensely proud to be a civil servant and of the work of the service and the wider public sector during the pandemic. | source = Sue Gray, "Findings of Second Permanent Secretary's Investigation into Alleged Gatherings on Government Premises During Covid Restrictions"<ref name=grayfinal>{{Cite web|title=Findings of Second Permanent Secretary's Investigation into Alleged Gatherings on Government Premises During Covid Restrictions|url=https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/1078404/2022-05-25_FINAL_FINDINGS_OF_SECOND_PERMANENT_SECRETARY_INTO_ALLEGED_GATHERINGS.pdf|date=2022-05-25|publisher=UK Government|access-date=25 May 2022|archive-date=25 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220525103046/https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/1078404/2022-05-25_FINAL_FINDINGS_OF_SECOND_PERMANENT_SECRETARY_INTO_ALLEGED_GATHERINGS.pdf|url-status=live}} Text available under [http://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/version/3/ Open Government Licence v3.0] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170628175632/https://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/version/3/ |date=28 June 2017 }}</ref> }} [[File:Sue Gray report Fig 4.jpg|thumb|An image of the 19 June 2020 gathering in the Cabinet Room in No 10 Downing Street on the Prime Minister's birthday, taken from Gray's final report]] The report included a few photographs of the gathering.<ref name=":15" /> There were eight covering two of the events: the prime minister's birthday event in June 2020, and a leaving event in November 2020.<ref name=":14" /> More than 300 photographs had been submitted.<ref name=":17" />
The names of only 15 of the attendees were given, the rest remained anonymous.<ref name=":17" /> Gray explained that she decided to name only the most senior staff, those "who knew about and/or attended an event" or were involved in the organisation of one, adding that was "given their wider responsibility for the leadership and culture of the departments, subject to some limited exceptions based on personal circumstances".<ref name=":17" /> Boris Johnson, the prime minister was named, as were senior officials Simon Case, and [[Martin Reynolds (civil servant)|Martin Reynolds]] who, it was said, appeared to be particularly involved in planning events.<ref name=":14" /> Dominic Cummings was also said to have been at at least one event, but it was said that evidence to back his claims that he warned against them could not be found.<ref name=":18" />
Gray chose not to investigate allegations of a party in flat on 13 November 2020,<ref name=":14" /> and did not fully investigate that "ABBA party".<ref name=":18" /><ref name=":17" />
Each of the events covered was individually described, and details of the regulations in force at the time, what happened and who attended was also given.<ref name=":15" /> At a socially-distanced drinks event on 20 May 2020,<ref name=":16" /> staff were warned not to "wave around" bottles of wine.<ref name=":16" /> At a leaving event on 18 June 2020, there was karaoke, there was pizza and prosecco, there was excessive alcohol consumption, someone vomited, there was a minor altercation, staff stayed beyond 3am and the cabinet secretary allowed his office to be used for this event.<ref name=":14" /><ref name=":16" /><ref name=":17" /> There were two leaving events on 14 April 2021 which merged in the No 10 garden, with drunkenness and people leaving after 4am.<ref name=":14" /> With an event on 16 April, the last person left at 4:20am.<ref name=":16" /><ref name=":17" /> At the 15 December Christmas quiz,<ref name=":16" /> staff were told to leave by back door because of drunkenness.<ref name=":16" /> At a Christmas party in December 2020, red wine was spilt on a wall and on stationery supplies,<ref name=":14" /> and at a 18 December 2020 event, a panic button was triggered and police turned up.<ref name=":16" /><ref name=":17" />
=== Police investigation === A police inquiry, called Operation Hillman, was started into 12 gatherings on eight different days,<ref name="bbcpoliceemail" /> seven of which [[Boris Johnson]] was alleged to have attended.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-calls-lawyers-handle-26220312|title=Boris Johnson hires hot-shot private lawyers to handle police's party questions|first=Aletha|last=Adu|date=13 February 2022|work=Daily Mirror|access-date=18 February 2022|archive-date=14 February 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220214163017/https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-calls-lawyers-handle-26220312|url-status=live}}</ref> As of 10 February 2022, the inquiry was led by Commander Catherine Roper.<ref>"'Partygate' commander", ''Camden New Journal'', 10 February 2022, p. 17</ref>
On 24 January 2022, the Metropolitan Police contacted the Cabinet Office asking for all relevant information from the Gray inquiry.<ref name=sgrp /> On 25 January they announced the launch of their investigation into potential breaches of COVID regulations in Whitehall and Downing Street during the pandemic.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Police to investigate Downing Street lockdown parties |author=<!--not stated--> |website=BBC News |date=25 January 2022 |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60123850 |access-date=25 January 2022 |archive-date=25 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220125101652/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60123850 |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Cressida Dick]], the [[Metropolitan Police Commissioner]], said though they would not normally investigate breaches so far back, such investigations happened where there was evidence of "serious and flagrant breach" of regulations. The threshold criteria were "that those involved knew or ought to have known that what they were doing was an offence;" that "not investigating would significantly undermine the legitimacy of the law", and "there was little ambiguity around the absence of any reasonable defence".<ref>{{Cite news|date=27 January 2022|title=Boris Johnson in Commons fight as No 10 parties report looms|work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60135309|access-date=3 May 2024|archive-date=21 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621084845/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60135309|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="criminal investigation 25 Jan 22">{{cite web | last1=Bentham | first1=Martin | last2=Talora | first2=Joe | last3=Burford | first3=Rachael | last4=Cecil | first4=Nicholas | last5=Bond | first5=David | title=Metropolitan Police launch criminal investigation into Downing Street parties | website=Evening Standard | date=25 January 2022 | url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/london/metropolitan-police-downing-street-parties-cressida-dick-b978615.html | access-date=13 June 2022 | archive-date=13 June 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220613055521/https://www.standard.co.uk/news/london/metropolitan-police-downing-street-parties-cressida-dick-b978615.html | url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="What is Beergate 8 May" /> Sky News reported that the Metropolitan Police did not object to Gray's inquiry being released before its investigation and that Gray was in communication with the police.<ref name="sgrp">{{Cite news|last1=McGuinness|first1=Alan|last2=Morris|first2=Sophie|date=25 January 2022|title=Sue Gray report: Partygate inquiry could be released in full in coming days despite police investigation|work=Sky News|url=https://news.sky.com/story/sue-gray-report-partygate-inquiry-could-be-released-in-full-in-coming-days-despite-police-investigation-12525162|access-date=25 January 2022|archive-date=25 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220125183819/https://news.sky.com/story/sue-gray-report-partygate-inquiry-could-be-released-in-full-in-coming-days-despite-police-investigation-12525162|url-status=live}}</ref> The police investigation had more than 500 pages of documents and more than 300 images.<ref name=":25" />
On 31 January, it was announced that the police were not planning to name anyone given an FPN in relation to their investigation.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-party-sue-gray-report-b2004983.html?r=71828|title=No 10 may not announce if Boris Johnson fined by police over Partygate investigation|work=Independent|date=February 2022|access-date=3 May 2024|archive-date=21 June 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230621085231/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-party-sue-gray-report-b2004983.html?r=71828|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=Public may not be told if Johnson fined for partygate Covid rule breaches |last=Hughes |first=David |work=Evening Standard |date=1 February 2022 |url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/uk/boris-johnson-dominic-raab-downing-street-keir-starmer-prime-minister-b980009.html |access-date=10 February 2022 |archive-date=10 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220210211741/https://www.standard.co.uk/news/uk/boris-johnson-dominic-raab-downing-street-keir-starmer-prime-minister-b980009.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The police said they would announce the total number of penalties issued and what they were issued for. After some initial confusion, the Government said it would publish "everything we can", including if Johnson<ref name=allwecan/> or the Cabinet Secretary was given one.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Kuenssberg |first=Laura |date=29 March 2022 |title=Police fines yet to pull rug out from under Boris Johnson |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60922399 |access-date=30 March 2022 |archive-date=30 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220330105223/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60922399 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In early February, the police said they would email a questionnaire to up to 90 people alleged to have been present at events,<ref>{{Cite news |title=Lockdown parties are part of work, Boris Johnson will tell police |last1=Swinford |first1=Steven |last2=Grylls |first2=George |work=The Times |date=14 February 2022 |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/lockdown-parties-are-part-of-work-boris-johnson-will-tell-police-chwdtms7g |url-access=subscription |access-date=18 February 2022 |archive-date=18 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220218221106/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/lockdown-parties-are-part-of-work-boris-johnson-will-tell-police-chwdtms7g |url-status=live }}</ref> including Johnson and also, it was expected, his wife Carrie.<ref name="bbcpoliceemail" /> Rishi Sunak, Simon Case and Martin Reynolds were also sent questionnaires.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Bloom |first=Dan |date=24 February 2022 |title=Rishi Sunak issued with questions by Met Police about involvement in Partygate |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/rishi-sunak-issued-questions-met-26324022 |website=[[Daily Mirror]] |access-date=24 February 2022 |archive-date=24 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220224225141/https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/rishi-sunak-issued-questions-met-26324022 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="g_20220328">{{Cite news |last1=Allegretti |first1=Aubrey |last2=Elgot |first2=Jessica |date=28 March 2022 |title=No 10 lockdown breaches: Met police expected to issue first fines |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/mar/28/no-10-lockdown-breaches-met-police-expected-to-issue-first-fines |access-date=3 May 2024 |archive-date=23 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230623192122/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/mar/28/no-10-lockdown-breaches-met-police-expected-to-issue-first-fines |url-status=live }}</ref> On 11 February 2022, 10 Downing Street confirmed that Johnson had received a questionnaire, which must be responded to within seven days. The police said it asked what happened and "must be answered truthfully". Such questionnaires have the same status as information given in an interview under caution.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-60356373 |title=Boris Johnson sent Downing Street lockdown party questionnaire by police |work=BBC News |date=12 February 2022 |access-date=12 February 2022 |archive-date=12 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220212135704/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-60356373 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Stewart |first=Heather |date=2022-02-22 |title=Leak confirms Partygate questionnaire includes police caution |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/22/leak-confirms-partygate-questionnaire-includes-police-caution |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077 |archive-date=22 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220222200358/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/22/leak-confirms-partygate-questionnaire-includes-police-caution |url-status=live }}</ref> Johnson was thus the first British Prime Minister to have been asked questions under caution.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Elgot |first1=Jessica |last2=Allegretti |first2=Aubrey |date=2022-02-23 |title=Johnson tells ministers he wants to give his side of story over parties |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/23/johnson-parties-met-investigation |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077 |archive-date=24 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220224110342/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/23/johnson-parties-met-investigation |url-status=live }}</ref> Questions included:
*"Did you participate in a gathering on a specific date?" *"What was the purpose of your participation in that gathering?" *"Did you interact with, or undertake any activity with, other persons present at the gathering? If yes, please provide details". *"What, if any, lawful exception applied to the gathering and/or what reasonable excuse did you have for participating in the gathering?"
Also covered was what times someone attended an event and how many others were present.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Craig |first=Jon |date=23 February 2022 |title=Downing Street parties: No taxpayers' money was spent on food or drink consumed at partygate events being probed, No 10 insists |url=https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-no-taxpayers-money-was-spent-on-food-or-drink-consumed-at-partygate-events-being-probed-no-10-insists-12549054 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=Sky News |language=en |archive-date=23 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220223085136/https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-no-taxpayers-money-was-spent-on-food-or-drink-consumed-at-partygate-events-being-probed-no-10-insists-12549054 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Gray made her interview notes with staff available to those sent questionnaires by the police.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Singh |first=Arj |date=18 February 2022 |title=New Sue Gray evidence decision 'may give impression of cover up' |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/downing-street-parties-sue-gray-decision-to-allow-staff-to-see-evidence-may-give-impression-of-cover-up-1470448 |access-date=18 February 2022 |website=inews.co.uk |archive-date=18 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220218182112/https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/downing-street-parties-sue-gray-decision-to-allow-staff-to-see-evidence-may-give-impression-of-cover-up-1470448 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 21 March 2022, the police announced that they had sent out more than 100 questionnaires and had started to interview witnesses.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-03-21 |title=Police send over 100 questionnaires on No 10 parties |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60827451 |access-date=2023-03-27 |archive-date=27 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230327071251/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60827451 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 21 April 2022, it was reported that the police would not provide any further updates on their investigation until after 5 May. This was because local elections were due on that date and guidance from the [[National Police Chiefs' Council]] to avoid communications that might influence the outcome of elections.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Evans |first=Martin |date=21 April 2022 |title=Exclusive: No updates on 'partygate' fines until after May local elections |work=The Telegraph |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/04/21/exclusive-no-downing-street-party-fines-issued-may-local-elections/ |via=www.telegraph.co.uk |access-date=21 April 2022 |archive-date=21 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220421174017/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/04/21/exclusive-no-downing-street-party-fines-issued-may-local-elections/ |url-status=live }}</ref> On 23 April 2022, however, it was announced that further FPNs had been issued to some of those who had attended the drinks party in the Downing Street garden in May 2020. Johnson was not among those receiving one.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Lee |first=Dulcie |date=23 April 2022 |title=Partygate: PM has not received any further fines - No 10 |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61201166 |access-date=24 April 2022 |archive-date=24 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220424132803/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61201166 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web | url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-04-22/no-10-staff-receive-fines-for-byob-party-pm-attended-sources-tell-itv-news | title=No 10 officials receive fines for BYOB party PM attended, sources tell ITV News | date=22 April 2022 | access-date=24 April 2022 | archive-date=24 April 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220424102802/https://www.itv.com/news/2022-04-22/no-10-staff-receive-fines-for-byob-party-pm-attended-sources-tell-itv-news | url-status=live }}</ref> More questionnaires were sent out in early May 2022.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Coates |first=Sam |date=12 May 2022 |title=Downing Street parties: Political rigor mortis may be taking hold in Whitehall as day of reckoning looms |url=https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-political-rigor-mortis-may-be-taking-hold-in-whitehall-as-day-of-reckoning-looms-12611637 |website=Sky News |access-date=15 May 2022 |archive-date=15 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220515122018/https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-political-rigor-mortis-may-be-taking-hold-in-whitehall-as-day-of-reckoning-looms-12611637 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 19 May 2022, the police said that they had completed their investigations and concluded that offences were committed on 8 dates.<ref name=":9">{{Cite news |date=19 May 2022 |title=Met Police end Partygate inquiry with more fines |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61508110 |access-date=19 May 2022 |archive-date=19 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220519100922/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61508110 |url-status=live }}</ref> The investigation involved 12 detectives, 345 documents (including emails, door logs, diary entries and witness statements), 510 photographs or CCTV images, and 204 questionnaires. The cost of the enquiry was £460,000.<ref name="Partygate live"/>
====Fixed penalty notices==== On 29 March 2022, the police said that they were to refer 20 FPNs for £50 to the [[Association of Chief Police Officers#ACPO Criminal Records Office|ACRO Criminal Records Office]], which administers the process and collects any penalties. As an individual might receive more than one notice, it was not known how many people were affected.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/12/boris-johnson-and-rishi-sunak-fined-for-breaking-covid-lockdown-laws |title=Boris Johnson defies calls to quit after he and Rishi Sunak fined |first1=Rowena |last1=Mason |first2=Aubrey |last2=Allegretti |date=12 April 2022 |work=The Guardian |access-date=13 April 2022 |archive-date=9 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220709005208/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/12/boris-johnson-and-rishi-sunak-fined-for-breaking-covid-lockdown-laws |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Rojas |first=John-Paul Ford |date=29 March 2022 |title=Partygate: Met Police to issue 20 fines over Downing Street parties that breached lockdown rules |work=Sky News |url=https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-met-police-to-issue-20-fines-over-downing-street-parties-that-breached-lockdown-rules-12577256 |access-date=29 March 2022 |archive-date=29 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220329104646/https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-met-police-to-issue-20-fines-over-downing-street-parties-that-breached-lockdown-rules-12577256 |url-status=live }}</ref> Recipients of such notices had 28 days in which to either pay or contest the notice. If contested, the case would then be reviewed and either the notice is withdrawn or the case is referred to the courts.<ref>{{Cite news |date=29 March 2022 |title=Twenty fines to be issued initially over No 10 lockdown parties |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60911798 |access-date=29 March 2022 |archive-date=29 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220329083024/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60911798 |url-status=live }}</ref> Payment of the penalty does not constitute an admission of guilt.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mason |first=Rowena |date=14 February 2022 |title=Boris Johnson expected to deny wrongdoing over No 10 parties |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/14/boris-johnson-expected-to-deny-wrongdoing-over-no-10-parties |access-date=2 April 2022 |archive-date=2 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220402095014/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/14/boris-johnson-expected-to-deny-wrongdoing-over-no-10-parties |url-status=live }}</ref> In response to the FPNs, [[Dominic Raab]], the deputy prime minister, said it was clear that the law had been broken and said that Johnson "accepts responsibility" for what happened. On 30 March, Johnson in Parliament,<ref name="auto13">{{Cite news |date=30 March 2022 |title=Boris Johnson must resign over lawbreaking at No 10 - Starmer |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60928083 |access-date=30 March 2022 |archive-date=30 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220330202123/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60928083 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Bloom |first=Dan |date=30 March 2022 |title=Keir Starmer says partying Johnson lied to Parliament - 'why is he still here?' |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/partygate-keir-starmer-suggests-boris-26592096 |website=mirror |access-date=1 April 2022 |archive-date=1 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220401104022/https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/partygate-keir-starmer-suggests-boris-26592096 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Woodcock |first=Andrew |date=31 March 2022 |title=Clash over Partygate as PM refuses to endorse Raab's admission laws were broken |work=Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-party-dominic-raab-b2047245.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-party-dominic-raab-b2047245.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref> and his spokesperson to the press, repeatedly declined to agree that laws had been broken.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Sparrow |first1=Andrew |last2=Stewart |first2=Heather |date=30 March 2022 |title=UK politics: Boris Johnson grilled by MPs at liaison committee – live |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/mar/30/uk-politics-live-boris-johnson-partygate-raab-pmqs-keir-starmer-latest-updates?page=with%3Ablock-624455e78f08abb4071ad73a |via=www.theguardian.com |access-date=2 April 2022 |archive-date=2 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220402161524/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/mar/30/uk-politics-live-boris-johnson-partygate-raab-pmqs-keir-starmer-latest-updates?page=with:block-624455e78f08abb4071ad73a |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Bloom |first=Dan |date=30 March 2022 |title=No10 refuse to say 20 Partygate staff broke the law - the toe-curling exchange |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/downing-street-refuse-say-20-26594338 |website=mirror |access-date=1 April 2022 |archive-date=1 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220401103047/https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/downing-street-refuse-say-20-26594338 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 4 April 2022, further details about the first batch of notices was reported, for a gathering on 16 April 2021, a leaving party for the former No 10 director of communications James Slack. ''The Daily Telegraph'' reported that former Director General for Propriety and Ethics in the Cabinet Office and former Deputy Cabinet Secretary [[Helen MacNamara]] was a recipient of one.<ref name="BBC">{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-60978069|title=Partygate: Police issue fines over No 10 gathering on eve of Prince Philip's funeral|work=BBC News|date=4 April 2022|access-date=27 May 2022|archive-date=27 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220527162017/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-60978069|url-status=live}}</ref> They said MacNamara attended a leaving do in June 2020 in the Cabinet Office.
