Allegedly, roughly 20 people were there and alcohol was drunk.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Morris |first=Sophie |date=4 April 2022 |title=Downing Street parties: Minister says now not the time for 'self-indulgent leadership contest' - even if Boris Johnson is fined over partygate |url=https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-welsh-secretary-says-now-is-not-the-time-for-a-self-indulgent-leadership-contest-even-if-the-pm-is-fined-over-partygate-12582027 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=Sky News |language=en |archive-date=4 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404101231/https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-welsh-secretary-says-now-is-not-the-time-for-a-self-indulgent-leadership-contest-even-if-the-pm-is-fined-over-partygate-12582027 |url-status=live }}</ref> MacNamara said she "accepted and paid the fixed penalty notice ... I am sorry for the error of judgement I have shown".<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-04-04 |title=Ex-government ethics chief Helen MacNamara admits to lockdown party fine |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60983517 |access-date=2023-03-27 |archive-date=8 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220408164754/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60983517 |url-status=live }}</ref> ''BBC News'' reported that Johnson would not comment on whether laws had been broken. They also reported [[Dominic Raab]] as having said earlier that he thought it was clear that the law had been broken, and that Johnson 'accepts responsibility' for what happened.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-03-30 |title=Boris Johnson must resign over lawbreaking at No 10 - Starmer |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60928083 |access-date=2023-03-27 |archive-date=30 March 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220330202123/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60928083 |url-status=live }}</ref> ''The Independent'' stated: "However, Downing Street continues to refuse to accept the law had been broken, despite the Met issuing 20 FPNs".<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Zakir-Hussain |first1=Maryam |last2=Sarkar |first2=Alisha Rahaman |date=2022-04-04 |title=PM 'misled public,' says Starmer as ethics chief apologises- follow live |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-news-partygate-fined-b2049950.html |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=The Independent |language=en |archive-date=4 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220404132929/http://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-news-partygate-fined-b2049950.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 12 April 2022, the police made at least 30 more referrals to the ACRO Criminal Records Office for FPNs for breaches of COVID-19 regulations.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/downing-street-covid-fines-met-police-fixed-penalty-notices-partygate-probe-1570369 |last=Ferguson |first=Emily |title=Downing Street Covid fines: Met Police has now handed out over 50 fixed penalty notices in Partygate probe |work=i |date=12 April 2022 |access-date=12 April 2022 |archive-date=12 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220412105706/https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/downing-street-covid-fines-met-police-fixed-penalty-notices-partygate-probe-1570369 |url-status=live }}</ref> Downing Street later confirmed that Johnson, his wife and Sunak would be receiving FPNs, who all apologised and paid the penalties.<ref>{{cite news |last=Scott |first=Jennifer |date=12 April 2022 |title=Boris Johnson and Rishi Sunak to be fined over lockdown parties |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61083402 |access-date=12 April 2022 |archive-date=12 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220412124621/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61083402 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Clinton |first=Jane |date=13 April 2022 |title=How much was Boris Johnson fined? PM's Covid fixed penalty notice explained and what we know |work=[[i (newspaper)|i]] |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-how-much-fined-fixed-penalty-notice-covid-what-amount-explained-1572775 |access-date=11 May 2022 |archive-date=4 May 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240504005219/https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-how-much-fined-fixed-penalty-notice-covid-what-amount-explained-1572775 |url-status=live }}</ref> A spokesperson for Carrie Johnson said that she, "accepts the Met Police's findings and apologises unreservedly."<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.spectator.co.uk/article/carrie-apologies-for-lockdown-fine|title=Boris and Carrie apologise for lockdown fines | The Spectator|website=www.spectator.co.uk|date=12 April 2022|access-date=12 May 2022|archive-date=12 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220512120931/https://www.spectator.co.uk/article/carrie-apologies-for-lockdown-fine|url-status=live}}</ref> Sunak said he respected the police's decision.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/uk/rishi-sunak-boris-johnson-imf-washington-b995530.html|title=Rishi Sunak says he is 'sincerely sorry' for breach of lockdown rules|first=Gavin|last=Cordon|date=21 April 2022|website=Evening Standard|access-date=12 May 2022|archive-date=26 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221026210556/https://www.standard.co.uk/news/uk/rishi-sunak-boris-johnson-imf-washington-b995530.html|url-status=live}}</ref> ITV News later reported that some of Sunak's junior aides at the time suggested he should resign in order to challenge Johnson's leadership, but he chose not to.<ref name=podcast5>{{cite podcast |title=Partygate: The Inside Story - new revelations and our whistleblowers in their own words: 5. Fine Details |website=ITV News |date=12 January 2023 |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |access-date=3 May 2024 |archive-date=6 April 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240406105915/https://www.itv.com/news/2023-01-11/partygate-the-inside-story-reveals-more-on-downing-streets-notorious-parties |url-status=live }}</ref> ''The Telegraph'' reported that Sunak did plan to resign, but was persuaded not to by newspaper executives.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2023/09/15/murdoch-team-told-sunak-not-to-quit-over-partygate/ | title=Rishi Sunak told by Murdoch team not to quit over partygate | newspaper=The Telegraph | date=15 September 2023 | last1=Riley-Smith | first1=Ben | access-date=16 September 2023 | archive-date=16 September 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230916072019/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2023/09/15/murdoch-team-told-sunak-not-to-quit-over-partygate/ | url-status=live }}</ref>
Matt Fowler of [[Covid-19 Bereaved Families for Justice]] said: "It's plain as day that there was a culture of boozing and rule breaching at the highest level of government, whilst the British public was making unimaginable sacrifices to protect their loved ones and communities".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61081386 |title=Partygate: Met Police issue 30 more Downing Street lockdown fines |work=BBC News |date=12 April 2022 |access-date=12 April 2022 |archive-date=12 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220412103705/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61081386 |url-status=live }}</ref> [[Keir Starmer]] said: "Boris Johnson and Rishi Sunak have broken the law and repeatedly lied to the British public. They must both resign". [[Ed Davey]] suggested that parliament be recalled to hold a vote of no confidence in Johnson.<ref>{{cite news |last=Scott |first=Jennifer |date=12 April 2022 |title=Boris Johnson and Rishi Sunak to be fined over lockdown parties |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61083402 |access-date=12 April 2022 |archive-date=12 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220412124617/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61083402 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 19 April 2022, in his first Commons statement since being issued with an FPN for being at a gathering in Downing Street on his birthday in 2020, Johnson apologised, adding: "It did not occur to me then or subsequently that a gathering in the Cabinet Room just before a vital meeting on Covid strategy could amount to a breach of the rules".<ref name=":5">{{Cite news |date=20 April 2022 |title=Boris Johnson sorry for party as Labour calls apology a joke |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61154461 |access-date=20 April 2022 |archive-date=19 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220419235912/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61154461 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=James |first1=William |last2=Macaskill |first2=Andrew |last3=Smout |first3=Alistair |date=19 April 2022 |title=UK PM Johnson apologises to parliament over lockdown breaches |publisher=Reuters |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/uks-johnson-apologise-parliament-over-lockdown-fine-2022-04-19/ |access-date=20 April 2022 |archive-date=20 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220420034911/https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/uks-johnson-apologise-parliament-over-lockdown-fine-2022-04-19/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Boris Johnson: PM's response to party fine a joke, Starmer tells MPs |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-politics-61143220 |website=BBC News |date=19 April 2022 |access-date=19 April 2022 |archive-date=19 June 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220619230048/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-politics-61143220 |url-status=live }}</ref> Before the apology was made, Starmer alleged Johnson was using the [[Russian invasion of Ukraine]] as a "shield" to stay in office and said that he found it "pretty offensive".<ref>{{cite news |date=19 April 2022 |title=Partygate fines: Boris Johnson set to apologise to MPs over lockdown breach |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61137123 |access-date=19 April 2022 |archive-date=19 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220419102018/https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-61137123 |url-status=live }}</ref> After labelling the apology as "a joke", and saying the Prime Minister had been "dishonest", Starmer withdrew the remark after being admonished by the Speaker, [[Lindsay Hoyle]], who found him in breach of the rule that prohibits MPs from accusing each other of dishonesty during debates.<ref name=":5" />
On 12 May 2022, the Metropolitan Police announced that they had now issued more than 100 FPNs in relation to parties at Downing Street and Whitehall.<ref>{{cite news |last=Forrest |first=Adam |date=12 May 2022 |title=More than 100 fines handed out over Partygate, says Met Police |work=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-covid-met-police-fines-b2077321.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |access-date=12 May 2022 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-covid-met-police-fines-b2077321.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref> New FPNs are thought to relate, in part, to the 18 December 2020 Christmas party.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Parkinson |first=Justin |date=12 May 2022 |title=Police issue 50 more fines over Covid-rule breaking in Downing Street |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61422144 |access-date=20 May 2022 |archive-date=20 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220520193928/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61422144 |url-status=live }}</ref> BBC reporting believes FPNs have only so far been in connection with four out of the twelve events being investigated by the police.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Mason |first=Chris |date=12 May 2022 |title=Anger at Boris Johnson cools but Partygate is far from over |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61431313 |access-date=20 May 2022 |archive-date=20 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220520193929/https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61431313 |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 19 May 2022, the police said they had finished their investigation and had issued or were in the process of issuing a total of 126 FPNs to 83 people.<ref name=":9" /> This included 28 people who received between two and five FPNs.<ref name=":9" /> Neither Boris nor Carrie Johnson were issued with any further FPNs. Simon Case did not receive an FPN.<ref name="Partygate live" /> It was later reported that Steve Higham was also "said to have been issued" with one.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Karim |first=Fariha |date=5 August 2022 |title=Navy captain on leave after cabin 'mentor session' claims |newspaper=[[The Times]] |language=en |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/navy-captain-on-leave-cabin-mentor-session-claims-6d2d7hl5k |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0140-0460 |archive-date=5 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220805113041/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/navy-captain-on-leave-cabin-mentor-session-claims-6d2d7hl5k |url-status=live }}</ref> 53 FPNs were issued to men and 73 to women.<ref name="Partygate live" /><ref>{{cite news |last=Parekh |first=Marcus |date=19 May 2022 |title=Met Police conclude 'partygate' investigation with 126 fines handed out |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/19/met-police-conclude-partygate-investigation-126-fines-handed/ |work=The Telegraph |access-date=19 May 2022 |archive-date=19 May 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220519103900/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/19/met-police-conclude-partygate-investigation-126-fines-handed/ |url-status=live }}</ref> This made Downing Street the address with the most COVID-19 regulation penalties in the country.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/12/fresh-partygate-fines-met-police-downing-street-parties/|title=Downing Street is the most fined address in the country for Covid breaches|first1=Martin|last1=Evans|first2=Ben|last2=Riley-Smith|work=The Telegraph|date=12 May 2022|via=www.telegraph.co.uk|access-date=24 May 2022|archive-date=24 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220524071451/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/12/fresh-partygate-fines-met-police-downing-street-parties/|url-status=live}}</ref> FPNs were issued for the following offences:
* Regulation 6 of the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on leaving, or being outside of, the place where you were living without reasonable excuse. * Regulation 7 of the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering consisting of two or more people. * Regulation 8 of the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (England) (No. 4) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering consisting of two or more people. * Paragraph 1 of Schedule 3 to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering in the Tier 3 area consisting of two or more people. * Paragraph 3 of Schedule 3A to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (All Tiers) (England) Regulations 2020 – Restriction on participating in an indoor gathering in the Tier 4 area consisting of two or more people. * Paragraph 2 of Schedule 2 to the Health Protection (Coronavirus, Restrictions) (Steps) (England) Regulations 2021 - Restriction on participating in an outdoor gathering in the Step 2 area consisting of more than six people.<ref name="Partygate live"/>
There was media speculation about why some individuals were issued an FPN for certain events and others were not. Pippa Crerar reported that Simon Case was not issued one even though he was at events where others were because he was not captured in any pictures, and she speculated that police may have required photographic evidence as their threshold before issuing an FPN.<ref name="Partygate live" /> However, an ITV report said there were photographs of Case at the 19 June 2020 gathering, with one report even saying Case was "perplexed" as to why he had not received a penalty.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-05-19/sue-gray-plans-to-identify-senior-civil-servants-in-partygate-report|title=Sue Gray plans to identify senior civil servants in partygate report|first=Anushka|last=Asthana|date=19 May 2022|website=ITV News}}</ref> ''The Guardian'' quoted a senior civil servant who said that many were issued FPNs because they admitted to Gray that they were at events, and Gray had handed their names to the police. However, the police did not then establish who else was at these events, including very senior individuals. While 83 people were issued with FPNs, the source said over 300 were present at the various gatherings. This had left junior staff who had confessed to Gray angry.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Helm |first=Toby |date=22 May 2022 |title='She has been horribly isolated': friends say Sue Gray was deliberately ostracised while investigating Partygate |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/may/22/she-has-been-horribly-isolated-friends-say-sue-gray-was-deliberately-ostracised-while-investigating-partygate}}</ref><ref name=podcast5 /> Barrister [[Adam Wagner]] suggested that Johnson might not have been issued one for the 20 May 2020 event because the regulations only sanctioned attendees not hosts of garden parties until the rule was changed on 31 May 2020.<ref name="Partygate live" />
==== Scrutiny of the investigation ==== On 24 May 2022, [[Sadiq Khan]] – who oversees the Metropolitan Police as [[Mayor of London]] – asked for a "detailed explanation" from them of how FPNs were decided in the investigation. Khan said he feared a "lack of clarity" could erode "trust" in the police.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2022-05-24 |title=London Mayor Sadiq Khan asks police for explanation of No 10 Partygate fines |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61562629 |access-date=2023-03-27}}</ref> Specifically, Khan wanted to know why Johnson was not issued with an FPN over [[Lee Cain]]'s leaving party, after ITV News obtained photos of him raising a toast in Cain's honour.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Taylor |first1=Harry |last2=Sparrow |first2=Andrew |date=2022-05-24 |title=Boris Johnson would throw 'entire team under bus' to survive Partygate, No 10 official says, amid claims over lockdown events – as it happened |language=en-GB |work=the Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/may/24/boris-johnson-sue-gray-report-partygate-photos-uk-politics-latest |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> On 25 May, legal action was started against the Metropolitan Police by [[Brian Paddick]]. Lawyers for Paddick wrote in a letter they want to judicially review the "apparent failure of the Metropolitan police service to adequately investigate or investigate at all the prime minister Boris Johnson's participation in three unlawful gatherings held at 10 Downing Street". It is believed Johnson did not even receive a questionnaire over his presence at three leaving parties. The letter states: "We do not understand the decision to investigate some attendees but not the prime minister. ... The MPS can not reasonably have reached any conclusion as to reasonable excuse without first having investigated the prime minister's conduct by way of a questionnaire or interview." Johnson attended several events for which he was not issued with an FPN but other attendees were.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Dodd |first=Vikram |date=2022-05-25 |title=Met police faces legal action over 'irrational' handling of Partygate |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/may/25/met-police-facing-legal-action-over-handling-of-partygate |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>
On 26 May 2022, [[Stephen House]], the acting [[commissioner of the Metropolitan Police]], appeared before the London Assembly's Police and Crime Committee to answer questions related to the police's handling of its investigation and how it decided who should be issued with FPNs.<ref name=":13">{{Cite news |date=27 May 2022 |title=No evidence Boris Johnson was serial Covid rule-breaker, Met Police chief says |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61592733}}</ref> He told the committee that he was confident with the findings of the investigation and that he had been personally involved in the decision-making, and is "not particularly concerned about what the prime minister thinks, I do my job without fear or favour".<ref name=":13" /> Asked why the prime minister had only received one FPN, House answered that there was no clear evidence of any other breaches by him. House was also asked why the police officer on duty in Downing Street had not challenged the "large number of people" that Gray's report said he had seen, after responding to a panic alarm, in a "crowded and noisy" event where "some members of staff drank excessively". He replied that Downing Street officers were not there to "police what goes on inside the building", but for security purposes, and that he did not "believe that the officer that we're talking about felt that they were seeing something that necessarily breached coronavirus regulations".<ref name=":13" /> House also told the committee how the police had handled the investigation, describing how officers had examined the activity of each individual at each event, including how long the event was and how long the individual was at it, and referencing "hundreds of documents, including emails, electronic door logs, diary entries, witness statements, photographs, CCTV images, and we sent questionnaires to people who we felt may have breached legislation".<ref name=":13" />
Following legal action, on 25 July 2022, the Metropolitan Police confirmed that Johnson had not received questionnaires in relation to some of the lockdown gatherings.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Quinn |first1=Ben |title=Met admits not sending Boris Johnson questionnaires over Partygate gatherings |url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/jul/25/met-finally-admits-not-sending-boris-johnson-questionnaires-over-partygate-gatherings |access-date=25 July 2022 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=25 July 2022}}</ref>
=== Privileges Committee investigation === {{main|Commons Privileges Committee investigation into Boris Johnson}} On 19 April 2022, Speaker Hoyle approved an application, from the Labour leader and other leading opposition MPs,{{efn|The motion was in the name of the following MPs: {{bulleted list|[[Keir Starmer]], leader of the [[Labour Party (UK)|Labour Party]]|[[Ian Blackford]], Commons leader of the [[Scottish National Party|SNP]]|[[Ed Davey]], leader of the [[Liberal Democrats (UK)|Liberal Democrats]]|[[Liz Saville Roberts]], Commons leader of [[Plaid Cymru]]|[[Colum Eastwood]], leader of the [[SDLP]]|[[Caroline Lucas]], [[Green Party of England and Wales|Green Party]]|[[Stephen Farry]], deputy leader of the [[Alliance Party of Northern Ireland|Alliance Party]]}}<ref>{{cite web|url=https://commonsbusiness.parliament.uk/document/56344/html#_idTextAnchor005|title=Order Paper for Thursday 21 April 2022|publisher=[[House of Commons]]|date=21 April 2022|access-date=21 April 2022}}</ref>}} to table a motion for debate and vote on 21 April, on whether Johnson should be referred to the [[Commons Select Committee of Privileges|Parliamentary Privileges Committee]] to investigate whether he knowingly misled Parliament.<ref name=":8">{{Cite news |last=Ferguson |first=Emily |date=19 April 2022 |title=Boris Johnson to face Commons vote on whether committee should investigate if PM misled Parliament |work=i |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/politics/boris-johnson-face-commons-votes-committee-investigate-pm-parliament-1582770?ico=in-line_link}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://news.sky.com/video/partygate-speaker-sir-lindsay-hoyle-announces-vote-on-investigation-into-boris-johnson-misleading-parliament-12593873|title=Partygate: Speaker Sir Lindsay Hoyle announces vote on investigation into Boris Johnson misleading parliament|website=Sky News}}</ref> The motion was tabled on 21 April 2022, with Conservative MPs told not to oppose it.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Johnston |first=John |date=21 April 2022 |title=Boris Johnson To Face Commons Probe Over Partygate After 'Humiliating' Government U-turn |url=https://www.politicshome.com/news/article/tory-mps-to-get-free-vote-on-commons-partygate-probe-after-government-uturn |website=Politics Home}}</ref> The Commons approved the motion without a vote.<ref>{{cite news|first=Sophie|last=Morris|url=https://news.sky.com/story/downing-street-parties-boris-johnson-to-face-investigation-into-whether-he-misled-mps-about-partygate-12595185|title=Downing Street parties: Boris Johnson to face investigation into whether he misled MPs about partygate|date=21 April 2022|access-date=21 April 2022|work=[[Sky News]]}}</ref>
The committee began its considerations on 29 June 2022.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://committees.parliament.uk/publications/23198/documents/169500/default/|title=Parliament documents|access-date=13 June 2023}}</ref> On 3 March 2023, the committee published its summary of issues to be raised with Johnson. It raised at least four occasions on which the Commons might have been misled when Johnson said regulations were followed, and suggested that breaches of COVID-19 guidance would have been obvious to Johnson.<ref>[https://www.itv.com/news/2023-03-04/pressure-on-johnson-over-whether-mps-misled-on-partygate Mounting pressure on Boris Johnson over whether MPs were misled about Partygate] ''[[ITV (TV network)|ITV]]''</ref> Johnson submitted a response, which was published 21 March 2023.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Boris Johnson says he did not intentionally or recklessly mislead MP over Partygate |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-politics-65025438 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=BBC News|date=21 March 2023 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://committees.parliament.uk/writtenevidence/119498/default/ |title=Submission of RT Hon Boris Johnson MP|website=parliament.uk|access-date=13 June 2023}}</ref> Johnson faced questioning by the committee at a televised hearing on 22 March 2023.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2023-03-14 |title=Boris Johnson to give evidence to Partygate inquiry next week |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-64954751 |access-date=2023-03-27}}</ref>
The Committee sent Johnson their provisional report, ahead of its public release, on 8 June 2023.<ref>{{cite web | title=Boris Johnson sent findings of Privileges Committee Partygate probe | website=ITV News | date=8 June 2023 | url=https://www.itv.com/news/2023-06-08/boris-johnson-sent-findings-of-privileges-committee-partygate-probe | access-date=6 August 2023}}</ref> The next day, Johnson resigned as an MP, critical of the investigation and insisting he had not lied.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2023/jun/09/conservatives-labour-rishi-sunak-keir-starmer-boris-johnson-honours-uk-politics-news|title=Boris Johnson resigns as MP with immediate effect and says he is 'bewildered and appalled' at being 'forced out' – as it happened|first1=Charlie|last1=Moloney|first2=Tom|last2=Bryant|first3=Sammy|last3=Gecsoyler|first4=Miranda|last4=Bryant|first5=Charlie Moloney (now); Tom|last5=Bryant|first6=Miranda|last6=Bryant (earlier)|newspaper=The Guardian |date=9 June 2023|access-date=13 June 2023|via=www.theguardian.com}}</ref><ref name="auto15"/> The final report was released on 15 June 2023 and Johnson condemned it as a "protracted political assassination". It concluded Johnson was guilty of deliberately misleading Parliament and further contempt of Parliament. It stated that, were he still an MP, they would be recommending a 90-day suspension.<ref name="BBC_65876914">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-politics-65876914|title=Boris Johnson report latest: Covid bereaved seek ex-PM apology after Partygate report|date=15 June 2023|website=BBC News}}</ref> Johnson called the committee "beneath contempt" and dismissed their findings as "complete tripe". Former minister [[Michael Heseltine]] accused Johnson of telling "a pack of lies".<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-lies-worth-commons-b2358452.html |title=Boris Johnson the liar: How ex-PM lied and lied again to Commons |first1=Kate |last1=Devlin |first2=Adam |last2=Forrest |date=15 June 2023 |publisher=independent.co.uk/ |access-date=15 June 2023}}</ref>
The Committee concluded that Johnson's actions were "more serious" because they were committed when he was Prime Minister. They noted that there was no precedent for a PM being found to have deliberately misled Parliament.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/politics/london-politics-latest-live-partygate-boris-johnson-report-privileges-committee-b1087960.html|title=Tory civil war tensions as MPs given vote on Boris Partygate report|first=Josh Salisbury, Bill McLoughlin, Claudia|last=Marquis|date=15 June 2023|website=Evening Standard}}</ref> The report stated that Johnson tried to "rewrite the meaning" of COVID rules "to fit his own evidence" for example that "a leaving gathering or a gathering to boost morale was a lawful reason to hold a gathering."<ref>{{cite news |last1=Castle |first1=Stephen |title=Boris Johnson Misled Parliament Over Covid Lockdown Parties, Report Says |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/06/15/world/europe/boris-johnson-report-covid-lockdown-parties-uk.html |access-date=16 June 2023 |work=The New York Times |date=15 June 2023 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230615110139/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/06/15/world/europe/boris-johnson-report-covid-lockdown-parties-uk.html |archive-date=15 June 2023}}</ref> They concluded he was guilty of further contempts of Parliament and that he breached confidentiality requirements by criticising the committee's provisional findings when he resigned. They said he was complicit in a "campaign of abuse" against those investigating him.<ref name="BBC_65876914" /> The Commons debated the report on 19 June 2023,.<ref name=debateandvote /><ref name="BBC_65876914" /> The same day, House of Commons MPs held a [[free vote]] which saw MPs vote 354 to 7 to accept both the report's findings and its recommendation to revoke Johnson parliament's pass which gave him special parliament access.<ref name=debateandvote>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-65953605|title=Boris Johnson: MPs back Partygate report as just seven vote against|first=Becky|last=Morton|publisher=BBC News|date=19 June 2023|access-date=20 June 2023}}</ref><ref name=revoked /> Eight cabinet ministers, including Commons Leader [[Penny Mordaunt]], Justice Secretary [[Alex Chalk]], Education Secretary [[Gillian Keegan]] and Chief Whip [[Simon Hart]] backed the report, as did former Prime Minister [[Theresa May]].<ref name=revoked />
== Reactions == Since early December 2021, some British media referred to the controversy over the events as "Partygate".<ref>{{Cite news|last=Colchester|first=Max|date=12 January 2022|title=Boris Johnson Apologizes for Party at Downing Street During U.K. Lockdown|work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/boris-johnson-apologizes-for-attending-party-at-downing-street-during-lockdown-11641994454}}</ref> The term is similar to that used for previous [[List of -gate scandals and controversies|political scandals and controversies]].
Some commentators made comparisons between these possible social gatherings and the lack of social contact when observing COVID restrictions when people were dying or at funerals.<ref>*{{Cite news|title='People died... they broke rules to have wine'|work=[[BBC News]]|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/av/uk-59963012|access-date=13 January 2022}} {{Date missing}} *{{Cite news|date=12 January 2022|title=Boris Johnson Christmas Party Scandal: Tory MPs Fear Next Misstep Could be Fatal|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2021-12-11/boris-johnson-christmas-party-scandal-tory-mps-fear-next-misstep-could-be-fatal|access-date=13 January 2022|work=Bloomberg}} *{{Cite news|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2021-12-08/no-10-staff-at-alleged-christmas-party-danced-on-graves-of-covid-victims|title=No 10 staff at alleged Christmas party 'danced on graves' of Covid victims|work=[[ITV News]]|date=8 December 2021}} *{{Cite web|url=https://www.politicshome.com/thehouse/article/while-families-protected-loved-ones-and-stuck-to-the-rules-this-government-flouted-them-boris-johnson-should-resign|title = While my father was dying from Covid in hospital, No. 10 were flouting the rules at a Christmas party|work=politicshome.com|date = 10 December 2021}} *{{Cite news|url=https://inews.co.uk/news/mothers-anger-no10-wine-gathering-after-10-people-allowed-teenage-sons-funeral-1360865|title=Mother's rage at No 10 wine gathering after just 10 people could attend teen son's funeral|work=i |date=20 December 2021}} *{{Cite news|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2021-12-10/no10-cancels-its-christmas-party-over-omicron-but-urges-others-to-continue|work=[[ITV News]]|title = No10 cancels its Christmas party over Omicron – but urges others to continue|date = 10 December 2021}} </ref> For example, at Prime Minister's Questions on 8 December 2021, [[Keir Starmer]], the leader of the Opposition, raised the example of [[Trisha Greenhalgh]] being unable to visit her dying mother in December 2020.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Gelder |first=Sam |date=8 December 2021 |title=Boris Johnson says 'stop playing politics' over Downing Street Christmas party |work=The Big Issue |url=https://www.bigissue.com/news/politics/boris-johnson-says-stop-playing-politics-over-downing-street-christmas-party/}}</ref> The Conservative backbench MP [[Tracey Crouch]] said: "My constituents have every right to be angry. Their memories of lost loved ones are traumatised knowing that they died alone, first and last Christmases passed by, and many spent what is usually a special day by themselves".<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Mason |first1=Rowena |last2=Allegretti |first2=Aubrey |date=8 December 2021 |title=No 10 faces Tory and public backlash over Christmas party video |work=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2021/dec/08/no-10-faces-tory-and-public-backlash-over-christmas-party-video}}</ref>
On 7 December, the story became the subject of political satire by [[Ant & Dec]] on the [[ITV (TV network)|ITV]] entertainment show ''[[I'm a Celebrity...Get Me Out of Here! (British TV series)|I'm a Celebrity...Get Me Out of Here!]]'' A clip of this on social media was viewed five million times.<ref name="theguardian.com">{{Cite news |last=Belam |first=Martin |date=8 December 2021 |title=No 10 Christmas party scandal prompts flood of memes online |work=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/media/2021/dec/08/no-10-christmas-party-scandal-prompts-flood-of-memes-online}}</ref>
From December 2021 onwards, UK and international journalists suggested Johnson's political career was threatened by the controversy.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Kuenssberg |first=Laura |date=17 December 2021 |title=Boris Johnson has been put on notice by his own side |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-59707541}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last1=Landler|first1=Mark|last2=Castle|first2=Stephen|date=25 January 2022|title=U.K. Police Say They Are Investigating Downing Street Lockdown Parties |work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/01/25/world/europe/uk-boris-johnson-parties-police.html|access-date=26 January 2022|issn=0362-4331}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|last=Castle|first=Stephen|date=25 January 2022|title=How Boris Johnson Could Lose His Job as U.K. Prime Minister: Latest Details|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/article/boris-johnson-prime-minister-explained.html|access-date=26 January 2022|issn=0362-4331}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=McTague|first=Tom|date=24 January 2022|title=The Tears of a Clown|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2022/01/boris-johnson-party-pressure/621325/|access-date=2 February 2022|website=The Atlantic}}</ref>
There was debate as to whether public disquiet about the controversy might lead to the public being less willing to adhere to new restrictions brought in response to the [[Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2]], which began spreading in the UK in December 2021.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Parsley |first=David |date=10 December 2021 |title=No 10 party will lead to public breaking Covid rules, says Government's behaviour adviser |work=[[i (newspaper)|i]] |url=https://inews.co.uk/news/downing-street-party-will-lead-to-public-breaking-covid-restrictions-says-governments-own-behaviour-adviser-1346474 }}</ref> However, [[Steve Reicher]] argued in ''[[The BMJ]]'' that any effect was likely to be small and that it could lead some individuals to be more adherent.<ref name="auto6">{{Cite journal|last1=Reicher|first1=Stephen|date=24 December 2021|title=It is wrong to claim that the public won't follow covid rules just because the government don't|url=https://www.bmj.com/content/375/bmj.n3150|journal=BMJ|volume=375|article-number=n3150|doi=10.1136/bmj.n3150|doi-access=free|pmid=34952834|s2cid=245426151}}</ref>
''[[The Week]]'',<ref>{{Cite news|date=27 December 2021|title=The most striking news photographs of 2021|url=https://www.theweek.co.uk/news/world-news/954795/the-most-striking-photographs-of-2021|work=The Week}}</ref> the BBC<ref>{{Cite news |last=Whannel |first=Kate |date=30 December 2021 |title=From Jackie Weaver to Peppa Pig – the political year in pictures |work=[[BBC News]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-59739737}}</ref> and ''The Daily Telegraph''<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2021/12/28/review-2021-iconic-photographs-news-roundup-things-will-get-better/|title=The iconic pictures of 2021 – a year of angry protests, emotional farewells, famous victories... and more Covid|first=Guy|last=Kelly|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|date=28 December 2021}}</ref> all selected a photo of [[Allegra Stratton]] giving her resignation statement as a key image of 2021.
The reported gathering on 16 April 2021, on the day before the funeral of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, was compared by opposition politicians and ''The Guardian'' to the social distancing rules that applied to the funeral attended by Queen Elizabeth II.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite news |last=Culbertson |first=Alix |date=17 January 2022 |title=A year of political scandals – but will Christmas parties tip Boris Johnson over the edge? |work=[[Sky News]] |url=https://news.sky.com/story/a-year-of-political-scandals-but-will-christmas-parties-tip-boris-johnson-over-the-edge-12498416}}</ref> [[JD Wetherspoon]] founder [[Tim Martin (businessman)|Tim Martin]] accused Johnson of "hypocrisy", arguing that much of the controversy would have been avoided if Downing Street staff had been able to visit [[pub]]s, which at the time were closed due to lockdown restrictions.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/business/2022/01/19/no-10-staff-should-have-able-party-pub-says-wetherspoons-boss/|title=No 10 staff should have been able to party in a pub, says Wetherspoons boss|last=Boland|first=Hannah|date=19 January 2022|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref> On the same day, the campaign group [[Led By Donkeys]] produced a spoof video of Johnson being questioned about the controversy by characters from the TV drama ''[[Line of Duty]]''. It was viewed five million times with a day of publication.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Moore |first=Sam |date=19 January 2022 |title=Boris Johnson 'interrogated by AC-12' in Line of Duty parody |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/tv/news/boris-johnson-line-of-duty-b1995484.html |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/arts-entertainment/tv/news/boris-johnson-line-of-duty-b1995484.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |website=Independent}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Sherwood |first1=Harriet |title=Boris Johnson grilled by Line of Duty team in spoof video viewed by 5m |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/19/boris-johnson-grilled-by-line-of-duty-team-in-spoof-video-viewed-by-5-million |access-date=19 January 2022 |work=[[The Guardian]] |date=19 January 2022}}</ref>
[[Philip Mawer]], former [[Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards]], and [[Alex Allan]], Johnson's former independent advisor on ministerial standards, both said they felt Partygate and other scandals have eroded public confidence in the present system. Both maintained there needed to be an independent system for investigating ministers accused of problems. Currently, the Prime Minister can decide not to hold an investigation and the public feel this can prevent desirable investigations being held.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Merrick |first=Rob |date=2022-03-15 |title=Partygate shows system for investigating PM's 'behaviour' failing, ex-adviser says |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-no-10-party-investigation-b2036073.html |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
After FPNs were issued in March 2022, Starmer said that Johnson was either "trashing the ministerial code or he's claiming he was repeatedly lied to by his own advisers, that he didn't know what was going on in his own house and his own office". He said Johnson had to resign, a position also supported by the Liberal Democrats and SNP.<ref name="auto13"/>
On 20 April 2022, the COVID-19 Bereaved Families for Justice group described Johnson's apology as "the words of someone who is sorry they have been caught, not someone who regrets the harm they have done". Safiah Ngah, a spokesperson for the group said: "[Johnson's] claim that he didn't realise rules were being broken is just laughable and shows he still takes us for idiots."<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/20/insincere-covid-bereaved-man-80-rejects-boris-johnson-partygate-apology |title='Insincere': Covid-bereaved man, 80, rejects Boris Johnson Partygate apology |first=Robert |last=Booth |work=[[The Guardian]]|date=20 April 2022 |access-date=20 April 2022}}</ref>
On 23 May 2022, Starmer said: "The culture is set at the top, the can should be carried by the prime minister. He has responsibility. I doubt he will, because he doesn't take responsibility for anything he's done in his life. But the culture in Downing Street is set from the top, as it is with any organisation, and that culture has led to industrial-scale law-breaking."<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Slawson |first1=Nicola |last2=Sparrow |first2=Andrew |date=2022-05-23 |title=Partygate: pictures emerge showing Boris Johnson drinking at No 10 leaving do during lockdown – as it happened |language=en-GB |work=the Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2022/may/23/sue-gray-report-partygate-boris-johnson-downing-street-uk-politics-latest |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> The same day, pictures obtained by ITV News showed Johnson raising a glass of wine in front of a table with bottles on it at the leaving party for Lee Cain on 13 November 2020.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Elgot |first=Jessica |date=23 May 2022 |title=Photos show Boris Johnson with glass of wine at No 10 party he was not fined for |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2022/may/23/partygate-photos-boris-johnson-dominic-cummings-lockdown-breaches-sue-gray-report}}</ref> Scotland Yard were urged to explain why Johnson had not been issued with an FPN for attending the gathering.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/05/23/pressure-police-partygate-pictures-boris-johnson-raising-toast/|title=Pressure on police over partygate pictures of Boris Johnson raising a toast in No 10|first1=Martin|last1=Evans|first2=Tony|last2=Diver|first3=Robert|last3=Mendick|work=The Telegraph |date=23 May 2022|via=www.telegraph.co.uk}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61560535|title=Partygate: Boris Johnson facing questions after photos emerge|work=BBC News |date=24 May 2022}}</ref>
In September 2023, the [[National Audit Office (United Kingdom)|National Audit Office]] criticised the government's justification for paying a £265,000 bill for Boris Johnson's legal fees relating to Partygate.<ref>{{Cite news |date=2023-09-19 |title=Watchdog criticises decision to pay Johnson's £265,000 Partygate bill |language=en-GB |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-66852723 |access-date=2023-09-19}}</ref>
In a June 2024 [[Investigatory Powers Tribunal]] hearing, a lawyer for [[Christine Lee (lawyer)|Christine Lee]] (who was accused of political interference activities on behalf of China) read a message sent from [[Barry Gardiner]] to Lee, who wrote that "many people" believed that alerting lawmakers of potential spy activity was timed to divert attention from the Partygate scandal.<ref>{{cite web |last=Holden |first=Michael |title=UK's MI5 issued Chinese spy alert to distract from PM scandal, court hears |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/uks-mi5-issued-chinese-spy-alert-divert-attention-pm-scandal-court-hears-2024-06-17/ |publisher=Reuters |date=17 June 2024}}</ref>
===Within the Conservative Party=== ====Initial responses==== On 12 January 2022, many senior figures of the Scottish Conservatives called for Johnson to resign, including leader Douglas Ross and former leaders [[Ruth Davidson]] and [[Jackson Carlaw]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Andrews |first1=Kieran |title=Ruth Davidson: Boris Johnson should quit to restore Tories' reputation |url=https://www.thetimes.com/uk/politics/article/ruth-davidson-boris-johnson-should-quit-to-restore-tories-reputation-mfg7ctvr7 |access-date=20 January 2022 |work=[[The Times]] |date=4 January 2022}}</ref><ref name="Ross letter">{{cite news |last1=McDonald |first1=Andrew |title=Scottish Tory leader Douglas Ross urges Boris Johnson to quit over lockdown party |url=https://www.politico.eu/article/scottish-tory-leader-douglas-ross-urge-boris-johnson-quit-lockdown-party/ |access-date=20 January 2022 |work=Politico |date=12 January 2022}}</ref> On 13 January 2022, 27 of the 31 Conservative Scottish MSPs came out publicly against Johnson after his appearance in Parliament the previous day.<ref name=msps27/> [[William Wragg]], the Conservative Chair of the [[Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Select Committee]], called for Johnson's resignation.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.itv.com/news/granada/2022-01-12/senior-tory-backbencher-william-wragg-calls-for-pm-to-go |title=Senior Tory backbencher calls for PM to go |work=[[ITV News]] |date=12 January 2022 |access-date=12 January 2022}}</ref> On 13 January, Conservative MP Andrew Bridgen stated that he had submitted a letter of no confidence in the prime minister, and Conservative MP for [[Romsey and Southampton North]], [[Caroline Nokes]], also called for him to resign.<ref name="Bridgen letter">{{cite news |last=Bridgen |first=Andrew |date=13 January 2022 |title=Boris Johnson delivered on Brexit and vaccines; all that's left to deliver is his resignation |work=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/13/boris-johnson-delivered-brexit-vaccines-left-deliver-resignation/ |access-date=13 January 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last=Stanley |first=Megan |title= Conservative MP Caroline Nokes calls for 'damaging' Boris Johnson to resign following Downing Street party |url=https://www.hampshirelive.news/news/hampshire-news/conservative-mp-caroline-nokes-calls-6483788 |work= HampshireLive |access-date=3 February 2022 |date=13 January 2022}}</ref> The [[Sutton Coldfield]] [[Conservative Association]] unanimously passed a motion calling for the prime minister to stand down.<ref>{{cite news |title=Sutton Coldfield Tories vote unanimously against PM |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-birmingham-59993504 |work=[[BBC News]] |access-date=18 January 2022 |date=15 January 2022}}</ref> A poll of [[Conservative Party (UK)|Conservative Party]] members in early January 2022 by [[YouGov]] found 38% felt that Johnson was doing a bad job as prime minister and 34% wanted him to resign, which were big increases than when YouGov had last polled Conservative members in the summer of 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Kirk|first=Isabelle|date=10 January 2022|title=Does Boris Johnson still have the support of Conservative party members?|url=https://yougov.co.uk/topics/politics/articles-reports/2022/01/10/does-boris-johnson-still-have-support-conservative|access-date=14 January 2022|website=YouGov}}</ref>
On 15 January, former Conservative minister [[Tobias Ellwood]] told the BBC that Johnson had to "lead or step aside". Former children's minister and MP for [[East Worthing and Shoreham]], [[Tim Loughton]] called for Johnson to resign.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Stone |first=Jon |date=15 January 2022 |title=Furious Tory MPs put Boris Johnson on notice over No.10 lockdown party revelations |work=Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-andrew-bridgen-lockdown-parties-no10-b1993908.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-andrew-bridgen-lockdown-parties-no10-b1993908.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref>
Johnson faced more pressure from rebel MPs as they arrived back at Westminster on 17 January, after dealing with negative responses from Conservative associations and constituents. Some reported they had received almost 1,000 emails from dissatisfied voters.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Allegretti |first1=Aubrey |last2=Murray |first2=Jessica |last3=Morris |first3=Steven |last4=Brown |first4=Mark |date=2022-01-18 |title='Operation Rinka': rebel Tories raise pressure on Boris Johnson to resign |language=en-GB |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/18/operation-rinka-rebel-tories-up-pressure-on-boris-johnson-to-resign-downing-street-parties |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>
''The Times'' reported that, on the evening of 18 January, more than 20 Conservative MPs first elected in 2019 had met to discuss Johnson's leadership, with some preparing to submit letters of no confidence after Prime Minister's Questions on 19 January.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.thetimes.com/article/tory-mps-team-up-to-topple-boris-johnson-over-lockdown-parties-3xwd0lbgc |title=Tory MPs team up to topple Boris Johnson over lockdown parties |author1=Steven Swinford |author2=Henry Zeffman |author3=George Grylls |author4=Patrick Maguire |work=[[The Times]] |date=19 January 2022 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> The ''[[Evening Standard]]'' said that as many as 20 of the MPs were preparing to submit the letters.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/uk/boris-johnson-bid-to-save-leadership-by-ending-plan-b-b977453.html |title=Boris Johnson expected to set out plan to ease Plan B restrictions |first=Barney Davis, Matt |last=Watts |date=19 January 2022 |work=[[Evening Standard]] |access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref> This was dubbed the 'Pork Pie Plot' (or 'Putsch'<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.gazetteherald.co.uk/news/national/uk-today/19857872.pork-pie-plot-boris-johnson-putsch-meaning/ |title=What is the 'Pork Pie Plot' to oust Boris Johnson? |work=Gazette & Herald |date=19 January 2022 |access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref>) by a Johnson loyalist minister as one of the MPs who was said to be involved, [[Alicia Kearns]], represents [[Rutland and Melton]], the town of [[Melton Mowbray]] being famous for its [[Melton Mowbray pork pie|pork pies]]. Kearns has denied being an organiser of the rebellion.<ref>{{cite news |last=Forrest |first=Adam |date=19 January 2022 |title=What is the 'pork pie plot'? Tory bid to oust Boris Johnson explained |work=[[The Independent|Independent]] |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/pork-pie-plot-meaning-boris-johnson-b1996101.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |access-date=19 January 2022 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/pork-pie-plot-meaning-boris-johnson-b1996101.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref>
On 19 January, [[Bury South]] MP [[Christian Wakeford]] said he had submitted a letter of no confidence.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/news/greater-manchester-news/greater-manchester-mp-becomes-latest-22797651|title=Greater Manchester MP becomes latest Tory to submit vote of no confidence in Boris Johnson over partygate scandal|last=Halle-Richards|first=Sophie|date=19 January 2022|work=[[Manchester Evening News]]|access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref> Later that day, shortly before Prime Minister's Questions, Wakeford defected from the Conservative Party to the Labour Party,<ref>{{cite news |last=Stirzaker |first=Ted |date=19 January 2022 |title=Bury South MP Christian Wakeford resigns from Conservative to join Labour |work=Bury Times |url=https://www.burytimes.co.uk/news/19858532.bury-south-mp-christian-wakeford-resigns-conservative-join-labour/ |access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref> although his first contact with Labour predated the controversy and he was initially motivated by other issues.<ref name="BBC_60062913">{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60062913|title=Boris Johnson's future: PM holds on as MPs await Sue Gray's report|work=BBC News|date=20 January 2022}}</ref> It was later reported that up to seven MPs had withdrawn their letters of no confidence.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Up to seven no confidence letters withdrawn as Christian Wakeford defection 'saves' Boris Johnson |last1=Hope |first1=Christopher |last2=Penna |first2=Dominic |work=The Telegraph |date=3 February 2022 |url= https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/20/christian-wakeford-has-saved-boris-seven-no-confidence-letters/}}</ref> By the end of 19 January, some Conservative MPs told the BBC that Wakeford's defection had caused a change in mood, namely that there was a "stepping back" from immediate attempts to obtain a no-confidence vote in Johnson's leadership and a wish to wait until after Sue Gray's report was published.<ref name="BBC_60062913" /><ref>{{cite news|last1=Hancock|first1=Sam|last2=Gregory|first2=Andy|date=19 January 2022|title=Boris Johnson news - live: Tories shelve no-confidence vote until partygate report, as MP defects to Labour|work=[[The Independent|Independent]]|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-news-live-vote-party-update-b1995994.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-news-live-vote-party-update-b1995994.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|access-date=19 January 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Nellan|first=Catherine|date=19 January 2021|title=Tory MPs look to Sue Gray partygate report for 'justification' for no-confidence letters in Boris Johnson|work=[[Business Insider]]|url=https://www.businessinsider.com/sue-gray-report-reason-oust-boris-johnson-tory-mps-2022-1|access-date=20 January 2021}}</ref>
[[File:Official portrait of Rt Hon David Davis MP crop 2.jpg|thumb|upright=0.75|Conservative MP [[David Davis (British politician)|David Davis]] called on 19 January for Johnson to resign, making reference to the 1940 [[Norway Debate]] that led to [[Neville Chamberlain]]'s resignation.]]
At the end of Prime Minister's Questions on 19 January, Conservative former minister [[David Davis (British politician)|David Davis]] called for Johnson to resign, quoting [[Leo Amery]] calling on [[Neville Chamberlain]] to resign during the [[Norway Debate]] in 1940,<ref>{{Cite news|last=Lewis|first=Helen|date=19 January 2022|title='In the Name of God, Go'|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/ideas/archive/2022/01/boris-johnson-winston-churchill/621294/|access-date=2 February 2022|work=The Atlantic}}</ref> and saying: "You have sat there too long for all the good you have done. In the name of God, go". BBC ''[[Newsnight]]'''s political editor [[Nicholas Watt|Nick Watt]] said Davis would have intended it to be "particularly devastating" to Johnson.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Merrick|first=Rob|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/david-davis-boris-johnson-pmqs-b1996225.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/david-davis-boris-johnson-pmqs-b1996225.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title='In the name of God, go': Tory MP David Davis tells Boris Johnson to quit|date=19 January 2022|work=[[The Independent|Independent]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/live/uk-politics-60046632|title=Boris Johnson: Tory MP defects as ex-minister tells Johnson to go|date=19 January 2022|work=[[BBC News]]}}</ref>
On 26 January, Nicky Wylie, the leader of the Conservative group on [[Cheshire East Council]], quit the party citing the controversy.<ref>{{Cite news|date=25 January 2022|title=Downing Street party claims lead Cheshire councillor to quit |work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-england-manchester-60128066|access-date=26 January 2022}}</ref>
====Alleged intimidation of MPs opposed to Johnson==== On 20 January, Conservative MP [[William Wragg]], chair of the Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Select Committee, accused 10 Downing Street staff of threatening him and other colleagues over their opposition to Johnson's leadership. He said "the intimidation of a member of parliament is a serious matter" and "The reports of which I'm aware would seem to constitute blackmail".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-20/tory-mp-accuses-no10-of-threatening-to-withdraw-funding-over-opposition-to-pm |title=Top Tory accuses No10 of 'blackmailing' MPs over opposition to Boris Johnson |date=20 January 2022 |work=ITV News}}</ref> Wragg said damaging publicity had been threatened, as had removal of government investment in MPs' constituencies. Wragg advised MP's affected to contact the police or the Commons Speaker.<ref name=":6">{{cite news |date=21 January 2022 |title=Boris Johnson: I've seen no evidence of plotters being blackmailed |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60068612 |access-date=22 January 2022}}</ref>
Johnson said he had "seen no evidence [and] heard no evidence" of what Wragg said and that he would look into them. The [[Secretary of State for Culture]], [[Nadine Dorries]], called Wragg's accusations "attention-seeking behaviour" and "nonsense". Energy minister and former government whip Greg Hands said of what Wragg said that they "not been borne out by anybody else", and that "This is not something that happens".<ref name=":6" />
[[Chris Bryant]], Chair of the [[Commons Select Committee on Standards]], said roughly a dozen Conservative MPs had made similar allegations of whips threatening to withdraw funding for their constituencies in the past few days; threats included withholding funds for campaigning and infrastructure such as by-passes and schools.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60093893|title=MP blackmail claims: Tory William Wragg to meet police |date=22 January 2022 |work=BBC News|access-date=22 January 2022}}</ref> Bryant said, "I have even heard MPs alleging that the prime minister himself has been doing this. What I have said to all of those people is that that is [[Malfeasance in office#England and Wales|misconduct in public office]]. The people who should be dealing with such allegations are the police. It is illegal. We are meant to operate as MPs without fear or favour. The allocation of taxpayer funding to constituencies should be according to need, not according to the need to keep the prime minister in his job".<ref name="guardian22/1/2022">{{cite news|last=Skopeliti|first=Clea |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/22/about-a-dozen-tory-mps-said-to-have-accused-party-whips-of-blackmail |title=About a dozen Tory MPs said to have accused party whips of blackmail |work=[[The Guardian]]|date=22 January 2022|access-date=2 February 2022}}</ref>
====After the Sue Gray update==== Following the release of Sue Gray's update on 31 January 2022, Conservative MP [[Andrew Mitchell]] said he no longer supported Johnson.<ref name="BBC News">{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-england-birmingham-60205883|title=Sutton Coldfield Tory MP withdraws support for Boris Johnson|date=31 January 2022|work=BBC News}}</ref> Former prime minister [[Theresa May]] also questioned Johnson's response to Gray's report, saying that people "had a right to expect their prime minister to have read the rules, to understand the meaning of the rules" and to "set an example". She said No. 10 "was not observing the regulations they had imposed on members of the public" and accused Johnson of "either not understanding the rules or believing they did not apply to his team".<ref name=reaction>{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/politics/2022/01/31/sue-gray-boris-johnson-report-nhs-mandatory-vaccine-brexit/|title=Boris Johnson launches bid to win over MPs as government minister resigns|first1=Dominic|last1=Penna|first2=Matthew|last2=Robinson|work=The Telegraph|date=31 January 2022|access-date=31 January 2022}}</ref><ref name="Parry">{{cite news|last=Parry|first=Mared|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/tv/news/theresa-may-boris-johnson-report-v780928d5 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/tv/news/theresa-may-boris-johnson-report-v780928d5 |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Theresa May questions whether Boris Johnson understood Covid rules|work=Independent|date=31 January 2022|access-date=31 January 2022}}</ref><ref name="Tory MPs are frogs in boiling water">{{cite news|last=Allegretti|first=Aubrey|url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/jan/31/tory-mps-are-frogs-in-boiling-water-amid-johnsons-partygate-defence|title=Tory MPs are frogs in boiling water amid Johnson's 'partygate' defence|work=The Guardian|date=31 January 2022|access-date=31 January 2022}}</ref> In protest over Johnson's handling of the controversy, Guildford MP [[Angela Richardson]] resigned from her position as [[parliamentary private secretary]] to [[Michael Gove]], the [[Secretary of State for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities]].<ref name="Woodcock">{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/angela-richardson-partygate-boris-johnson-b2004501.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/angela-richardson-partygate-boris-johnson-b2004501.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Conservative MP steps down from government job over Boris Johnson's handling of Partygate scandal|first=Andrew|last=Woodcock|work=Independent|date=31 January 2022|access-date=1 February 2022}}</ref>
On 1–2 February, the Conservative MP for [[Waveney (UK Parliament constituency)|Waveney]], [[Peter Aldous]], submitted a letter of no confidence.<ref name="Aldous letter">{{cite news |last=James |first=William |date=1 February 2022 |title=UK lawmaker calls for Johnson to resign, submits no-confidence letter |work=Reuters |editor-last=Holton |editor-first=Kate |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/britain-politics-aldous/uk-lawmaker-calls-for-johnson-to-resign-submits-no-confidence-letter-idINL8N2UC575 |url-status=live |url-access=registration |access-date=1 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220203001726/https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/uk-lawmaker-calls-johnson-resign-submits-no-confidence-letter-2022-02-01/ |archive-date=3 February 2022}}</ref> Three others—[[Tobias Ellwood]], MP for [[Bournemouth East]] and Chair of the [[Defence Select Committee]]; [[Anthony Mangnall]], MP for [[Totnes (UK Parliament constituency)|Totnes]]; and [[Gary Streeter]], MP for [[South West Devon]]—submitted letters of no confidence in him.<ref name="Streeter letter">{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-60227531 |title=Boris Johnson facing further calls to resign amid parties row |work=[[BBC]]|date=2 February 2022|access-date=2 February 2022}}</ref> By 3 February 2022, ITV News reported that 12 Conservative MPs, 21 MSPs (including [[Douglas Ross (Scottish politician)|Douglas Ross]], who is both an MP and MSP), and two peers had called for Johnson's resignation,<ref name="itv.com">{{Cite web|url=https://www.itv.com/news/2022-01-13/here-are-the-tories-calling-for-boris-johnson-to-resign|title=These are the Tories calling for Boris Johnson to resign as prime minister|date=2 February 2022|website=ITV News}}</ref> and ''BBC News'' reported that 17 MPs had submitted letters of no confidence.<ref name="bbc.com">{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60227531|title=Boris Johnson facing further calls to resign amid parties row|work=BBC News|date=3 February 2022}}</ref> [[Roger Gale]] and [[Charles Walker (British politician)|Charles Walker]] both wanted Johnson to resign.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{Cite news |last=Bryant |first=Miranda |date=2022-02-05 |title=Full list of Tory MPs who have urged Boris Johnson to stand down |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/05/full-list-of-tory-mps-who-have-urged-boris-johnson-to-stand-down |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref><ref name="ReferenceB">{{Cite news |last1=Helm |first1=Toby |last2=Townsend |first2=Mark |last3=Tapper |first3=James |date=2022-02-05 |title='Partygate': Johnson's removal is now inevitable, warns loyalist |work=The Observer |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/05/partygate-johnsons-removal-is-now-inevitable-warns-loyalist |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0029-7712}}</ref>
On 4 February, [[Newcastle-under-Lyme (UK Parliament constituency)|Newcastle]] MP [[Aaron Bell (politician)|Aaron Bell]] said he had submitted a letter of no confidence.<ref name="bbc4/2/2022"/><ref name="ReferenceC">{{Cite web |last=Stewart |first=Heather |date=4 February 2022 |title=Tory MP Aaron Bell goes public on letter of no confidence in Boris Johnson |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/04/aaron-bell-is-latest-tory-mp-to-submit-letter-of-no-confidence-in-boris-johnson |website=The Guardian}}</ref> The same day, [[Nick Gibb]], former [[Minister of State for School Standards]], also submitted one.<ref name="The Independent">{{Cite news |last=Churchman |first=Laurie |date=4 February 2022 |title=Ex-minister Nick Gibb calls for Boris Johnson to resign |work=The Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/nick-gibb-no-confidence-letter-boris-johnson-b2008159.html |url-status=live |url-access=subscription |access-date=4 February 2022 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/nick-gibb-no-confidence-letter-boris-johnson-b2008159.html |archive-date=25 May 2022}}</ref> [[Alec Shelbrooke]], the MP for [[Elmet and Rothwell]], described Johnson's position as, "indefensible".<ref name="ReferenceD">{{Cite news |last1=Allegretti |first1=Aubrey |last2=Stewart |first2=Heather |date=2022-02-18 |title=No 10 staff facing police over Partygate can see notes on their evidence |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2022/feb/18/no-10-staff-facing-police-over-partygate-can-see-notes-on-their-evidence |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> On 6 February, Tory MPs [[Iain Duncan Smith]] and [[Kwasi Kwarteng]] both called for Johnson to be given more time.<ref name="Mason">{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/feb/06/give-boris-johnson-time-to-fix-his-crises-says-iain-duncan-smith |last=Mason |first=Rowena |title=Give Boris Johnson time to fix his crises, says Iain Duncan Smith |work=The Guardian |date=6 February 2022 |access-date=8 February 2022}}</ref>
On 10 February, former Conservative prime minister [[John Major]] said Johnson, "broke lockdown laws" and added in his opinion the government felt it "need not obey the rules. ... Brazen excuses were dreamed up. Day after day the public was asked to believe the unbelievable. Ministers were sent out to defend the indefensible – making themselves look gullible or foolish".<ref name="ReferenceE">{{Cite news |last=Parkinson |first=Justin |date=10 February 2022 |title=Johnson broke law over No 10 parties, says ex-PM Sir John Major |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60331189 }}</ref>
Following the 2022 [[Russian invasion of Ukraine]] on 24 February, Ross withdrew his letter of no confidence, saying he felt a leadership contest would be inappropriate during the conflict,<ref name="ReferenceF">{{Cite news |date=29 March 2022 |title=Partygate: Met Police to issue first fines, sources say |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-60906757 |access-date=29 March 2022}}</ref> as did Bridgen.<ref name="g_20220328" />
[[Alex Chalk]], the [[Solicitor General for England and Wales|Solicitor General]] and MP for [[Cheltenham (UK Parliament constituency)|Cheltenham]], would not defend Johnson after Johnson received an FPN; instead Chalk said senior politicians should "act in a way that is beyond reproach". [[Karen Bradley]], former [[Secretary of State for Northern Ireland]] and MP for [[Staffordshire Moorlands (UK Parliament constituency)|Staffordshire Moorlands]], and [[Neil Hudson (politician)|Neil Hudson]], MP for [[Penrith and The Border]], among others, criticised Johnson. Bradley said: "I do wish to make it clear that if I had been a minister found to have broken the laws that I passed, I would be tendering my resignation now." Hudson said he would not "defend the indefensible" and it was "extremely disappointing" that Johnson and Sunak had been given FPNs. Hudson added, "The fact that the lawmakers went on to break those very laws they brought in to keep us all safe is deeply damaging for our democracy. That situation is untenable." Bradley and Hudson both expressed unease about changing prime minister during the [[2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine]].<ref name="ReferenceG">{{Cite news |last=Allegretti |first=Aubrey |date=2022-04-14 |title=Fresh calls for Boris Johnson to resign over Partygate fines |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/14/boris-johnson-refuses-to-say-whether-he-will-resign-over-covid-fines |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> Caroline Nokes called on Johnson to resign for a second time.<ref name="Observer17/4/2022">{{Cite news |last1=Savage |first1=Michael |last2=Townsend |first2=Mark |date=16 April 2022 |title=Senior Conservatives fear traditional voters will desert them after Partygate |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/16/senior-conservatives-partygate-fines-boris-johnson-rishi-sunak}}</ref>
On 13 April, Tory backbencher [[Nigel Mills]], MP for [[Amber Valley (UK Parliament constituency)|Amber Valley]], said that Johnson's position was "untenable". Separately, Justice Minister [[Lord David Wolfson]] resigned in protest.<ref name="independent.co.uk">{{Cite news|last=Woodcock |first=Andrew |date=13 April 2022 |title=Blow to PM as minister quits over his failure to resign after Partygate fine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-parties-resign-wolfson-b2057439.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-parties-resign-wolfson-b2057439.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=13 April 2022 |work=Independent}}</ref><ref name="ReferenceH">{{Cite news |last=Allegretti |first=Aubrey |date=13 April 2022 |title=Justice minister resigns over No 10 Partygate revelations |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/13/justice-minister-david-wolfson-resigns-partygate-boris-johnson |access-date=13 April 2022 |work=The Guardian}}</ref>
On 19 April, senior Conservative MP [[Mark Harper]] said Johnson was now "no longer worthy" of remaining Prime Minister.<ref name="Forrest">{{cite news |last=Forrest |first=Adam |date=19 April 2022 |title=Boris Johnson 'no longer worthy' of being PM says senior Tory MP calling for resignation |work=Independent |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-resignation-tory-mark-harper-b2060965.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220525/https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/boris-johnson-resignation-tory-mark-harper-b2060965.html |archive-date=25 May 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live |access-date=19 April 2022}}</ref> On 21 April, Tory MP [[Steve Baker (politician)|Steve Baker]], the former chair of the [[European Research Group]], publicly stated that "the gig is up" and that Johnson should be "long gone by now".<ref name="Reuters">{{cite news |last1=James |first1=William |last2=Smout |first2=Alistair |date=21 April 2022 |title=Influential UK lawmaker tells PM Johnson: 'The gig's up' |publisher=Reuters |url=https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/influential-uk-lawmaker-tells-pm-johnson-gigs-up-2022-04-21/ |url-status=live |url-access=registration |access-date=22 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220421125141/https://www.reuters.com/world/uk/influential-uk-lawmaker-tells-pm-johnson-gigs-up-2022-04-21/ |archive-date=21 April 2022}}</ref> On 23 April, [[Toby Helm]] and [[Michael Savage]] of ''The Guardian'' suggested that increasing numbers of Conservative MPs believed Johnson needed to be replaced as prime minister soon, and that many were waiting until after the result of the [[2022 United Kingdom local elections|May 2022 local elections]] to decide whether to replace him.<ref name="ReferenceI">{{Cite news |last1=Helm |first1=Toby |last2=Savage |first2=Michael |date=2022-04-23 |title=Boris Johnson should go sooner rather than later, say top Tory MPs |work=The Observer |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/apr/23/boris-johnson-should-go-sooner-rather-than-later-say-top-tory-mps |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0029-7712}}</ref> Barry Macleod-Cullinane, who was deputy leader in [[Harrow, London]], said: "We now know that Boris Johnson broke the law and has lied repeatedly to parliament and to us. He's taking us for fools – and we can't let him get away with it."<ref name="independent4/5/2022">{{Cite web |last=Merrick |first=Rob |date=2022-05-04 |title=Boris Johnson hit by grassroots Tory revolt over Partygate as voters go to the polls |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/local-elections-boris-johnson-partygate-b2071636.html |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=The Independent}}</ref>
Following the publication of the Sue Gray report, [[Julian Sturdy]], [[John Baron (politician)|John Baron]], [[David Simmonds]] and [[Stephen Hammond]] called for Johnson to resign.<ref name="mirror.co.uk">{{Cite news |last1=Buchan |first1=Lizzy |last2=Updated |date=26 May 2022 |title=Senior Tory demands Boris Johnson resigns – and suggests he lied to Parliament |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/politics/breaking-senior-tory-demands-boris-27069702 |access-date=26 May 2022 |work=The Mirror}}</ref> On 27 May, [[Paul Holmes (Eastleigh MP)|Paul Holmes]], MP for [[Eastleigh (UK Parliament constituency)|Eastleigh]], resigned from his position as Parliamentary Private Secretary to Home Secretary [[Priti Patel]] over concerns around "toxic culture" at the heart of government he said was described in the report.<ref name="thenational.scot">{{Cite web |last=Webster |first=Laura |date=27 May 2022 |title=Tory MP resigns from UK Government over partygate 'distress' |url=https://www.thenational.scot/news/20170974.tory-mp-paul-holmes-resigns-uk-government-partygate-distress/ |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=The National}}</ref> Discontent with Johnson among Conservative MPs was growing and many MPs feared losing their seats.<ref name="ReferenceJ">{{Cite news |last=Clinton |first=Jane |date=2022-05-28 |title=Tory discontent with Boris Johnson spreads as MPs fear losing seats |work=The Guardian |url=https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2022/may/28/boris-johnson-tory-mps-discontent-losing-seats-partygate |access-date=2023-03-27 |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> In the days following the Sue Gray report, further MPs ([[Elliot Colburn]], [[Bob Neill]], [[Alicia Kearns]], [[John Stevenson (British politician)|John Stevenson]]<ref name="news.sky.com">{{Cite web |last=Culbertson |first=Alix |date=1 June 2022 |title=Partygate: John Stevenson submits letter of no confidence in Johnson – 28th Tory MP to publicly call for him to go |url=https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-john-stevenson-submits-letter-of-no-confidence-in-johnson-28th-tory-mp-to-publicly-call-for-him-to-go-12624754 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=Sky News}}</ref> and [[Steve Brine]]) publicly declared that they had submitted letters of no confidence, whilst others such as Jeremy Wright called for Johnson's resignation without declaring whether they had submitted a letter.<ref name="bbc.co.uk">{{Cite news |date=2022-05-30 |title=Ex-attorney general Jeremy Wright joins Tory calls for PM to quit |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-politics-61636151 |access-date=2023-03-27}}</ref> Andrew Bridgen also stated that he had resubmitted his letter after previously withdrawing it.<ref name="ReferenceK">{{Cite web |last=Culbertson |first=Alix |date=30 May 2022 |title=Partygate: Three more Tory MPs urge Boris Johnson to quit – how many now want him to resign? |url=https://news.sky.com/story/partygate-three-more-tory-mps-urge-boris-johnson-to-quit-how-many-now-want-him-to-resign-12624248 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=Sky News}}</ref> On 31 May 2022, [[Andrea Leadsom]], a former Conservative minister, wrote, "it is painfully clear to me that given the extent and severity of rule-breaking taking place over a 20-month period, it is extremely unlikely that senior leaders were unaware of what was going on".<ref name="ReferenceL">{{Cite web |last=Culbertson |first=Alix |date=31 May 2022 |title=Partygate: Ex-cabinet minister Andrea Leadsom condemns Boris Johnson's 'unacceptable failings of leadership' |url=https://news.sky.com/story/ex-cabinet-minister-andrea-leadsom-condemns-boris-johnsons-unacceptable-failings-of-leadership-over-partygate-12624626 |access-date=2023-03-27 |website=Sky News}}</ref>
On 1 June 2022, two of Johnson's cabinet colleagues challenged the speculation that he would have to resign. The culture secretary, [[Nadine Dorries]], said that the "overwhelming" majority of party MPs still backed him.<ref name=":19">{{Cite news |date=1 June 2022 |title=Boris Johnson victim of Tory leadership plotters, says ally Nadine Dorries |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-politics-61657209}}</ref> Dorries said that Partygate was now only a [[Westminster bubble]] issue as the public was "ready to move on" and that "The people who most want to get rid of Boris Johnson are Keir Starmer and the SNP".<ref name=":19" />
====Confidence vote==== {{See also|2022 vote of confidence in the Conservative Party leadership of Boris Johnson}}
On 6 June 2022, the threshold for the number of letters of no confidence, from Conservative MPs, was reached and a confidence vote in [[Boris Johnson]]'s leadership was called.
